items.situation: An arriving aircraft checks in with the correct ATIS code and adds, 'have numbers.' Another controller wants to restate the runway, wind, and altimeter anyway and skip the traffic pattern entry instruction and current wind shear advisory because the pilot already has the ATIS.
items.para_context: Under 3-10-1, landing information already contained in the ATIS may be omitted when the pilot states the appropriate ATIS code. Runway, wind, and altimeter may also be omitted when the pilot uses the phrase 'have numbers.' Even then, issue the remaining applicable landing information, such as traffic pattern information, supplementary information, requested position reports, low-level wind shear or microburst advisories, braking action, and other required arrival information.

choices.text: Omit the ATIS items as authorized, including runway, wind, and altimeter when the pilot has the ATIS and says 'have numbers,' but still issue the applicable pattern entry and any other required supplemental information such as wind shear.
Repeat the runway, wind, and altimeter every time, because 'have numbers' does not allow any omission of landing information.
Omit all further landing information once the pilot reports the ATIS code, because ATIS receipt replaces the rest of the arrival briefing.
Issue only the landing clearance, because traffic pattern information and supplementary advisories are optional whenever ATIS is current.

items.explanation: Per 3-10-1, ATIS-covered items may be omitted when authorized, and 'have numbers' permits omission of runway, wind, and altimeter, but the controller still issues the other applicable landing information.

items.situation: An aircraft has already started the takeoff roll when a traffic-management issue appears with its EDCT. Another controller wants to cancel the takeoff clearance immediately so the slot can be reworked.
items.para_context: Under 3-9-11, cancel a previously issued takeoff clearance and inform the pilot of the reason if circumstances require. Once an aircraft has started the takeoff roll, cancel the takeoff clearance only for the purpose of safety. The note states a takeoff clearance must not be canceled after takeoff roll begins solely to meet traffic management requirements or EDCT.

choices.text: Do not cancel the takeoff clearance for the EDCT issue alone, because once the roll has begun a cancellation is used only for safety.
Cancel the takeoff clearance immediately and cite the EDCT, because traffic-management compliance takes priority over a takeoff already in progress.
Wait until rotation and then cancel the takeoff clearance in the air, because the safety-only limitation applies only before liftoff.
Keep the aircraft rolling but cancel the beacon code instead, because that meets the traffic-management requirement without affecting the takeoff clearance.

items.explanation: Per 3-9-11, once takeoff roll has started, a takeoff clearance is canceled only for safety, not for EDCT or traffic-management purposes.

items.situation: A departing IFR military turbojet that is not a transport or cargo type launches from your runway. Another controller wants to have it switch frequency and squawk changes immediately after liftoff at 1,000 feet AGL because nearby traffic will need urgent instructions if something develops.
items.para_context: Under 3-9-3, issue departure control frequency and beacon code before takeoff, with the departure frequency omittable when a SID is assigned and the frequency is published on it. For departing IFR military turboprop and turbojet aircraft, except transport and cargo types, inform them before takeoff to change to departure control frequency. After takeoff, do not request those military aircraft to make radio frequency or radar beacon changes before they reach 2,500 feet above the surface. If the local controller has departure frequency override, urgent instructions may be transmitted on that frequency; otherwise use the emergency frequency.

choices.text: Give the military aircraft the published before-takeoff change-to-departure instruction, but do not request further radio or beacon changes after takeoff until it reaches 2,500 feet above the surface; use override or emergency procedures for urgent instructions if needed.
Have the aircraft contact departure and make any needed beacon change immediately after liftoff, because the 2,500-foot restriction applies only to civil aircraft.
Delay all departure-control instructions until the aircraft is airborne, because military turbojets are not given change-to-departure instructions before takeoff.
Use the SID frequency omission rule to omit both the departure frequency and the beacon code whenever a SID is assigned.

items.explanation: Per 3-9-3, special military departure-control instructions apply before takeoff, and post-takeoff radio or beacon changes are withheld until 2,500 feet above the surface for the listed military aircraft types.

items.situation: You are issuing a departure clearance to an aircraft that will operate in an approved altitude reservation. Another controller wants to skip the ALTRV phrase because the mission name is already in the flight data and they think the approval is implied.
items.para_context: Under 4-2-7, when the aircraft will operate in an approved ALTRV, use the phrase 'via approved altitude reservation (mission name) flight plan.' The note explains that an ALTRV normally includes the departure, climb, cruise, and arrival phases of flight up to and including the holding pattern or the point and time at which ATC provides separation between aircraft.

choices.text: Include the published ALTRV phraseology, 'via approved altitude reservation (mission name) flight plan,' because the clearance must identify operation in the approved altitude reservation.
Omit the ALTRV phrase as long as the mission name appears in the flight data, because the reservation is implied once the route is accepted.
Use only 'via mission flight plan' because altitude reservation wording is reserved for en route amendments, not the initial clearance.
Issue the ALTRV phrase only after the aircraft reaches cruise altitude, because the reservation does not normally include departure and climb.

items.explanation: Per 4-2-7, the clearance explicitly states operation via the approved altitude reservation flight plan.

items.clearance: TRAFFIC ALERT N123AB TWELVE O'CLOCK ONE MILE ADVISE YOU TURN LEFT IMMEDIATELY.

choices.text: Traffic alert, turning left immediately, N123AB.
Traffic alert, maintaining present course, N123AB.
Low altitude alert, turning left immediately, N123AB.
Traffic in sight, no turn required, N123AB.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-6, a traffic alert may direct an immediate turn, climb, or descent when an aircraft is in unsafe proximity to other traffic.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: LOW ALTITUDE ALERT N123AB CHECK YOUR ALTITUDE IMMEDIATELY.
items.display_text: LOW ALTITUDE ALERT N123AB CHECK YOUR HEADING IMMEDIATELY
items.explanation: Per 2-1-6, the terrain or obstruction alert instructs the pilot to check altitude immediately.

items.situation: You observe an aircraft descending toward terrain below a safe altitude. The pilot has not yet reported the problem, and you are the first controller to recognize it.
items.para_context: Issue a safety alert immediately when you are aware an aircraft is in unsafe proximity to terrain, obstructions, or other aircraft.

choices.text: Issue a safety alert immediately. Once the pilot reports taking action to resolve the situation, you may discontinue further alerts.
Wait to see whether the pilot reports the unsafe situation before transmitting an alert.
Assume the next controller has already seen the problem and will handle it.
Coordinate with a supervisor first and issue the alert only if workload permits.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-6, issue the safety alert when you are aware of the unsafe proximity and do not assume another controller has already done so.

items.instruction: The controller issued this transmission. Which pilot acknowledgment is correct?
items.clearance: CONTACT GROUND POINT SEVEN.

choices.text: Ground point seven, United 234.
Monitor ground point seven, United 234.
Ground point eight, United 234.
United 234, contact tower point seven.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-17, the transfer should specify the facility/frequency and the pilot should acknowledge the assigned contact frequency accurately.

items.situation: An aircraft is about to enter the receiving controller's area of jurisdiction. No special coordination or letter of agreement changes the normal transfer procedure.
items.para_context: Transfer radio communications before the aircraft enters the receiving controller's area unless otherwise coordinated or specified by a letter of agreement or facility directive.

choices.text: Transfer the aircraft before it enters the receiving controller's area of jurisdiction.
Wait until the aircraft checks in with the receiving controller on its own.
Delay the transfer until five minutes after the aircraft enters the next sector.
Keep the aircraft on your frequency until the pilot requests the next frequency.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-17, the normal rule is to transfer communications before the aircraft enters the receiving controller's area.

items.situation: A VFR aircraft is following a Heavy aircraft and is not being radar vectored. In your judgment, wake turbulence could adversely affect the smaller aircraft.
items.para_context: Issue wake turbulence cautionary advisories when required and include the word Heavy or Super when that traffic category is known.

choices.text: Issue a wake turbulence cautionary advisory with traffic position, altitude if known, direction of flight, and identify the traffic as Heavy.
Do not issue anything because the pilot is responsible for wake turbulence avoidance in all cases.
Issue traffic information only and omit any wake turbulence reference.
Wait until the pilot reports a wake encounter before providing any cautionary information.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-20, issue the wake turbulence cautionary advisory when the rule applies and identify Super/Heavy traffic in the advisory.

items.situation: Two VFR aircraft outside Class B/Class C airspace are converging on your frequency. No formal separation minima apply, but in your judgment their proximity warrants controller action.
items.para_context: Issue traffic advisories when proximity warrants it, even where no separation minima apply.

choices.text: Issue traffic advisories to both aircraft and provide additional assistance such as vectors only if requested and within your jurisdiction or after coordination.
Do nothing because traffic advisories are not used when no separation minima apply.
Issue vectors immediately whether or not the aircraft are in your jurisdiction.
Wait until one pilot reports the other traffic in sight before making any advisory transmission.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-21, traffic advisories are issued when, in your judgment, proximity warrants them, including some situations where no separation minima apply.

items.situation: An aircraft is flying an instrument approach and will remain on your approach control frequency through the final approach fix.
items.para_context: Approach/arrival control or GCA must issue the wheels down check before the aircraft passes the outer marker/final approach fix when the aircraft remains on the facility frequency.

choices.text: Issue the wheels down check before the aircraft passes the outer marker or final approach fix.
Wait until after touchdown clearance is issued, then remind the pilot.
Do not issue the check unless the pilot specifically asks for it.
Issue the check only after the aircraft reports the runway in sight.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-25, the wheels down check is a reminder to the pilot and must be issued at the prescribed point in the approach.

items.situation: Ground control wants to authorize a vehicle to cross an active runway. The runway crossing point is known and the operation has not yet been coordinated with local control.
items.para_context: Ground control must obtain approval from local control before authorizing an aircraft or vehicle to cross or use any portion of an active runway, including the point/intersection involved.

choices.text: Obtain local control approval first and coordinate the point or intersection where the runway operation will occur.
Authorize the crossing as long as the vehicle operator can see the runway environment.
Issue the crossing and advise local control after the vehicle is clear.
Use a standing facility approval instead of coordinating the specific point or intersection.

items.explanation: Per 3-1-3, positive coordination and approval from local control are required before crossing or using any portion of an active runway.

items.situation: A departure is holding in position on one runway while another aircraft has just begun departure on an intersecting runway and has not yet passed the intersection.
items.para_context: Do not allow the second departure to begin takeoff roll until one of the required intersecting-runway conditions exists.

choices.text: Hold the second departure until the preceding aircraft has departed and passed the intersection or is turning to avert any conflict.
Launch the second departure as soon as the first aircraft rotates.
Issue takeoff clearance if both pilots report each other in sight.
Launch the second departure if runway centerline lights are operating at high intensity.

items.explanation: Per 3-9-8, intersecting-runway operations require the controller to ensure one of the specified conditions exists before the next takeoff roll begins.

items.instruction: The controller issued this altitude instruction. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: CLIMB AND MAINTAIN ONE THREE THOUSAND.

choices.text: Climb and maintain one three thousand, United 234.
Descend and maintain one three thousand, United 234.
Climb and maintain one four thousand, United 234.
Maintain three thousand, United 234.

items.explanation: Per 4-5-7, altitude instructions are issued with standard phraseology and the pilot readback should preserve the assigned altitude accurately.

items.situation: You are issuing a cruise clearance to an airport clearance limit and the aircraft will use an unpublished route segment before reaching any point where altitude information is available to the pilot.
items.para_context: Issue an appropriate crossing altitude to assure IFR obstruction clearance until the aircraft reaches a fix, point, or route where altitude information is available.

choices.text: Include an appropriate crossing altitude that protects IFR obstruction clearance until the aircraft reaches a point where altitude information is available.
Issue cruise only; no crossing altitude is needed when the route segment is unpublished.
Issue a visual approach instead of an altitude instruction.
Delay the altitude assignment until the aircraft reports the airport in sight.

items.explanation: Per 4-5-7, an unpublished route used with an airport clearance limit requires a crossing altitude that maintains IFR obstruction clearance.

items.situation: You have completed a radar handoff and frequency change to the receiving controller under a facility directive, but another aircraft for which you still have separation responsibility could conflict with the flight if no further action is taken.
items.para_context: Transfer control only at a prescribed/coordinated location, time, fix, or altitude, or after an authorized handoff/frequency change, and only after eliminating any potential conflict with other aircraft for which you have separation responsibility.

choices.text: Do not transfer control yet; eliminate the potential conflict first, then complete the transfer under the authorized handoff procedure.
Transfer control immediately because radio communications have already been transferred.
Assume the receiving controller automatically accepted the conflict when the pilot changed frequency.
Wait until the aircraft exits your area of jurisdiction before taking any action.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-15, control transfer requires both an authorized transfer condition and elimination of any potential conflict for which you retain separation responsibility.

items.situation: A pilot reports a flock of geese at one o'clock, seven miles, northbound, last seen at four thousand feet. Another controller says a single advisory is enough and there is no need to keep passing the information or tell the next facility unless another pilot complains.
items.para_context: Under 2-1-23, issue advisory information on pilot-reported, tower-observed, or radar-observed and pilot-verified bird activity. Include position, species or size if known, course of flight, and altitude, and do so for at least 15 minutes unless later reports or observation show it is no longer a factor. Relay the information to adjacent facilities and FSSs whenever it appears it will become a factor in their areas.

choices.text: Issue bird activity advisories including position, species or size if known, course, and altitude for at least 15 minutes unless the activity is no longer a factor, and relay it to adjacent facilities and FSS if it may affect them.
Issue the advisory once, then stop using it unless another pilot asks about birds on frequency.
Pass only the altitude and position because bird species and direction are optional details that should not be transmitted.
Wait for radar confirmation before issuing any pilot-reported bird advisory.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-23, bird activity advisories include position, species or size if known, course, and altitude, continue for at least 15 minutes unless no longer a factor, and are relayed to adjacent facilities and FSS when appropriate.

items.situation: Operational responsibility for a radar position is being handed from one specialist to another during a position relief briefing.
items.para_context: Accomplish transfer of position responsibility in accordance with the SOP for Transfer of Position Responsibility and appropriate facility directives each time operational responsibility is transferred from one specialist to another.

choices.text: Use the SOP for Transfer of Position Responsibility and the applicable facility directives for the handoff.
Use an informal briefing only if both specialists are familiar with the traffic.
Skip the formal transfer if the relieving specialist monitored the position for several minutes.
Delay the transfer until the next scheduled traffic lull even if the position must change immediately.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-24, every transfer of position responsibility must follow the SOP and appropriate facility directives.

items.situation: You discover that an aircraft's equipment suffix is wrong in automation. A trainee wants to correct it by typing a new slant suffix directly into the strip because it is faster than touching the ICAO equipment codes.
items.para_context: Under 2-3-8, the aircraft equipment suffix is generated by automation from the aircraft's CNS equipment codes. To change the suffix, amend the CNS equipment codes so automation translates them into the proper suffix. Directly changing the suffix with a slant entry may unintentionally alter or delete other equipment codes.

choices.text: Amend the CNS equipment codes so automation generates the proper suffix instead of directly overwriting the suffix with a slant entry.
Type the new slant suffix directly because the suffix is the only part of the aircraft equipment data that matters to ATC.
Delete the aircraft suffix entirely and let the adjacent facility restore it on the next handoff.
Tell the pilot to change the suffix over the radio because controllers may not amend equipment codes.

items.explanation: Per 2-3-8, suffix changes are made by amending CNS equipment codes, not by directly substituting a slant suffix.

items.situation: Two aircraft on frequency have similar sounding call signs. A controller wants to keep using abbreviated replies to save time because both pilots have already checked in.
items.para_context: Under 2-4-20, use the full identification in reply to aircraft with similar sounding identifications. For other aircraft, the same identification the pilot used may be used in reply, except that the correct identification must be used after communications have been established.

choices.text: Use the full identification when replying to the similar sounding aircraft so the call signs remain distinct.
Use abbreviated replies anyway because once communications are established similar sounding aircraft no longer require special handling.
Use the pilot's first name instead of the call sign to avoid confusion.
Stop replying to one of the aircraft until the frequencies can be split.

items.explanation: Per 2-4-20, full identification is used in reply to aircraft with similar sounding identifications.

items.instruction: The controller issued this instruction. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: HOLD SHORT OF RUNWAY ONE EIGHT.

choices.text: Hold short of Runway One Eight, Cessna Three Alpha Bravo.
Roger, Cessna Three Alpha Bravo.
Hold short of Runway One Seven, Cessna Three Alpha Bravo.
Cross Runway One Eight, Cessna Three Alpha Bravo.

items.explanation: Per 2-4-3, hold short instructions require an accurate readback and the acknowledgment should include the aircraft call sign or registration.

items.situation: A pilot reads back a taxi clearance but omits the hold short instruction and does not use the aircraft call sign in the acknowledgment.
items.para_context: Ensure pilots acknowledge ATC instructions, ensure hold short instructions are read back, and ensure pilots use call signs or registration numbers in the acknowledgment.

choices.text: Obtain a correct readback of the hold short instruction and ensure the acknowledgment includes the aircraft call sign or registration.
Accept the acknowledgment because any response implies compliance.
Wait to see whether the aircraft stops before correcting the readback.
Reissue only the call sign and ignore the omitted hold short instruction.

items.explanation: Per 2-4-3, the controller must ensure the hold short instruction is read back correctly and that the pilot acknowledgment identifies the aircraft.

items.situation: Ground control has taxied Citation 52 to Runway 27 at Taxiway Charlie for an intersection departure, and local control has not yet been advised.
items.para_context: Ground and local controllers must exchange aircraft identification and applicable runway, intersection, and taxiway information, including intersection departures and departures taxied to a runway other than one previously designated as active.

choices.text: Notify local control of Citation 52 and the runway/intersection information before the aircraft is released to local control.
Wait until the aircraft calls ready for departure on local control frequency before passing the information.
Advise local control only if wake turbulence separation will be required.
Assume the flight progress strip is sufficient even if local control has not been told about the intersection departure.

items.explanation: Per 3-1-4, local and ground controllers must exchange the aircraft ID and applicable runway/intersection information needed for safe runway operations.

items.situation: A maintenance vehicle is still known to be near the departure runway, and a departing aircraft is ready for takeoff.
items.para_context: Ensure that the runway to be used is free of all known ground vehicles, equipment, and personnel before a departing aircraft starts takeoff or a landing aircraft crosses the runway threshold.

choices.text: Keep the aircraft from departing until the runway is free of all known vehicles, equipment, and personnel.
Issue takeoff clearance as long as the vehicle is in two-way communications with ATC.
Tell the vehicle to proceed as requested on the runway while the aircraft departs.
Issue takeoff clearance if the pilot reports the vehicle in sight.

items.explanation: Per 3-1-5, the runway must be free of all known vehicles, equipment, and personnel before takeoff or before a landing aircraft crosses the threshold.

items.instruction: The controller issued this clearance. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: RUNWAY ONE EIGHT, LINE UP AND WAIT.

choices.text: Runway One Eight, line up and wait, United Five.
Runway One Eight, cleared for takeoff, United Five.
Behind landing traffic, line up and wait, United Five.
Runway One Seven, line up and wait, United Five.

items.explanation: Per 3-9-4, LUAW uses standard phraseology with the runway number stated first and does not substitute a takeoff clearance or conditional phrase.

items.situation: At a facility without full core safety logic protection, one aircraft is holding in position on Runway 23 and another aircraft is about to be cleared for a full-stop landing on the same runway.
items.para_context: Do not clear an aircraft for a full-stop, touch-and-go, stop-and-go, low approach, or option on the same runway with an aircraft holding in position or taxiing to line up and wait until the aircraft in position has exited the runway or starts takeoff roll.

choices.text: Withhold the landing clearance until the aircraft in position exits the runway or starts takeoff roll.
Issue the landing clearance as long as both pilots report each other in sight.
Issue the landing clearance if the aircraft in position has been waiting less than 90 seconds.
Tell the aircraft in position to monitor traffic and continue holding while the arrival lands.

items.explanation: Per 3-9-4, same-runway landing and LUAW combinations are restricted until the aircraft in position exits or starts takeoff roll, unless the authorized safety logic exception applies.

items.instruction: The controller issued this clearance. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: RUNWAY TWO-SEVEN SHORTENED, CLEARED FOR TAKEOFF.

choices.text: Runway Two-Seven shortened, cleared for takeoff, American 482.
Runway Two-Seven, cleared for takeoff, American 482.
Runway Two-Six shortened, cleared for takeoff, American 482.
Runway Two-Seven shortened, line up and wait, American 482.

items.explanation: Per 3-9-10, when a runway has been shortened, the takeoff clearance must include the word 'shortened' as part of the runway identification.

items.situation: Construction has temporarily shortened the departure runway, and you are about to issue takeoff clearance to an aircraft at the full length.
items.para_context: Whenever a runway length has been temporarily or permanently shortened, state the word 'shortened' immediately following the runway number as part of the takeoff clearance.

choices.text: Issue the takeoff clearance with the word 'shortened' immediately after the runway number.
Use the normal takeoff clearance and mention the shortened runway only if the pilot asks.
Use the term 'full length' because the aircraft is departing from the end of the runway.
Delay the mention of the shortened runway until after the aircraft starts takeoff roll.

items.explanation: Per 3-9-10, the word 'shortened' is mandatory in the takeoff clearance whenever the runway length is temporarily or permanently shortened.

items.situation: You are issuing a clearance to an airborne USAF aircraft and must assign an altitude. You also want to include a frequency change, a heading, and an altimeter setting.
items.para_context: When issuing a clearance to an airborne USAF aircraft containing an altitude assignment, do not include more than one of the listed items in the same transmission, including a frequency change, heading, or altimeter setting.

choices.text: Split the instructions so the altitude assignment is combined with no more than one of those listed items in the same transmission.
Issue all of the items together in one transmission so the pilot hears everything at once.
Omit the altitude assignment and issue only the heading, frequency, and altimeter setting.
Issue the heading and altimeter setting with the altitude, then give the frequency change later because only frequency changes are restricted.

items.explanation: Per 4-2-1, an altitude assignment to an airborne USAF aircraft may be combined with no more than one of the listed additional items in the same transmission.

items.instruction: The controller issued this release instruction. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: N123AB HOLD FOR RELEASE, EXPECT TEN MINUTES DEPARTURE DELAY.

choices.text: Hold for release, expect ten minutes departure delay, N123AB.
Released for departure, N123AB.
Hold for release, expect one minute departure delay, N123AB.
Hold short for release, N123AB.

items.explanation: Per 4-3-4, hold for release instructions tell the pilot the departure clearance is not valid until further instructions and must include departure delay information.

items.situation: An IFR aircraft at an airport without an operating control tower cannot be released immediately, and the pilot must not depart until additional instructions are received.
items.para_context: Departures from an airport without an operating control tower must be issued either a departure release, a hold for release, or a release time, and hold for release instructions must include departure delay information.

choices.text: Issue hold for release instructions and include the expected departure delay.
Issue the clearance and let the pilot depart when ready since the airport is uncontrolled.
Delay all instructions until the pilot calls airborne.
Issue only a void time because hold for release never applies at uncontrolled airports.

items.explanation: Per 4-3-4, untowered departures require a departure release, hold for release, or release time, and hold for release includes expected delay information.

items.instruction: The controller issued this approach clearance. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: AT RDFSH, CLEARED ILS RUNWAY TWO SEVEN APPROACH.

choices.text: At RDFSH, cleared ILS Runway Two Seven approach, United 234.
Cleared visual approach Runway Two Seven, United 234.
At RDFSH, cleared ILS Runway Two Eight approach, United 234.
Direct to the final approach fix, cleared ILS Runway Two Seven approach, United 234.

items.explanation: Per 4-8-1, the approach clearance should preserve the published approach name and any clearance limit or fix stated by ATC.

items.situation: An aircraft is operating on an unpublished route and you want to clear it for an RNAV approach before it reaches a published segment of the procedure.
items.para_context: For aircraft on unpublished routes, issue the approach clearance only after the aircraft is established on a segment of a published route or instrument approach procedure, or assign an altitude to maintain until the aircraft is established on a published segment.

choices.text: Assign an altitude that protects IFR obstruction clearance until the aircraft is established on the published segment, then issue the approach clearance.
Clear the aircraft for the approach immediately because the pilot can descend at discretion to join the procedure.
Clear the aircraft direct to the final approach fix regardless of whether it is an IAF.
Wait to issue any altitude until the aircraft reports the runway environment in sight.

items.explanation: Per 4-8-1, approach clearance on unpublished routing requires either established status on a published segment or an altitude to maintain until established.

items.instruction: The controller issued this taxi clearance. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: CROSS RUNWAY TWO-EIGHT LEFT AT TAXIWAY ALPHA, HOLD SHORT OF RUNWAY TWO-EIGHT RIGHT.

choices.text: Cross Runway Two-Eight Left at Alpha, hold short of Runway Two-Eight Right, Cessna Two One Alfa.
Cross both runways at Alpha, Cessna Two One Alfa.
Hold short of Runway Two-Eight Left at Alpha, Cessna Two One Alfa.
Taxi to Runway Two-Eight Right via Alpha, Cessna Two One Alfa.

items.explanation: Per 3-7-2, taxi and crossing instructions must specify the route to follow and where to hold short when required.

items.situation: An aircraft is taxiing to Runway 27 from the ramp. The route requires crossing one runway and holding short of another before reaching the departure runway.
items.para_context: Issue the specific taxi route in concise terms, confirm the correct runway assignment when taxiing to a runway, and include hold short instructions where required.

choices.text: State the departure runway followed by the specific taxi route, include the required crossing and hold short instructions, and confirm the aircraft has the correct runway assignment.
Tell the aircraft to taxi to the active runway and work out the route while moving.
Issue only the crossing clearance because the pilot is responsible for the rest of the route on the movement area.
Withhold the departure runway designation until the aircraft reaches the hold short line.

items.explanation: Per 3-7-2, taxi clearances must include the specific route to follow and, when taxiing to a runway, confirmation of the correct runway assignment.

items.instruction: The controller issued this hold instruction. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: HOLD SHORT OF RUNWAY TWO-SEVEN, LANDING TRAFFIC.

choices.text: Hold short of Runway Two-Seven, landing traffic, Skyhawk Five One Two.
Cross Runway Two-Seven after the landing traffic, Skyhawk Five One Two.
Hold short of Taxiway Two-Seven, Skyhawk Five One Two.
Proceed to the runway, Skyhawk Five One Two.

items.explanation: Per 3-7-4, hold instructions keep aircraft or vehicles clear of the runway and may include traffic information as necessary.

items.situation: A taxiing aircraft is approaching Runway 27 while another aircraft is on short final. You need to keep the taxiing aircraft clear of the runway.
items.para_context: Hold the aircraft or vehicle short of a specific runway or at a specified point, and issue traffic information as necessary.

choices.text: Instruct the aircraft to hold short of Runway 27 or at a specified point and include traffic information as necessary.
Tell the aircraft to continue toward the runway and decide where to stop on its own.
Allow the aircraft to enter the runway because the landing traffic is already in sight.
Issue only traffic information and omit the hold instruction unless the pilot asks for it.

items.explanation: Per 3-7-4, runway protection is accomplished with a specific hold instruction and traffic information as necessary.

items.situation: An arriving aircraft is on short final behind another aircraft using the same runway. The preceding aircraft has landed but no listed same-runway separation condition exists yet.
items.para_context: Ensure the arriving aircraft does not cross the landing threshold until one of the required same-runway separation conditions exists, unless otherwise authorized.

choices.text: Do not allow the following aircraft to cross the landing threshold until one of the required same-runway separation conditions exists.
Rely on a wake turbulence caution alone and continue the arrival.
Allow the arrival to continue as soon as the preceding aircraft touches down.
Issue landing clearance immediately because both aircraft are on the same frequency.

items.explanation: Per 3-10-3, same-runway separation requires the following arrival to remain outside the threshold until a listed condition exists.

items.instruction: The controller issued this landing clearance. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: RUNWAY ONE EIGHT, CLEARED TO LAND, HOLD SHORT OF RUNWAY ONE FOUR LEFT, TRAFFIC LANDING RUNWAY ONE FOUR LEFT.

choices.text: Runway One Eight, cleared to land, hold short of Runway One Four Left, traffic landing Runway One Four Left, United Two Thirty-Four.
Runway One Eight, cleared to land, United Two Thirty-Four.
Runway One Four Left, cleared to land, hold short of Runway One Eight, United Two Thirty-Four.
Runway One Eight, line up and wait, United Two Thirty-Four.

items.explanation: Per 3-10-5, landing clearances state the runway first, and LAHSO clearances require an accurate readback of the hold short instruction.

items.situation: You issue a land-and-hold-short clearance, but the pilot acknowledges the landing clearance without reading back the hold short instruction.
items.para_context: When issuing a clearance to land and hold short, exchange traffic information as necessary and request a readback of the hold short instruction when it is not received.

choices.text: Request a readback of the hold short instruction and ensure the necessary traffic information has been exchanged.
Assume the pilot understood the hold short instruction because the landing clearance was acknowledged.
Cancel the landing clearance immediately and close the runway.
Wait until the aircraft is on short final to see whether the pilot stops.

items.explanation: Per 3-10-5, LAHSO operations require the controller to exchange traffic information as necessary and request the hold short readback if it is not received.

items.situation: An arriving aircraft appears to have committed a regulatory violation on approach, but the exact reason is not yet clear and the situation could involve an emergency.
items.para_context: Do not withhold a landing clearance indefinitely because an apparent violation may actually be the result of an emergency, and assist the pilot to the extent possible.

choices.text: Do not withhold the landing clearance indefinitely; assist the pilot to the extent possible.
Withhold the landing clearance until enforcement implications are sorted out.
Require the pilot to explain the suspected violation before issuing any clearance.
Cancel all communication until a supervisor joins the frequency.

items.explanation: Per 3-10-8, an apparent violation is not a basis for indefinitely withholding landing clearance; the pilot may need assistance.

items.instruction: The controller issued this runway exit instruction. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: TURN RIGHT NEXT TAXIWAY, CROSS RUNWAY TWO ONE, CONTACT GROUND POINT SEVEN.

choices.text: Turn right next taxiway, cross Runway Two One, contact ground point seven, American Four Ninety-Two.
Turn right next taxiway, hold short of Runway Two One, American Four Ninety-Two.
Turn left next taxiway, cross Runway Two One, contact ground point seven, American Four Ninety-Two.
Cross Runway Two One after contact ground, American Four Ninety-Two.

items.explanation: Per 3-10-9, runway exiting instructions may include the turn-off point, a required crossing or hold short instruction, and the frequency change.

items.situation: After landing, an aircraft must enter another active runway to taxi clear of the landing runway before it can switch to ground control.
items.para_context: The local controller must provide taxi instructions when compliance with ATC instructions is required before switching to ground control or when the aircraft must enter an active runway to taxi clear of the landing runway.

choices.text: Issue the required taxi and runway instructions before the aircraft changes to ground control.
Tell the aircraft to contact ground immediately and let ground control resolve the runway crossing.
Assume the pilot will taxi clear without further instructions because pilots are expected to clear the runway.
Delay all runway exit instructions until the aircraft is already on the intersecting runway.

items.explanation: Per 3-10-9, the local controller retains responsibility for runway exit and taxi instructions needed before the aircraft can safely switch to ground control.

items.instruction: The controller issued this amendment. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: CESSNA TWO ONE ALFA, CLEARED DIRECT FRANK INTERSECTION.

choices.text: Cleared direct Frank intersection, Cessna Two One Alfa.
Cleared direct Delta V-O-R, Cessna Two One Alfa.
Change Frank intersection to read Delta, Cessna Two One Alfa.
Maintain as filed, Cessna Two One Alfa.

items.explanation: Per 4-2-5, one method of amending a previously issued route is to clear the aircraft direct to a point on the previously issued route.

items.situation: You are amending a previously issued clearance because the aircraft must now follow a different routing and comply with altitude restrictions already associated with the route.
items.para_context: Amend the route using an approved method and restate all applicable altitude restrictions when route or altitude in a previously issued clearance is amended.

choices.text: Issue the route amendment using approved amendment phraseology and restate all applicable altitude restrictions.
Issue only the routing change because previously assigned altitude restrictions remain implied.
Clear the aircraft direct to the destination and leave the pilot to infer the rest of the route.
Cancel the original clearance entirely before issuing any new routing information.

items.explanation: Per 4-2-5, route or altitude amendments must preserve the operationally relevant restrictions by restating all applicable altitude restrictions.

items.instruction: The controller issued this arrival clearance. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: ROSEWOOD ONE ARRIVAL, DELTA TRANSITION.

choices.text: Rosewood One arrival, Delta transition, United Three Twelve.
Rosewood One arrival, Runway Nine Right, United Three Twelve.
Delta arrival, Rosewood transition, United Three Twelve.
Cleared direct Rosewood, United Three Twelve.

items.explanation: Per 4-7-1, arrival clearance phraseology must include the STAR and transition, if necessary.

items.situation: A STAR transition serves multiple runways, but the runway assignment has not been issued by the time the aircraft is within 10 miles of the runway transition waypoint.
items.para_context: If the runway assignment or a subsequent runway change is not issued before 10 NM from the runway transition waypoint, radar vectors to final must be provided.

choices.text: Provide radar vectors to the final approach course.
Keep the aircraft on the STAR without assigning a runway and expect the pilot to self-select.
Clear the aircraft for the approach immediately regardless of runway assignment.
Delay all descent instructions until the runway assignment is accepted by the pilot.

items.explanation: Per 4-7-1, once the 10 NM point to the runway transition waypoint is reached without a runway assignment, the aircraft must be vectored to final.

items.situation: Before issuing an approach clearance, you know the destination airport has an abnormal approach aid condition and field condition information that is not included in the current ATIS, and the pilot has not reported the ATIS code.
items.para_context: Before issuing an approach clearance, and subsequently as changes occur, inform the aircraft of abnormal approach or landing aid operation and destination airport conditions that might restrict an approach or landing unless the information is in the ATIS and the pilot reports having it.

choices.text: Issue the abnormal aid and airport condition information before the approach clearance and update the aircraft if conditions change.
Omit the information because airport conditions are the pilot's responsibility once vectors begin.
Wait until after the aircraft is established on final to mention the restriction.
Withhold the information unless the pilot specifically asks for a field condition report.

items.explanation: Per 4-7-12, controllers must provide abnormal aid and airport condition information before approach clearance unless it is already in the ATIS and the pilot reports the code.

items.situation: An aircraft is executing an instrument approach, but traffic or airspace conditions require an alternate missed approach procedure that is not already published as effective by NOTAM.
items.para_context: If an alternate missed approach procedure is necessary, issue the description exactly as published on the appropriate FAA or military form unless the alternate procedure is already published and in effect by NOTAM.

choices.text: Issue the alternate missed approach procedure as published on the approved form before it is needed.
Wait until the pilot reports missed approach and then improvise a new route and altitude.
Assume the standard depicted missed approach automatically changes to fit the traffic situation.
Clear the aircraft for the approach without mentioning the alternate procedure because all missed approaches are pilot's discretion.

items.explanation: Per 4-8-9, alternate missed approach procedures must be issued as published unless already active by NOTAM.

items.situation: Two aircraft are using intersecting runways. One aircraft is arriving on short final while the other has begun departure roll on the crossing runway and has not yet passed the intersection or turned to avert conflict.
items.para_context: Issue traffic information to each aircraft and ensure the arriving aircraft does not cross the landing threshold or intersecting flight path until one of the required separation conditions exists.

choices.text: Issue traffic information to both aircraft and keep the arrival from crossing the threshold or flight path until the departure has passed the intersection or is turning to avert conflict.
Continue the arrival because the departure is already rolling and will probably clear in time.
Issue wake turbulence caution only and allow the arrival to continue regardless of the intersection status.
Transfer responsibility to the pilots once both aircraft report each other in sight.

items.explanation: Per 3-10-4, intersecting-runway operations require traffic information to each aircraft and a listed threshold or flight-path separation condition before the arrival proceeds.

items.situation: An IFR departure is ready to launch, but a ground stop applicable to that aircraft is currently in effect and the originator of the ground stop has not approved release.
items.para_context: Do not release an aircraft if a ground stop applicable to that aircraft is in effect without the approval of the originator of the ground stop.

choices.text: Do not release the aircraft until the originator of the ground stop approves the release.
Release the aircraft if the pilot states the delay will create a fuel concern.
Release the aircraft if the local facility did not originate the ground stop.
Issue a release and let the receiving facility sort out the ground stop after departure.

items.explanation: Per 4-3-5, an applicable ground stop blocks release unless the originator approves otherwise.

items.situation: An en route aircraft will execute an instrument approach at a nonapproach control tower. You are close enough to arrival that the tower needs time to adjust traffic flow.
items.para_context: Forward arrival information soon enough to permit traffic adjustment, including aircraft identification, type, ETA, and the type of instrument approach procedure to be executed.

choices.text: Forward the arrival information soon enough for the tower to adjust traffic flow, including the aircraft ID, type, ETA, and approach type.
Wait until the aircraft is on final so the tower receives the most current ETA.
Forward only the aircraft identification because the rest can be inferred from the flight plan.
Delay the information until after radio communications are transferred.

items.explanation: Per 4-7-6, arrival information must be forwarded early enough for the receiving facility to use it operationally.

items.situation: A military turbojet is receiving normal arrival handling for a destination with a published high altitude approach. You are about to descend the aircraft below the highest initial approach fix altitude, and the current weather is below a 1,000-foot ceiling with visibility under three miles.
items.para_context: Before issuing descent below the highest initial approach fix altitude established for any high altitude approach, tell the aircraft the approach to expect, that radar vectors will be provided to final approach course, and the current weather when ceiling or visibility is below the stated thresholds.

choices.text: Before issuing the descent, advise the aircraft of the approach to expect, that radar vectors will be provided to final approach course, and the current reported weather.
Issue the descent immediately and pass the approach type and weather later if the pilot asks for them.
Descend the aircraft below the highest initial approach fix altitude only after the pilot specifically requests a high altitude approach.
Terminate the en route descent whenever convenient because the pilot's consent is not required during normal operations.

items.explanation: Per 4-7-5, military turbojets normally receive standard arrival handling, but before descent below the highest initial approach fix altitude for a high altitude approach, the controller must provide the required approach, vector, and weather information.

items.situation: An arrival is inbound to an airport where the latest official weather is below a 1,000-foot ceiling and visibility is under three miles. The weather will be relayed through a nonapproach control facility rather than transmitted directly by the center controller.
items.para_context: Transmit the weather report and any special weather observations to the arriving aircraft prior to or as part of the approach clearance when the official weather is below the stated ceiling or visibility thresholds. When relayed through a communications station or nonapproach control facility, you may do this by telling that station or facility to issue current weather.

choices.text: Ensure the weather and any special observations are issued before or as part of the approach clearance, and you may satisfy the relay requirement by telling the nonapproach control facility to issue current weather.
Wait until the aircraft reports the field in sight before passing the below-minimum weather information.
Omit the weather because relayed reports do not count unless they come from an air carrier company radio.
Issue only the altimeter setting and let the pilot obtain the rest of the weather after the approach clearance.

items.explanation: Per 4-7-7, when weather is below the stated thresholds, the report and special observations must reach the arriving aircraft prior to or as part of the approach clearance, and a nonapproach facility may be told to issue current weather.

items.situation: An arriving aircraft reports the observed weather is below the pilot's landing minima while other aircraft are also sequenced for approaches to the same airport.
items.para_context: When an arriving aircraft reports weather below landing minima, issue instructions to hold or proceed to another airport as appropriate, and adjust the landing sequence and approach clearances of other aircraft as necessary. Determination of whether the weather is adequate remains the responsibility of the pilot or operator.

choices.text: Issue holding or diversion instructions as appropriate for that aircraft and adjust the landing sequence and approach clearances of other arrivals as needed.
Require the pilot to continue the approach because the controller determines whether the weather is above or below the pilot's minima.
Keep the aircraft in the same sequence and wait to see whether the pilot changes the report after crossing the final approach fix.
Cancel all other approach clearances automatically, regardless of traffic or runway use.

items.explanation: Per 4-7-8, the controller issues holding or alternate-airport instructions as appropriate and adjusts the rest of the arrival sequence, while the pilot or operator remains responsible for deciding whether the weather is adequate for landing.

items.situation: An aircraft is approaching its clearance limit, and the receiving facility still needs time to issue a clearance beyond that point.
items.para_context: Transfer radio communications and control responsibility early enough to allow the receiving facility to clear the aircraft beyond the clearance limit before the aircraft reaches it.

choices.text: Transfer communications and control responsibility early enough for the receiving facility to issue the onward clearance before the aircraft reaches the clearance limit.
Wait until the aircraft reaches the clearance limit, then hand it off immediately.
Keep the aircraft on your frequency until it reports level at the clearance limit.
Delay transfer until the pilot requests the next clearance.

items.explanation: Per 4-7-9, the transfer must occur early enough that the receiving facility can act before the aircraft reaches its current limit.

items.instruction: The controller issued this approach clearance. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: CLEARED V-O-R APPROACH TO SPRINGFIELD AIRPORT.

choices.text: Cleared V-O-R approach to Springfield Airport, Bonanza Six Five Bravo.
Cleared V-O-R approach, Bonanza Six Five Bravo.
Cleared visual approach to Springfield Airport, Bonanza Six Five Bravo.
Cleared V-O-R approach to Shelby Airport, Bonanza Six Five Bravo.

items.explanation: Per 4-8-2, when issuing an approach clearance to an uncontrolled airport, the controller states the name of the airport in the clearance.

items.situation: You are clearing an aircraft for an instrument approach at an airport where the control tower is closed for the night.
items.para_context: When issuing an approach clearance at locations without an operating control tower or where part-time towers are closed, state the name of the airport.

choices.text: Include the airport name in the approach clearance.
Omit the airport name because only one airport is in the sector.
Delay the approach clearance until the pilot switches to advisory frequency.
Issue only the approach type and let the pilot determine the airport.

items.explanation: Per 4-8-2, the airport name is part of the required clearance phraseology at uncontrolled or tower-closed airports.

items.instruction: The controller issued this circling instruction. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: CIRCLE SOUTH OF THE AIRPORT FOR A LEFT BASE TO RUNWAY TWO TWO.

choices.text: Circle south of the airport for a left base to Runway Two Two, Baron Three One Delta.
Circle north of the airport for a left base to Runway Two Two, Baron Three One Delta.
Straight-in Runway Two Two approved, Baron Three One Delta.
Circle south of the airport for a right downwind to Runway Two Two, Baron Three One Delta.

items.explanation: Per 4-8-6, when the direction of the circling maneuver is required, the controller states the direction relative to the airport or runway and the appropriate left or right base or downwind leg.

items.situation: An aircraft is flying an instrument approach to a runway that is not aligned with the runway currently in use at a towered airport. You need the aircraft to circle to the runway in use, but the requested maneuver could drift well outside the protected circling area.
items.para_context: Circling approach instructions may be issued only at airports with operational control towers. Include instructions to circle to the runway in use when landing will be on a different runway, state direction and base or downwind if required, and do not issue instructions such as extending the downwind leg if they could cause the aircraft to exceed the circling area.

choices.text: Issue circling instructions to the runway in use, include direction and base or downwind if needed, and avoid any clearance that could take the aircraft outside the protected circling area.
Issue an extend-downwind instruction if it helps sequence traffic, even if it could carry the aircraft beyond the circling area.
Issue circling instructions at any airport as long as the pilot accepts responsibility for obstacle clearance.
Clear the aircraft for a straight-in landing on the runway in use even though the instrument approach is aligned to a different runway and circling instructions are required.

items.explanation: Per 4-8-6, circling instructions are limited to airports with operational towers and must keep the aircraft within the protected circling area.

items.instruction: The controller issued this side-step clearance. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: CLEARED I-L-S RUNWAY SEVEN LEFT APPROACH. SIDE-STEP TO RUNWAY SEVEN RIGHT.

choices.text: Cleared I-L-S Runway Seven Left approach, side-step to Runway Seven Right, Learjet Two Three Lima.
Cleared I-L-S Runway Seven Right approach, Learjet Two Three Lima.
Cleared visual approach Runway Seven Right, Learjet Two Three Lima.
Cleared I-L-S Runway Seven Left approach, land Runway Seven Left, Learjet Two Three Lima.

items.explanation: Per 4-8-7, when the procedure authorizes it, the controller may clear the aircraft for an approach to one runway and advise that landing will be made on the parallel runway via a side-step maneuver.

items.situation: An aircraft is established for an instrument approach to Runway 7 Left, and the published procedure authorizes a side-step maneuver to the parallel runway that is in use for landing.
items.para_context: When authorized by an instrument approach procedure, you may clear the aircraft for an approach to one runway and inform the aircraft that landing will be made on the parallel runway using side-step phraseology.

choices.text: Issue the approach clearance to the instrument runway and include the side-step to the parallel landing runway.
Clear the aircraft directly for the parallel runway's instrument approach even though the published procedure authorizes only a side-step from the other runway.
Use a side-step maneuver at any airport when traffic favors the parallel runway, whether or not the procedure authorizes it.
Treat the side-step as a visual approach and omit the instrument approach clearance entirely.

items.explanation: Per 4-8-7, the side-step maneuver is authorized only when the instrument procedure authorizes it, and the clearance states the approach runway and the parallel runway to which the aircraft will side-step.

items.instruction: The controller issued this frequency change. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: CHANGE TO ADVISORY FREQUENCY APPROVED.

choices.text: Change to advisory frequency approved, Cherokee Eight One Four.
Contact tower approved, Cherokee Eight One Four.
Change to advisory frequency denied, Cherokee Eight One Four.
Remain this frequency until touchdown, Cherokee Eight One Four.

items.explanation: Per 4-8-8, the controller may approve a change to advisory frequency once direct communications are no longer required.

items.situation: An IFR aircraft is inbound to an airport without a tower or FSS, and you no longer need to maintain direct communications with it.
items.para_context: Approve a change to the advisory service frequency when direct communications are no longer required.

choices.text: Approve the change to advisory frequency so the aircraft can receive timely local traffic information.
Keep the aircraft on your frequency until it is on the ground regardless of workload or need.
Require the aircraft to remain with center until canceling IFR on the runway.
Delay the frequency change until after the missed approach point is crossed.

items.explanation: Per 4-8-8, once direct communications are no longer needed, approving the advisory frequency change supports timely local traffic awareness.

items.instruction: The controller issued this weather advisory. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: METRO AWOS WEATHER AVAILABLE ON ONE ONE NINER POINT TWO FIVE.

choices.text: Metro AWOS weather available on one one niner point two five, Citation Four Six X-Ray.
Metro tower on one one niner point two five, Citation Four Six X-Ray.
Metro AWOS weather available on one two zero point two five, Citation Four Six X-Ray.
Weather not available, Citation Four Six X-Ray.

items.explanation: Per 4-7-10, controllers must provide the automated weather frequency on pilot request and issue the last disseminated weather if the pilot cannot receive the broadcast.

items.situation: A pilot checks in for an airport you serve with approach control. The airport has no ATIS, the pilot has not yet reported receiving the automated weather, and ceiling and visibility are below the stated thresholds.
items.para_context: Provide current approach information on initial contact or as soon as possible thereafter, including expected approach, runway if different, surface wind, ceiling/visibility when below thresholds, and altimeter unless the information may be omitted.

choices.text: Issue the current approach information, including the expected approach, runway if different, surface wind, ceiling and visibility, and altimeter setting.
Wait until the pilot requests the approach clearance before issuing any weather or approach information.
Issue only the altimeter because the rest is the pilot's responsibility at non-ATIS airports.
Omit all approach information because automated weather exists at the airport.

items.explanation: Per 4-7-10, approach information is provided on initial contact or as soon as possible, with limited omissions only when the pilot has the ATIS or automated weather.

items.situation: You are working a TRACON and need to pass arrival data to a collocated tower that has STARS data displayed on its CTRD. A facility directive exists authorizing STARS use for transferring arrival data.
items.para_context: Forward the required arrival information soon enough for traffic adjustment, and where authorized by directive or agreement, STARS modify or quick look functions may be used to transfer the data.

choices.text: Use the authorized STARS data-transfer method and ensure the required arrival information is forwarded soon enough for the tower to adjust traffic flow.
Wait until the aircraft is on short final because the tower can infer the arrival from the display.
Send only the aircraft identification because the approach type is already implied.
Avoid using STARS for transfer of arrival data even though local procedures specifically authorize it.

items.explanation: Per 4-7-11, approach control facilities forward arrival information soon enough for operational use and may use STARS transfer methods when the required directive or agreement exists.

items.instruction: The controller issued this approach information. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: INITIAL APPROACH AT THREE THOUSAND, PROCEDURE TURN AT THREE THOUSAND, TEN MILES NORTH, FINAL APPROACH ON THE V-O-R ZERO NINER ZERO RADIAL AT TWO THOUSAND.

choices.text: Initial approach at three thousand, procedure turn at three thousand, ten miles north, final approach on the V-O-R zero niner zero radial at two thousand, Bonanza Six Five Bravo.
Initial approach at two thousand, procedure turn at three thousand, ten miles north, Bonanza Six Five Bravo.
Initial approach at three thousand, procedure turn at three thousand, ten miles south, Bonanza Six Five Bravo.
Final approach on the localizer, Bonanza Six Five Bravo.

items.explanation: Per 4-8-10, when a pilot is unfamiliar with the procedure, the controller specifies the initial approach altitude, procedure turn information, final approach course, and related details.

items.situation: A pilot reports being unfamiliar with the instrument approach procedure you are about to clear them for.
items.para_context: Specify the initial approach altitude, procedure turn limits and altitude, final approach course and altitude, missed approach procedures if necessary, and any applicable chart notations that levy pilot responsibility.

choices.text: Provide the approach information required for an unfamiliar pilot, including procedure details and any applicable chart notations that require pilot action or compliance.
Issue only the approach clearance because the chart contains everything else the pilot needs.
Cancel the approach and require the pilot to pick a different procedure.
Provide only the missed approach procedure and let the pilot request the rest if needed.

items.explanation: Per 4-8-10, unfamiliar pilots receive additional procedural information in the clearance, including applicable chart notations that shift responsibility to the pilot.

items.instruction: The controller issued this practice-approach clearance. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: N123AB, MAINTAIN VFR, PRACTICE APPROACH APPROVED, NO SEPARATION SERVICES PROVIDED.

choices.text: Maintain VFR, practice approach approved, no separation services provided, N123AB.
Practice approach approved with IFR separation, N123AB.
Maintain IFR, practice approach approved, N123AB.
Practice approach denied, N123AB.

items.explanation: Per 4-8-11, when a facility directive does not require IFR separation for a VFR practice approach, the controller instructs the pilot to maintain VFR and advises that no separation services are provided.

items.situation: Traffic and workload prevent immediate approval of a requested practice instrument approach, but the delay is expected to be short.
items.para_context: Advise the pilot of the reason and, if applicable, the anticipated delay until the operation can be approved. Controller-imposed delays should not be indefinite, and normally approaches in progress should not be terminated.

choices.text: Advise the pilot of the reason for the delay and the anticipated delay if applicable, and avoid imposing an indefinite delay.
Deny the approach without explanation because practice approaches are always subordinate to operational traffic.
Leave the pilot waiting with no estimate until workload drops on its own.
Terminate another practice approach already in progress to make room for the new one.

items.explanation: Per 4-8-11, delays to practice approaches should be explained, should not be indefinite, and normally should not require terminating approaches already in progress.

items.instruction: The controller issued this low-approach climb-out instruction. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: AFTER COMPLETING LOW APPROACH, CLIMB AND MAINTAIN SIX THOUSAND. TURN RIGHT, HEADING THREE SIX ZERO.

choices.text: After completing low approach, climb and maintain six thousand, turn right heading three six zero, Skylane Seven Three Alpha.
After completing low approach, maintain VFR and contact tower, Skylane Seven Three Alpha.
After completing low approach, climb and maintain three thousand, turn right heading three six zero, Skylane Seven Three Alpha.
After completing low approach, climb and maintain six thousand, fly runway heading, Skylane Seven Three Alpha.

items.explanation: Per 4-8-12, before final descent the controller issues the departure instructions to be followed after the low approach, including a specific heading or route and altitude unless the aircraft will maintain VFR and contact the tower.

items.situation: An aircraft is cleared for a practice approach followed by a low approach. The pilot is about to begin final descent, and after the low approach the aircraft will remain IFR rather than maintain VFR to the tower.
items.para_context: Treat the aircraft as arriving until it touches down or crosses the threshold, then as departing. Before final descent, issue the departure instructions the pilot is to follow after completing the approach. Those climb-out instructions must include a specific heading or route of flight and altitude unless the aircraft will maintain VFR and contact the tower.

choices.text: Before final descent, issue the post-approach departure instructions, including a specific heading or route and altitude for the climb-out.
Wait until the aircraft crosses the threshold, then decide whether to issue any climb-out instructions.
Treat the aircraft as a departure as soon as it is cleared for the low approach, so no arrival handling applies on final.
Issue only 'maintain VFR, contact tower' even though the aircraft will not remain VFR after the low approach.

items.explanation: Per 4-8-12, the controller issues the post-approach departure instructions before final descent and includes a specific heading or route and altitude unless the aircraft will maintain VFR and contact the tower.

items.situation: You are working a terminal radar position, and ongoing electronic attack activity is interfering with the operational use of radar. The interference must stop immediately.
items.para_context: In terminal operations, request suspension of EA activity through the ARTCC. If immediate cessation is required, broadcast the stop request directly to the EA aircraft on the emergency frequency and notify the ARTCC of the direct broadcast as soon as possible.

choices.text: Request suspension through the ARTCC, and if immediate cessation is required, broadcast directly on the emergency frequency and notify the ARTCC as soon as possible.
Contact the aircraft directly on the working frequency and let the ARTCC discover the stop request later.
Continue operations without action because EA requests are handled only by the military unit once approved.
Suspend the narrowband radar first and wait for the Systems Engineer before making any stop request to the EA aircraft.

items.explanation: Per 5-1-3, terminal stop requests go through the ARTCC, but immediate cessation may be requested directly on the emergency frequency with prompt ARTCC notification.

items.situation: An IFR aircraft has been receiving radar services and has therefore stopped reporting over compulsory reporting points. A short time later, you lose radar contact with the aircraft.
items.para_context: After ATC advises an aircraft 'radar contact,' the aircraft discontinues reporting over compulsory reporting points. Normal position reporting resumes when ATC informs the aircraft 'radar contact lost' or 'radar service terminated.'

choices.text: Advise the aircraft that radar contact is lost or radar service is terminated so normal position reporting resumes.
Assume the aircraft will automatically resume compulsory reporting as soon as the radar target drops without any advisory from ATC.
Keep the aircraft exempt from compulsory reporting until it leaves your sector, even though radar contact is lost.
Cancel IFR and instruct the aircraft to continue position reporting only when requested by another facility.

items.explanation: Per 5-1-8, normal position reporting resumes only after ATC tells the aircraft that radar contact is lost or radar service is terminated.

items.situation: At a terminal facility outside Class A airspace, the primary radar is temporarily unusable and only secondary radar coverage remains available for a portion of the service area.
items.para_context: Secondary radar may be used as the sole display source outside Class A only when additional coverage exists beyond primary radar coverage or when the primary radar is temporarily unusable or out of service. When those conditions exist, advise pilots that primary radar is unavailable and radar services are available only to transponder or ADS-B equipped aircraft, unless the advisory is already provided by ATIS or other appropriate notice.

choices.text: Advise pilots that primary radar is unavailable and that radar services are available only to transponder or ADS-B equipped aircraft, unless the advisory has already been provided by ATIS or other appropriate notice.
Continue normal primary-radar services without any advisory because secondary radar alone is always interchangeable with primary radar in terminal airspace.
Conduct ASR final approaches normally using secondary radar only, even without an emergency or pilot concurrence.
Suspend all radar services to every aircraft until primary radar is restored, regardless of secondary radar coverage.

items.explanation: Per 5-1-2, secondary radar-only operations outside Class A are limited to specified conditions, and the required advisory must be issued unless already covered by ATIS or other appropriate notice.

items.situation: Two turbojet aircraft in RVSM airspace are opposite direction and likely to merge. They are vertically separated by 1,000 feet, and one pilot reports being unable RVSM due to turbulence.
items.para_context: Apply merging target procedures to specified radar-identified aircraft, issue traffic information when targets appear likely to merge unless separated by more than the appropriate vertical minima, and when both aircraft are in RVSM airspace and one pilot reports unable RVSM due to turbulence or mountain wave, use vectors to prevent the targets from merging.

choices.text: Issue timely traffic information and use vectors to prevent the targets from merging because one pilot is unable RVSM in RVSM airspace.
Rely on the existing 1,000-foot separation alone and do not vector because turbojets in RVSM airspace are exempt from merging target procedures.
Wait until the pilots request assistance before issuing any traffic information because closure rate is their responsibility.
Terminate radar service to both aircraft so they can maneuver visually around each other.

items.explanation: Per 5-1-4, likely merging targets require timely traffic information, and an unable-RVSM report in RVSM airspace triggers vectors to prevent the merge.

items.situation: An aircraft on an RNAV ATS route is drifting laterally and continuing on a track that will obviously carry it outside its protected airspace if no correction is made.
items.para_context: Inform an aircraft when it is observed in a position and on a track that will obviously cause it to deviate from its protected airspace area, and if necessary help the aircraft return to the assigned protected airspace.

choices.text: Advise the aircraft of the observed deviation and assist it back to the assigned protected airspace if necessary.
Wait until the aircraft actually leaves protected airspace before saying anything because advisories are only issued after the deviation occurs.
Cancel radar service and leave the aircraft to correct the route on its own because navigation remains solely a pilot issue.
Issue a traffic advisory only, because route-deviation advisories are not authorized on RNAV ATS routes.

items.explanation: Per 5-1-6, the controller acts before the aircraft obviously departs protected airspace and helps it return when necessary.

items.instruction: The controller terminated radar service with nonradar routing. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: RADAR SERVICE TERMINATED. PROCEED DIRECT MADISON V-O-R.

choices.text: Radar service terminated, proceed direct Madison V-O-R, Bonanza Four Six Kilo.
Radar contact, proceed direct Madison V-O-R, Bonanza Four Six Kilo.
Radar service terminated, remain on vectors to Madison V-O-R, Bonanza Four Six Kilo.
Radar service continued, proceed direct Madison V-O-R, Bonanza Four Six Kilo.

items.explanation: Per 5-1-9, when radar service is terminated, the aircraft is informed and any required nonradar routing is included in the termination phraseology.

items.situation: An arriving aircraft is inbound to a tower-controlled airport where radar coverage does not exist to within one-half mile of the runway end.
items.para_context: Inform aircraft when radar service is terminated. At tower-controlled airports where radar coverage does not exist to within one-half mile of the runway, arriving aircraft must be informed when radar service is terminated.

choices.text: Inform the aircraft when radar service is terminated because the radar coverage does not extend to within one-half mile of the runway end.
Assume radar service terminates automatically with no advisory because the aircraft is arriving at a tower-controlled airport.
Delay the termination advisory until after the aircraft has landed and cleared the runway.
Keep radar service active indefinitely even though coverage does not reach the runway environment.

items.explanation: Per 5-1-9, this is a specific case where the aircraft must be told radar service is terminated.

items.instruction: The controller established radar contact with an emergency aircraft and requested a discrete code change. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: RADAR CONTACT FIVE MILES SOUTH OF SPRINGFIELD. IF FEASIBLE, SQUAWK FOUR SIX TWO THREE.

choices.text: Radar contact five miles south of Springfield, if feasible squawk four six two three, Niner One Two Alpha Bravo.
Radar contact five miles south of Springfield, squawk mayday seven seven zero zero, Niner One Two Alpha Bravo.
Radar contact five miles north of Springfield, if feasible squawk four six two three, Niner One Two Alpha Bravo.
Radar service terminated, squawk four six two three, Niner One Two Alpha Bravo.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-3, after radio and radar contact are established, controllers may request certain emergency aircraft to change from Code 7700 to a computer-assigned discrete code if feasible.

items.situation: A pilot declares an emergency, but the aircraft is not yet radar identified.
items.para_context: Assign Code 7700 when the pilot declares an emergency and the aircraft is not radar identified. After radio and radar contact are established, you may request certain aircraft to change to a discrete code if feasible.

choices.text: Assign Code 7700 to the emergency aircraft.
Wait to assign any emergency code until the aircraft is radar identified.
Assign a random discrete code immediately because Code 7700 is used only after radar identification.
Terminate radar services and instruct the pilot to remain on the present code until landing.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-3, non-radar-identified emergency aircraft are assigned Code 7700 immediately.

items.instruction: The controller is validating an invalid Mode C readout below FL180. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: MADISON ALTIMETER THREE ZERO ONE TWO, VERIFY ALTITUDE.

choices.text: Madison altimeter three zero one two, verify altitude, Citation Two One Charlie.
Madison altimeter two niner niner two, verify altitude, Citation Two One Charlie.
Madison altimeter three zero one two, verify flight level, Citation Two One Charlie.
Stop altitude squawk, Citation Two One Charlie.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-15, when an aircraft below FL180 shows an invalid Mode C readout, issue the correct altimeter setting and verify the altitude.

items.situation: You observe an aircraft below FL180 with an invalid Mode C readout. After issuing the correct altimeter and confirming the pilot's reported altitude, the readout remains invalid.
items.para_context: When the invalid Mode C readout continues below FL180, instruct the pilot to turn off the altitude-reporting part of the transponder and include the reason, and notify the operations supervisor-in-charge of the aircraft call sign. Do not use unvalidated Mode C for separation.

choices.text: Instruct the pilot to stop altitude squawk with the reason and notify the operations supervisor-in-charge of the aircraft call sign.
Continue using the invalid Mode C readout for separation because the pilot verbally confirmed the altitude.
Ignore the problem unless the aircraft climbs above FL180.
Terminate the flight plan because any invalid Mode C readout requires immediate cancellation of radar service.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-15, once the invalid readout persists, altitude squawk is stopped, the supervisor is notified, and the invalid Mode C is not used for separation.

items.situation: You observe a departing target within one mile of the takeoff runway end at a towered airport, and your facility has already provided the required rolling notification for that departure.
