items.para_context: A primary, radar beacon, or ADS-B target may be identified by observing a departing aircraft target within one mile of the takeoff runway end at airports with an operating control tower, provided the required verbal or nonverbal rolling or boundary notification is accomplished.

choices.text: Use the observed departure target as a radar identification because the target is within one mile of the runway end and the required coordination was accomplished.
Delay identification until the aircraft reports over the first fix because departure observation is never sufficient at towered airports.
Identify the target only if it is already five miles from the runway end.
Use the target for identification even if no rolling or boundary notification had been made.

items.explanation: Per 5-3-2, departure observation within one mile of the runway end is an authorized identification method when the required coordination method has been completed.

items.situation: Two nearby targets have duplicated the same observed action, and you now doubt which target belongs to the aircraft you were tracking.
items.para_context: When proximity of targets, duplication of observed action, or any other circumstance causes doubt as to target identification, use more than one method of identification. If identification remains questionable for any reason, take immediate action to re-identify the aircraft or terminate radar service.

choices.text: Use more than one identification method immediately, and if identification remains questionable, re-identify the aircraft or terminate radar service.
Continue providing radar service on the original identification because duplication of observed action alone does not affect identification reliability.
Wait until the pilot reports a navigation problem before taking any action on the doubtful identification.
Switch off all primary targets instead of re-identifying the aircraft.

items.explanation: Per 5-3-5, doubtful identification requires more than one method and immediate re-identification or termination of radar service if the doubt remains.

items.instruction: The controller requested altitude confirmation from a Mode C aircraft. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: VERIFY AT FLIGHT LEVEL TWO THREE ZERO.

choices.text: Verify at flight level two three zero, Jetlink Four Five Two.
Verify assigned altitude six thousand, Jetlink Four Five Two.
Affirmative two three zero, Jetlink Four Five Two.
Radar contact Jetlink Four Five Two.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-16, when confirmation is required for a Mode C aircraft, the controller uses the verify-at or verify-assigned-altitude or flight-level phraseology appropriate to the situation.

items.situation: A Mode C-equipped aircraft makes initial contact with your sector. The pilot does not state the assigned altitude, you have not assigned a new altitude, the Mode C readout has not yet been validated as established at the assigned altitude, and the aircraft was not transferred intrafacility in a terminal environment.
items.para_context: Request a pilot to confirm assigned altitude on initial contact unless one of the listed exceptions applies, including the pilot stating the assigned altitude, a new altitude assignment, a valid Mode C readout showing the aircraft established at the assigned altitude, or a terminal intrafacility transfer.

choices.text: Request the pilot to confirm the assigned altitude or flight level on initial contact.
Skip altitude confirmation because Mode C-equipped aircraft never need confirmation on initial contact.
Wait until the pilot changes altitude before asking for any confirmation.
Assume the readout is valid even though the aircraft is not yet established at the assigned altitude.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-16, initial-contact altitude confirmation is required unless one of the specific exceptions applies.

items.instruction: The controller corrected an incorrect altitude readback from a non-Mode C aircraft. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: NEGATIVE. MAINTAIN SIX THOUSAND.

choices.text: Negative, maintain six thousand, Cherokee Three Eight Lima.
Affirmative six thousand, Cherokee Three Eight Lima.
Negative, climb and maintain six thousand, Cherokee Three Eight Lima.
Verify at six thousand, Cherokee Three Eight Lima.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-17, when a non-Mode C aircraft gives an incorrect altitude readback, the controller corrects it with the negative-maintain or negative-climb-descend-and-maintain phraseology.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: NEGATIVE. MAINTAIN SIX THOUSAND.
items.display_text: AFFIRMATIVE MAINTAIN SIX THOUSAND
items.explanation: Per 5-2-17, an incorrect altitude readback is corrected with 'NEGATIVE' before the correct altitude instruction is restated.

items.situation: A non-Mode C aircraft checks in on initial contact without stating its assigned altitude. You then issue an altitude, and the pilot reads back the wrong altitude.
items.para_context: Request a pilot to confirm assigned altitude on initial contact unless one of the listed exceptions applies. In the USA, reconfirm all pilot altitude readbacks. If the readback is incorrect, correct it with the negative-maintain or negative-climb-descend-and-maintain phraseology.

choices.text: Confirm the assigned altitude on initial contact if required, then reconfirm the incorrect altitude readback with the proper negative phraseology and the correct altitude.
Accept the incorrect readback because non-Mode C aircraft do not require altitude readback correction.
Wait for the next sector to correct the pilot's altitude because initial-contact confirmation is optional.
Use Mode C validation procedures even though the aircraft is non-Mode C.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-17, non-Mode C aircraft require assigned-altitude confirmation on initial contact unless an exception applies, and in the USA all altitude readbacks are reconfirmed.

items.instruction: The controller stopped ADS-B transmissions because the malfunction was affecting ATC operations. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: STOP ADS-B TRANSMISSIONS, AND IF ABLE, SQUAWK FOUR TWO SIX THREE.

choices.text: Stop ADS-B transmissions and if able squawk four two six three, Delta Eight Four One.
Continue ADS-B transmissions and squawk four two six three, Delta Eight Four One.
Stop altitude squawk and squawk four two six three, Delta Eight Four One.
Your ADS-B flight ID does not match your flight plan aircraft identification, Delta Eight Four One.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-22, when a malfunctioning ADS-B transmitter jeopardizes safe ATC functions, the aircraft is instructed to stop ADS-B transmissions and, if able, squawk the assigned code.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: STOP ADS-B TRANSMISSIONS, AND IF ABLE, SQUAWK FOUR TWO SIX THREE.
items.display_text: CONTINUE ADS-B TRANSMISSIONS AND IF ABLE SQUAWK FOUR TWO SIX THREE
items.explanation: Per 5-2-22, the aircraft is instructed to stop ADS-B transmissions when the malfunction jeopardizes ATC functions.

items.situation: An aircraft's ADS-B transmitter appears to be malfunctioning. The issue is now jeopardizing the safe execution of air traffic control functions.
items.para_context: Notify the OS/CIC of the aircraft call sign, location, and time of the occurrence in UTC. Except for DoD aircraft or those covered by paragraph 5-2-24, inform the pilot. If the malfunction jeopardizes safe execution of ATC functions, instruct the aircraft to stop ADS-B transmissions and notify the OS/CIC.

choices.text: Notify the OS/CIC, inform the pilot as applicable, and instruct the aircraft to stop ADS-B transmissions when the malfunction jeopardizes ATC functions.
Ignore the malfunction unless the pilot specifically requests ADS-B troubleshooting assistance.
Instruct the aircraft to stop altitude squawk only, because ADS-B transmissions cannot be stopped by ATC.
Report the event after the flight only; no immediate ATC action is authorized.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-22, ADS-B transmitter malfunctions are reported to the OS/CIC, and when they jeopardize safe ATC functions the aircraft is instructed to stop ADS-B transmissions.

items.instruction: The controller issued a Call Sign Mis-Match advisory. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: YOUR ADS-B FLIGHT ID DOES NOT MATCH YOUR FLIGHT PLAN AIRCRAFT IDENTIFICATION.

choices.text: Your ADS-B flight ID does not match your flight plan aircraft identification, SkyWest Fifty Two Thirty.
Your ADS-B transmitter appears to be inoperative, SkyWest Fifty Two Thirty.
Stop ADS-B transmissions and if able squawk four two six three, SkyWest Fifty Two Thirty.
Your ADS-B flight ID matches your flight plan aircraft identification, SkyWest Fifty Two Thirty.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-23, a Call Sign Mis-Match alert requires the controller to inform the aircraft that the transmitted ADS-B flight identification does not match the flight plan aircraft identification.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: YOUR ADS-B FLIGHT ID DOES NOT MATCH YOUR FLIGHT PLAN AIRCRAFT IDENTIFICATION.
items.display_text: YOUR ADS-B FLIGHT ID DOES NOW MATCH YOUR FLIGHT PLAN AIRCRAFT IDENTIFICATION
items.explanation: Per 5-2-23, the advisory states that the ADS-B flight ID does not match the flight plan aircraft identification.

items.situation: A Call Sign Mis-Match alert appears for an aircraft, and shortly afterward the mismatch is jeopardizing the safe execution of air traffic control functions.
items.para_context: Inform the aircraft of a Call Sign Mis-Match alert. If a CSMM or Duplicate ICAO address jeopardizes the safe execution of ATC functions, instruct the aircraft to stop ADS-B transmissions and notify the OS/CIC.

choices.text: Inform the aircraft of the Call Sign Mis-Match and, if the alert jeopardizes ATC functions, instruct it to stop ADS-B transmissions and notify the OS/CIC.
Take no action unless a duplicate ICAO address alert is also present, because CSMM alerts are advisory only.
Immediately terminate radar service without advising the aircraft or the OS/CIC.
Instruct the aircraft to stop altitude squawk only, since ADS-B alerts never require stopping ADS-B transmissions.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-23, CSMM alerts are advised to the aircraft, and if a CSMM or duplicate ICAO alert jeopardizes ATC functions, ADS-B transmissions are stopped and the OS/CIC is notified.

items.situation: A pilot asks you for approval to conduct ADS-B Out OFF operations, and the OS/CIC has not yet coordinated any approval for that aircraft. The aircraft is currently in your area of jurisdiction.
items.para_context: Operators requesting an exception to ADS-B Out transmission must obtain authorization from FAA System Operations Security. Controllers do not approve pilot requests for ADS-B Out OFF operations and must notify the OS/CIC of the request, including the aircraft call sign and location. If the OS/CIC has coordinated authorized ADS-B Out OFF operations, do not advise that the transmitter appears inoperative.

choices.text: Do not approve the request and notify the OS/CIC of the aircraft call sign and location.
Approve the request if the aircraft is already in your sector and appears otherwise normal.
Advise the pilot that the ADS-B transmitter appears inoperative and continue normal malfunction procedures.
Wait until the aircraft exits your airspace before notifying the OS/CIC of the request.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-24, FAA System Operations Security authorizes ADS-B Out OFF operations; controllers do not approve pilot requests and notify the OS/CIC of the aircraft call sign and location.

items.instruction: The controller used a beacon identification method. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: SQUAWK FOUR TWO SIX THREE AND IDENT.

choices.text: Squawk four two six three and ident, Baron Eight Five Juliet.
Squawk standby then normal, Baron Eight Five Juliet.
Radar contact, Baron Eight Five Juliet.
Stop altitude squawk, Baron Eight Five Juliet.

items.explanation: Per 5-3-3, an authorized beacon/ADS-B identification method is to request a specific code and IDENT, then observe the identification display.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: SQUAWK STANDBY, THEN SQUAWK NORMAL.
items.display_text: SQUAWK IDENT THEN SQUAWK NORMAL
items.explanation: Per 5-3-3, one authorized identification method is to request standby, observe the target disappear, then request a return to normal.

items.situation: You are using only beacon or ADS-B information to identify a target. You instruct the pilot to squawk standby, observe the target disappear for sufficient scans, then instruct the pilot to squawk normal and observe the target reappear.
items.para_context: Under 5-3-3, a beacon/ADS-B target may be identified by requesting standby, observing the target disappear for sufficient scans, then requesting a return to normal operation and observing the target reappear. Other approved methods include IDENT, code change observation, and en route automatic association of a full data block with a discrete assigned code.

choices.text: Consider the target identified because the standby-disappear-normal-reappear method has been completed.
Treat the method as incomplete because standby identification is not authorized for ADS-B or beacon targets.
Wait for the pilot to report over a fix because beacon-only identification always requires a position report.
Cancel the discrete code and terminate radar service immediately after the target reappears.

items.explanation: Per 5-3-3, observing disappearance in standby and reappearance in normal operation is an approved identification method.

items.situation: A terminal auto-acquired aircraft has a visible data block. The tagged target identity has already been confirmed through approved radar/beacon identification procedures, and the data block is properly aligned with the target.
items.para_context: Under 5-3-4, consider an auto-acquired aircraft identified when the data block is displayed and visible and either approved radar or beacon identification procedures confirmed the tagged target, or a qualifying NAS automated handoff exists. Maintain target identity with the data block unless it is in coast status or displaced, and keep displaced data blocks updated at all times.

choices.text: Consider the aircraft identified and use the data block to maintain target identity while it remains properly aligned and not in coast status.
Refuse to use the data block because terminal automation identification always requires a fresh IDENT after auto-acquisition.
Wait until the aircraft leaves terminal airspace because auto-acquired targets cannot be identified until en route handoff.
Ignore any future displacement because once the tag is identified it never needs updating.

items.explanation: Per 5-3-4, a visible auto-acquired data block may establish identification under the listed conditions, and the data block is then used to maintain identity unless it is coasted or displaced.

items.instruction: The controller advised the aircraft that radar contact was lost and issued an alternate instruction. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: RADAR CONTACT LOST. FLY HEADING ZERO NINER ZERO.

choices.text: Radar contact lost, fly heading zero niner zero, Learjet Four Six Golf.
Radar contact, fly heading zero niner zero, Learjet Four Six Golf.
Radar service terminated, squawk VFR, Learjet Four Six Golf.
Radar contact lost, ident, Learjet Four Six Golf.

items.explanation: Per 5-3-7, when radar contact is lost the aircraft is informed with 'RADAR CONTACT LOST' plus alternative instructions when required.

items.situation: Radar identification has just been reestablished on an aircraft after radar contact was previously lost.
items.para_context: Under 5-3-7, inform an aircraft of radar contact when initial radar identification is established and when radar identification is reestablished after loss of radar contact or termination of radar service. Inform the aircraft when radar contact is lost.

choices.text: Advise the aircraft of radar contact again because radar identification has been reestablished.
Do not say anything because radar contact is announced only once per flight.
Wait until the next frequency change before advising radar contact.
Terminate radar service immediately because reestablished contact cannot be used.

items.explanation: Per 5-3-7, radar contact is stated when it is initially established and again when it is reestablished after being lost or after radar service had been terminated.

items.situation: You previously issued approval for an aircraft to operate with a failed transponder or ADS-B Out transmitter. Traffic complexity has increased and the operation can no longer be accommodated safely in your sector.
items.para_context: Under 5-2-14, requests to operate with a failed transponder or ADS-B Out transmitter may be disapproved, or previously issued approval withdrawn, solely on the basis of traffic conditions or other operational factors.

choices.text: Withdraw the previously issued approval because traffic conditions and operational factors no longer support it.
Keep the approval in effect because once issued it cannot be withdrawn for traffic reasons.
Approve all failed-transponder operations automatically unless the aircraft declares an emergency.
Deny the flight only if the pilot first refuses a beacon code assignment.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-14, traffic conditions and other operational factors are valid grounds to disapprove or withdraw approval to operate with a failed transponder or ADS-B Out transmitter.

items.instruction: The controller wants the aircraft to begin automatic altitude reporting. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: SQUAWK ALTITUDE.

choices.text: Squawk altitude, Archer Three Two Papa.
Stop altitude squawk, Archer Three Two Papa.
Squawk standby, Archer Three Two Papa.
Radar contact, Archer Three Two Papa.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-18, use 'SQUAWK ALTITUDE' when you want the aircraft to turn on automatic altitude reporting.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: STOP ALTITUDE SQUAWK.
items.display_text: START ALTITUDE SQUAWK
items.explanation: Per 5-2-18, 'STOP ALTITUDE SQUAWK' is the correct phraseology to turn off automatic altitude reporting.

items.situation: You need an aircraft to stop transmitting automatic altitude reports, but you still want it to continue beacon code operation if the equipment allows.
items.para_context: Under 5-2-18, inform the aircraft whenever you want it to turn the automatic altitude reporting feature on or off. Controllers should also recognize that some aircraft cannot disengage altitude reporting independently from the beacon code squawk.

choices.text: Issue 'STOP ALTITUDE SQUAWK' and be aware some aircraft may not be able to separate altitude reporting from beacon code operation.
Say nothing, because controllers do not direct the altitude reporting feature in flight.
Always instruct the pilot to squawk standby because that is the only approved way to stop altitude reporting.
Terminate radar service before asking the aircraft to stop altitude reporting.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-18, the controller explicitly directs automatic altitude reporting on or off with the published phraseology.

items.situation: A VFR aircraft with an inoperative transponder requests an inflight deviation to continue through affected airspace. The pilot has not filed IFR, has not yet provided a VFR route of flight, and is asking for immediate approval.
items.para_context: Under 5-2-19, suggest flight in unaffected airspace, suggest filing IFR, and suggest a VFR route of flight with maintained radio contact. Do not approve an inflight deviation unless the aircraft has filed IFR or a VFR route is provided and radio contact with ATC is maintained.

choices.text: Do not approve yet; suggest unaffected airspace, suggest IFR, or obtain a VFR route of flight with maintained radio contact before approving.
Approve immediately because inflight requests from VFR aircraft with inoperative transponders are automatically allowed if the aircraft is already airborne.
Deny the request without offering any alternatives because the paragraph does not authorize controller suggestions.
Approve as long as the aircraft remains VFR, even if no route is provided and radio contact is not maintained.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-19, the controller follows the stated sequence of suggestions and does not approve unless IFR is filed or a VFR route is provided and radio contact is maintained.

items.situation: You identify an aircraft by a beacon identification method under 5-3-3 rather than by position correlation. The aircraft was not a departure identified within one mile of the runway end.
items.para_context: Under 5-3-6, inform an aircraft of its position whenever radar identification is established by identifying turns or by any of the beacon identification methods in 5-3-3. Position information is not required when identification is established by position correlation or when a departing aircraft is identified within one mile of the takeoff runway end.

choices.text: Inform the aircraft of its position because identification was established by a beacon identification method.
Do not provide position information because it is required only after position correlation.
Wait until the aircraft requests a position report because position information is never issued automatically.
Withhold position information unless the aircraft is on an IFR flight plan.

items.explanation: Per 5-3-6, position information is given when identification is established by identifying turns or beacon identification methods, except for the listed exceptions.

items.instruction: The controller told the aircraft to reset and resquawk the assigned beacon code. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: NOVEMBER THREE TWO ONE ALFA BRAVO, RESET TRANSPONDER, SQUAWK FOUR TWO SIX THREE.

choices.text: November Three Two One Alfa Bravo, reset transponder, squawk four two six three.
November Three Two One Alfa Bravo, your transponder appears inoperative, squawk standby.
November Three Two One Alfa Bravo, stop squawk, code four two six three.
November Three Two One Alfa Bravo, beacon interrogator malfunctioning.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-12, when an operable transponder is not displaying the assigned code, instruct the aircraft to reset the transponder and squawk the appropriate code.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: RESET TRANSPONDER, SQUAWK FOUR TWO SIX THREE.
items.display_text: RESET TRANSPONDER SQUAWK FOUR TWO SIX FIVE
items.explanation: Per 5-2-12, the aircraft is instructed to squawk the assigned appropriate code, so the displayed code digit is wrong.

items.situation: An aircraft with an operable transponder is not displaying the assigned beacon code. After you instruct it to reset and resquawk, the transponder still appears inoperative, and the aircraft is about to be handed to the next control position.
items.para_context: Under 5-2-12, inform an aircraft with an operable transponder that the assigned beacon code is not being displayed and instruct it to reset the transponder and squawk the appropriate code. If the transponder appears inoperative or malfunctioning, advise the aircraft, and ensure the next control position or facility is notified.

choices.text: Advise the aircraft that the transponder appears inoperative or malfunctioning and notify the next control position or facility of the problem.
Take no further action because the reset instruction ends the controller's responsibility.
Tell the aircraft to stop squawk and do not notify the next controller because the malfunction is already obvious on the display.
Wait for the next facility to discover the problem independently before mentioning it.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-12, continuing transponder malfunction requires advising the aircraft and notifying the subsequent control position or next facility.

items.instruction: The controller advised aircraft of a ground equipment problem. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: MADISON APPROACH BEACON INTERROGATOR MALFUNCTIONING.

choices.text: Madison Approach beacon interrogator malfunctioning, Cessna Six Five Two Delta.
Madison Approach transponder malfunctioning, Cessna Six Five Two Delta.
Madison Approach stop squawk, Cessna Six Five Two Delta.
Madison Approach radar contact lost, Cessna Six Five Two Delta.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-13, aircraft concerned are informed when the ground interrogator appears inoperative or malfunctioning.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: MADISON APPROACH BEACON INTERROGATOR MALFUNCTIONING.
items.display_text: MADISON APPROACH BEACON TRANSPONDER MALFUNCTIONING
items.explanation: Per 5-2-13, the advisory is about the ground interrogator, not the aircraft transponder.

items.situation: Your facility's ground interrogator appears to be malfunctioning, and several aircraft are relying on beacon services in your airspace.
items.para_context: Under 5-2-13, inform aircraft concerned when the ground interrogator appears to be inoperative or malfunctioning, using the facility or control-function advisory phraseology.

choices.text: Inform the aircraft concerned that the beacon interrogator is inoperative or malfunctioning.
Keep the problem internal and do not advise aircraft unless they report a transponder failure first.
Tell every aircraft to stop squawk immediately because the rule does not permit any other response.
Advise only the next facility, since aircraft are not informed of interrogator malfunctions.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-13, aircraft concerned are advised when the ground interrogator appears inoperative or malfunctioning.

items.instruction: The controller wants the aircraft's transponder and ADS-B Out turned off. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: STOP SQUAWK.
