Stop altitude squawk, Bonanza Four Six Eight Kilo.
Squawk standby, Bonanza Four Six Eight Kilo.
Reset transponder and squawk four two six three, Bonanza Four Six Eight Kilo.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-20, use 'STOP SQUAWK' when you want the aircraft's transponder and ADS-B Out turned off.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: STOP SQUAWK.
items.display_text: START SQUAWK
items.explanation: Per 5-2-20, the published phraseology is 'STOP SQUAWK.'

items.situation: You want an aircraft's transponder and ADS-B Out turned off before it leaves your area of responsibility.
items.para_context: Under 5-2-20, inform the pilot when you want the aircraft's transponder and ADS-B Out turned off by using 'STOP SQUAWK.' For a military aircraft, if you do not know whether the service requires continued operation on another mode, use 'STOP SQUAWK (mode in use).'

choices.text: Inform the pilot with 'STOP SQUAWK,' or for a military aircraft use 'STOP SQUAWK (mode in use)' when appropriate.
Use 'STOP ALTITUDE SQUAWK' because that always turns off the entire transponder and ADS-B Out system.
Tell the pilot nothing because beacon termination is handled automatically by the equipment.
Issue 'SQUAWK STANDBY' because the paragraph does not authorize any stop-squawk phraseology.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-20, 'STOP SQUAWK' is the published phraseology for beacon termination, with a military variant when the mode in use should be specified.

items.situation: You are setting terminal altitude filters for a stratified position that is responsible down to the airport field elevation. The display is moderately busy, but not so cluttered that the manager has suspended normal filter requirements.
items.para_context: Under 5-2-21, set altitude filters to encompass all altitudes within the controller's jurisdiction. Set the upper limit no lower than 1,000 feet above the highest altitude for which the controller is responsible. In stratified positions, set the lower limit to 1,000 feet or more below the lowest altitude for which the controller is responsible. When responsibility extends to airport field elevation, the lower limit normally encompasses the field elevation. Managers may temporarily suspend that requirement when target clutter is excessive.

choices.text: Set the filters to encompass all altitudes in your jurisdiction, with the upper limit at least 1,000 feet above the highest altitude, the lower limit at least 1,000 feet below the lowest assigned altitude, and normally include field elevation.
Set the upper limit exactly at the highest altitude you control and the lower limit exactly at the lowest altitude, because extra buffer is not authorized.
Exclude field elevation whenever the position is stratified, regardless of airport responsibility.
Ignore the published filter requirements unless target clutter has already caused a safety alert.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-21, terminal altitude filters encompass the controller's jurisdiction with the published upper and lower buffers, and normally include field elevation when the position's responsibility goes to the airport surface.

items.instruction: The controller wants the aircraft to place the transponder or ADS-B in standby. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: SQUAWK STANDBY.

choices.text: Squawk standby, Piper Three Five Seven Echo.
Squawk normal, Piper Three Five Seven Echo.
Stop squawk, Piper Three Five Seven Echo.
Reset transponder, Piper Three Five Seven Echo.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-10, when the controller wants the aircraft placed in standby, the phraseology is 'SQUAWK STANDBY.'

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: SQUAWK STANDBY.
items.display_text: SQUAWK NORMAL
items.explanation: Per 5-2-10, 'SQUAWK STANDBY' is the correct instruction when placing the transponder or ADS-B in standby.

items.situation: You are working a multi-target area with excessive clutter, and temporarily placing one aircraft on standby will help. You still need that aircraft returned to normal operation as soon as possible afterward.
items.para_context: Under 5-2-10, a controller may instruct an aircraft on an assigned code to squawk standby when necessary to reduce clutter in a multi-target area, provided the pilot is instructed to return the transponder or ADS-B to normal as soon as possible thereafter. Standby may also be used about 15 miles from destination when operation is no longer desired.

choices.text: Instruct the aircraft to squawk standby, then instruct it to return to normal as soon as possible thereafter.
Use standby indefinitely because once clutter is reduced there is no need to restore normal operation.
Use stop squawk instead, because standby is not authorized for clutter reduction.
Leave the aircraft on the assigned code and wait for the pilot to decide whether standby is needed.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-10, standby may be used to reduce clutter in a multi-target area, but the aircraft is returned to normal as soon as possible.

items.situation: Your area of responsibility is adjacent to a VR route and is receiving heavy VFR target presentations that are beginning to derogate IFR separation. You are not working an area that includes only Class A airspace.
items.para_context: Under 5-2-11, continuously monitor assigned codes and also monitor Codes 1200, 1202, 1203, 1255, and 1277 unless the area includes only Class A airspace. When the area contains or is immediately adjacent to a restricted area, warning area, VR route, or another category where Code 4000 is appropriate, monitor Code 4000 and any substitute code. Monitoring of the listed VFR codes may be temporarily discontinued when excessive VFR target presentations derogate IFR separation.

choices.text: Monitor Code 4000 because of the adjacent VR route, and you may temporarily discontinue monitoring the listed VFR codes if the excessive VFR presentations are derogating IFR separation.
Stop monitoring Code 4000 because only the adjacent facility is responsible for it, and continue monitoring all VFR codes regardless of IFR impact.
Discontinue all code monitoring immediately because VFR clutter automatically suspends the entire code-monitor requirement.
Monitor only assigned IFR codes because Code 4000 and the listed VFR codes are never required outside Class A airspace.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-11, adjacent special-use areas trigger Code 4000 monitoring, and the listed VFR-code monitoring may be temporarily discontinued when excessive VFR target presentations derogate IFR separation.

items.situation: You have separation responsibility for an en route aircraft, a paired track exists in ERAM, and the aircraft is still a point out with unresolved potential conflicts inside delegated airspace.
items.para_context: Under 5-3-8, retain data blocks associated with the correct target symbol to maintain continuous identity until the aircraft exits the sector or delegated airspace and all potential conflicts are resolved, including point-outs. The data block must display flight identification and altitude information at a minimum. In ERAM, when you have separation responsibility and a paired track exists, display a full data block.

choices.text: Retain the associated data block and display a full data block because the aircraft is still within delegated airspace, potential conflicts remain, and a paired ERAM track exists.
Drop the data block now because point-outs are exempt from the retention requirement once the aircraft enters delegated airspace.
Use only the target symbol because flight identification and altitude information are optional after initial identification.
Suppress the paired track because ERAM full data blocks are not displayed when separation responsibility exists.

items.explanation: Per 5-3-8, retain the associated data block until the aircraft exits the sector or delegated airspace and conflicts are resolved, and display a full ERAM data block when a paired track exists and you have separation responsibility.

items.situation: During prearranged coordination, you will penetrate another controller's airspace with one of their aircraft under your responsibility.
items.para_context: Under 5-3-9, terminal controllers retain associated data blocks until the aircraft exits the sector or delegated airspace and conflicts are resolved. During prearranged coordination, the controller who penetrates another controller's airspace must display that controller's aircraft data block information containing, at a minimum, the position symbol and altitude information. A VFR aircraft clear of Class B or Class C airspace after radar services have been terminated no longer requires retention where delegated airspace extends beyond those classes.

choices.text: Display that controller's aircraft data block information with at least the position symbol and altitude information.
Use only a target symbol because prearranged coordination removes the need for any data block information.
Drop the other controller's aircraft entirely from the display because only the owning controller keeps the data block.
Display flight identification only; altitude information is not required during prearranged coordination.

items.explanation: Per 5-3-9, during prearranged coordination the penetrating controller displays the other controller's aircraft data block information with at least the position symbol and altitude information.

items.situation: You observe a Code 7600 display for an aircraft under your control, indicating a radio failure.
items.para_context: Under 5-2-4, when a Code 7600 display is observed, apply the procedures in paragraph 10-4-4, Communications Failure. ERAM may display RDOF and STARS/MEARTS may display RF instead of 7600.

choices.text: Apply the communications-failure procedures in paragraph 10-4-4.
Treat the aircraft as hijacked and apply the procedures for Code 7500.
Assume the transponder is simply malfunctioning and wait for voice confirmation before taking action.
Instruct the aircraft to squawk standby because Code 7600 only indicates a display error.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-4, observing Code 7600 requires applying the communications-failure procedures.

items.situation: You observe a Code 7500 display for an aircraft under your control, indicating possible hijack or unlawful interference.
items.para_context: Under 5-2-5, when a Code 7500 display is observed, apply the procedures in paragraph 10-2-6, Hijacked Aircraft. ERAM may display HIJK and STARS/MEARTS may display HJ instead of 7500.

choices.text: Apply the hijacked-aircraft procedures in paragraph 10-2-6.
Apply the communications-failure procedures because 7500 is treated the same as 7600.
Assume the transponder is in standby and take no further action until the pilot confirms otherwise.
Immediately instruct the aircraft to reset the transponder and squawk the previously assigned code.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-5, observing Code 7500 requires applying the hijacked-aircraft procedures.

items.situation: A UAS target displays Code 7400, and you have reason to believe the control link has been lost. The flight's certificate materials are available at your position.
items.para_context: Under 5-2-6, when Code 7400 is observed, determine the lost link procedure from the Special Airworthiness Certificate or COA, coordinate as required to allow the UAS to execute the procedure, advise the OS/CIC when feasible, and if the UAS deviates from the programmed lost-link procedure or another anomaly occurs, treat the situation per Chapter 10, paragraph 10-1-1c.

choices.text: Determine the lost-link procedure from the certificate or COA, coordinate to allow it to be executed, and advise the OS/CIC when feasible.
Treat the code as a routine radio failure and wait for the pilot to reestablish contact before taking any other action.
Assume every UAS will fly the same lost-link route, so specific certificate or COA information is unnecessary.
Ignore the display unless the UAS also squawks 7500 or 7600.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-6, Code 7400 lost-link events are handled through the aircraft-specific lost-link procedure, coordination, and OS/CIC notification when feasible.

items.instruction: The controller is terminating radar advisory service for a VFR aircraft. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: SQUAWK VFR.

choices.text: Squawk VFR, Skyhawk Two Eight Mike.
Squawk IFR, Skyhawk Two Eight Mike.
Squawk standby, Skyhawk Two Eight Mike.
Reset transponder, Skyhawk Two Eight Mike.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-7, when radar advisory service is terminated for a VFR aircraft, or an IFR aircraft cancels IFR and is not requesting advisories, the aircraft is instructed to squawk VFR.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: SQUAWK VFR.
items.display_text: SQUAWK IFR
items.explanation: Per 5-2-7, the published phraseology is 'SQUAWK VFR' or 'SQUAWK 1200.'

items.situation: A VFR aircraft outside your area of responsibility requests continued radar advisories on your frequency because keeping it with you will provide an operational benefit. The aircraft has been positively identified, but you have not yet coordinated with the other facility.
items.para_context: Under 5-2-7, issue a computer-assigned beacon code to VFR aircraft receiving radar advisories. If the aircraft is outside your area of responsibility and you will retain it on your frequency for service, coordination must be effected as soon as possible after positive identification and before issuing control instructions or services other than safety alerts or traffic advisories.

choices.text: Effect coordination as soon as possible after positive identification and before issuing control instructions or non-alert services.
Keep providing any control instruction you want without coordination because the aircraft is already positively identified.
Terminate radar advisories immediately because VFR aircraft may never remain on frequency outside your area.
Wait to coordinate until after the aircraft leaves the other facility's airspace, because only safety alerts require early coordination.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-7, when retaining a VFR aircraft outside your area of responsibility for service, coordination follows positive identification and must precede control instructions or non-alert services.

items.instruction: The controller issued a beacon code assignment. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: SQUAWK FOUR TWO SIX THREE.

choices.text: Squawk four two six three, Citation Five Two Bravo.
Squawk VFR, Citation Five Two Bravo.
Stop squawk, Citation Five Two Bravo.
Squawk standby, Citation Five Two Bravo.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-1, standard beacon code assignment phraseology is 'SQUAWK (code)' or 'SQUAWK THREE/ALFA (code).'

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: SQUAWK FOUR TWO SIX THREE.
items.display_text: SQUAWK FOUR TWO SIX FIVE
items.explanation: Per 5-2-1, the assigned beacon code must be read back exactly as issued.

items.situation: An aircraft is operating on a flight plan requiring frequent or rapid assigned altitude changes in more than one stratum, and the operation is not compatible with a normal discrete code assignment.
items.para_context: Under 5-2-1c, Code 4000 should be assigned when aircraft are operating on a flight plan specifying frequent or rapid changes in assigned altitude in more than one stratum or another category of flight not compatible with a discrete code assignment.

choices.text: Assign Code 4000 because the operation is not compatible with a normal discrete code assignment.
Keep any random discrete code because Code 4000 is reserved only for radio failure and emergency situations.
Assign Code 1200 because VFR and special handling flights share the same assignment criteria.
Tell the pilot to stop squawk until the altitude changes are complete.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-1c, Code 4000 is used for flights requiring frequent or rapid altitude changes in more than one stratum or similar categories not compatible with normal discrete coding.

items.situation: An aircraft is still in the transferring facility's area of responsibility, and you want it to change from the code it was squawking there. No directive, letter of agreement, or handoff coordination has authorized an earlier code change.
items.para_context: Under 5-2-2, do not request an aircraft to change from the code it was squawking in the transferring facility's area until the aircraft is within your area of responsibility unless otherwise specified in a directive, letter of agreement, or coordinated at the time of handoff.

choices.text: Wait until the aircraft is within your area of responsibility before requesting the code change.
Request the code change immediately because acceptance of the handoff automatically authorizes it.
Direct the aircraft to squawk VFR until it reaches your boundary, then assign a new code.
Tell the transferring facility to terminate radar identification so the aircraft may change code sooner.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-2, absent another directive, LOA, or specific coordination, the receiving controller waits until the aircraft is within the area of responsibility before requesting a code change.

items.situation: Two pressure suit flights unexpectedly end up in the same or adjacent ARTCC airspace at the same time while using the same special-use discrete beacon code, and the duplicate code is beginning to create an operational problem.
items.para_context: Under 5-2-8, pressure suit flights and flights above FL 600 normally remain on the filed special-use code. If unforeseen events place more than one aircraft in the same or adjacent ARTCC airspace at the same time on the same special-use discrete code, you may request a code change, squawk standby, or stop squawk as appropriate.

choices.text: If necessary, request a code change, squawk standby, or stop squawk as appropriate to resolve the special-use code conflict.
Take no action because special-use flights must remain on the same code under all circumstances.
Assign Code 1200 because all special-use code conflicts are resolved with standard VFR coding.
Cancel the flights' special handling and issue ordinary terminal discrete codes regardless of mission limitations.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-8, special-use flights normally remain on the filed code, but an unforeseen same-code conflict may be resolved by requesting a code change, standby, or stop squawk when necessary.

items.situation: You are working an en route exercise FAKER aircraft that is already on the discrete beacon code filed in its exercise flight plan.
items.para_context: Under 5-2-9, ensure exercise FAKER aircraft remain on the exercise flight plan filed discrete beacon code. NORAD is responsible for flight-planning and same-code deconfliction unless operational requirements necessitate otherwise.

choices.text: Ensure the aircraft remains on the exercise flight plan filed discrete beacon code.
Assign a different ordinary discrete code unless the pilot specifically asks to keep the filed code.
Tell the aircraft to squawk VFR because exercise codes are used only before entering en route airspace.
Place the aircraft on standby until NORAD calls to confirm the exercise plan.

items.explanation: Per 5-2-9, exercise FAKER aircraft remain on the discrete beacon code filed in the exercise flight plan.

items.situation: You need another controller to allow an identified aircraft into that controller's airspace, but radio communications will remain with you. A trainee starts a handoff because the target is already identified on both displays.
items.para_context: Under 5-4-2, a point out is used to transfer radar identification to another controller when radio communications will not be transferred. 'Point Out Approved' means the aircraft is identified and approved to enter the receiving controller's airspace as coordinated, without a communications transfer.

choices.text: Use a point out and obtain point out approval, because communications will remain with your position.
Initiate a handoff, because every transfer of radar identification requires a communications transfer.
Issue traffic only, because point outs are used only after communications have already transferred.
Do nothing, because the other controller may not approve entry unless the pilot is already on the receiving frequency.

items.explanation: Per 5-4-2, a point out is the action used when radar identification is transferred but radio communications are not.

items.situation: Radar surveillance exists, but for a specified period it is not operationally feasible to base control on a radar handoff between your position and the receiving controller.
items.para_context: Under 5-4-1, interfacility and intrafacility transfers of radar identification must be accomplished in all areas of radar surveillance except where that is not operationally feasible. When such constraints exist, they must be covered in an LOA stating control will not be based on a radar handoff, or they must be coordinated by the transferring and receiving controllers for a specified period of time.

choices.text: Use an LOA that clearly states control will not be based on a radar handoff, or coordinate the exception with the other controller for the specified period.
Skip the radar handoff whenever workload is high, without any LOA or coordination, because radar service continues anyway.
Transfer communications first and let the receiving controller determine later whether radar identification was transferred.
Terminate radar service to the aircraft until the constraint no longer exists.

items.explanation: Per 5-4-1, transfers of radar identification are required except where not operationally feasible, and any such exception must be handled by LOA or specific controller coordination.

items.instruction: One word in this controller-to-controller phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: POINT OUT APPROVED.
items.display_text: POINT OUT OBSERVED
items.explanation: Per 5-4-3c, the correct acknowledgment for a point out is 'POINT OUT APPROVED.' 'TRAFFIC OBSERVED' is a different response.

items.situation: You are making a verbal handoff and need to relay all required information to the receiving controller.
items.para_context: Under 5-4-3b, when making a handoff, point out, or issuing traffic restrictions, relay the target position first, then aircraft identification, then assigned altitude and any restrictions or climb/descent information if applicable, and finally any other pertinent information not already contained in the data block or flight data.

choices.text: Relay the target position first, then the aircraft identification, then altitude and restrictions if applicable, then any other pertinent information not already available.
Start with any restrictions, then aircraft identification, and omit target position unless the receiving controller asks for it.
Give the aircraft call sign first, then any pertinent information, and state position only after the point out is approved.
Use whatever order is most convenient, because 5-4-3 does not prescribe one for controller-to-controller coordination.

items.explanation: Per 5-4-3b, the prescribed order is position, identification, altitude or restrictions if applicable, then any other pertinent information.

items.situation: You have initiated a handoff and then realize you want to change the aircraft's assigned altitude before the receiving controller has accepted responsibility.
items.para_context: Under 5-4-5b, unless otherwise coordinated or specified in an LOA or facility directive, the transferring controller must verbally obtain the receiving controller's approval before making any changes to the aircraft's flight path, altitude, speed, or data block information while the handoff is being initiated or after acceptance.

choices.text: Verbally obtain the receiving controller's approval before changing the aircraft's altitude.
Make the altitude change immediately because the aircraft is still under your control until communications are transferred.
Cancel the handoff, change the altitude, and then assume the receiving controller will accept the new condition automatically.
Change the data block first and tell the receiving controller after the pilot starts the climb or descent.

items.explanation: Per 5-4-5b, changes to flight path, altitude, speed, or data block information require verbal approval from the receiving controller during or after handoff initiation unless otherwise coordinated.

items.situation: You are about to transfer control of a track, and the data block shows 'CST' during the handoff.
items.para_context: Under 5-4-5e, the transferring controller must initiate verbal coordination before transferring control of a track when 'CST,' 'FAIL,' 'NONE,' 'IF,' 'NT,' or 'TRK' is displayed in the data block.

choices.text: Initiate verbal coordination before transferring control of the track.
Transfer control normally because the receiving controller can resolve any data block issue after communications are transferred.
Delete the track and re-enter it so verbal coordination is no longer required.
Wait for the pilot to check in on the next frequency before telling the receiving controller about the indication.

items.explanation: Per 5-4-5e, certain data block indications require verbal coordination before transfer of control.

items.situation: A handoff is being offered to you, but the target position does not match the position relayed by the transferring controller and the automated association is questionable.
items.para_context: Under 5-4-6a, the receiving controller must ensure the target position corresponds with the position given by the transferring controller, or that there is an appropriate association between the automated data block and the target being transferred, before accepting a handoff.

choices.text: Do not accept the handoff until the target position corresponds or the association is positively established.
Accept the handoff first and confirm the target later, since the transferring controller already initiated it.
Assume the data block is correct if the aircraft is near the boundary and accept the handoff without further verification.
Take control immediately and resolve the identification issue only if separation later becomes a factor.

items.explanation: Per 5-4-6a, the receiving controller verifies target position or automated association before accepting the handoff.

items.situation: You are working ERAM and an accepted handoff displays 'MISM' in the data block.
items.para_context: Under 5-4-6g, the receiving controller must notify the OS/CIC when a MISM is displayed in the data block.

choices.text: Notify the OS/CIC that a MISM is displayed in the data block.
Ignore it unless the pilot reports a navigation problem.
Immediately terminate radar service to the aircraft.
Tell the pilot to recycle the transponder before coordinating with anyone else.

items.explanation: Per 5-4-6g, a displayed MISM requires notification to the OS/CIC.

items.situation: You are working an en route point out using automated approval in lieu of verbal coordination, but the receiving controller takes no action while the aircraft is approaching the delegated airspace.
items.para_context: Under 5-4-7a1(a), en route automated approval may be used in lieu of verbal approval for a point out, but if the receiving controller takes no action, you must revert to verbal procedures. Approval is required before permitting the aircraft to enter the receiving controller's delegated airspace.

choices.text: Revert to verbal procedures and obtain approval before allowing the aircraft to enter the receiving controller's delegated airspace.
Assume the lack of response is approval and continue because the point out was sent electronically.
Transfer communications and let the receiving controller decide later whether to approve the point out.
Cancel radar service so the aircraft may cross the boundary without point out approval.

items.explanation: Per 5-4-7a1(a), if the receiving controller takes no action on an automated en route point out, revert to verbal procedures; approval is still required before the aircraft enters delegated airspace.

items.situation: A point out has already been approved, and you now need to change the aircraft's altitude before it enters the receiving controller's delegated airspace.
items.para_context: Under 5-4-7a2, after a point out has been approved, the transferring controller must obtain the receiving controller's approval before making any changes to the aircraft's flight path, altitude, speed, or data block information.

choices.text: Obtain the receiving controller's approval before changing the altitude.
Issue the altitude change immediately because the point out approval already covers any later change you make.
Change the altitude in the data block and advise the receiving controller only if the pilot reads it back incorrectly.
Wait until the aircraft is inside the receiving controller's delegated airspace and then change the altitude without coordination.

items.explanation: Per 5-4-7a2, once a point out has been approved, later changes to flight path, altitude, speed, or data block information still require the receiving controller's approval.

items.situation: You want to transfer altitude control information without verbal coordination during a radar handoff, and the information is visible in a full data block, but your facility has no AIT directive or LOA covering that transfer.
items.para_context: Under 5-4-8, automated information transfer may be used without verbal coordination only during radar handoff, via information displayed in full data blocks, and when following procedures specified in a facility AIT directive and/or LOA.

choices.text: Do not use AIT without verbal coordination; follow verbal procedures unless the transfer is covered by a facility AIT directive or LOA.
Use AIT anyway because radar handoff and a full data block are the only required conditions.
Enter the information in free text and assume the receiving controller will treat it as authorized AIT coordination.
Delay the handoff until the aircraft leaves radar coverage so verbal coordination is no longer required.

items.explanation: Per 5-4-8, AIT without verbal coordination requires all listed conditions, including facility procedures in an AIT directive or LOA.

items.situation: You want to let an aircraft under your control enter another controller's area of jurisdiction using prearranged coordination, but no published facility directive establishes the required procedures.
items.para_context: Under 5-4-9, prearranged coordination allowing aircraft under your control to enter another controller's area of jurisdiction may only be approved when procedures are established and published in a facility directive in accordance with FAA Order JO 7210.3. Under no circumstances may one controller permit an aircraft to enter another's airspace without proper coordination.

choices.text: Do not use prearranged coordination in that situation; use a proper coordination method unless published facility procedures authorize the prearranged process.
Use prearranged coordination anyway because efficient traffic movement takes priority over publication requirements.
Let the aircraft enter the other controller's airspace and coordinate only if a conflict later develops.
Transfer communications first and treat the check-in as implied coordination.

items.explanation: Per 5-4-9, prearranged coordination is allowed only when procedures are established and published in a facility directive; aircraft may not enter another controller's airspace without proper coordination.

items.instruction: One element in this fourth-line coordination entry is incorrect. Tap it.
items.explanation: Per 5-4-10d, assigned headings in the en route fourth line must use the designation character 'H' preceding a three-digit number.

items.situation: You need to forward additional control information that is not one of the authorized fourth-line entries specified in the section, a facility directive, or an LOA.
items.para_context: Under 5-4-10a and m, only messages specified in the section, a facility directive, or an LOA are authorized for forwarding control information in the fourth line. Any additional control information must be coordinated by other communication methods.

choices.text: Coordinate the additional control information by other communication methods instead of placing it in the fourth line.
Place the information in free text on the fourth line and leave it there through identification transfer.
Abbreviate the information however you want because the fourth line may carry any coordination message the sector team understands.
Wait for handoff acceptance, then modify the fourth line outside your area without coordination.

items.explanation: Per 5-4-10, only authorized fourth-line entries may be used for control information; all other control information must be coordinated by other methods.

items.situation: You need to separate a departure from another radar-identified aircraft, and the departure will be radar identified within 1 mile of the runway end.
items.para_context: Under 5-5-1b2, radar separation may be applied between an aircraft taking off and another radar-identified aircraft when the aircraft taking off will be radar identified within 1 mile of the runway end.

choices.text: You may apply radar separation between the departure and the other radar-identified aircraft.
You may not apply radar separation until the departure has crossed the sector boundary.
You must wait until the departure is 3 miles from the runway end before radar separation can be applied.
You must use only nonradar separation because departures cannot be part of radar separation until established on course.

items.explanation: Per 5-5-1b2, radar separation may be applied between a departure and another radar-identified aircraft when the departure will be radar identified within 1 mile of the runway end.

items.instruction: One word in this target-separation rule is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: BETWEEN THE CENTERS OF DIGITAL TARGETS.
items.display_text: BETWEEN THE ENDS OF DIGITAL TARGETS
items.explanation: Per 5-5-2d, on all-digital displays radar separation is applied between the centers of digital targets, and digital targets must not be allowed to touch.

items.situation: You are using target resolution between two correlated targets on a non-mosaic display.
items.para_context: Under 5-5-3, target resolution is a process to ensure correlated or digitized targets do not touch. When the procedure is used, mandatory traffic advisories and safety alerts must be issued.

choices.text: Ensure the targets do not touch and issue the required traffic advisories and safety alerts.
Use target resolution without any additional advisories because the targets remain displayed.
Allow the targets to touch briefly if the pilots are in communication with separate sectors.
Treat target resolution as available only on mosaic systems and discontinue it on non-mosaic displays.

items.explanation: Per 5-5-3, target resolution keeps targets from touching and requires mandatory traffic advisories and safety alerts when used.

items.situation: You are applying 3-mile separation in terminal Fusion when ISR unexpectedly appears in one aircraft's data block.
items.para_context: Under 5-5-4b2 and the associated note, when ISR is displayed in the data block, 5-mile separation applies. If ISR unexpectedly appears, the controller must transition from 3-mile to 5-mile separation, or establish some other approved separation, as soon as feasible.

choices.text: Transition to 5-mile separation, or establish another approved form of separation, as soon as feasible.
Continue using 3-mile separation because Fusion target symbol minima do not change after ISR appears.
Immediately break off every approach regardless of the traffic situation because ISR eliminates all radar separation authority.
Ignore the ISR indication unless both aircraft are above FL 600.

items.explanation: Per 5-5-4b, unexpected ISR requires a timely, reasonable transition from 3-mile to 5-mile separation or another approved form of separation.

items.situation: You want to assign an altitude to an aircraft, and another aircraft that was previously at that altitude has already been issued a climb. You are observing valid Mode C on the first aircraft.
items.para_context: Under 5-5-5b, you may assign an altitude to an aircraft after the aircraft previously at that altitude has been issued a climb or descent clearance and is observed on valid Mode C, or reports leaving the altitude.

choices.text: You may assign the altitude once the first aircraft is observed on valid Mode C leaving that altitude, or reports leaving it.
You must wait until the first aircraft reaches the next assigned altitude before the second aircraft may be cleared to the vacated altitude.
You may assign the altitude immediately after issuing the climb, even if the first aircraft has not yet been observed leaving it.
You may never use valid Mode C information to support this vertical application.

items.explanation: Per 5-5-5b, the vacated altitude may be reassigned after the first aircraft has been issued climb or descent and is observed on valid Mode C, or reports leaving the altitude.

items.situation: An aircraft previously at FL230 has been issued a pilot's discretion descent, and you want to assign FL230 to another aircraft.
items.para_context: Under 5-5-6b3, when the aircraft previously at that altitude has been issued a climb or descent at pilot's discretion, assign the altitude to another aircraft only after the first aircraft is observed at or passing through another altitude separated by the appropriate minima.

choices.text: Wait until the first aircraft is observed at or passing through another altitude separated by the appropriate minima before assigning FL230 to the second aircraft.
Assign FL230 immediately because pilot's discretion descent satisfies the same rule as an ordinary observed climb or descent.
Use Mode C alone to reassign FL230 even if the position symbol is lagging and the aircraft has not reached another separated altitude.
Apply the same procedure only if the first aircraft is on a cruise clearance, not on pilot's discretion descent.

items.explanation: Per 5-5-6b3, pilot's discretion climb or descent is one of the exceptions requiring the first aircraft to be observed at or passing another appropriately separated altitude before the old altitude is reassigned.

items.instruction: The controller issued a traffic-report instruction. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: REPORT PASSING THE TRAFFIC.

choices.text: Will report passing the traffic, November Four Six Five Lima.
Traffic in sight, maintain visual separation, November Four Six Five Lima.
Unable passing the traffic, November Four Six Five Lima.
Passing the traffic now, cleared direct, November Four Six Five Lima.

items.explanation: Per the example in 5-5-7, after the pilot reports the traffic in sight, the controller may instruct the pilot to report passing the traffic.

items.situation: You are in terminal single-site ASR or FUSION mode. Two aircraft on crossing courses have crossed the projected course of each other, their course difference is at least 15 degrees, and you are monitoring the tracks to ensure the targets will not touch.
items.para_context: Under 5-5-7a1, other approved separation may be discontinued and passing or diverging separation applied when one aircraft has crossed the projected course of the other with at least a 15-degree angular difference in single-site ASR or FUSION mode, provided the tracks are monitored so the targets will not touch.

choices.text: You may discontinue the other approved separation and apply passing or diverging separation.
You must maintain the previous separation until the aircraft are at least 45 degrees diverging.
You may apply passing or diverging separation only if both pilots report each other in sight, regardless of mode.
You may stop monitoring the targets once their projected courses have crossed.

items.explanation: Per 5-5-7a1, terminal single-site ASR or FUSION mode uses the 15-degree criterion once one aircraft has crossed the other's projected course and the targets are monitored to ensure they will not touch.

items.situation: You are separating a standard formation flight from other traffic using radar separation.
items.para_context: Under 5-5-8a, provide supplemental separation for a standard formation flight by adding 1 mile to the appropriate radar separation minima.

choices.text: Add 1 mile to the appropriate radar separation minima.
Apply the normal radar minima with no additional distance because the lead aircraft represents the entire formation.
Add 2 miles to the radar minima because every formation requires the same supplemental distance.
Apply wake turbulence separation instead of radar separation in every case.

items.explanation: Per 5-5-8a, a standard formation flight requires 1 additional mile beyond the appropriate radar separation minima.

items.situation: You are in terminal Fusion and must separate an aircraft from a prominent obstruction depicted on the radar display. The aircraft's data block shows ISR.
items.para_context: Under 5-5-9a4, when operating in FUSION, separation from a prominent obstruction is 3 miles for a Fusion target symbol and 5 miles when ISR is displayed.

choices.text: Apply 5 miles from the obstruction because ISR is displayed.
Apply 3 miles because Fusion always uses the same obstruction separation regardless of ISR.
Apply 1.5 miles because adjacent-airspace minima control obstruction separation in Fusion.
Ignore obstruction separation once the aircraft is radar identified.

items.explanation: Per 5-5-9a4, Fusion uses 3 miles for the Fusion target symbol and 5 miles when ISR is displayed.

items.situation: A VFR aircraft under your radar service is approaching Class D airspace controlled by another facility. You have not coordinated a radar handoff yet, and the aircraft will need ATC approval before entering that airspace.
items.para_context: Under 5-5-10d, a VFR aircraft approaching Class B, C, D, or TRSA airspace under another facility's control should either receive a radar handoff or be advised that radar service is terminated, given its position relative to the airspace, and given the ATC frequency if known, in sufficient time to obtain approval or avoid the airspace.

choices.text: Provide a radar handoff, or terminate radar service and advise the pilot of position and the frequency if known, in time to obtain approval or avoid the airspace.
Wait until the aircraft reaches the boundary, then let the pilot call the next facility without any advance advisory.
Allow the aircraft to enter and coordinate only if the other facility objects.
Keep the aircraft on your frequency and advise that Class D entry approval is not required for VFR targets in radar service.

items.explanation: Per 5-5-10d, this action must be taken early enough for the pilot to obtain the required ATC approval prior to entry or avoid the airspace.

items.situation: A radar-controlled aircraft is climbing through the altitude of another aircraft that has already been tracked to the edge of the scope. The climb occurs when the aircraft are 42 miles from the antenna, and nonradar separation has not yet been established.
items.para_context: Under 5-5-11, separate a radar-controlled aircraft climbing or descending through the altitude of an aircraft tracked to the edge of scope by 3 miles from the edge when less than 40 miles from the antenna, and 5 miles from the edge when 40 miles or more from the antenna, until nonradar separation is established.

choices.text: Maintain 5 miles from the edge of scope until nonradar separation has been established.
Maintain 3 miles from the edge because edge-of-scope separation never changes with antenna distance.
Use 1.5 miles from the edge because adjacent-airspace minima apply.
Discontinue separation once the target reaches the edge of scope because it is no longer displayed.

items.explanation: Per 5-5-11b, when the aircraft are 40 miles or more from the antenna, use 5 miles from the edge of scope until nonradar separation is established.

items.situation: You are using a radar target display with a previously specified beacon target displacement to separate a beacon target from adjacent airspace.
items.para_context: Under 5-5-12, when using a display with a previously specified beacon target displacement to separate a beacon target from a primary target, adjacent airspace, obstructions, or terrain, add a 1 mile correction factor to the applicable minima. The maximum allowable displacement is 1/2 mile.

choices.text: Add a 1 mile correction factor to the applicable minima.
Add a 1/2 mile correction factor because the displacement itself is limited to 1/2 mile.
No correction factor is needed if the beacon target is already radar identified.
Double the normal minima in every case, regardless of the specified displacement.

items.explanation: Per 5-5-12, the correction factor is 1 mile, while the maximum allowable specified beacon target displacement is 1/2 mile.

items.situation: You want to apply mileage-based separation using DME between two aircraft, but pilot communications are currently being relayed through another aircraft instead of by direct pilot/controller voice communications.
items.para_context: Under 6-1-1, use mileage-based (DME and/or ATD) procedures and minima only when direct pilot/controller VHF or UHF voice communications are maintained.

choices.text: Do not use the mileage-based procedures unless direct pilot/controller VHF or UHF voice communications are maintained.
Use the mileage-based procedures because any relay communications satisfy the paragraph.
Use the mileage-based procedures whenever radar contact exists, even without direct voice communications.
Use the mileage-based procedures only if the pilot agrees, because the communications requirement is advisory.

items.explanation: Per 6-1-1, mileage-based procedures and minima require direct pilot/controller VHF or UHF voice communications.

items.situation: A position report that affects separation was due over a fix, but it has still not been received 5 minutes after the aircraft was estimated over that fix.
items.para_context: Under 6-1-2, when a position report affecting separation is not received, take action to obtain the report no later than 5 minutes after the aircraft was estimated over the fix.

choices.text: Take action now to obtain the position report.
Wait another 5 minutes because the paragraph allows 10 minutes after the estimate.
Do nothing unless the pilot misses a second report, because one missed report never affects separation.
Assume the aircraft crossed the fix and continue without seeking the report.

items.explanation: Per 6-1-2, action to obtain a position report affecting separation must be taken no later than 5 minutes after the estimated time over the fix.

items.situation: An aircraft is approaching a fix where two different facilities want the same position report from the pilot.
items.para_context: Under 6-1-3, do not require an aircraft to make the same position report to more than one facility.

choices.text: Do not require the aircraft to make the same position report to more than one facility.
Require the aircraft to give the same report to each facility if both ask for it.
Require the aircraft to repeat the report until both facilities acknowledge it on frequency.
Require duplicate reports only for IFR aircraft, because the restriction applies only to VFR operations.

items.explanation: Per 6-1-3, the same position report is not required to more than one facility.

items.situation: At adjacent airports under your control, a Category H IFR departure will cross the flight path of a Category B aircraft in terminal operations.
items.para_context: Under 6-1-4a, the ATC facility having control jurisdiction at adjacent airports must separate arriving or departing IFR aircraft on a course that will cross the flight path of an aircraft requiring wake turbulence separation. In terminal operations, Category B, C, D, E, F, G, H, or I aircraft behind Category B or D aircraft require 2 minutes.

choices.text: Apply 2 minutes of wake turbulence separation.
Apply 3 minutes because all adjacent-airport wake cases use the Category A minimum.
Apply no wake separation because adjacent-airport crossing flight paths are exempt from wake rules.
Apply 1 minute because terminal adjacent-airport operations use reduced wake separation.

items.explanation: Per 6-1-4a2, a Category H aircraft behind a Category B or D aircraft in terminal adjacent-airport operations requires 2 minutes.

items.situation: In terminal operations, a Category I IFR arrival will land behind a Category A arrival to the same runway.
items.para_context: Under 6-1-5a, separate IFR aircraft landing behind other arriving aircraft to the same runway, a parallel runway separated by less than 2,500 feet, or a crossing runway if projected flight paths will cross. Category H or I aircraft behind Category A aircraft require 4 minutes.

choices.text: Apply 4 minutes of wake turbulence separation.
Apply 3 minutes because all terminal arrivals behind Category A use the same minimum.
Apply 2 minutes because both aircraft are IFR arrivals.
Apply no wake turbulence separation unless the runways intersect.

items.explanation: Per 6-1-5a2, a Category H or I aircraft behind a Category A aircraft requires 4 minutes in terminal arrival operations.

items.situation: At a terminal facility, two aircraft are departing in the same direction from different runways whose centerlines are parallel and separated by 3,500 feet. Both will fly diverging courses immediately after takeoff.
items.para_context: Under 6-2-1b, between aircraft departing in the same direction from different runways whose centerlines are parallel and separated by at least 3,500 feet, authorize simultaneous takeoffs when the aircraft will fly diverging courses immediately after takeoff.

choices.text: Authorize simultaneous takeoffs.
Delay the second takeoff for 3 minutes because parallel runways may not use simultaneous departures on diverging courses.
Require 5 miles of separation before takeoff because all diverging-course departures use distance instead of runway geometry.
Withhold takeoff clearance until the first aircraft passes the departure end of the runway because runway spacing alone is never sufficient.

items.explanation: Per 6-2-1b, simultaneous takeoffs are authorized in terminal operations when same-direction departures use different runways with centerlines at least 3,500 feet apart and the aircraft will fly diverging courses immediately after takeoff.

items.situation: Two aircraft will fly the same course after departure, and the following aircraft will climb through the altitude assigned to the leading aircraft. You are applying time-based initial separation rather than DME or ATD distance.
items.para_context: Under 6-2-2, when aircraft will fly the same course and the following aircraft will climb through the altitude assigned to the leading aircraft, use 3 minutes until the following aircraft passes through the assigned altitude of the leading aircraft; or use 5 miles between qualifying DME/ATD-equipped aircraft.

choices.text: Use 3 minutes until the following aircraft passes through the assigned altitude of the leading aircraft.
Use 1 minute because both aircraft are on the same course.
Use 2 minutes until the following aircraft is 1,000 feet above the leading aircraft.
Use no separation once the second aircraft is airborne because climb-through cases are self-separating.

items.explanation: Per 6-2-2, the time-based minimum is 3 minutes until the following aircraft passes through the altitude assigned to the leading aircraft.

items.situation: A departing aircraft and an arriving aircraft are using the same airport. The departure's takeoff direction differs by at least 45 degrees from the reciprocal of the final approach course, and the arrival is inbound to a fix 5 miles from the airport.
items.para_context: Under 6-3-1a, separate a departing aircraft from an arriving aircraft making an instrument approach to the same airport by having the departing aircraft take off before the arriving aircraft leaves a fix inbound not less than 4 miles from the airport when the takeoff direction differs by at least 45 degrees from the reciprocal of the final approach course.

choices.text: Have the departing aircraft take off before the arriving aircraft leaves that inbound fix.
Wait until the arriving aircraft reaches the runway threshold because departure-before-fix separation is not authorized.
Apply the rule only if the departure differs by at least 30 degrees from the reciprocal of the final approach course.
Launch the departure after the arrival leaves a 2-mile fix because any inbound fix distance is acceptable.

items.explanation: Per 6-3-1a, when the takeoff direction differs by at least 45 degrees from the reciprocal of the final approach course, the departure must take off before the arriving aircraft leaves an inbound fix not less than 4 miles from the airport.

items.instruction: The controller issued a time restriction at a fix. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: CROSS DULUTH VORTAC AT OR BEFORE ONE FIVE FOUR ZERO.

choices.text: Cross Duluth VORTAC at or before one five four zero.
Cross Duluth VORTAC at or after one five four zero.
Hold at Duluth VORTAC until one five four zero.
Cross Duluth VORTAC at exactly one five zero four zero.

items.explanation: Per 6-4-1, longitudinal separation may be applied by requiring an aircraft to arrive at a fix at a specified time, using phraseology such as 'CROSS (fix) AT OR BEFORE (time).'

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: CROSS DULUTH VORTAC AT OR BEFORE ONE FIVE FOUR ZERO.
items.display_text: CROSS DULUTH VORTAC AT OR AFTER ONE FIVE FOUR ZERO
items.explanation: Per 6-4-1, 'at or before' and 'at or after' have different meanings. The displayed clearance changes the required crossing time.

items.situation: A following aircraft will climb through the altitude assigned to a leading aircraft on the same course. Both aircraft qualify for DME separation, and the descending-aircraft-leading/climbing-aircraft-following condition is met.
items.para_context: Under 6-4-2c1, when an aircraft is climbing or descending through the altitude of another aircraft, use 10 miles between DME-equipped aircraft, RNAV aircraft using ATD, and qualifying mixed DME/ATD aircraft if the descending aircraft is leading or the climbing aircraft is following.

choices.text: Apply 10 miles longitudinal separation.
Apply 5 miles because all climb-through longitudinal separation uses the 44-knot speed differential minimum.
Apply 3 minutes because distance minima are not authorized when altitude changes are involved.
Apply no additional separation once the altitude change begins.

items.explanation: Per 6-4-2c1, the climb-through or descend-through case uses 10 miles for qualifying DME/ATD aircraft when the descending aircraft is leading or the climbing aircraft is following.

items.situation: Two aircraft are traveling opposite courses. They have reported passing the same intersection, and their reports show they were 4 minutes apart.
items.para_context: Under 6-4-3, opposite-course aircraft are separated by assigning different altitudes from 10 minutes before until 10 minutes after the estimated passing time. Vertical separation may be discontinued after both aircraft have reported passing the same intersection or waypoint and they are at least 3 minutes apart.

choices.text: You may discontinue vertical separation because both aircraft reported passing the same intersection and they were at least 3 minutes apart.
You must continue vertical separation until both aircraft are 10 minutes past the intersection, regardless of their reports.
You may discontinue vertical separation only if both aircraft are RNAV-equipped.
You must continue vertical separation until radar identifies both aircraft on diverging airways.

items.explanation: Per 6-4-3b, once both aircraft have reported passing the same intersection or waypoint and are at least 3 minutes apart, vertical separation may be discontinued.

items.instruction: The controller authorized pilot-maintained longitudinal separation. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: MAINTAIN AT LEAST TWO ZERO MILES SEPARATION FROM UNITED FOUR THIRTY TWO.

choices.text: Maintain at least two zero miles separation from United Four Thirty Two.
Maintain at least one zero minutes separation from United Four Thirty Two.
Maintain two miles separation from United Four Thirty Two.
Cross behind United Four Thirty Two at or before two zero miles.

items.explanation: Per 6-4-4, when pilots on the same course concur and are in direct radio communication, the following aircraft may be authorized to maintain 10 minutes or 20 miles longitudinal separation, using the published phraseology.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: MAINTAIN AT LEAST ONE ZERO MINUTES SEPARATION FROM UNITED FOUR THIRTY TWO.
items.display_text: MAINTAIN AT LEAST ONE ZERO MILES SEPARATION FROM UNITED FOUR THIRTY TWO
items.explanation: Per 6-4-4, the controller may authorize either 10 minutes or 20 miles separation, but the displayed phraseology changed the published time-based clearance.

items.instruction: The controller is applying DME separation to an RNAV aircraft on a VOR route. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: USE DME DISTANCES.

choices.text: Use DME distances.
Use ATD distances.
Use slant-range corrected distances.
Use RNAV route distances only.

items.explanation: Per 6-4-5, when applying DME separation to an RNAV aircraft operating along VOR airways or routes, advise the pilot to use DME distances.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: USE DME DISTANCES.
items.display_text: USE ATD DISTANCES
items.explanation: Per 6-4-5, the pilot is advised to use DME distances, not ATD distances, when DME separation is being applied along VOR airways or routes.

items.situation: You are applying DME separation to an RNAV aircraft operating along a VOR airway. The pilot's navigation system uses area navigation distance data that may not match the direct DME readout.
items.para_context: Under 6-4-5, advise the pilot to use DME distances when applying DME separation to an RNAV aircraft operating along VOR airways or routes. ATD derived from area navigation devices having slant-range correction will not coincide with the direct DME readout.

choices.text: Advise the pilot to use DME distances.
Advise the pilot to use ATD distances because they are always identical to DME distances.
Do not issue any advisory because RNAV aircraft automatically convert to the correct DME distance.
Use the aircraft's slant-range corrected ATD as the controlling separation reference.

items.explanation: Per 6-4-5, pilots are told to use DME distances because slant-range corrected ATD may not coincide with the direct DME readout.

items.situation: Two departing aircraft will not be assigned different airways or fixes, but you can assign headings that will diverge immediately after departure.
items.para_context: Under 6-5-1d, aircraft may be separated laterally by clearing departing aircraft to fly specified headings which diverge by at least 45 degrees.

choices.text: Clear the departing aircraft to fly specified headings that diverge by at least 45 degrees.
Use any divergent headings, even if they differ by less than 45 degrees.
Assign the same heading and rely on pilot visual separation instead.
Use lateral separation only if the aircraft are already established on different airways.

items.explanation: Per 6-5-1d, specified headings diverging by at least 45 degrees are an approved method of lateral separation for departing aircraft.

items.situation: Two aircraft are established on radials of the same NAVAID that diverge by 15 degrees. One aircraft is still inside the airspace that must be protected for the other aircraft.
items.para_context: Under 6-5-2a1, separation exists between aircraft established on radials of the same NAVAID that diverge by at least 15 degrees when either aircraft is clear of the airspace to be protected for the other aircraft.

choices.text: Continue other separation until one aircraft is clear of the airspace to be protected for the other aircraft.
Treat lateral separation as established immediately because the radials already diverge by 15 degrees.
Apply the procedure only if the radials diverge by at least 45 degrees.
Wait until both aircraft are clear of the protected airspace because one aircraft being clear is never enough.

items.explanation: Per 6-5-2a1, the radials must diverge by at least 15 degrees and either aircraft must be clear of the airspace to be protected for the other aircraft.

items.instruction: The controller assigned a DME arc. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: VIA ONE ZERO MILE ARC EAST OF DULUTH VORTAC.

choices.text: Via one zero mile arc east of Duluth VORTAC.
Via one zero mile airway east of Duluth VORTAC.
Via five mile arc east of Duluth VORTAC.
Cross Duluth VORTAC at one zero miles east.

items.explanation: Per 6-5-3, the published phraseology is 'VIA (number of miles) MILE ARC (direction) OF (name of DME NAVAID).'

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: VIA ONE ZERO MILE ARC EAST OF DULUTH VORTAC.
items.display_text: VIA ONE ZERO MILE AIRWAY EAST OF DULUTH VORTAC
items.explanation: Per 6-5-3, the phraseology assigns a DME arc, not an airway.

items.situation: A GNSS-equipped RNAV aircraft will be provided nonradar separation on a random RNAV route segment from XYZ direct ABC. The segment uses database waypoints shown on your map, the distance between points is within limits, and the aircraft has not yet confirmed it is established on the route segment at an altitude meeting the highest MIA along that segment.
items.para_context: Under 6-5-4a4, GNSS-equipped RNAV aircraft provided nonradar separation on random RNAV routes must be cleared via or reported to be established on point-to-point route segments. Protect 4 miles either side of the route centerline, and assigned altitudes must be at or above the highest MIA along the projected route segment being flown, including the protected airspace.

choices.text: Ascertain that the aircraft is established on the point-to-point route segment and at or above the highest MIA before issuing the nonradar route clearance.
Issue the clearance immediately because GNSS capability alone satisfies the route-establishment and altitude requirements.
Clear the aircraft on the random segment as long as the points are within 700 miles of each other, regardless of altitude.
Delay the clearance until radar identification is lost because random RNAV route protection applies only after the aircraft enters nonradar airspace without controller verification.

items.explanation: Per 6-5-4a4, you must ascertain that the aircraft is established on the point-to-point route segment and at or above the highest MIA along that segment before issuing the clearance.

items.situation: Two RNAV aircraft are on diverging routes that are more than 8 miles wide in protected airspace. One aircraft has passed the point where the protected airspace no longer overlaps, but it is only 7 miles beyond that point.
items.para_context: Under 6-5-5, lateral separation exists when an RNAV aircraft is beyond the point where its lateral protected airspace has ceased to overlap another aircraft's protected airspace by at least 4 miles when operating along a route 8 miles or less in width, or 9 miles when the route segment is greater than 8 miles in width, except that 15 miles applies where extended usable distance limitation beyond 130 miles of the reference facility is required.

choices.text: Continue other separation until the aircraft is at least 9 miles beyond the point where the protected airspace ceased to overlap.
Treat lateral separation as established immediately because the protected airspace no longer overlaps.
Apply only 4 miles because all RNAV diverging-course lateral minima use the narrow-route value.
Require 15 miles in every case because all RNAV diverging-course separations use the extended-distance minimum.

items.explanation: Per 6-5-5b, routes greater than 8 miles in width require the aircraft to be at least 9 miles beyond the point where the protected airspace ceased to overlap, except where the 15-mile extended-distance condition applies.

items.instruction: The controller asked for a vertical-separation report. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: REPORT LEAVING FLIGHT LEVEL TWO THREE ZERO.

choices.text: Report leaving flight level two three zero.
Report reaching flight level two three zero.
Say altitude or flight level two three zero.
Report leaving odd flight levels only.

items.explanation: Per 6-6-1, one of the published reports used for vertical separation is 'REPORT LEAVING/REACHING (altitude/flight level).'

items.situation: An aircraft's position relative to the lowest usable flight level is unknown, and you need the pilot to report the current level for vertical-separation purposes.
items.para_context: Under 6-6-1, if an aircraft is known to be operating below the lowest usable flight level, use 'SAY ALTITUDE.' If known to be at or above it, use 'SAY FLIGHT LEVEL.' If the aircraft's position relative to the lowest usable flight level is unknown, use 'SAY ALTITUDE OR FLIGHT LEVEL.'

choices.text: Request 'SAY ALTITUDE OR FLIGHT LEVEL.'
Request 'SAY ALTITUDE.' because altitude phraseology is always used unless the aircraft is known to be in Class A airspace.
Request 'SAY FLIGHT LEVEL.' because the lowest usable flight level never affects the phraseology choice.
Do not request a report because the current level is irrelevant once Mode C is displayed.

items.explanation: Per 6-6-1, when the aircraft's position relative to the lowest usable flight level is unknown, the correct phraseology is 'SAY ALTITUDE OR FLIGHT LEVEL.'

items.situation: An aircraft previously assigned FL230 has been cleared to descend at pilot's discretion. It reports leaving FL230, but it has not yet reported at or passing through an altitude separated from FL230 by the appropriate minimum. Another aircraft requests FL230.
items.para_context: Under 6-6-2, when the aircraft previously at the altitude has been issued a clearance permitting climb or descent at pilot's discretion, or has been cleared to cruise, assign that altitude to another aircraft only after the first aircraft has reported at or passing through another altitude separated from the first by the appropriate minimum.

choices.text: Do not assign FL230 yet; wait until the first aircraft reports at or passing through another altitude separated from FL230 by the appropriate minimum.
Assign FL230 immediately because a report of leaving the altitude is always sufficient, even on pilot's-discretion descent.
Assign FL230 as soon as Mode C shows the aircraft 300 feet away from FL230, because pilot's-discretion operations may use reduced separation.
Assign FL230 immediately if the aircraft is on a cruise clearance, because cruise clearances do not create a protected block of airspace.

items.explanation: Per 6-6-2, with pilot's-discretion or cruise clearances, you must wait until the first aircraft reports at or passing through another altitude separated from the original altitude by the appropriate minimum.

items.situation: An IFR aircraft operating in Class A airspace asks for a VFR-on-top clearance so it can continue visually above a cloud layer.
items.para_context: Under 7-1-1, do not apply visual separation or issue VFR or VFR-on-top clearances in Class A airspace.

choices.text: Do not issue the VFR-on-top clearance in Class A airspace.
Issue the clearance if the pilot accepts responsibility for remaining VMC.
Issue the clearance if the aircraft is above FL180 and radar identified.
Apply visual separation instead of VFR-on-top because only VFR clearances are prohibited in Class A.

items.explanation: Per 7-1-1, neither visual separation nor VFR or VFR-on-top clearances are used in Class A airspace.

items.instruction: The controller issued a VFR-conditions restriction. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: MAINTAIN VFR CONDITIONS UNTIL DULUTH VORTAC.

choices.text: Maintain VFR conditions until Duluth VORTAC.
Maintain IFR conditions until Duluth VORTAC.
Descend VFR below Duluth VORTAC.
Maintain VFR-on-top until Duluth VORTAC.

items.explanation: Per 7-1-2, the published phraseology includes 'MAINTAIN VFR CONDITIONS UNTIL (time or fix).'

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: MAINTAIN VFR CONDITIONS UNTIL DULUTH VORTAC.
items.display_text: MAINTAIN IFR CONDITIONS UNTIL DULUTH VORTAC
items.explanation: Per 7-1-2, the clearance is to maintain VFR conditions, not IFR conditions.

items.situation: You have cleared an IFR aircraft to maintain VFR conditions, but weather ahead is deteriorating and you have reason to believe continuing in VFR conditions may become impractical.
items.para_context: Under 7-1-2b, when there is reason to believe that flight in VFR conditions may become impractical, issue an alternative clearance that will ensure separation from all other aircraft for which you have separation responsibility.

choices.text: Issue an alternative clearance that will ensure separation from all other aircraft for which you have separation responsibility.
Wait until the pilot declares inability to continue VFR before planning any alternative clearance.
Cancel the IFR flight plan because VFR-conditions clearances transfer all separation responsibility to the pilot.
Maintain the existing clearance because weather impracticality affects only pilot decision-making, not ATC separation responsibilities.

items.explanation: Per 7-1-2b, an alternative clearance must be issued when VFR conditions may become impractical.

items.situation: A VFR arrival contacts approach for landing information and says, 'Have numbers.'
items.para_context: Under 7-1-3a, wind, runway, and altimeter setting may be omitted if they are contained in the ATIS and the pilot states the appropriate ATIS code or uses the phrase 'have numbers.' However, pilot use of 'have numbers' does not indicate receipt of the ATIS broadcast.

choices.text: You may omit wind, runway, and altimeter if the procedures allow, but do not treat 'have numbers' as confirmation that the pilot received ATIS.
Assume the pilot received the ATIS and omit all remaining landing information, including tower contact instructions.
Ignore the phrase 'have numbers' because only the explicit ATIS code permits omission of wind, runway, and altimeter.
Issue no traffic information because the pilot's use of 'have numbers' transfers landing-information responsibility to tower.

items.explanation: Per 7-1-3, 'have numbers' may support omission of wind, runway, and altimeter, but it does not mean the pilot received the ATIS broadcast.

items.situation: You need to hold multiple VFR aircraft at the same visual holding fix near the airport while arrival demand is high.
items.para_context: Under 7-1-4, clear aircraft to selected prominent geographical fixes that can be easily recognized from the air, preferably depicted on sectional charts, and issue traffic information to aircraft cleared to hold at the same fix.

choices.text: Use a selected prominent geographical fix that can be easily recognized from the air and issue traffic information to aircraft holding at the same fix.
Hold the aircraft at any convenient point, even if it is not easily recognized from the air, because recognition is the pilot's responsibility.
Withhold traffic information because aircraft cleared to the same visual holding fix are responsible for their own sequencing.
Avoid depicted VFR checkpoints unless the pilot specifically requests them, because sectional-chart checkpoints are a last resort.

items.explanation: Per 7-1-4, prominent recognized fixes are preferred and traffic information is issued to aircraft holding at the same fix.

items.instruction: The controller issued a pilot-applied visual-separation instruction. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: N123AB, MAINTAIN VISUAL SEPARATION.

choices.text: N123AB, maintain visual separation.
N123AB, maintain wake separation.
N123AB, visual approach approved.
N123AB, report the traffic in sight only.

items.explanation: Per 7-2-1, once the pilot has the traffic in sight, the controller may instruct the pilot to maintain visual separation.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: N123AB, MAINTAIN VISUAL SEPARATION.
items.display_text: N123AB MAINTAIN WAKE SEPARATION
items.explanation: Per 7-2-1, the published instruction is to maintain visual separation, not wake separation.

items.situation: You are considering visual separation between two aircraft in terminal airspace below FL180. The lead aircraft is a super and the weather would otherwise permit the operation.
items.para_context: Under 7-2-1, visual separation is not authorized when the lead aircraft is a super. Tower-applied visual separation is also not authorized when wake turbulence separation is required.

choices.text: Do not apply visual separation because it is not authorized when the lead aircraft is a super.
Apply visual separation if both pilots report each other in sight, because pilot acknowledgment overrides the lead-aircraft restriction.
Apply tower-applied visual separation if weather permits, even though the lead aircraft is a super.
Use visual separation only after the aircraft reach FL180, because the super restriction applies only below that altitude.

items.explanation: Per 7-2-1, visual separation is not authorized when the lead aircraft is a super.

items.instruction: The controller is clearing an aircraft through weather to VFR-on-top. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: CLIMB TO AND REPORT REACHING VFR-ON-TOP.

choices.text: Climb to and report reaching VFR-on-top.
Climb and maintain VFR conditions.
Maintain VFR-on-top until further advised.
Climb to and report reaching visual approach.

items.explanation: Per 7-3-1b, the published phraseology includes 'CLIMB TO AND REPORT REACHING VFR-ON-TOP.'

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: MAINTAIN VFR-ON-TOP.
items.display_text: MAINTAIN IFR-ON-TOP
items.explanation: Per 7-3-1, the published clearance is 'MAINTAIN VFR-ON-TOP.'

items.situation: A pilot requests a VFR-on-top clearance to climb through a cloud layer. You have no tops report available and need to preserve separation from other traffic if the aircraft cannot get on top by the requested altitude.
items.para_context: Under 7-3-1b, when clearing an aircraft to climb through weather and then maintain VFR-on-top, tell the pilot either the reported tops altitude or that no tops reports are available. When necessary, ensure separation with an alternative clearance, and if the aircraft reaches VFR-on-top, reclear it to maintain VFR-on-top.

choices.text: Advise that no tops reports are available and, if necessary, issue an alternative clearance such as 'IF NOT ON TOP AT (altitude), MAINTAIN (altitude), AND ADVISE.'
Issue the VFR-on-top clearance without any tops advisory or backup instruction because the pilot accepted the risk by requesting it.
Deny the request automatically because VFR-on-top is not authorized when tops reports are unavailable.
Treat the aircraft as operating under visual separation from all other traffic once it begins climbing through the cloud layer.

items.explanation: Per 7-3-1b, when no tops report is available you tell the pilot that fact, and when necessary you ensure separation with an alternative clearance.

items.situation: An IFR aircraft executes a go-around from a visual approach at an airport with an operating control tower. You direct it to proceed as otherwise instructed by ATC rather than reenter the traffic pattern.
items.para_context: Under 7-4-1a2, when an aircraft executing a go-around from a visual approach is directed to proceed as otherwise instructed by ATC, the pilot is expected to comply with assigned instructions and maintain terrain and obstruction avoidance until reaching an ATC assigned altitude. ATC must provide instructions to facilitate a climb to the minimum altitude for instrument operations and must provide approved separation or visual separation from other IFR aircraft.

choices.text: Provide instructions that facilitate a climb to the minimum altitude for instrument operations and provide approved separation or visual separation from other IFR aircraft.
Assume the visual approach clearance remains in effect and let the pilot determine a missed-approach path without further ATC instructions.
Provide no further IFR separation because a visual approach ends ATC separation responsibility once the go-around starts.
Require the pilot to cancel IFR immediately before any additional instructions can be issued.

items.explanation: Per 7-4-1a2, ATC must facilitate the climb to the minimum altitude for instrument operations and provide separation from other IFR aircraft.

items.instruction: The controller is vectoring an aircraft for a visual approach. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: TURN RIGHT HEADING TWO SEVEN ZERO, VECTOR FOR VISUAL APPROACH TO DULUTH.

choices.text: Turn right heading two seven zero, vector for visual approach to Duluth.
Turn right heading two seven zero, cleared visual approach to Duluth.
Turn right direct Duluth for visual separation.
Fly heading two seven zero, maintain VFR conditions to Duluth.

items.explanation: Per 7-4-2, the vector phraseology is 'TURN RIGHT/LEFT HEADING (degrees) VECTOR FOR VISUAL APPROACH TO (airport name).'
