items.explanation: Per 7-4-2, the published phraseology vectors the aircraft by heading, not by 'direct' language.

items.situation: You want to vector an aircraft for a visual approach at an airport without weather reporting service. Weather at nearby locations and recent PIREPs suggest the descent and flight to the airport can be made visually.
items.para_context: Under 7-4-2, vectors for a visual approach may be initiated at airports without weather reporting service if there is reasonable assurance that descent and flight to the airport can be made visually, and the pilot must be informed that weather information is not available.

choices.text: You may initiate the vector if there is reasonable assurance the flight can be made visually, and you must inform the pilot that weather information is not available.
You may initiate the vector only if the airport has an official ceiling and visibility report that meets the standard weather minima.
You may initiate the vector without any weather advisory because the absence of weather reporting is assumed when the pilot accepts the visual approach.
You may not initiate vectors for a visual approach at an airport without weather reporting service under any circumstances.

items.explanation: Per 7-4-2, vectors may be initiated with reasonable assurance of visual conditions, but the pilot must be told that weather information is not available.

items.clearance: N123AB, CLEARED VISUAL APPROACH RUNWAY TWO SEVEN.

choices.text: N123AB, cleared visual approach runway two seven.
N123AB, cleared instrument approach runway two seven.
N123AB, cleared visual approach to the airport.
N123AB, maintain visual separation runway two seven.

items.explanation: Per 7-4-3, at an airport with an operating control tower the clearance specifies the runway for the visual approach.

items.situation: A pilot reports the airport in sight at a non-towered airport where the part-time tower is closed and requests a visual approach to Runway 18. Weather information is not available at the airport.
items.para_context: Under 7-4-3, at locations without an operating control tower or where part-time towers are closed, do not specify a runway when issuing a visual approach clearance; issue the clearance to the airport only. If weather information is not available, the pilot must be advised accordingly.

choices.text: Clear the aircraft for a visual approach to the airport only, do not specify the runway, and advise that weather information is not available if applicable.
Clear the aircraft visual approach Runway 18 because the pilot requested that runway and already has the airport in sight.
Deny the visual approach because a visual approach can never be issued at an airport when the tower is closed.
Issue a contact approach clearance instead because runway-specific visual approach clearances are not available when the tower is closed.

items.explanation: Per 7-4-3, at a non-towered airport or when a part-time tower is closed, clear the aircraft for a visual approach to the airport only, not to a specific runway.

items.situation: You are conducting simultaneous visual approaches to parallel runways separated by less than 2,500 feet. The succeeding aircraft has not yet reported the preceding aircraft in sight and visual separation has not been applied.
items.para_context: Under 7-4-4a1(a), when visual approaches are being conducted to parallel runways separated by less than 2,500 feet, controllers must maintain approved separation until the succeeding aircraft reports the preceding aircraft in sight and is instructed to follow and maintain visual separation, or another authorized separation method is applied.

choices.text: Maintain approved separation until the succeeding aircraft reports the preceding aircraft in sight and is instructed to follow and maintain visual separation, or another authorized separation method is applied.
Allow the radar targets to merge because both aircraft have been cleared for visual approaches to separate runways.
Rely on runway assignment alone and discontinue separation once both crews acknowledge the visual approach clearance.
Transfer both aircraft to the tower immediately because approach control separation is no longer required once visual approaches begin.

items.explanation: Per 7-4-4, approved separation must continue until visual separation is properly established or another authorized separation method applies.

N45CD, cleared special approach into Duluth surface area.

items.explanation: Per 7-5-1, the phraseology uses 'CLEARED TO ENTER/OUT OF/THROUGH ... SURFACE AREA' and 'MAINTAIN SPECIAL V-F-R CONDITIONS.'

items.display_text: MAINTAIN BASIC V-F-R CONDITIONS
items.explanation: Per 7-5-1, the controller instructs the pilot to maintain special VFR conditions, not basic VFR conditions.

items.situation: A pilot requests special VFR to enter a Class D surface area below 10,000 feet MSL because the reported weather is below basic VFR minima.
items.para_context: Under 7-5-1, special VFR may be authorized only within the lateral boundaries of Class B, Class C, Class D, or Class E surface areas, below 10,000 feet MSL, not in Class E extension areas, and only when requested by the pilot.

choices.text: You may authorize special VFR if the operation is within the eligible surface area, below 10,000 feet MSL, the pilot requested it, and the other 7-5-1 conditions are satisfied.
You may authorize special VFR automatically whenever the surface weather is below basic VFR, even if the pilot did not request it.
You may authorize special VFR in a Class E extension area so long as the aircraft remains clear of clouds.
You must deny special VFR below 10,000 feet MSL because it is available only above 10,000 feet MSL.

items.explanation: Per 7-5-1, SVFR is pilot-requested and limited to the eligible surface areas below 10,000 feet MSL.

items.situation: A special VFR departure is already taxiing for takeoff when an arriving IFR aircraft becomes a factor. Allowing the special VFR operation already in progress to complete would avoid a longer overall delay for everyone involved.
items.para_context: Under 7-5-2, SVFR flights may be approved only if arriving and departing IFR flights are not delayed. However, IFR priority should not be applied so rigidly that overall efficiency is reduced, and a controller may allow an SVFR operation already in progress to complete if better efficiency results.

choices.text: You may allow the special VFR operation already in progress to complete if doing so results in better overall efficiency and does not create an improper IFR delay.
You must automatically cancel the special VFR operation the moment any IFR aircraft becomes a factor, regardless of efficiency or stage of progress.
You must give the special VFR aircraft absolute priority over all IFR arrivals and departures once the clearance has been issued.
You should convert the special VFR aircraft to ordinary VFR to avoid any IFR-priority restriction.

items.explanation: Per 7-5-2, IFR priority should not be applied so rigidly that it causes inefficiency, and an SVFR operation already in progress may be allowed to complete when appropriate.

items.clearance: N123AB, VFR-ON-TOP CRUISING LEVELS FOR YOUR DIRECTION OF FLIGHT ARE ODD ALTITUDES PLUS FIVE HUNDRED FEET.

choices.text: N123AB, VFR-on-top cruising levels for our direction of flight are odd altitudes plus five hundred feet.
N123AB, VFR-on-top cruising levels for our direction of flight are even altitudes plus five hundred feet.
N123AB, VFR-on-top cruising levels are odd flight levels only.
N123AB, maintain even altitudes until further advised.

items.explanation: Per 7-3-2, the aircraft should be informed of the proper VFR-on-top cruising levels for its direction of flight.

items.explanation: For an eastbound magnetic course under 7-3-2, the correct advisory is odd altitudes plus 500 feet.

items.situation: A VFR-on-top aircraft reports westbound at 5,500 feet MSL, more than 3,000 feet above the surface. The report indicates the pilot is not using the proper cruising altitude for the direction of flight.
items.para_context: Under 7-3-2, when a report indicates a pilot maintaining VFR-on-top is not complying with 14 CFR section 91.159(a), the controller must inform the aircraft of the proper VFR-on-top cruising levels for its direction of flight. For magnetic courses 180-359, the proper cruising altitudes are even altitudes plus 500 feet.

choices.text: Inform the aircraft that the proper VFR-on-top cruising levels for that westbound direction of flight are even altitudes plus 500 feet.
Leave the altitude unchanged because VFR-on-top aircraft may choose any cruising altitude above 3,000 feet AGL.
Assign the aircraft a fixed IFR altitude because VFR-on-top cruising rules no longer apply once the aircraft reports above the surface by more than 3,000 feet.
Tell the aircraft that westbound VFR-on-top traffic should use odd altitudes plus 500 feet.

items.explanation: Per 7-3-2 and 14 CFR section 91.159(a), westbound VFR-on-top cruising levels more than 3,000 feet above the surface to FL 180 are even altitudes plus 500 feet.

N123AB, cleared visual approach Runway Two Eight.
N123AB, cleared RNAV visual Runway Two Eight approach.
N123AB, cleared contact approach Runway Two Eight.

items.explanation: Per 7-4-5, the published name of the charted visual flight procedure and the landing runway are specified in the approach clearance.

items.situation: At an airport with an operating control tower, the weather is at least 500 feet above the MVA/MIA with 4 miles visibility. An aircraft not following other traffic reports the charted visual landmark in sight and asks for the published charted visual procedure.
items.para_context: Under 7-4-5, a CVFP may be issued when there is an operating control tower, the published procedure name and landing runway are included in the clearance, weather meets the published minima, multiple-runway criteria are applied as required, and the pilot reports the charted visual landmark in sight or reports the preceding aircraft landing on the same runway in sight and is instructed to follow it.

choices.text: You may clear the aircraft for the published CVFP, specifying the published procedure name and landing runway, because the operating-tower, weather, and visual-reference conditions are met.
You may clear the aircraft for the CVFP only if the pilot reports any traffic in sight, even if that traffic is landing on a different runway.
You may clear the aircraft for the CVFP even if there is no operating control tower, provided visibility is at least 3 miles.
You must use a standard visual approach clearance instead of the published CVFP whenever the pilot reports the charted visual landmark in sight.

items.explanation: Per 7-4-5, a CVFP is authorized only when the operating-tower, weather, and required visual-reference conditions are met and the published name and runway are specified.

items.situation: A pilot requests an RVFP at a towered airport and reports the preceding aircraft in sight. The weather meets the published RVFP minima, but you have not yet assigned an altitude at or above the MVA/MIA before issuing the approach clearance.
items.para_context: Under 7-4-6, an altitude at or above the MVA/MIA must be assigned before issuing the RVFP approach clearance. The pilot must report the airport in sight or, at locations with an operating control tower, the preceding aircraft in sight, and the published procedure name and runway are specified in the clearance.

choices.text: Assign an altitude at or above the MVA/MIA before issuing the RVFP clearance, then clear the aircraft for the published RNAV visual runway approach if the other conditions remain satisfied.
Issue the RVFP clearance immediately because the pilot has the preceding aircraft in sight, then assign an altitude afterward if needed.
Deny the RVFP because a pilot must always report the airport in sight; seeing the preceding aircraft never qualifies at a towered airport.
Issue the RVFP and join the aircraft to any waypoint on the chart, including one that begins an RF leg, because MVA/MIA restrictions do not apply after the request is made.

items.explanation: Per 7-4-6, assign an altitude at or above the MVA/MIA before issuing the RVFP clearance.

N123AB, cleared visual approach. If not possible, fly heading zero niner zero, maintain three thousand.
N123AB, cleared contact approach. If not possible, cancel IFR and proceed VFR.
N123AB, cleared contact approach at pilot's discretion below three thousand.

items.display_text: CLEARED VISUAL APPROACH

items.situation: An IFR aircraft is inbound in marginal weather and appears able to continue visually beneath the cloud bases, but the pilot has not requested a contact approach.
items.para_context: Under 7-4-7, a contact approach may be cleared only if the pilot requests it. Controllers are not to initiate or suggest a contact approach. Additional requirements include at least 1 statute mile ground visibility, a functioning published instrument approach, approved separation, and an alternative clearance if the contact approach may be impracticable.

choices.text: Do not initiate or suggest a contact approach; only clear one if the pilot requests it and the other 7-4-7 conditions are met.
Suggest a contact approach to save time, then issue it if the pilot does not object.
Issue the contact approach automatically because the aircraft is already in visual meteorological conditions beneath the cloud bases.
Cancel the IFR flight plan and instruct the aircraft to proceed VFR whenever a contact approach would be more efficient.

items.explanation: Per 7-4-7, controllers do not initiate or suggest a contact approach; it must be pilot requested.

items.situation: A fixed-wing aircraft requests an SVFR transition through the surface area while an IFR arrival is inbound. You are considering using radar separation to keep the two aircraft apart because the radar display shows adequate spacing.
items.para_context: Under 7-5-3, nonradar or visual separation is applied between SVFR fixed-wing aircraft and IFR aircraft. Radar separation is not authorized during SVFR fixed-wing operations, although radar vectors may be used to expedite entrance, exit, and transition.

choices.text: Use nonradar or visual separation between the SVFR fixed-wing aircraft and the IFR arrival; do not rely on radar separation alone for the fixed-wing SVFR operation.
Apply IFR radar separation between the SVFR fixed-wing aircraft and the IFR arrival because radar always takes priority over nonradar or visual separation.
Use no separation because the SVFR fixed-wing aircraft is solely responsible for avoiding IFR traffic inside the surface area.
Deny all SVFR fixed-wing operations whenever IFR traffic is present because no authorized separation methods exist.

items.explanation: Per 7-5-3, radar separation is not authorized for SVFR fixed-wing operations; use nonradar or visual separation.

N123AB, cleared special V-F-R, maintain two thousand five hundred.

items.explanation: Per 7-5-4, the phraseology is 'AT OR BELOW,' not 'AT OR ABOVE,' when using vertical separation for SVFR.

items.situation: You are applying vertical separation between an SVFR aircraft and conflicting IFR traffic at 3,000 feet MSL. The SVFR aircraft can safely remain below 2,500 feet and still stay above the minimum safe altitude.
items.para_context: Under 7-5-4, do not assign a fixed altitude when applying vertical separation. Instead, clear the SVFR aircraft at or below an altitude at least 500 feet below conflicting IFR traffic, but not below the minimum safe altitude prescribed in 14 CFR section 91.119.

choices.text: Clear the SVFR aircraft to maintain special V-F-R conditions at or below 2,500 feet rather than assigning a fixed altitude.
Assign the SVFR aircraft a fixed altitude of 2,500 feet because vertical separation requires a specific altitude assignment.
Clear the SVFR aircraft at or above 2,500 feet because the IFR aircraft is above it.
Use any altitude below the IFR aircraft even if it is below the minimum safe altitude.

items.explanation: Per 7-5-4, the SVFR aircraft is cleared at or below an altitude at least 500 feet below conflicting IFR traffic, not assigned a fixed altitude.

N123AB, local V-F-R operations in the immediate vicinity of Duluth Airport are authorized until two zero three zero.

items.explanation: Per 7-5-5, the authorization is for local special VFR operations, not local basic VFR operations.

items.situation: A pilot requests multiple local SVFR pattern operations in the immediate vicinity of the airport for a short training period. The aircraft can be recalled immediately if traffic or weather deteriorates.
items.para_context: Under 7-5-5, local SVFR operations may be authorized for a specified period upon request if the aircraft can be recalled when traffic or weather conditions require.

choices.text: You may authorize local SVFR operations for a specified period because the aircraft requested them and can be recalled if traffic or weather requires.
You may authorize local SVFR operations only if the aircraft agrees to remain indefinitely in the pattern until weather improves.
You must deny all local SVFR operations because SVFR may be used only for entries and exits, not local work.
You may authorize local operations without a time limit because recall capability eliminates the need for a specified period.

items.explanation: Per 7-5-5, local SVFR operations may be authorized for a specified period if the aircraft can be recalled.

N123AB, cleared special V-F-R to climb to an assigned altitude within Duluth surface area.

items.explanation: Per 7-5-6, the phraseology authorizes the aircraft to climb to VFR, not descend to VFR.

items.situation: A pilot requests to climb through a layer to reach VFR-on-top. Surface area visibility is restricted, but no other weather limitation prevents the aircraft from reaching VFR once above the reduced visibility layer.
items.para_context: Under 7-5-6, an aircraft may be authorized to climb to VFR upon request if the only weather limitation is restricted visibility. The aircraft maintains special VFR conditions until reaching VFR.

choices.text: You may authorize the aircraft to climb to VFR if the request is made and the only weather limitation is restricted visibility, while requiring special VFR conditions until VFR is reached.
You may authorize climb to VFR whenever the pilot requests it, even if ceilings and cloud clearance restrictions are also the limiting factors.
You must deny all climb-to-VFR requests because special VFR may only be used for arrival, departure, or surface-area transitions.
You may clear the aircraft directly to a fixed IFR altitude instead of using climb-to-VFR phraseology because restricted visibility eliminates the need for special VFR conditions.

items.explanation: Per 7-5-6, climb to VFR is authorized only when the only weather limitation is restricted visibility, and the aircraft maintains special VFR conditions until reaching VFR.

items.situation: A fixed-wing aircraft already operating SVFR inside the Class D surface area calls inbound to land. The officially reported ground visibility at the airport drops to less than 1 mile.
items.para_context: Under 7-5-7, an arriving aircraft operating VFR or SVFR within a Class B, C, D, or E surface area must be told that ground visibility is less than 1 mile and asked to advise intentions. A departing fixed-wing aircraft may not be issued the clearance, and an arriving aircraft outside the surface area may not be issued the clearance unless an emergency exists.

choices.text: Advise that visibility is less than 1 mile and request the pilot's intentions.
Issue the SVFR landing clearance automatically because the aircraft is already inside the surface area.
Deny all further communication and require the pilot to remain outside the airport traffic area.
Issue a departure-style advisory that a clearance cannot be issued and terminate radar service immediately.

items.explanation: Per 7-5-7c, for an arriving aircraft already operating VFR or SVFR within the surface area, advise that visibility is less than 1 mile and request intentions.

items.situation: Weather is not reported at the airport. An arriving fixed-wing aircraft inside the Class D surface area reports flight visibility less than 1 mile and requests special VFR handling to continue inbound.
items.para_context: Under 7-5-8, when weather is not reported and a pilot advises flight visibility less than 1 mile, a departing aircraft cannot be issued the clearance, an arriving aircraft outside the surface area cannot be issued the clearance unless an emergency exists, and an arriving aircraft within the Class B, C, D, or E surface area must be asked to advise intentions.

choices.text: Request the pilot's intentions because the aircraft is arriving within the surface area and flight visibility is reported below 1 mile.
Issue the special VFR clearance immediately because there is no official ground-visibility report.
Tell the pilot a clearance cannot be issued unless the aircraft first exits the surface area.
Treat the report as basic VFR because the airport has no official weather reporting.

items.explanation: Per 7-5-8c, for an arriving aircraft operating within the surface area, request the pilot's intentions.

items.situation: A VFR aircraft requests basic radar service in terminal airspace. You can provide normal terminal radar service, and the pilot also asks for vectors toward the airport.
items.para_context: Under 7-6-1, basic radar services for VFR aircraft must include safety alerts, traffic advisories, limited radar vectoring when requested by the pilot, and sequencing where procedures or LOAs exist. When arriving VFR aircraft are handled by approach control, paragraph 7-1-3 and applicable Chapter 5 vectoring procedures also apply.

choices.text: Provide safety alerts, traffic advisories, and limited radar vectoring as requested, and sequence the aircraft if local procedures or an LOA provide for sequencing.
Provide only traffic advisories because safety alerts and vectoring are optional extras for VFR aircraft.
Deny all vectors because VFR aircraft may not receive radar vectors under basic radar service.
Sequence the aircraft only if it is on an IFR flight plan, because VFR sequencing is prohibited.

items.explanation: Per 7-6-1, basic radar services for VFR aircraft include safety alerts, traffic advisories, limited radar vectoring when requested, and sequencing where established.

items.situation: An arriving VFR aircraft calls for terminal radar service, but a radar outage prevents that service from being provided. The aircraft is on initial contact and still needs normal arriving-VFR handling.
items.para_context: Under 7-6-2a, inform the aircraft on initial contact whenever the service cannot be provided because of radar outage and apply paragraph 7-1-3, Approach Control Service for VFR Arriving Aircraft. Paragraph 7-6-2c also states that arriving aircraft are assumed to want radar service unless the pilot says negative radar service.

choices.text: Advise on initial contact that radar service cannot be provided because of the outage and apply 7-1-3 arriving-VFR procedures.
Wait until the aircraft reaches the airport traffic area before mentioning the outage, because service availability is implied by the initial call-up.
Assume the aircraft does not want radar service unless the pilot specifically asks again after hearing the outage advisory.
Continue to advertise radar sequencing as available because local procedures normally provide it to the primary airport.

items.explanation: Per 7-6-2a, the aircraft must be informed on initial contact when radar service cannot be provided due to radar outage.

items.situation: A VFR aircraft calls the tower for the first time and the local controller already has the aircraft in sight. The controller wants to fit the aircraft into the landing sequence immediately.
items.para_context: Under 7-6-3, an aircraft sighted by the local controller at the time of first radio contact may be positioned in the landing sequence after coordination with approach control.

choices.text: Coordinate with approach control, then the aircraft may be positioned in the landing sequence.
Place the aircraft in the landing sequence immediately because visual contact by the local controller removes the need for coordination.
Refuse sequencing until the aircraft has completed at least one approach to the airport traffic area.
Transfer the aircraft to approach control because only approach may establish any VFR landing sequence.

items.explanation: Per 7-6-3, the aircraft may be positioned in the landing sequence only after coordination with approach control.

items.situation: A VFR aircraft is inbound and you want to move it into the approach sequence, but the aircraft has not yet been positively identified.
items.para_context: Under 7-6-4, identify the aircraft before taking action to position it in the approach sequence.

choices.text: Identify the aircraft first, then take action to position it in the approach sequence.
Position it in the sequence first and complete identification later if traffic permits.
Assign a landing sequence number immediately because sequencing itself establishes identification.
Treat the aircraft as IFR so identification is not required before sequencing.

items.explanation: Per 7-6-4, identification comes before taking action to position the aircraft in the approach sequence.

items.situation: Several VFR arrivals are converging on the airport and you need to delay one aircraft to establish an orderly approach sequence.
items.para_context: Under 7-6-5, when holding is required to establish an approach sequence, hold VFR aircraft over the initial reporting fix or a fix near the airport.

choices.text: Hold the VFR aircraft over the initial reporting fix or a fix near the airport to establish the approach sequence.
Send the aircraft away from the airport with no holding fix because VFR aircraft cannot be held for sequencing.
Assign the aircraft an IFR holding pattern on the published missed approach fix regardless of location.
Issue landing sequence numbers instead of holding because holding may not be used with VFR traffic.

items.explanation: Per 7-6-5, holding VFR aircraft over the initial reporting fix or a fix near the airport is the prescribed method when holding is required to establish an approach sequence.

items.situation: Approach is arranging inbound VFR traffic and a controller wants to assign landing sequence numbers to the aircraft to speed coordination, but there is no LOA or facility directive delegating that responsibility.
items.para_context: Under 7-6-6, do not assign landing sequence numbers when establishing aircraft in the approach sequence unless that responsibility has been delegated in an LOA or facility directive. The landing sequence is ordinarily established by the tower.

choices.text: Do not assign landing sequence numbers; that responsibility stays with the tower unless delegated by LOA or facility directive.
Assign the landing sequence numbers anyway because approach always controls the approach sequence once radar service is being provided.
Assign landing sequence numbers whenever more than one VFR aircraft is inbound, because that automatically establishes tower coordination.
Issue sequence numbers only to the first aircraft and let tower decide the rest without coordination.

items.explanation: Per 7-6-6, landing sequence numbers are not assigned unless the responsibility has been delegated in an LOA or facility directive.

items.instruction: The controller issued this instruction. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: FOLLOW THE CESSNA ON LEFT BASE.

choices.text: Follow the Cessna on left base.
Follow the runway on left base.
Enter left base and maintain own spacing.
Number two behind the Cessna, cleared to land.

items.explanation: Per 7-6-7, when sequencing depends on following another aircraft, inform the pilot which aircraft to follow and provide instruction to follow that aircraft.

items.situation: A VFR aircraft is inbound and you want to vector it into the approach sequence behind another VFR arrival. The aircraft has not yet been radar contacted, and the sequencing plan depends on the pilot following the preceding traffic.
items.para_context: Under 7-6-7, establish radar contact before instructing a VFR aircraft to enter the traffic pattern at a specified point or vectoring it to a position in the approach sequence. When the integrity of the approach sequence depends on following a preceding aircraft, inform the pilot of the aircraft to follow, ensure visual contact is established, and provide instruction to follow that aircraft.

choices.text: Establish radar contact first, then ensure the pilot has visual contact with the aircraft to follow and instruct the pilot to follow it.
Vector the aircraft into the sequence immediately and assume the pilot will acquire the preceding traffic after turning final.
Issue a landing sequence number instead of identifying the traffic to follow, because the number alone preserves the sequence.
Use overtaking on a close parallel final whenever wake turbulence separation is required, because radar sequencing replaces visual-follow procedures.

items.explanation: Per 7-6-7, radar contact is established first, and when following another aircraft is required, the pilot must identify that aircraft and be instructed to follow it.

items.instruction: One word in this instruction is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: FOLLOW THE CESSNA ON LEFT BASE.
items.display_text: PASS THE CESSNA ON LEFT BASE
items.explanation: Per 7-6-7, the instruction is to follow the preceding aircraft, not to pass it.

items.situation: A VFR arrival is approaching the airport. The tower has not yet been advised of the aircraft's position, and approach is deciding whether to switch the aircraft to tower now.
items.para_context: Under 7-6-8, inform the tower of the aircraft's position and then instruct the pilot to contact the tower. The pilot may be told to contact the tower before the tower is advised only if the tower reports the aircraft in sight and space is available in the landing sequence. STARS/TDW transfer points may also be used when specified by facility directive.

choices.text: Advise the tower of the aircraft's position first, then instruct the pilot to contact the tower unless the tower already has the aircraft in sight and space is available in the sequence.
Switch the pilot to tower immediately because control transfer always precedes position advisories.
Keep the aircraft on approach frequency until it lands because tower contact may not occur before touchdown.
Use early tower contact whenever the aircraft is inside 5 miles, even if the tower has not reported the aircraft in sight.

items.explanation: Per 7-6-8, tower is advised first unless the specific conditions for earlier tower contact are met.

items.situation: An aircraft under tower control abandons the approach. Tower coordinates with approach control and learns there is no immediate space available in the approach sequence.
items.para_context: Under 7-6-9, when an aircraft under tower control abandons the approach and approach control coordination reveals no immediate space in the approach sequence, instruct the aircraft to change to approach control for sequencing.

choices.text: Instruct the aircraft to contact approach control for sequencing.
Keep the aircraft on tower frequency and continue resequencing locally regardless of approach availability.
Issue a landing sequence number and send the aircraft back into the pattern without coordination.
Require the pilot to remain in visual holding until another tower aircraft lands.

items.explanation: Per 7-6-9, the aircraft changes to approach control for resequencing when no immediate space exists in the approach sequence.

items.situation: A departing VFR aircraft asks for radar traffic advisories. The aircraft is about to depart and wants to know how to obtain the service after takeoff.
items.para_context: Under 7-6-10, inform departing VFR aircraft who request radar traffic advisories when to contact departure control and which frequency to use. Traffic advisories are then provided after the departure is radar identified.

choices.text: Tell the pilot when to contact departure control and what frequency to use, then provide traffic advisories after radar identification.
Wait until the aircraft is airborne and already radar identified before giving any departure-control frequency information.
Provide traffic advisories immediately on the ground without departure-control coordination because the request itself is enough.
Deny the request unless the aircraft first departs IFR and then cancels in the air.

items.explanation: Per 7-6-10, the departing VFR aircraft is told when to contact departure and what frequency to use, and traffic advisories follow after radar identification.

items.instruction: The controller issued this termination. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: RADAR SERVICE TERMINATED, SQUAWK VFR, FREQUENCY CHANGE APPROVED.

choices.text: Radar service terminated, squawk VFR, frequency change approved.
Radar service terminated, maintain this code, frequency change approved.
Radar service approved, squawk VFR, remain on frequency.
Radar service terminated, contact tower now, squawk one two zero zero only after landing.

items.explanation: Per 7-6-11, the termination phraseology includes radar service terminated, a VFR squawk instruction, and an approved frequency change or contact instruction.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: RADAR SERVICE TERMINATED, SQUAWK VFR, FREQUENCY CHANGE APPROVED.
items.display_text: RADAR SERVICE CONTINUED SQUAWK VFR FREQUENCY CHANGE APPROVED
items.explanation: Per 7-6-11, the phraseology is 'RADAR SERVICE TERMINATED,' not 'RADAR SERVICE CONTINUED.'

items.situation: A VFR aircraft is landing at an airport where radar sequencing service is not provided. Workload permits normal service, and the aircraft is close enough that it will soon need the local advisory or airport frequency for traffic and airport information.
items.para_context: Under 7-6-11, basic radar services should be provided to the extent possible, workload permitting. Terminate radar service to aircraft landing at airports other than those where sequencing service is provided at a sufficient distance to permit the pilot to change to the appropriate frequency for traffic and airport information.

choices.text: Terminate radar service far enough from the airport to let the pilot change to the appropriate advisory or airport frequency.
Continue radar service until touchdown because any early termination would cancel the landing clearance.
Terminate radar service only after the pilot has already switched frequencies without approval.
Keep the aircraft on approach frequency regardless of workload because VFR radar service may not be terminated before landing.

items.explanation: Per 7-6-11, service is terminated at a sufficient distance to permit the pilot to change to the proper frequency for traffic and airport information.

items.situation: The tower is closed. A VFR arrival asks for pattern sequencing and wind information. Wind information is not available from FSS or a weather observer.
items.para_context: Under 7-6-12, when the tower is not in operation, provide wind direction and velocity if available, provide traffic information, and inform aircraft when radar service is terminated. If wind information is unavailable, advise the pilot accordingly. Do not assign a landing sequence.

choices.text: Provide traffic information, tell the pilot wind information is not available, terminate radar service when appropriate, and do not assign a landing sequence.
Assign the aircraft a landing sequence number because the tower is closed and approach inherits all sequencing duties automatically.
Invent a best-estimate wind from nearby traffic reports instead of telling the pilot wind information is unavailable.
Continue radar service until landing so no advisory frequency change is required while the tower is closed.

items.explanation: Per 7-6-12, provide available wind and traffic information, advise when radar service terminates, and do not assign landing sequence when the tower is inoperative.

items.situation: A VFR aircraft is receiving terminal basic radar service inside the published TRSA and asks what additional service applies there.
items.para_context: Under 7-7-1, TRSA procedures are applied within the designated TRSA in addition to the basic services described in Chapter 7, Visual, Section 6, Basic Radar Service to VFR Aircraft-Terminal.

choices.text: Apply TRSA procedures in addition to the basic terminal radar services already provided to the VFR aircraft.
Replace all basic radar services with TRSA procedures because the two sets of services do not operate together.
Suspend basic radar services and provide only traffic advisories inside the TRSA.
Treat the aircraft as IFR automatically because TRSA procedures supersede VFR radar service.

items.explanation: Per 7-7-1, TRSA procedures supplement, rather than replace, the basic services in Section 6.

items.situation: A VFR aircraft is being held in connection with TRSA sequencing. The pilot asks when further clearance can be expected.
items.para_context: Under 7-7-2, inform the pilot when to expect further clearance when VFR aircraft are held either inside or outside the TRSA.

choices.text: Provide an expect-further-clearance time or advisory to the pilot while the aircraft is being held.
Do not give any estimate because VFR aircraft in a TRSA are expected to self-release from holding when ready.
Provide an EFC only to IFR aircraft; VFR aircraft are never given one inside or outside the TRSA.
Terminate radar service immediately instead of issuing any further-clearance expectation.

items.explanation: Per 7-7-2, tell the pilot when to expect further clearance whenever a VFR aircraft is held inside or outside the TRSA.

items.situation: A participating VFR aircraft inside the TRSA must be kept separated from an IFR arrival and another participating VFR aircraft. The surveillance display is available, and wake turbulence may be a factor behind a heavy aircraft.
items.para_context: Under 7-7-3, separate participating VFR aircraft from IFR aircraft and other participating VFR aircraft by visual separation, 500 feet vertical separation, or target resolution except when ISR is displayed. Issue wake turbulence cautionary advisories as required and apply wake turbulence separation when required.

choices.text: Use an authorized separation method such as visual separation, 500 feet vertical separation, or target resolution if ISR is not displayed, and issue wake turbulence advisories or separation when required.
Provide traffic advisories only because participating VFR aircraft in a TRSA do not require separation from IFR aircraft.
Use target resolution regardless of ISR display status because ISR has no effect on available separation methods.
Apply only standard IFR radar separation and omit wake turbulence cautions because those apply only to IFR-to-IFR traffic.

items.explanation: Per 7-7-3, participating VFR aircraft are separated from IFR and other participating VFR aircraft by visual separation, 500 feet vertical separation, or target resolution except when ISR is displayed, with wake turbulence handling applied as required.

items.situation: Two helicopters and one powered-lift aircraft are maneuvering within the TRSA pattern area. The pilot of one helicopter asks whether you are required to separate them from each other.
items.para_context: Under 7-7-4, helicopters and powered-lift aircraft need not be separated from other helicopters or powered-lift aircraft. Exchange traffic information as necessary.

choices.text: No separation is required between helicopters and powered-lift aircraft under this rule, but traffic information must be exchanged as necessary.
Apply the same separation required between fixed-wing aircraft and do not issue traffic information unless the pilot reports the traffic in sight.
Separate helicopters from powered-lift aircraft, but not helicopters from helicopters.
Terminate TRSA service immediately because mixed helicopter and powered-lift operations are not authorized.

items.explanation: Per 7-7-4, helicopters and powered-lift aircraft need not be separated from each other, but traffic information is exchanged as necessary.

items.clearance: RESUME APPROPRIATE VFR ALTITUDES.

choices.text: Resume appropriate VFR altitudes.
Resume assigned VFR altitude.
Maintain present altitude until entering Class E.
Resume IFR altitude now.

items.explanation: Per 7-7-5, when a VFR aircraft has been assigned an altitude contrary to 14 CFR 91.159 for separation purposes, advise it to resume appropriate VFR altitudes when that altitude is no longer required or when the aircraft leaves the TRSA.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: RESUME APPROPRIATE VFR ALTITUDES.
items.display_text: RESUME ASSIGNED VFR ALTITUDES
items.explanation: Per 7-7-5c, the phraseology is 'RESUME APPROPRIATE VFR ALTITUDES.'

items.situation: You assigned a participating VFR aircraft an altitude that does not match 14 CFR 91.159 so you could maintain separation in the TRSA. The conflict is over and the aircraft is about to leave the TRSA.
items.para_context: Under 7-7-5, altitude information issued to VFR aircraft must meet MVA, MSA, or minimum IFR altitude criteria. If an altitude is assigned contrary to 14 CFR 91.159 for separation purposes, advise the aircraft to resume appropriate VFR altitudes when the altitude is no longer required or when the aircraft leaves the TRSA.

choices.text: Advise the aircraft to resume appropriate VFR altitudes.
Leave the aircraft on the assigned altitude until it lands because a nonstandard VFR altitude may continue indefinitely inside the TRSA.
Cancel TRSA services and tell the pilot to pick any altitude without further advisories.
Issue an IFR altitude assignment because VFR altitude rules no longer apply once separation has been provided.

items.explanation: Per 7-7-5c, when the special altitude is no longer needed, or the aircraft leaves the TRSA, advise it to resume appropriate VFR altitudes.

items.situation: Approach control is sequencing several TRSA arrivals to the airport. A trainee asks who is responsible for specifying the approach interval to be used.
items.para_context: Under 7-7-6, the tower must specify the approach interval.

choices.text: The tower must specify the approach interval.
Approach control specifies the interval whenever radar is used, regardless of tower preference.
The pilot chooses the interval after traffic is issued.
The interval is fixed by the chart and does not need to be specified by either facility.

items.explanation: Per 7-7-6, the tower specifies the approach interval.

items.clearance: RADAR SERVICE TERMINATED, SQUAWK ONE TWO ZERO ZERO.

choices.text: Radar service terminated, squawk one two zero zero.
Radar service terminated, remain this frequency.
Resume own navigation, squawk VFR when entering the TRSA.
Frequency change approved, squawk assigned code.

items.explanation: Per 7-7-7 phraseology, one of the appropriate instructions when a participating VFR aircraft leaves the TRSA is 'RADAR SERVICE TERMINATED, SQUAWK ONE TWO ZERO ZERO.'

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: RADAR SERVICE TERMINATED, SQUAWK ONE TWO ZERO ZERO.
items.display_text: RADAR SERVICE CONTINUED SQUAWK ONE TWO ZERO ZERO
items.explanation: Per 7-7-7 phraseology, the correct instruction is 'RADAR SERVICE TERMINATED,' not 'RADAR SERVICE CONTINUED.'

items.situation: A properly equipped VFR aircraft is departing a controlled airport inside the TRSA and will remain within the TRSA after departure. The pilot has not declined TRSA service.
items.para_context: Under 7-7-7, inform a departing aircraft operating within the TRSA when to contact departure control and which frequency to use. If the aircraft is properly equipped, ground control or clearance delivery must issue the appropriate beacon code. Provide separation until the aircraft leaves the TRSA and inform participating VFR aircraft when they are leaving the TRSA.

choices.text: Provide the departure-control contact instructions and frequency, ensure the appropriate beacon code is issued, and provide separation until the aircraft leaves the TRSA.
Wait for the pilot to request TRSA service after takeoff because departures are not assumed to want it.
Issue only the local tower frequency after departure because departure control instructions are not used for VFR TRSA aircraft.
Assign a beacon code only after the aircraft leaves the TRSA and then terminate all services immediately.

items.explanation: Per 7-7-7, departing aircraft are assumed to want TRSA service unless they decline it, and they must receive departure contact instructions, the proper beacon code if equipped, and separation until leaving the TRSA.

items.situation: A VFR aircraft with an inoperative transponder needs to enter the Class C area under a standing special arrangement with the Class C controlling facility. Another controller says the operation must be denied because a radio and transponder are always mandatory in Class C.
items.para_context: Under 7-8-1, apply Class C service procedures within the designated Class C airspace and the associated outer area. Two-way radio communications and an operational transponder are normally required within Class C airspace, but operations without radio communications or transponder can be conducted by LOA, facility directive, or special arrangement with the controlling facility.

choices.text: Allow the operation under the approved special arrangement and apply Class C procedures as appropriate, even though radio or transponder equipment is normally required.
Deny the operation automatically because Class C equipment requirements have no exceptions under any local arrangement.
Treat the aircraft as excluded from all ATC services because Class C procedures apply only to aircraft with fully functioning equipment.
Allow the aircraft only in the outer area because special arrangements can never apply inside designated Class C airspace.

items.explanation: Per 7-8-1, radio and transponder equipment are normally required, but operations without them may be conducted by LOA, facility directive, or special arrangement with the Class C controlling facility.

items.situation: The preferred radar sensor for a Class C area fails. Backup radar covers most of the airspace, but one portion has limited coverage. A controller proposes suspending all Class C radar service throughout the area.
items.para_context: Under 7-8-2, provide Class C services to all aircraft in Class C airspace and all participating aircraft in the outer area. If an outage affects the preferred radar sensor, advise aircraft that Class C services are not available and, if appropriate, when to contact the tower. If other radar resources are available and coverage is sufficient, continue radar services to that extent. Limited radar coverage in one portion of the Class C area does not justify denial of Class C radar service in the entire area.

choices.text: Continue radar services where alternate radar coverage is sufficient, advise aircraft appropriately where Class C service is unavailable, and do not deny the entire area solely because one portion has limited coverage.
Suspend all Class C radar service immediately because any preferred-sensor outage requires shutting down the entire Class C operation.
Continue providing full Class C services everywhere without advising aircraft about the outage because backup coverage exists somewhere in the area.
Hold all arriving and departing aircraft until the preferred radar sensor returns, because Class C aircraft should always be held during any outage.

items.explanation: Per 7-8-2 and its note, service continues to the extent coverage is sufficient, and limited coverage in one portion does not justify denying radar service throughout the entire Class C area.

items.situation: A VFR aircraft in Class C must be separated from an IFR arrival. ISR is being displayed, and a heavy aircraft ahead may also make wake turbulence separation necessary.
items.para_context: Under 7-8-3, separate VFR aircraft from IFR aircraft by visual separation, 500 feet vertical separation, or target resolution except when ISR is being displayed. Issue wake turbulence cautionary advisories as required and apply wake turbulence separation when required.

choices.text: Use visual separation or 500 feet vertical separation, not target resolution while ISR is displayed, and issue wake turbulence advisories or separation if required.
Use target resolution because ISR display does not affect separation methods between VFR and IFR aircraft in Class C airspace.
Provide only traffic advisories because VFR aircraft in Class C are not separated from IFR aircraft.
Ignore wake turbulence because Class C separation rules replace all wake turbulence requirements.

items.explanation: Per 7-8-3, target resolution is not used when ISR is displayed, and wake turbulence handling still applies when required.

items.clearance: N123AB REMAIN OUTSIDE CHARLIE AIRSPACE AND STANDBY.

choices.text: N123AB remaining outside Charlie airspace and standing by.
N123AB entering Charlie airspace and standing by.
N123AB cleared into Charlie airspace, standby.
N123AB remain outside Bravo airspace and standby.

items.explanation: Per 7-8-4 phraseology, when Class C services cannot be provided immediately, instruct the pilot to remain outside Charlie airspace and standby.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: REMAIN OUTSIDE CHARLIE AIRSPACE AND STANDBY.
items.display_text: REMAIN INSIDE CHARLIE AIRSPACE AND STANDBY
items.explanation: Per 7-8-4 phraseology, the aircraft must remain outside Charlie airspace until services can be provided.

items.situation: A VFR aircraft calls approach just before entering Class C airspace. Traffic and workload prevent you from providing Class C services immediately.
items.para_context: Under 7-8-4, pilots must establish two-way radio communications before entering Class C airspace. If the controller responds with the aircraft call sign and 'standby,' communications are established. If workload or traffic conditions prevent immediate Class C services, inform the pilot to remain outside Class C airspace until conditions permit the services to be provided.

choices.text: Respond with the aircraft call sign and instruct it to remain outside Charlie airspace and standby until services can be provided.
Tell the pilot that any radio contact automatically authorizes entry into Class C even if services cannot yet be provided.
Do not answer until ready to provide full Class C service because communications are not considered established by a standby response.
Authorize entry into Class C and then sort out services after the aircraft is inside the airspace.

items.explanation: Per 7-8-4, if services cannot be provided immediately, the pilot must be told to remain outside Class C airspace until conditions permit them.

items.situation: A VFR aircraft requests to enter Class B airspace, but the pilot reports the aircraft does not have the required altitude-reporting transponder equipment and no exception applies under 14 CFR 91.215.
items.para_context: Under 7-9-1, apply Class B services and procedures within the designated Class B airspace. No person may operate an aircraft within Class B airspace unless the aircraft has an operable two-way radio capable of communicating with ATC on the appropriate frequencies and is equipped with the applicable operating transponder and automatic altitude reporting equipment specified in 14 CFR 91.215(a), except as provided in 14 CFR 91.215(d).

choices.text: Do not authorize operation in Class B airspace unless the aircraft meets the required radio and transponder equipment rules or an applicable exception exists.
Allow the operation because Class B equipment requirements apply only to IFR aircraft.
Allow entry if the pilot can monitor the frequency, even without two-way radio capability or altitude reporting equipment.
Treat the aircraft as operating in Class C rules because the pilot requested VFR service rather than an IFR clearance.

items.explanation: Per 7-9-1, Class B operations require the specified two-way radio and transponder/altitude-reporting equipment unless the 14 CFR 91.215 exception applies.

items.clearance: CHANGE TO ADVISORY FREQUENCY APPROVED.

choices.text: Change to advisory frequency approved, N123AB.
Change to approach frequency approved, N123AB.
Remain on this frequency until touchdown, N123AB.
Cleared to leave Charlie service on your discretion, N123AB.

items.explanation: Per 7-8-8, one approved termination phrase is CHANGE TO ADVISORY FREQUENCY APPROVED.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: CHANGE TO ADVISORY FREQUENCY APPROVED.
items.display_text: CHANGE TO ARRIVAL FREQUENCY APPROVED
items.explanation: Per 7-8-8, the phraseology is CHANGE TO ADVISORY FREQUENCY APPROVED.

items.situation: A VFR aircraft receiving Class C service is landing at a secondary airport in the Class C outer area. Another controller wants to keep the aircraft on frequency until touchdown even though the pilot still needs time to switch over and obtain the local traffic and airport information.
items.para_context: Under 7-8-8, unless aircraft are landing at secondary airports or have requested termination while in the outer area, provide Class C services until the aircraft departs the associated outer area. For aircraft landing at other than the primary airport, terminate service at a sufficient distance to allow the pilot to change to the appropriate frequency for traffic and airport information.

choices.text: Terminate the service at a sufficient distance to let the pilot change to the appropriate advisory or airport-information frequency before landing at the secondary airport.
Keep the aircraft on Class C service until touchdown because secondary-airport arrivals must remain on approach frequency for the entire landing.
Terminate service immediately without regard to frequency-change timing because Class C service ends as soon as an aircraft announces a landing airport.
Transfer the aircraft to the primary airport tower frequency because all Class C terminations must use the primary-airport frequency first.

items.explanation: Per 7-8-8, aircraft landing at other than the primary airport are terminated at a sufficient distance to permit changing to the proper frequency for traffic and airport information.

items.clearance: N45CD CLEARED TO ENTER BRAVO AIRSPACE, MAINTAIN FOUR THOUSAND WHILE IN BRAVO AIRSPACE.

choices.text: N45CD cleared to enter Bravo airspace, maintain four thousand while in Bravo airspace.
N45CD entering Bravo airspace, maintain VFR at pilot's discretion.
N45CD remain outside Bravo airspace, maintain four thousand.
N45CD cleared through Charlie airspace, maintain four thousand.

items.explanation: Per 7-9-2 phraseology, VFR aircraft need an ATC clearance to operate in Class B airspace, and the clearance may include a route and altitude while in Bravo airspace.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: REMAIN OUTSIDE BRAVO AIRSPACE.
items.display_text: REMAIN INSIDE BRAVO AIRSPACE
items.explanation: Per 7-9-2 phraseology, when necessary the correct instruction is 'REMAIN OUTSIDE BRAVO AIRSPACE.'

items.situation: A VFR aircraft requests Class B transit, but workload and traffic conditions do not allow you to clear it in immediately. The aircraft is holding outside the Bravo waiting for a decision.
items.para_context: Under 7-9-2, VFR aircraft must obtain an ATC clearance to operate in Class B airspace. Approve or deny requests based on workload, operational limitations, and traffic conditions. Inform the pilot when to expect further clearance when VFR aircraft are held either inside or outside Class B airspace, and inform VFR aircraft when leaving Class B airspace.

choices.text: Keep the aircraft outside Bravo, approve or deny the request based on workload and traffic, and if the aircraft is being held give the pilot an expect-further-clearance advisory.
Allow the aircraft into Bravo without a clearance as long as the pilot remains VFR and watches for traffic.
Deny the request automatically because VFR aircraft may never be held outside Class B while awaiting further clearance.
Issue vectors inside Bravo first and decide whether to approve the request after the aircraft is already in the airspace.

items.explanation: Per 7-9-2, VFR aircraft need an ATC clearance for Class B, and when they are held inside or outside Bravo they must be told when to expect further clearance.

items.situation: A large turbine-powered arrival to the Class B primary airport can be vectored either through a published VFR corridor and airspace below the Bravo floor, or on a route that stays clear of those VFR areas. Later, spacing may require briefly extending the flight path outside Class B after the aircraft has already entered.
items.para_context: Under 7-9-3, to the extent practical, clear large turbine engine-powered airplanes to and from the primary airport using altitudes and routes that avoid VFR corridors and airspace below the Class B floor where VFR aircraft are operating. After entry, vector aircraft to remain in Class B airspace, and if the flight path must extend outside Class B for spacing, inform the aircraft when leaving and reentering Class B airspace.

choices.text: Use the route that avoids VFR corridors and airspace below the Bravo floor when practical, keep the aircraft in Bravo after entry, and if spacing requires leaving Bravo, tell the pilot when leaving and reentering.
Prefer the VFR corridor because large turbine aircraft should use the same routes as VFR traffic whenever possible.
Vector the aircraft outside Bravo without advising the pilot because leaving and reentering notices are not required for turbine traffic.
Keep the aircraft below the Bravo floor whenever possible because VFR corridors are reserved for ATC sequencing of turbine arrivals.

items.explanation: Per 7-9-3, large turbine aircraft should be kept clear of VFR corridors and below-floor VFR airspace to the extent practical, and aircraft must be informed when leaving and reentering Class B if spacing requires it.

items.situation: A VFR aircraft in Class B must be separated from a turbojet arrival. ISR is being displayed, and wake turbulence may be a factor behind the larger aircraft.
items.para_context: Under 7-9-4, VFR aircraft must be separated from VFR or IFR aircraft that weigh more than 19,000 pounds and turbojets by at least 1 1/2 miles, 500 feet vertical separation, or visual separation. When ISR is being displayed, discontinue 1 1/2-mile separation. Apply paragraph 5-5-4 when wake turbulence separation is required and issue wake turbulence cautionary advisories as required. For aircraft weighing 19,000 pounds or less, use target resolution except when ISR is displayed, 500 feet vertical separation, or visual separation.

choices.text: Use 500 feet vertical separation or visual separation in this case, not 1 1/2-mile or target-resolution separation while ISR is displayed, and apply wake turbulence handling if required.
Use 1 1/2-mile separation because it remains available against turbojets even when ISR is displayed.
Provide only traffic advisories because Class B VFR aircraft are not separated from turbojets.
Ignore wake turbulence because the Class B separation rule replaces all wake turbulence requirements.

items.explanation: Per 7-9-4, when ISR is displayed the 1 1/2-mile or target-resolution methods are discontinued, so the remaining options are 500 feet vertical separation or visual separation, with wake turbulence handled as required.

items.situation: Two aircraft in Class B are converging, and the radar display begins to show them as a merging target. A controller says traffic advisories are optional because everyone is already in Bravo.
items.para_context: Under 7-9-5, provide mandatory traffic advisories and safety alerts between all aircraft, and apply merging target procedures in accordance with paragraph 5-1-24.

choices.text: Issue the required traffic advisories and safety alerts, and apply the merging target procedures in paragraph 5-1-24.
Withhold the traffic advisory because aircraft in Class B are presumed to be separated and do not require traffic calls.
Issue traffic only if both pilots request it, because the Class B rule does not make advisories mandatory.
Cancel radar service and let the pilots resolve the merge visually because merging targets are exempt from advisory requirements.

items.explanation: Per 7-9-5, traffic advisories and safety alerts are mandatory between all aircraft, and merging target procedures still apply.

items.situation: A VFR helicopter and an IFR helicopter are both operating within the Class B area. A trainee asks whether normal Class B separation is required between the two rotorcraft.
items.para_context: Under 7-9-6, VFR helicopters and powered-lift aircraft need not be separated from VFR or IFR helicopters or powered-lift aircraft. Traffic advisories and safety alerts must be issued as appropriate.

choices.text: No separation is required under this rule between the helicopters, but traffic advisories and safety alerts must still be issued as appropriate.
Apply the same separation used between fixed-wing aircraft in every case and do not issue traffic unless requested.
Separate only the IFR helicopter from the VFR helicopter, but not the VFR helicopter from the IFR one.
Terminate Class B services because helicopter-to-helicopter operations are not authorized in Bravo.

items.explanation: Per 7-9-6, VFR helicopters and powered-lift aircraft need not be separated from VFR or IFR helicopters or powered-lift aircraft, but advisories and safety alerts are still issued as appropriate.

items.clearance: RESUME APPROPRIATE VFR ALTITUDES.

choices.text: Resume appropriate VFR altitudes.
Resume assigned VFR altitude.
Maintain present altitude until leaving radar service.
Resume IFR altitude now.

items.explanation: Per 7-9-7 phraseology, when the special altitude assignment is no longer needed or the aircraft leaves Class B airspace, advise the pilot to resume appropriate VFR altitudes.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: RESUME APPROPRIATE VFR ALTITUDES.
items.display_text: RESUME ASSIGNED VFR ALTITUDES
items.explanation: Per 7-9-7c, the phraseology is 'RESUME APPROPRIATE VFR ALTITUDES.'

items.situation: A VFR aircraft in Class B was assigned an altitude contrary to 14 CFR 91.159 to maintain separation. The conflict is over and the aircraft is about to leave Bravo.
items.para_context: Under 7-9-7, altitude information issued to VFR aircraft must meet MVA, MSA, or minimum IFR altitude criteria. If altitude assignments are required, they must be consistent with 14 CFR 91.119. Aircraft assigned altitudes contrary to 14 CFR 91.159 must be advised to resume altitudes appropriate for the direction of flight when the assignment is no longer required or when leaving Class B airspace.

choices.text: Advise the aircraft to resume appropriate VFR altitudes.
Leave the aircraft on the assigned altitude until landing because the Bravo assignment continues indefinitely.
Cancel Bravo services and tell the pilot to choose any altitude without further advisories.
Issue an IFR altitude assignment because VFR altitude rules no longer apply inside Class B.

items.explanation: Per 7-9-7c, once the special assignment is no longer needed or the aircraft is leaving Class B, the controller advises the aircraft to resume appropriate VFR altitudes.

items.situation: Approach is sequencing arrivals into a Class B primary airport. A trainee asks who is responsible for specifying the approach interval to be used.
items.para_context: Under 7-9-8, the tower must specify the approach interval.

choices.text: The tower must specify the approach interval.
Approach control specifies the interval whenever radar sequencing is being used.
Each pilot selects an interval based on traffic in sight.
The interval is fixed by Class B procedures and does not need to be specified.

items.explanation: Per 7-9-8, the tower specifies the approach interval.

items.situation: You are working oceanic controlled airspace. A local directive and an LOA prescribe a specific procedure and minima for a traffic flow that differ from the default Chapter 8 procedure.
items.para_context: Under 8-1-1, provide ATC service in oceanic controlled airspace in accordance with Chapter 8 except when other procedures or minima are prescribed in a directive or a letter of agreement.

choices.text: Apply the procedure or minima prescribed in the directive or LOA for that operation instead of the default Chapter 8 rule.
Ignore the directive and LOA because Chapter 8 always overrides local agreements in oceanic controlled airspace.
Apply the offshore-airspace rules in Chapter 8 only if the flight is VFR.
Suspend ATC service until headquarters publishes a national amendment to Chapter 8.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-1, Chapter 8 controls unless a directive or LOA prescribes different procedures or minima.

items.situation: You are handling traffic in an offshore airspace area and encounter a situation for which Chapter 8 does not provide a specific procedure.
items.para_context: Under 8-1-2, provide ATC service in offshore airspace areas in accordance with the procedures and minima in Chapter 8. For situations not covered by this chapter, the provisions in this Order apply.

choices.text: Use the Chapter 8 procedures and minima when they exist, and when Chapter 8 does not cover the situation, apply the provisions in the rest of the Order.
Stop providing service until a separate offshore supplement is issued for that exact situation.
Use oceanic-controlled-airspace procedures only, because the rest of the Order never applies offshore.
Apply ICAO procedures only and disregard JO 7110.65 whenever Chapter 8 is silent.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-2, Chapter 8 governs offshore service, but if it does not cover the situation, the rest of the Order applies.

items.situation: A pilot asks to operate on a VFR flight plan in the Oakland FIR during daylight, 80 NM seaward from the shoreline within controlled airspace, with weather equal to or better than 14 CFR 91.155 minimums.
items.para_context: Under 8-1-3, VFR flights in Oceanic FIRs may be conducted in meteorological conditions equal to or greater than 14 CFR 91.155. Operations on a VFR flight plan are permitted only between sunrise and sunset and only within the Miami, Houston, and San Juan Oceanic CTAs below FL180; within the Oakland FIR when operating less than 100 NM seaward from the shoreline within controlled airspace; or all Oceanic FIR airspace below the Oceanic CTAs.

choices.text: The operation may be conducted on a VFR flight plan because it is daytime, meets the weather minimums, and is within the Oakland FIR less than 100 NM seaward from the shoreline in controlled airspace.
The operation is not permitted because VFR flight plans are never allowed in the Oakland FIR.
The operation is not permitted because all VFR oceanic operations must be above FL180.
The operation is not permitted unless it is inside the Miami, Houston, or San Juan Oceanic CTA.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-3, the Oakland FIR exception allows VFR flight-plan operations when the aircraft is less than 100 NM seaward from the shoreline within controlled airspace, between sunrise and sunset, and in weather meeting 14 CFR 91.155.

items.situation: You are establishing separation in oceanic airspace. A trainee says only vertical separation counts there because radar coverage is limited and horizontal methods are not recognized.
items.para_context: Under 8-1-4, separation must consist of at least one of the following: vertical separation; horizontal separation, either longitudinal or lateral; or radar separation where radar coverage is adequate.

choices.text: Any one authorized method may be used: vertical separation, horizontal separation by longitudinal or lateral means, or radar separation where radar coverage is adequate.
Only vertical separation may be used in oceanic airspace regardless of radar coverage or procedural alternatives.
Only radar separation may be used because oceanic procedures prohibit longitudinal and lateral separation.
Separation is optional as long as position reports are current.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-4, the acceptable separation types are vertical, horizontal by either longitudinal or lateral means, or radar separation where coverage is adequate.

items.situation: You are assigning altitude in an oceanic control area. No special charting or direction provides a different altimeter-setting procedure.
items.para_context: Under 8-1-5, within oceanic control areas, unless directed or charted otherwise, altitude assignment must be based on flight levels and a standard altimeter setting of 29.92 inches Hg.

choices.text: Base the altitude assignment on flight levels using a standard altimeter setting of 29.92 inches Hg.
Base the assignment on the nearest coastal altimeter setting because oceanic flight levels are optional unless weather is IFR.
Assign altitude in feet MSL using the destination altimeter regardless of published oceanic procedures.
Delay the altitude assignment until the pilot reports a local altimeter setting from another aircraft.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-5, oceanic control-area altitude assignments use flight levels and the standard 29.92 setting unless another procedure is directed or charted.

items.situation: A position report that affects separation has not been received. The aircraft has now passed the control estimate by 10 minutes, and no other instruction specifies a different timing requirement.
items.para_context: Under 8-1-6, when a position report affecting separation is not received, take action to obtain the report no later than 10 minutes after the control estimate unless otherwise specified.

choices.text: Take action now to obtain the position report because the latest time is 10 minutes after the control estimate unless otherwise specified.
Wait another 10 minutes because the rule allows action only after 20 minutes past the estimate.
Take no action if the flight was previously in contact, because missed reports do not affect separation until the next fix.
Cancel separation responsibility and assume the aircraft crossed on time.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-6, action to obtain a missed report affecting separation is required no later than 10 minutes after the control estimate unless another time is specified.

items.situation: You identify a gross navigation error in oceanic airspace and a trainee asks whether there is a standard FAA reporting procedure for that event and related height, time, intervention, and SAO-verification issues.
items.para_context: Under 8-1-7, FAA Order JO 7210.632 establishes procedures for reporting gross navigation errors, height errors, time (longitudinal) errors, intervention, and Special Area of Operations verification in oceanic airspace. The data supports risk modeling for separation standard reductions.

choices.text: Follow the reporting procedures in FAA Order JO 7210.632 for those oceanic error and verification events.
Log the event locally only, because JO 7110.65 does not reference any formal FAA reporting procedure for oceanic errors.
Treat only gross navigation errors as reportable and ignore height, time, intervention, and SAO verification issues.
Report the event only if separation was actually lost, because risk-modeling data is not collected for other oceanic errors.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-7, JO 7210.632 establishes the reporting procedures for those oceanic error categories and SAO verification.

items.situation: You are applying time-based separation in oceanic airspace. A trainee asks whether the automated system estimate can be ignored because only pilot position reports count for separation.
items.para_context: Under 8-1-8, control estimates are the estimated position of aircraft with reference to time as determined by the ATC automation system in use or calculated by the controller using known wind patterns, previous aircraft transit times, pilot progress reports, and pilot estimates. They may be updated through automated position reports or manually by the controller, and they must be used when applying time-based separation minima.

choices.text: Use control estimates when applying time-based separation minima and update them through automated position reports or manual controller updates as needed.
Ignore control estimates for time-based separation because only live pilot reports may be used to establish separation.
Use control estimates only when radar separation is being applied, not for time-based separation.
Freeze the original estimate and do not update it once the flight enters oceanic airspace.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-8, control estimates must be used for time-based separation and may be updated by automation or manually by the controller.

items.situation: A non-RVSM aircraft wants to transit oceanic RVSM airspace after losing RVSM approval because of an equipment failure. The pilot says the flight is repositioning to maintenance, but no approval from the operations supervisor/CIC has been obtained yet.
items.para_context: Under 8-1-9, non-RVSM aircraft may not be permitted in RVSM airspace unless they meet the criteria of excepted aircraft and are previously approved by the operations supervisor/CIC. Excepted aircraft include certain delivery, maintenance-ferry, mercy/humanitarian, and under-wing spare-engine operations in the Oakland, Anchorage, and Arctic FIRs. These exceptions are accommodated on a workload or traffic-permitting basis, and all other requirements of paragraph 2-1-29 remain applicable.

choices.text: Do not permit the aircraft in RVSM airspace until it qualifies as an excepted aircraft and has been previously approved by the operations supervisor or CIC.
Allow the aircraft automatically because any maintenance repositioning flight may enter RVSM airspace without prior approval.
Allow the aircraft if the pilot accepts responsibility for non-RVSM operations, because supervisor approval is only needed outside oceanic airspace.
Deny the flight in all cases because no non-RVSM aircraft can ever be accommodated in oceanic RVSM airspace.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-9, an excepted non-RVSM aircraft still needs prior approval from the operations supervisor/CIC before it may be permitted in RVSM airspace.

items.clearance: UNABLE RIGHT OF COURSE DEVIATION, TRAFFIC IS DAL45, 40 MILES AHEAD, FLIGHT LEVEL THREE FIVE ZERO, WESTBOUND, SAY INTENTIONS.

choices.text: Unable right of course deviation, traffic is DAL45, 40 miles ahead, flight level three five zero, westbound, say intentions.
Approved right of course deviation, traffic no factor, continuing present clearance.
Unable deviation, descend at pilot discretion, deviation approved when able.
Traffic observed, deviation approved without restrictions.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-10 phraseology, when ATC cannot clear the requested deviation, the controller advises unable, gives conflicting traffic information, and requests the pilot's intentions.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: UNABLE RIGHT OF COURSE DEVIATION, TRAFFIC IS DELTA FOUR FIVE, FOUR ZERO MILES AHEAD, FLIGHT LEVEL THREE FIVE ZERO, WESTBOUND, SAY INTENTIONS.
items.display_text: UNABLE RIGHT OF COURSE DEVIATION TRAFFIC IS DELTA FOUR FIVE FOUR ZERO MILES AHEAD FLIGHT LEVEL THREE FIVE ZERO WESTBOUND SAY APPROVED
items.explanation: Per 8-1-10 phraseology, the controller ends the transmission with 'SAY INTENTIONS,' not 'SAY APPROVED.'

items.situation: An aircraft in oceanic controlled airspace requests a weather deviation. Conflicting traffic exists in the horizontal dimension, and you cannot establish the required separation for the deviation clearance.
items.para_context: Under 8-1-10, weather deviations require an ATC clearance and should take priority over routine requests. If there is no conflicting traffic in the horizontal dimension, issue clearance to deviate. If there is conflicting traffic in the horizontal dimension, establish vertical separation and then clear the deviation. If required separation cannot be established, advise the pilot unable, advise of conflicting traffic, and request the pilot's intentions.

choices.text: Advise that you are unable to issue the requested deviation, provide the conflicting traffic information, and request the pilot's intentions.
Approve the deviation immediately because weather deviations always override separation requirements.
Delay any response until the aircraft begins deviating, then coordinate after the fact.
Issue a vector instead of a clearance because oceanic weather deviations do not require pilot intentions when traffic conflicts exist.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-10, if conflicting traffic exists and required separation cannot be established, the controller must advise unable, give traffic information, and ask for intentions.

items.situation: An IFR flight is nearing an adjacent ATS facility boundary. Its estimate has changed by 4 minutes, the protected airspace will overlap the common boundary, and the receiving facility needs time to analyze the new information.
items.para_context: Under 8-2-1, ARTCCs must forward current flight plan and control information as a flight progresses, coordinate in sufficient time for the receiving facility to analyze the data and accomplish additional coordination, coordinate when protected airspace overlaps a common boundary, forward revisions of estimates of 3 minutes or more, and coordinate with adjacent facilities on IFR and VFR flights to ensure continuation of appropriate services.

choices.text: Forward the updated flight plan and control information, coordinate early enough for analysis, coordinate the boundary overlap, and send the revised estimate because the change is 3 minutes or more.
Wait until the aircraft checks in with the next facility, because estimate revisions are forwarded only after transfer of communications.
Coordinate only the overlap issue, because estimate revisions are not forwarded unless they exceed 5 minutes.
Handle the flight as IFR-only coordination and do not pass revised control information until requested by the receiving facility.

items.explanation: Per 8-2-1, ARTCC coordination includes timely forwarding of flight plan/control information, boundary-overlap coordination, and forwarding estimate revisions of 3 minutes or more.

items.situation: You are the accepting unit for a nonradar transfer. The aircraft has not yet reached the agreed transfer point, you need a change to its clearance to accept it, and communications handoff timing is approaching the boundary.
