items.para_context: Under 8-2-2, only one ATC unit controls an aircraft at a time. Control transfers at the estimated control boundary or another agreed time or point. The transferring unit forwards changed flight plan or control data pertinent to the transfer. The accepting unit must notify the transferring unit if unable to accept under the specified terms or must state the changes or conditions required. The accepting unit must not alter the clearance before the transfer point without prior approval of the transferring unit. Where nonradar separation minima are applied, transfer of air-ground communications must be made 5 minutes before the aircraft reaches the boundary unless otherwise agreed.

choices.text: Tell the transferring unit what change or condition is required to accept the aircraft, do not alter the clearance before the transfer point without prior approval, and make the communications transfer 5 minutes before the boundary unless otherwise agreed.
Change the aircraft's clearance immediately because the accepting unit may alter it at any time before the transfer point if separation will improve.
Wait until after the boundary crossing to mention the needed condition because the accepting unit may not object before taking control.
Hold communications on the transferring frequency until the aircraft checks in across the boundary because nonradar transfers never use a 5-minute communications lead time.

items.explanation: Per 8-2-2, the accepting unit must state any required changes to accept the aircraft, may not alter the clearance before the transfer point without prior approval, and in nonradar operations communications transfer is made 5 minutes before the boundary unless otherwise agreed.

items.situation: Two facilities with AIDC capability want to begin using interfacility data communications for coordination on a route structure. One controller proposes using it ad hoc without checking the regional interface requirements or the facility LOA.
items.para_context: Under 8-2-3, where interfacility data communications capability has been implemented, its use for ATC coordination should be accomplished in accordance with regional Interface Control Documents and supported by letters of agreement between the facilities concerned.

choices.text: Use AIDC coordination in accordance with the regional Interface Control Documents and the supporting letter of agreement between the facilities.
Use AIDC in any manner the facilities prefer because regional interface documents and LOAs are optional once the capability exists.
Avoid AIDC entirely because automated interfacility coordination may not be used in oceanic operations.
Use voice coordination only until national headquarters issues a separate AIDC order for that route.

items.explanation: Per 8-2-3, implemented interfacility data communications are used in accordance with regional Interface Control Documents and supported by letters of agreement.

items.situation: You are applying time-based longitudinal separation. An updated control estimate shows the spacing will drop below the minimum previously being applied, and the two aircraft require different time-based standards.
items.para_context: Under 8-3-1, longitudinal separation must ensure that spacing between estimated aircraft positions is never less than the prescribed minimum. If one aircraft requires a different time-based longitudinal standard than another, apply the larger of the two. If an updated control estimate indicates the minimum no longer exists, controllers must ensure separation is reestablished and issue traffic information as necessary.

choices.text: Apply the larger of the two required time-based standards, reestablish separation once the update shows the minimum no longer exists, and issue traffic information as necessary.
Keep the smaller standard because it was already in use, and wait to see whether pilot reports restore the minimum before taking action.
Treat the update as advisory only because longitudinal separation is based on the original estimate until the next fix is reported.
Convert immediately to distance-based separation without further coordination because time-based minima cannot be reestablished once lost.

items.explanation: Per 8-3-1, the larger applicable time-based standard must be used, and if an updated estimate shows the minimum no longer exists, the controller must reestablish separation and issue traffic information as necessary.

items.situation: Two aircraft are on reciprocal tracks under longitudinal separation. They have been vertically separated for the required interval before and after the estimated passing time. One controller wants to discontinue vertical separation immediately after the estimated pass, even though the crews have not yet both reported passing fixes or significant points that confirm they are past each other.
items.para_context: Under 8-3-2, reciprocal tracks are treated the same as reciprocal courses. Aircraft on reciprocal tracks must be vertically separated for a time interval equal to the applicable minimum before and after they are estimated to pass. Vertical separation may be discontinued only after both aircraft have reported passing a significant point and are separated by at least the same-direction longitudinal minimum, or after both have reported passing ground-based NAVAIDs or DME fixes indicating they have passed each other.

choices.text: Keep the vertical separation in place until one of the specified reciprocal-track discontinuance conditions is met, such as both aircraft reporting passed points with the required same-direction spacing or both reporting passed NAVAIDs/DME fixes showing they are past each other.
Discontinue vertical separation immediately after the estimated passing time because the reciprocal-track requirement ends automatically at that point.
Treat reciprocal tracks the same as same-track operations and use the same estimated-spacing rule with no vertical-separation requirement.
Convert the operation to visual separation because reciprocal tracks may not continue under longitudinal standards after the estimated pass.

items.explanation: Per 8-3-2, reciprocal-track aircraft remain vertically separated for the required interval and vertical separation is not discontinued until one of the listed report-based conditions is satisfied.

items.clearance: MAINTAIN MACH POINT EIGHT FOUR OR GREATER.

choices.text: Maintain Mach point eight four or greater.
Maintain Mach point eight four or less.
Maintain speed two eight zero knots or greater.
Maintain Mach point eight two or greater; do not exceed Mach point eight three.

items.explanation: Per 8-3-3, Mach number technique phraseology includes assignments such as 'Maintain Mach point eight four or greater.'

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: MAINTAIN MACH POINT EIGHT FOUR OR GREATER.
items.display_text: MAINTAIN MACH POINT EIGHT FOUR OR LESS
items.explanation: Under 8-3-3, this phraseology example is 'Maintain Mach point eight four or greater,' not 'or less.'

items.situation: You want to apply Mach number technique to two turbojet aircraft on the same track. They have not both reported over a common point yet, but your estimates show the required time interval exists now and will still exist at the common point. A trainee says you can use the reduced minimum without issuing Mach assignments because the estimates already work.
items.para_context: Under 8-3-3, Mach number technique allows reduced longitudinal separation minima only when the aircraft are turbojets, follow the same track or continuously diverging tracks, and the required time interval exists and will exist at a common point or at significant points if no common point exists. A Mach number or, when appropriate, a Mach range must be issued to each aircraft unless otherwise prescribed by ICAO regional agreement.

choices.text: Use Mach number technique only after issuing the required Mach number or Mach range to each aircraft and confirming the route and time-interval conditions continue to be met.
Use the reduced Mach-technique minimum without Mach assignments because the current estimates already show adequate spacing.
Apply Mach number technique to any aircraft type as long as both aircraft eventually reach the same fix.
Wait until both aircraft leave level flight because Mach number technique may only be applied in cruise and not during climbs or descents.

items.explanation: Per 8-3-3, Mach number technique is limited to turbojets meeting the route and spacing conditions, and Mach assignments must be issued to each aircraft unless otherwise prescribed.

items.situation: You are separating two aircraft laterally in the Gulf of America Low Offshore airspace on published Grid System waypoints. The proposed routes are only 10 NM apart, and another controller argues that any distinct routes are sufficient because the paths are not identical.
items.para_context: Under 8-4-1, lateral separation is applied by assigning different flight paths whose widths or protected airspace do not overlap. Within the Gulf of America Low Offshore airspace, use 12 NM between aircraft whose flight paths are defined by published Grid System waypoints.

choices.text: Do not use the 10 NM spacing; assign flight paths whose protected airspace does not overlap, and within the Gulf Grid System use 12 NM between the published waypoint-defined paths.
Approve the routes because any two nonidentical tracks automatically satisfy lateral separation in offshore airspace.
Use 5 NM because oceanic grid-system spacing is based on radar standards once both aircraft are identified.
Rely on longitudinal separation instead, because lateral separation may not be applied on published Grid System waypoints.

items.explanation: Per 8-4-1, flight paths must be separated so protected airspace does not overlap, and within the Gulf Grid System the minimum is 12 NM.

items.situation: Two aircraft are crossing an oceanic boundary into airspace with a larger lateral minimum than the airspace they are leaving. The smaller separation still exists at the boundary, but the flight paths diverge by only 10 degrees. Another controller says that is close enough to use the boundary exception.
items.para_context: Under 8-4-2, lateral separation may exist for aircraft crossing an oceanic boundary into airspace with a larger lateral minimum only if the smaller separation exists at the boundary and the flight paths diverge by 15 degrees or more until the larger minimum is established. Other methods include nonoverlapping reduced route protected airspace and intersecting-flight-path measurements based on the required perpendicular distances.

choices.text: Do not use that boundary-crossing method yet; for the larger-minimum transition, the smaller separation must exist at the boundary and the flight paths must diverge by 15 degrees or more until the larger minimum is established.
Use the method because any divergence greater than 5 degrees is sufficient once the smaller separation exists at the boundary.
Use the method because the larger lateral minimum does not apply until both aircraft are well inside the next airspace.
Discard lateral separation entirely because boundary-crossing aircraft must be handled only with vertical separation.

items.explanation: Per 8-4-2a3, the boundary-crossing method requires the smaller separation at the boundary and at least 15 degrees of divergence until the larger minimum is established.

items.situation: You want to reduce route protected airspace for an aircraft at FL270. The route has been satisfactorily flight checked and users were notified. A trainee proposes reducing the protected airspace to 5 miles on each side of centerline for the first 57.14 miles from the NAVAID because that is the below-FL240 rule.
items.para_context: Under 8-4-3, reduced route protected airspace may be used only after the routes have been satisfactorily flight checked and notice has been given to users. At and above FL240, the protected airspace may be reduced to 10 miles on each side of the route centerline to a distance of 114.29 miles from the NAVAID, then increasing in width on a 5-degree angle from the route centerline to the maximum width allowable within the lateral minima.

choices.text: Use the at-or-above-FL240 standard instead: reduce to 10 miles on each side to 114.29 miles from the NAVAID, then widen on a 5-degree angle to the maximum allowable width.
Use the below-FL240 standard because once the route is flight checked and users are notified, altitude no longer affects the reduced-width segment.
Reduce the protected airspace to 10 miles indefinitely with no widening segment because the flight is at FL270.
Do not reduce the protected airspace at all because reduced route protected airspace may never be used above FL240.

items.explanation: Per 8-4-3b, at and above FL240 the reduced segment is 10 miles on each side to 114.29 miles from the NAVAID, followed by widening on a 5-degree angle.

items.situation: Two aircraft are established on radials from the same VORTAC that diverge by 20 degrees. A controller wants to treat them as track separated immediately, even though neither aircraft has yet flown far enough to be clear of the airspace that must be protected for the other aircraft under the divergence-distance table.
items.para_context: Under 8-4-4, track separation on radials of the same VOR/VORTAC/TACAN exists only when the radials diverge by at least 15 degrees and either aircraft is clear of the airspace to be protected for the other aircraft, using Table 8-4-1 to determine the required flight distance. Separate rules also apply for same-NAVAID NDB tracks, different NAVAIDs, and dead reckoning tracks.

choices.text: Do not apply track separation yet; for same-NAVAID VOR/VORTAC/TACAN tracks, the radials must diverge by at least 15 degrees and one aircraft must also be clear of the other aircraft's protected airspace using the table distance.
Apply track separation immediately because any divergence greater than 15 degrees is sufficient with no additional distance requirement.
Apply track separation only after the aircraft are 15 miles from the VORTAC, because that same-distance rule applies to all same-NAVAID methods.
Do not use track separation at all for aircraft on radials from the same VORTAC; only longitudinal separation may be used.

items.explanation: Per 8-4-4a1, same-NAVAID VOR/VORTAC/TACAN track separation requires at least 15 degrees of divergence and sufficient distance for one aircraft to be clear of the airspace protected for the other.

items.situation: Two aircraft are crossing the offshore/oceanic boundary below FL180 with vertical separation applied. The local altimeter setting differs noticeably from standard, and a trainee says the assigned altitudes are still 1,000 feet apart so no additional action is needed.
items.para_context: Under 8-5-1, when vertical separation is applied between aircraft crossing the offshore/oceanic airspace boundary below FL180, control action must ensure that differences between the standard altimeter setting (QNE) and local altimeter setting (QNH) do not compromise separation.

choices.text: Take control action to ensure the difference between QNE and QNH does not compromise the required vertical separation as the aircraft cross the boundary below FL180.
Ignore the altimeter-setting difference because a nominal 1,000-foot assignment always guarantees the required separation across the boundary.
Convert the operation to lateral separation only, because vertical separation may not be used below FL180 at the offshore/oceanic boundary.
Use the local altimeter setting for both airspace areas and disregard the standard setting to simplify the transition.

items.explanation: Per 8-5-1, control action is required so QNE/QNH differences do not erode vertical separation below FL180 during the boundary transition.

items.situation: Two aircraft are entering oceanic airspace. They are horizontally radar separated and the separation is increasing at the edge of known radar coverage, but their courses diverge by only 12 degrees. Another controller says that the increasing radar spacing alone is enough to establish the oceanic separation.
items.para_context: Under 8-5-2, separation will exist in oceanic airspace when aircraft are established on courses that diverge by at least 15 degrees until oceanic lateral separation is established, and the aircraft are horizontally radar separated with separation increasing at the edge of known radar coverage.

choices.text: Do not rely on the transition yet; the aircraft must be on courses diverging by at least 15 degrees until oceanic lateral separation is established, and they must also be horizontally radar separated with separation increasing at the edge of known radar coverage.
Use the transition because increasing radar separation at the edge of coverage is sufficient even if course divergence is less than 15 degrees.
Use the transition only if the aircraft are vertically separated as well, because radar separation cannot support the offshore/oceanic handoff.
Delay the transition until both aircraft are fully inside oceanic airspace, because offshore radar conditions do not matter at the boundary.

items.explanation: Per 8-5-2, both conditions are required: at least 15 degrees of course divergence until oceanic lateral separation exists, and increasing horizontal radar separation at the edge of known radar coverage.

items.situation: An outbound aircraft is transitioning from offshore airspace to oceanic airspace and requests a climb through opposite-direction oceanic traffic. You can establish vertical separation above that traffic only after the outbound has crossed the offshore/oceanic boundary and about 10 minutes before the aircraft are estimated to pass.
items.para_context: Under 8-5-3, when transitioning from an offshore airspace area to oceanic airspace, an aircraft may climb through opposite-direction oceanic traffic only if vertical separation above that traffic is established before the outbound crosses the offshore/oceanic boundary and 15 minutes before the aircraft are estimated to pass.

choices.text: Do not approve the climb yet; vertical separation above the opposite-direction traffic must be established before the outbound crosses the boundary and 15 minutes before the aircraft are estimated to pass.
Approve the climb because once the aircraft is outbound, vertical separation may be established any time before the actual pass.
Approve the climb because opposite-direction transition climbs require only that the outbound be radar identified before the boundary.
Delay the climb until after the aircraft have passed each other, because a climb through opposite-direction traffic is never authorized during offshore-to-oceanic transition.

items.explanation: Per 8-5-3, the climb is permitted only if the higher vertical separation is in place before the boundary crossing and at least 15 minutes before the estimated pass.

items.situation: A following aircraft on the same course requests a climb through the altitude of the preceding aircraft during the offshore-to-oceanic transition. The preceding aircraft is level and not slower, 5 minutes spacing exists, and both are in communication with ATC, but the following aircraft is 5,000 feet below the preceding aircraft when you are about to issue the climb clearance.
items.para_context: Under 8-5-4, a 5-minute same-direction minimum may be used while a following aircraft climbs through the altitude of a preceding aircraft only if the preceding aircraft is level at the assigned altitude and maintaining a speed equal to or greater than the following aircraft, 5 minutes minimum exists, the following aircraft is separated by not more than 4,000 feet when the climb clearance is issued, the climb begins within 10 minutes after passing the required point or radar-observed position of the preceding aircraft, and the following aircraft remains in direct communication with ATC until vertical separation is established.

choices.text: Do not use the 8-5-4 climb-through procedure yet, because the following aircraft must be no more than 4,000 feet below the preceding aircraft when the climb clearance is issued.
Issue the climb because the 5-minute spacing alone is sufficient as long as both aircraft remain on the same course.
Issue the climb because the altitude-difference limit applies only after the aircraft enter oceanic airspace, not during the transition.
Issue the climb because the direct-communication requirement replaces the 4,000-foot requirement once the preceding aircraft is level.

items.explanation: Per 8-5-4c, the following aircraft must be separated by not more than 4,000 feet from the preceding aircraft when the climb clearance is issued.

items.situation: You want to apply radar separation between a radar-identified aircraft and another aircraft just entering known radar coverage from oceanic airspace. The transiting aircraft appears to meet the equipment and communications conditions, but traffic and workload are such that if radar identification is delayed you would not have enough time to establish another approved separation method safely.
items.para_context: Under 8-5-5, radar separation may be applied between a radar-identified aircraft and a transiting aircraft not yet identified only if several conditions are met, including communications capability, RNAV equipage, adequate radar/system performance, transponder equipage, maintaining radar separation from other observed targets until identification or nonradar separation exists, and facility SOP/training for known radar coverage. The procedure is not authorized if there is insufficient time to establish another approved separation method in the event radar identification cannot be established promptly.

choices.text: Do not use the procedure in that situation, because it is not authorized when there is insufficient time to establish another approved separation method if radar identification is delayed or cannot be established.
Use the procedure anyway, because once the transiting aircraft is RNAV equipped and in communication the fallback-separation concern no longer applies.
Use the procedure as long as the transiting aircraft has a standard transponder, even if there is no practical backup if identification is delayed.
Use the procedure only after the aircraft has already been radar identified, because 8-5-5 never permits application before identification.

items.explanation: Per 8-5-5h, the procedure is not authorized if there is insufficient time to establish another approved separation method in the event radar identification is delayed or cannot be established.

items.situation: A route would pass near a temporary stationary airspace reservation. The route’s protected airspace would overlap the reservation boundary laterally, but another controller says you can still clear the aircraft through it as long as the aircraft stays vertically separated only within the reservation itself, without accounting for any surrounding buffer.
items.para_context: Under 8-6-1, aircraft may be separated from a temporary stationary reservation either laterally, by ensuring the route protected airspace does not overlap the reservation area, or vertically, by ensuring vertical separation exists within the reservation area plus a buffer around the perimeter equivalent to one-half the lateral separation minimum.

choices.text: Do not use that shortcut; either keep the route protected airspace from overlapping the reservation laterally, or apply vertical separation within the reservation plus the required perimeter buffer equal to one-half the lateral separation minimum.
Clear the aircraft through the area because only the reservation itself matters; no lateral-overlap check or perimeter buffer is required.
Use vertical separation only inside the exact reservation boundary, because the half-lateral-minimum buffer applies only to moving reservations.
Ignore the route-protected-airspace overlap as long as the aircraft remains RNAV equipped and on the cleared centerline.

items.explanation: Per 8-6-1, separation from a temporary stationary reservation may be accomplished laterally with no protected-airspace overlap, or vertically within the reservation plus a perimeter buffer equal to one-half the lateral separation minimum.

items.situation: A pilot refuses to accept your clearance to avoid an airspace reservation and intends to continue through it. A trainee suggests that once the refusal is on the tape, no further advisory is needed and the using agency does not need to know.
items.para_context: Under 8-6-2, if a pilot refuses an avoidance clearance, the controller must inform the pilot of the potential hazard, advise that services will not be provided while the flight is within the reservation, and, if possible, inform the appropriate using agency.

choices.text: Inform the pilot of the potential hazard, advise that services will not be provided while the flight is within the reservation, and, if possible, inform the appropriate using agency.
Record the refusal and continue normal services through the reservation because the pilot has accepted responsibility.
Cancel IFR services immediately without explaining the hazard or notifying the using agency.
Wait until the aircraft exits the reservation before telling the pilot any services will be withheld.

items.explanation: Per 8-6-2, the controller must advise the pilot of the hazard, state that services will not be provided within the reservation, and notify the using agency if possible.

items.situation: A temporary moving airspace reservation is advancing along a published route. A controller wants to clear another aircraft through the same area using vertical separation only inside the moving reservation itself, without accounting for any perimeter buffer, because the reservation is not stationary.
items.para_context: Under 8-6-3, aircraft may be separated from a temporary moving airspace reservation laterally by keeping route protected airspace from overlapping the time-dependent reservation area, longitudinally by keeping the required minimum ahead of the first or behind the last aircraft operating within the reservation, or vertically within a time-dependent geographical area defined as the moving reservation plus a perimeter buffer equal to one-half the lateral separation minimum.

choices.text: Do not use that shortcut; if using vertical separation, apply it within the moving reservation plus the required perimeter buffer equal to one-half the lateral separation minimum, or use an approved lateral or longitudinal method instead.
Use vertical separation only inside the exact moving reservation because the half-lateral-minimum buffer applies only to stationary reservations.
Ignore the time-dependent position of the reservation as long as the aircraft route never crosses the original activation point.
Use any reduced spacing you consider practical because moving reservations are handled only by coordination with the using agency.

items.explanation: Per 8-6-3, moving reservations may be protected laterally, longitudinally, or vertically, and the vertical method includes a perimeter buffer equal to one-half the lateral separation minimum.

items.situation: You are providing ATC services in the North Atlantic ICAO Region. A trainee wants to use a different set of procedures and minima from outside this section even though there is no note authorizing a different rule for the situation.
items.para_context: Under 8-7-1, air traffic control services in the North Atlantic ICAO Region are provided with the procedures and minima contained in Section 7 except when otherwise noted.

choices.text: Use the procedures and minima contained in Section 7 unless a note specifically provides otherwise.
Use whichever offshore or domestic procedure is most convenient because Section 7 is advisory guidance only.
Ignore Section 7 entirely and apply only local facility procedures for all North Atlantic operations.
Use another section's minima first and consult Section 7 only if separation is later lost.

items.explanation: Per 8-7-1, services in the North Atlantic ICAO Region use the procedures and minima contained in that section except when otherwise noted.

items.situation: While working North Atlantic traffic, a trainee says the region has its own separate vertical-separation rule set, so Chapter 4 altitude-assignment and verification standards no longer apply.
items.para_context: Under 8-7-2, vertical separation in the North Atlantic ICAO Region is provided in accordance with Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5, Altitude Assignment and Verification.

choices.text: Apply vertical separation in accordance with Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5, Altitude Assignment and Verification.
Use only the North Atlantic longitudinal rules because Chapter 4 vertical standards do not apply in oceanic airspace.
Use local altimetry practices instead of Chapter 4 whenever the aircraft is beyond radar coverage.
Delay any altitude verification until the aircraft exits the North Atlantic ICAO Region.

items.explanation: Per 8-7-2, North Atlantic vertical separation follows Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5.

items.situation: You are using ADS-C longitudinal separation in the North Atlantic ICAO Region. An ADS-C periodic report becomes overdue by 3 minutes, and another controller wants to keep waiting without taking any action because the next report might still arrive soon.
items.para_context: Under 8-7-3, North Atlantic longitudinal separation includes ADS-C-based minima and explicit overdue-report rules. If an ADS-C periodic or waypoint change event report is overdue by 3 minutes, the controller must take action to obtain an ADS-C report. If no report is received within 6 minutes of when the original report was due, the controller must take action to apply another form of separation.

choices.text: Take action immediately to obtain an ADS-C report, and if no report is received within 6 minutes of the original due time, take action to apply another form of separation.
Wait until the report is 6 minutes overdue before taking any action, because no controller action is required at the 3-minute point.
Continue using ADS-C separation indefinitely as long as voice communication remains available, even if no further report arrives.
Terminate all North Atlantic services immediately when a report is 3 minutes overdue, because no backup separation is permitted.

items.explanation: Per 8-7-3e3 and e4, action to obtain a report is required at 3 minutes overdue, and another form of separation must be applied if no report is received within 6 minutes of the original due time.

items.situation: Two aircraft in airspace designated for 23 NM lateral separation otherwise meet the approval standards, but the required ADS-C contracts are not being maintained and monitored by the automated flight data processor because of a systems issue. Another controller wants to keep using 23 NM because voice communication with both aircraft still exists.
items.para_context: Under 8-7-4, 23 NM lateral separation may be applied only to approved aircraft, at a minimum RNP 4, RCP 240, and RSP 180, operating in airspace designated for 23 NM lateral separation when direct controller/pilot communication via voice or CPDLC exists and the required ADS-C contracts are maintained and monitored by an automated flight data processor such as ATOP.

choices.text: Do not use 23 NM until all required conditions are met, including the maintained and monitored ADS-C contracts by the automated flight data processor.
Use 23 NM anyway because voice communication alone is enough once the aircraft are approved for RNP 4.
Use 23 NM if the aircraft agree to maintain their own lateral spacing.
Reduce the spacing to 1 degree latitude because the automation outage allows any reduced lateral standard to be substituted.

items.explanation: Per 8-7-4a, 23 NM lateral separation requires the stated aircraft approvals, direct communications, and required ADS-C contracts maintained and monitored by an automated flight data processor.

items.situation: You are providing ATC services in the Caribbean ICAO Region. A trainee wants to apply a different set of procedures from outside this section even though there is no note authorizing a different rule for the situation.
items.para_context: Under 8-8-1, provide air traffic control services in the Caribbean ICAO Region with the procedures and minima contained in Section 8 except when otherwise noted.

choices.text: Use the procedures and minima contained in Section 8 unless a note specifically provides otherwise.
Use whichever domestic or offshore procedure seems most convenient because Section 8 is only advisory.
Ignore Section 8 and use only local facility preferences for all Caribbean operations.
Apply another section first and consult Section 8 only after a conflict develops.

items.explanation: Per 8-8-1, Caribbean ICAO Region services use the procedures and minima contained in the section except when otherwise noted.

items.situation: While working Caribbean ICAO Region traffic, a trainee says the region has its own separate vertical-separation rule set, so Chapter 4 altitude-assignment and verification standards do not apply.
items.para_context: Under 8-8-2, vertical separation in the Caribbean ICAO Region is provided in accordance with Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5, Altitude Assignment and Verification.

choices.text: Apply vertical separation in accordance with Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5, Altitude Assignment and Verification.
Use only Caribbean longitudinal rules because Chapter 4 vertical standards do not apply in oceanic airspace.
Use local altimetry practices instead of Chapter 4 whenever the aircraft is offshore.
Delay altitude verification until the aircraft exits the Caribbean ICAO Region.

items.explanation: Per 8-8-2, Caribbean vertical separation follows Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5.

items.situation: A pilot requests an ADS-B In Trail Procedure climb in the Caribbean ICAO Region, but the reported ITP distance to one reference aircraft is only 12 NM. Another controller wants to approve the climb anyway because the aircraft are on the same track and the altitude difference is only 1,000 feet.
items.para_context: Under 8-8-3e, an ADS-B In Trail Procedure climb or descent may be cleared only when all listed conditions are satisfied, including that the pilot requests it and the reported ITP distance between the ITP aircraft and any reference aircraft is 15 NM or more.

choices.text: Do not approve the ITP climb yet, because the reported ITP distance to each reference aircraft must be 15 NM or more before the clearance may be issued.
Approve the climb because any positive spacing is acceptable when both aircraft are on the same track.
Approve the climb if the pilot agrees to maintain visual separation from the reference aircraft.
Approve the climb now and verify the ITP distance requirement after the aircraft reports level.

items.explanation: Per 8-8-3e3, the reported ITP distance between the ITP aircraft and any reference aircraft must be 15 NM or more before the ITP climb or descent may be cleared.

items.situation: Two aircraft in the Caribbean ICAO Region otherwise qualify for reduced lateral spacing, but one aircraft has a degraded track-keeping capability due to a navigation problem. Another controller wants to keep using the reduced lateral minimum because both aircraft remain in communication and still appear on course.
items.para_context: Under 8-8-4, reduced lateral separation minima such as 23 NM, 50 NM, 60 NM, and 90 NM may be applied only under the stated aircraft approval and area conditions, and the reduced lateral separation must not be used if the track-keeping capability of the aircraft has been reduced for any reason.

choices.text: Do not use the reduced lateral separation while the aircraft has degraded track-keeping capability; apply another authorized lateral minimum instead.
Continue the reduced minimum because communications are established and the aircraft is still generally on track.
Keep the reduced minimum as long as the pilot accepts responsibility for navigation accuracy.
Convert the reduced minimum to visual separation because the aircraft is offshore.

items.explanation: Per 8-8-4, reduced lateral separation must not be used if the aircraft's track-keeping capability has been reduced for any reason.

items.situation: An IFR aircraft in the Miami CTA requests a VFR climb near dusk, but sunset has already passed. The pilot still wants the climb because conditions are currently VMC.
items.para_context: Under 8-8-5, IFR flights in the Houston, Miami, and San Juan CTAs may be cleared to climb and descend in VFR conditions only when requested by the pilot and only between sunrise and sunset. If there is a possibility VFR conditions may become impractical, issue alternative instructions, and issue traffic information to aircraft not separated by the section minima.

choices.text: Do not clear the VFR climb under 8-8-5 because that authority applies only between sunrise and sunset.
Approve the VFR climb because pilot request alone is sufficient regardless of time of day.
Approve the VFR climb if the pilot agrees to cancel IFR after the climb begins.
Delay the decision until the aircraft reports whether traffic is in sight, because sunset does not affect VFR climb authorization.

items.explanation: Per 8-8-5a, IFR flights may be cleared to climb and descend in VFR conditions only when requested by the pilot and only between sunrise and sunset.

items.situation: You are providing ATC services in the Pacific ICAO Region. A trainee wants to use a different set of procedures from outside this section even though there is no note authorizing a different rule for the situation.
items.para_context: Under 8-9-1, provide air traffic control services in the Pacific ICAO Region with the procedures and minima contained in Section 9 except when otherwise noted.

choices.text: Use the procedures and minima contained in Section 9 unless a note specifically provides otherwise.
Use whichever offshore or domestic procedure seems most convenient because Section 9 is only advisory.
Ignore Section 9 and rely only on local facility preferences for Pacific operations.
Apply another section first and consult Section 9 only if a conflict develops.

items.explanation: Per 8-9-1, Pacific ICAO Region services use the procedures and minima contained in the section except when otherwise noted.

items.situation: While working Pacific ICAO Region traffic, a trainee says the region has its own separate vertical-separation rule set, so Chapter 4 altitude-assignment and verification standards do not apply.
items.para_context: Under 8-9-2, vertical separation in the Pacific ICAO Region is provided in accordance with Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5, Altitude Assignment and Verification.

choices.text: Apply vertical separation in accordance with Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5, Altitude Assignment and Verification.
Use only Pacific longitudinal rules because Chapter 4 vertical standards do not apply in oceanic airspace.
Use local altimetry practices instead of Chapter 4 whenever the aircraft is offshore.
Delay altitude verification until the aircraft exits the Pacific ICAO Region.

items.explanation: Per 8-9-2, Pacific vertical separation follows Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5.

items.situation: You are applying Pacific longitudinal separation without ADS-C. An aircraft fails to report its position by the expected time, and 4 minutes have now passed with no report. Another controller wants to keep waiting because the aircraft usually checks in a little late.
items.para_context: Under 8-9-3d3, if an aircraft fails to report its position within 3 minutes after the expected time, the controller must take action to establish communication. If communication is not established within 8 minutes after the time the report should have been received, the controller must take action to apply another form of separation.

choices.text: Take action now to establish communication, and if communication is still not established within 8 minutes after the report should have been received, take action to apply another form of separation.
Wait until the aircraft is 8 minutes late before taking any action, because no controller action is required at the 3-minute point.
Continue using the existing longitudinal minimum indefinitely as long as the aircraft was previously on course.
Cancel the oceanic clearance immediately because any late report automatically ends ATC service.

items.explanation: Per 8-9-3d3, action to establish communication is required at 3 minutes overdue, and another form of separation is required if communication is not established within 8 minutes after the report should have been received.

items.situation: Two Pacific ICAO Region aircraft meet the RNP 4 or RNP 10 approval requirement for 50 NM lateral separation, but another controller wants to apply 23 NM instead even though the airspace is not designated for 23 NM separation and the required ADS-C contracts are not being monitored by automation.
items.para_context: Under 8-9-4, 50 NM may be applied within authorized RNP areas to RNP 4 or RNP 10 approved aircraft. The 23 NM standard applies only to approved aircraft, at a minimum RNP 4, RCP 240, and RSP 180, within airspace designated for 23 NM lateral separation when direct voice or CPDLC communications exist and the required ADS-C contracts are maintained and monitored by an automated flight data processor.

choices.text: Do not use 23 NM; use only the lateral minimum whose specific prerequisites are satisfied, such as 50 NM in the authorized RNP area.
Use 23 NM whenever the aircraft are RNP 4 or RNP 10 approved, even if the airspace is not designated for that standard.
Use 23 NM if the pilots acknowledge traffic advisories, because automation monitoring is optional.
Reduce spacing to 23 NM whenever CPDLC is unavailable, because voice communication alone permits any reduced standard.

items.explanation: Per 8-9-4, 23 NM and 50 NM lateral separation each have specific prerequisites and may be used only when those conditions are met.

items.situation: You are providing ATC services in the North American ICAO Region. A trainee wants to use a different set of procedures from another section instead of the procedures in this section.
items.para_context: Under 8-10-1, provide air traffic control services in the North American ICAO Region with the procedures and minima contained in Section 10.

choices.text: Use the procedures and minima contained in Section 10 for North American ICAO Region services.
Use whichever offshore or domestic procedure seems most convenient because Section 10 is only advisory.
Ignore Section 10 and rely only on local facility preferences for all operations in the region.
Apply another section first and consult Section 10 only if a conflict develops.

items.explanation: Per 8-10-1, North American ICAO Region services use the procedures and minima contained in Section 10.

items.situation: While working North American ICAO Region traffic, a trainee says vertical separation only needs to follow Chapter 4 and that local facility directives about the transition between flight levels and metric altitudes can be ignored.
items.para_context: Under 8-10-2, vertical separation is provided in accordance with Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5, Altitude Assignment and Verification, and facility directives depicting the transition between flight levels and metric altitudes.

choices.text: Apply vertical separation in accordance with Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5 and the facility directives depicting the transition between flight levels and metric altitudes.
Use Chapter 4 only, because facility directives do not apply in the North American ICAO Region.
Use facility directives only and ignore Chapter 4 altitude-assignment and verification requirements.
Delay applying the metric-altitude transition directives until the aircraft leaves the region.

items.explanation: Per 8-10-2, both Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5 and facility directives depicting the transition between flight levels and metric altitudes apply.

items.situation: You are using DME/RNAV longitudinal separation between two turbojet aircraft on the NOPAC Route System in a listed high-control area. Direct voice communications have now been unavailable for more than 90 minutes, but another controller wants to continue using the same DME/RNAV separation because the aircraft are still providing RNAV distance reports.
items.para_context: Under 8-10-3d, DME/RNAV longitudinal separation may be applied in the listed high-control areas to turbojet aircraft on or transitioning to the NOPAC Route System only under the stated conditions, including that application of DME/RNAV separation without direct voice communications may not continue for more than 90 minutes.

choices.text: Do not continue using that DME/RNAV separation beyond 90 minutes without direct voice communications; establish another authorized means or another form of separation.
Continue indefinitely because RNAV distance reports alone remove the 90-minute communication limit.
Continue as long as the preceding aircraft is faster than the following aircraft, regardless of communications.
Cancel the trailing aircraft's clearance immediately because any loss of direct voice communications ends all ATC service.

items.explanation: Per 8-10-3d3, DME/RNAV separation without direct voice communications may not continue for more than 90 minutes.

items.situation: Two aircraft in the Anchorage Arctic CTA meet the equipment standards for 23 NM lateral separation, and another controller wants to apply the 23 NM standard because voice communication and ADS-C monitoring are available.
items.para_context: Under 8-10-4, 23 NM lateral separation applies only to approved aircraft operating within the Anchorage Oceanic CTA and Anchorage Continental CTA when the stated communications and ADS-C monitoring conditions are met. That minimum is not applicable within airspace in the Anchorage Arctic CTA.

choices.text: Do not use 23 NM in the Anchorage Arctic CTA; apply another authorized lateral minimum for that airspace.
Use 23 NM because the communication and ADS-C conditions are enough regardless of the CTA involved.
Use 23 NM only if both pilots agree to maintain their own lateral spacing.
Reduce the spacing to 50 NM only after separation is already lost, because the Arctic restriction is advisory only.

items.explanation: Per 8-10-4b and its note, the 23 NM minimum is not applicable within the Anchorage Arctic CTA.

items.situation: A NAVAID flight-inspection aircraft is conducting a scheduled inspection run. To reduce frequency congestion, a controller suggests using relay coordination through another position instead of keeping direct contact with the pilot, and plans to omit traffic updates unless a conflict becomes immediate.
items.para_context: Under 9-1-1, provide aircraft engaged in the flight inspection of NAVAIDs with maximum assistance. Unless otherwise agreed to, maintain direct contact with the pilot and exchange information regarding known traffic in the area and the pilot's intentions. Many of these flights rely on automatic recording equipment and uninterrupted flight is necessary for successful completion of the mission.

choices.text: Provide maximum assistance, maintain direct contact unless otherwise agreed, and exchange information on known traffic and the pilot's intentions.
Use relay coordination by default and avoid discussing nearby traffic unless separation is already lost.
Handle the flight like routine traffic because flight inspections do not require special assistance unless the pilot declares an emergency.
Delay any coordination until the inspection run is complete because uninterrupted flight means controllers should not communicate with the aircraft.

items.explanation: Per 9-1-1, flight-inspection aircraft are given maximum assistance, direct contact is maintained unless otherwise agreed, and traffic/intention information is exchanged.

items.situation: A Flight Check aircraft reports 'recorded run' while conducting an approach inspection. Another controller wants to turn it off the procedure to fit in other traffic, even though no emergency exists.
items.para_context: Under 9-1-2, clear the aircraft according to pilot request as soon as practicable and do not ask the pilot to deviate from planned action except to preclude an emergency situation. Flight Check aircraft using the term 'recorded run' indicate automated flight inspections are in progress, requiring the full runway length including protection of critical areas and the entire procedure to be flown, up to and including the missed approach as needed.

choices.text: Do not ask the aircraft to deviate just to fit traffic; provide the special handling requested and protect the recorded run unless deviation is needed to preclude an emergency situation.
Turn the aircraft off the procedure whenever traffic flow benefits, because recorded runs do not change controller handling requirements.
Treat the aircraft the same as a FLIGHT VAL aircraft and give it no additional handling beyond basic sequencing.
Cancel radar advisories because Flight Check aircraft on recorded runs must remain fully independent of ATC assistance.

items.explanation: Per 9-1-2, flight-inspection aircraft are cleared according to pilot request as soon as practicable and are not asked to deviate except to preclude an emergency; 'recorded run' indicates automated inspection activity requiring the full procedure.

items.situation: A flight check aircraft is conducting a special radar accuracy inspection. Another controller wants to assign a new discrete beacon code and delay the flight for sequencing convenience, even though no emergency exists.
items.para_context: Under 9-1-3, provide special handling as required to expedite flight inspection of NAVAIDs and RADAR by flight check aircraft. Avoid changes in route or altitude from that filed by the pilot in the initial flight plan, do not impose ATC delays except to preclude emergency situations, and do not change the previously assigned discrete beacon code of special radar accuracy flight check aircraft.

choices.text: Provide the special handling needed to expedite the inspection, avoid unnecessary route or altitude changes, do not impose delays except to preclude an emergency, and do not change the previously assigned discrete beacon code.
Reassign the beacon code and sequence the aircraft like routine traffic because flight check aircraft receive no special handling once airborne.
Delay the flight whenever workload is high because the expedite requirement applies only on final approach.
Change the route and beacon code as needed because special radar accuracy checks are exempt from the filed-flight-plan and code protections.

items.explanation: Per 9-1-3, flight check aircraft receive special handling to expedite the inspection, route or altitude changes are avoided, ATC delays are not imposed except to preclude emergencies, and the previously assigned discrete beacon code of special radar accuracy flight check aircraft is not changed.

items.situation: A military aircraft with 'dangerous cargo' in the flight plan remarks is descending en route. To resolve traffic, another controller wants to vector it off the planned route immediately without first coordinating with the pilot.
items.para_context: Under 9-2-1, when special handling for aircraft carrying dangerous materials applies, if it becomes necessary to amend route or altitude, advise the pilot of the proposed change and the expected delay if the present route or altitude must be maintained. If the pilot refuses the amendment, offer all possible assistance. When the aircraft is provided an en route descent, do not vector the aircraft from the planned route unless the pilot concurs.

choices.text: Do not vector the aircraft off the planned route during the en route descent unless the pilot concurs, and if a route or altitude amendment is needed, advise the pilot of the proposed change and expected delay.
Vector the aircraft immediately because dangerous-material flights must accept any ATC routing needed for traffic.
Cancel the descent and hold the aircraft without explanation because concurrence is never required for dangerous-material flights.
Keep the aircraft on course only if the pilot first declares an emergency or requests hazardous-cargo priority.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-1, the pilot is advised of proposed route or altitude amendments and expected delay, all possible assistance is offered if the pilot refuses, and an aircraft on en route descent is not vectored from the planned route unless the pilot concurs.

items.situation: Within the conterminous United States, a USAF aircraft requests celestial navigation training, but the aircraft has no operating transponder. Another controller wants to approve the training anyway because the crew is military and radar coverage is available.
items.para_context: Under 9-2-2, approve flight plans specifying celestial navigation only when requested for USAF or USN aircraft. Within conterminous U.S. airspace, limit celestial navigation training to transponder-equipped aircraft within areas of ARTCC radar coverage, and before control transfer ensure the receiving controller is informed of the nature of the celestial navigation training leg.

choices.text: Do not approve the celestial navigation training within the conterminous U.S. unless the aircraft is transponder-equipped and within ARTCC radar coverage.
Approve the training because any USAF or USN request is authorized regardless of transponder status in the conterminous U.S.
Approve the training if the pilot promises to remain within 30 NM of the route centerline, even without a transponder.
Delay the approval decision until after control transfer because only the receiving controller needs to know the aircraft is training.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-2, within conterminous U.S. airspace celestial navigation training is limited to transponder-equipped USAF or USN aircraft within ARTCC radar coverage.

items.situation: An experimental aircraft notifies you that it requires special handling and requests a specific routing. Traffic permits the request and it is not contrary to ATC procedures, but another controller wants to issue a different route for convenience after already approving the pilot's plan.
items.para_context: Under 9-2-3, when notified that an experimental aircraft requires special handling, clear the aircraft according to pilot requests as traffic permits and if not contrary to ATC procedures. Once approved, do not ask the pilot to deviate from a planned action except to preclude an emergency situation.

choices.text: Clear the aircraft according to the pilot's request as traffic permits and, once approved, do not ask for a deviation except to preclude an emergency situation.
Issue the different route for convenience because experimental aircraft special handling ends once the first request is approved.
Require the pilot to accept any reroute that improves sequencing because experimental aircraft do not receive route stability.
Deny all special handling unless a letter of agreement already exists at the facility.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-3, experimental aircraft requesting special handling are cleared according to pilot request as traffic permits and, once approved, are not asked to deviate except to preclude an emergency.

items.situation: An FAA research and development test aircraft requests a special flight procedure that was coordinated in advance, traffic permits it, and the facility air traffic manager has already approved the procedure for use. Another controller wants to deny it and keep the aircraft on standard handling because the request is unusual.
items.para_context: Under 9-2-4, when coordinated in advance and traffic permits, approve requests for special flight procedures from aircraft participating in FAA research and development test activities. These special procedures must be applied to participating aircraft and vehicles, and they must be approved by the facility air traffic manager prior to use.

choices.text: Approve the special flight procedure and apply it to the participating aircraft or vehicle because it was coordinated in advance, traffic permits it, and prior facility air traffic manager approval exists.
Deny the request because special procedures are never used for research and development flights once normal traffic is operating.
Approve the request only after the aircraft is airborne, because prior approval by the facility air traffic manager is not required.
Apply the special procedure to all aircraft in the area so controllers do not have to separate participating and nonparticipating traffic.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-4, when coordinated in advance and traffic permits, special flight procedures for FAA research and development activities are approved and applied to participating aircraft or vehicles, provided the facility air traffic manager approved them before use.

items.situation: You are the first facility to establish contact with an aircraft that uses the code word FLYNET. A trainee plans to handle it routinely and notify security coordination later after the flight is already handed off.
items.para_context: Under 9-2-5, the first ATC facility establishing contact with an aircraft using the code word FLYNET must provide expeditious handling, report it to the OS/CIC for reporting to the NTSO Air Traffic Security Coordinator through the Domestic Events Network, and add to the flight plan remarks that DEN notification has been accomplished.

choices.text: Provide expeditious handling, notify the OS/CIC so the NTSO ATSC is contacted through the DEN, and ensure the flight plan remarks show DEN notification has been accomplished.
Handle the aircraft routinely and wait to notify the OS/CIC until after the next facility has accepted the handoff.
Treat FLYNET only as an advisory phrase and omit DEN notification if the pilot does not explicitly request priority.
Provide expeditious handling only if the aircraft is carrying nuclear material; otherwise no special reporting is required.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-5, the first facility hearing FLYNET provides expeditious handling, reports it through the OS/CIC to the NTSO ATSC via DEN, and adds DEN notification to the flight plan remarks.

items.situation: An IFR military training route is designated MARSA by letter of agreement. Another controller wants to amend the route's operation to solve an ATC traffic problem even though the change would compromise the existing MARSA provisions.
items.para_context: Under 9-2-6, MTRs may be designated for MARSA operations by letter of agreement. ATC may amend or restrict operations on a route for ATC considerations, but where a route has been designated MARSA, ATC must not amend or restrict operations in a manner that would compromise MARSA provisions.

choices.text: Do not amend or restrict the MARSA operation in a way that would compromise MARSA provisions; use another lawful means to resolve the ATC problem.
Amend the MARSA operation whenever traffic requires it, because ATC considerations always override the MARSA agreement.
Cancel MARSA automatically any time nonparticipating IFR traffic is nearby, even if no new separation plan exists.
Issue the amendment without telling the military scheduling activity, because the letter of agreement controls only route entry and exit times.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-6h, ATC may amend or restrict operations for ATC considerations, but must not do so in a way that compromises MARSA provisions.

items.situation: An ADCF requests expedited transfer of control jurisdiction for interceptor aircraft on an active air defense scramble mission. A controller wants to delay the transfer until routine sector workload decreases, even though the mission has already been identified as active air defense.
items.para_context: Under 9-2-7, provide maximum assistance to expedite the movement of interceptor aircraft on active air defense scramble missions until the unknown aircraft is identified. The initiating ADCF must identify the mission as active air defense, ATC services must be used insofar as circumstances permit, and upon request the ATC facility must expedite transfer of control jurisdiction to the requesting ADCF.

choices.text: Provide maximum assistance and expedite the requested transfer of control jurisdiction to the ADCF because the mission is an active air defense scramble.
Delay the transfer until workload improves because scramble missions do not change normal control-jurisdiction procedures.
Refuse the transfer unless the unknown aircraft has already been identified.
Terminate ATC services immediately because active air defense missions cannot use ATC once airborne.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-7, interceptor aircraft on active air defense scramble missions receive maximum assistance, and the ATC facility expedites transfer of control jurisdiction to the requesting ADCF when asked.

items.situation: A pilot reports unusual low-level circling by another aircraft near a refinery. A controller wants to wait and see whether more calls come in before telling anyone because controllers are not normally assigned to monitor special-interest sites.
items.para_context: Under 9-2-8, immediately relay any reports or information regarding unusual aircraft activities in the vicinity of special interest sites such as nuclear power plants, power plants, dams, and refineries to supervisory/CIC personnel. Controllers have no responsibility to monitor or observe aircraft near those sites unless directed by supervisory/CIC personnel.

choices.text: Immediately relay the report or information to supervisory or CIC personnel.
Wait for multiple reports before notifying supervisory personnel because controllers are not security monitors by default.
Ignore the report unless the aircraft has already entered restricted airspace.
Personally begin continuous monitoring of all nearby aircraft without notifying supervisory or CIC personnel.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-8, unusual aircraft activity reports near special-interest sites are relayed immediately to supervisory or CIC personnel.

items.situation: A pilot operating inside a Special Flight Rules Area says the Part 93 procedures are optional unless the controller chooses to enforce them and asks to proceed contrary to the published special rules without specific ATC authorization.
items.para_context: Under 9-2-9, special air traffic rules are prescribed in 14 CFR part 93 for certain designated airspace. Special Flight Rules Areas are areas in which flight is subject to the special air traffic rules in 14 CFR part 93 unless otherwise authorized by ATC. Each person operating to, from, or within a SATR area or SFRA must adhere to the applicable special air traffic rules unless otherwise authorized or required by ATC.

choices.text: Require adherence to the applicable Part 93 special air traffic rules unless ATC specifically authorizes or requires something different.
Treat the rules as advisory only because Part 93 procedures in SFRAs apply only when the controller elects to use them.
Apply the special rules only if the area is labeled SFRA and never if it is merely a SATR area.
Disregard Part 93 entirely and use standard Class E rules unless the pilot requests special handling.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-9, operations in SATR areas and SFRAs follow the applicable special air traffic rules in 14 CFR part 93 unless otherwise authorized or required by ATC.

items.situation: An aircraft with an operating transponder but no operating Mode C requests authorization to operate in the Washington, DC SFRA. Another controller wants to approve it immediately because the aircraft can still be identified on the assigned code.
items.para_context: Under 9-2-10, two-way communications, flight planning, and an operational transponder on an assigned code are required in the DC SFRA. Aircraft with operating transponders but without operating Mode C require specific authorization from ATC, and ATC must coordinate with the Domestic Events Network before approval.

choices.text: Do not approve the operation until specific authorization is coordinated with the DEN.
Approve it immediately because Mode C is optional as long as the assigned code is observed.
Approve it if the pilot promises to remain clear of Class B, C, and D airspace, with no DEN coordination needed.
Deny all SFRA operations by aircraft without Mode C even if DEN authorization can be obtained.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-10a3, aircraft without operating Mode C need specific ATC authorization and DEN coordination before approval.

items.situation: You receive notification of a SECNOT on an aircraft, but your sector has no current information on it. A controller suggests waiting for the next handoff or pilot call before telling the OS/CIC anything.
items.para_context: Under 9-2-11, upon receiving notification of a SECNOT, the controller must forward all information on the subject aircraft to the OS/CIC. If information is not known, broadcast the call sign on all frequencies and advise the OS/CIC of the response.

choices.text: Advise the OS/CIC immediately, broadcast the call sign on all frequencies if information is not known, and pass along any response.
Wait for the aircraft to call first so the OS/CIC receives only confirmed information.
Broadcast the call sign only on the last assigned frequency and hold the information locally until the aircraft replies.
Forward information only if the aircraft is on an IFR flight plan; otherwise no SECNOT action is required.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-11, the controller forwards all available information to the OS/CIC and, if information is not known, broadcasts the call sign on all frequencies and advises the OS/CIC of any response.

items.situation: A sensitive government mission aircraft checks in using its full U.S. special call sign and a preassigned sensitive beacon code. Another controller wants to abbreviate the call sign in automation and assign a different discrete code to make the flight easier to track locally.
items.para_context: Under 9-2-12, ATC provides the maximum assistance possible to law enforcement aircraft when requested, but may not compromise approved separation minima or place the aircraft in unsafe proximity to terrain, obstructions, or other aircraft. For sensitive government missions, ATC must not change the sensitive beacon codes requested or displayed, must ensure the full call sign designator is entered into FAA automation systems to the maximum extent possible, and must not alter or abbreviate the U.S. special call signs used by these operators.

choices.text: Keep the requested or displayed sensitive beacon code, preserve the full special call sign designator, and provide assistance only within approved separation and safety limits.
Assign a new discrete code and abbreviate the call sign locally because tracking convenience takes priority once the flight is identified.
Change the beacon code but keep the full call sign because only call sign changes are restricted for sensitive government missions.
Provide any assistance requested even if it requires compromising approved separation minima, because sensitive government missions override standard ATC safety limits.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-12, ATC does not change sensitive beacon codes or alter special call signs for sensitive government missions, and assistance to law enforcement aircraft cannot compromise approved separation or safety.

items.clearance: CLEARED TO CONDUCT REFUELING ALONG 14 TRACK, MAINTAIN BLOCK FL210 THROUGH FL230.

choices.text: Cleared to conduct refueling along 14 track, maintain block FL210 through FL230.
Cleared to conduct refueling along 14 track, maintain FL210.
Cleared to conduct refueling from 14 track, descend to FL230.
Cleared to conduct refueling along 14 track, maintain block FL230 through FL210.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-13 phraseology, an aerial refueling clearance may be issued as 'CLEARED TO CONDUCT REFUELING ALONG (number) TRACK ... MAINTAIN BLOCK (altitude) THROUGH (altitude).'

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: COMMENCING AT FL270, DESCENDING TO FL250.
items.display_text: COMMENCING AT FL270 CLIMBING TO FL250
items.explanation: Per 9-2-13 phraseology, the format is 'COMMENCING AT (altitude), DESCENDING TO (altitude).'

items.situation: A receiver aircraft has departed the ARIP and is now 3 miles from the tanker during a refueling mission. Another controller wants to assign it a new beacon code immediately to simplify downstream coordination.
items.para_context: Under 9-2-13, do not request a receiver aircraft that has been cleared to conduct air refueling and has departed the ARIP to make code changes when it is less than 5 miles from the tanker. Once rendezvous is completed, the tanker must keep receiver aircraft within 3 miles until MARSA is terminated.

choices.text: Do not request the code change, because a receiver aircraft less than 5 miles from the tanker must not be asked to change codes.
Issue the code change now because 3 miles from the tanker is inside the normal refueling formation and therefore best for retagging.
Request the receiver to squawk standby instead, because code changes are prohibited only when the aircraft is inside 1 mile of the tanker.
Cancel MARSA first and then require the code change regardless of the receiver's distance from the tanker.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-13b1, do not request code changes from a receiver aircraft less than 5 miles from the tanker after it has departed the ARIP.

items.clearance: CLEARED AS FILED VIA ROUTE AND FLIGHT LEVELS.

choices.text: Cleared as filed via route and flight levels.
Cleared as filed via route and altitudes.
Cleared as filed direct first fix and flight levels.
Cleared as filed via route only, expect flight levels later.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-14 phraseology, approval of the flight plan is issued as 'CLEARED AS FILED VIA ROUTE AND FLIGHT LEVELS.'

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: CLEARED AS FILED VIA ROUTE AND FLIGHT LEVELS.
items.display_text: CLEARED AS FILED VIA ROUTE AND FLIGHT ALTITUDES
items.explanation: Per 9-2-14 phraseology, the clearance ends with 'FLIGHT LEVELS,' not 'ALTITUDES.'

items.situation: Two supersonic aircraft need separation above FL600. A trainee proposes keeping only 1,000 feet between them for now and, if needed, issuing vectors during the turn to sort it out later.
items.para_context: Under 9-2-14, use 5,000 feet vertical separation in lieu of the normal Chapter 4 vertical minimum. The primary method between two supersonic aircraft is usually to descend the aircraft at the lower flight level because the aircraft at the higher flight level may not be able to climb rapidly enough. Supersonic aircraft may also lose a few thousand feet in turns, and vectoring them seriously affects range and mission objectives.

choices.text: Use 5,000 feet vertical separation and, when practical, descend the lower aircraft rather than relying on a rapid climb by the higher aircraft or vectoring during turns.
Use standard 1,000-foot RVSM separation because supersonic handling above FL600 follows the same vertical rule as any other operation.
Keep present altitudes and solve the conflict with vectors during the turn because vectoring is the preferred supersonic-separation technique.
Climb the higher aircraft first, because supersonic aircraft above FL600 can always outclimb the lower aircraft's descent profile.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-14f and its note, use 5,000 feet vertical separation, and the preferred supersonic technique is usually to descend the lower aircraft rather than depend on the higher one to climb.

items.situation: A single-pilot ANG jet formation operating at night requests the special use frequency. The route later takes the flight into airspace assigned for special military operations, and another controller wants to leave the special use frequency assigned because the formation otherwise qualifies under the nighttime rule.
items.para_context: Under 9-2-15, special use frequencies may be assigned to certain USAF, U.S. Navy, and ANG single-pilot jet formations at night or in instrument weather, plus other listed military operations. However, do not assign the special use frequency to those aircraft when they will operate in airspace assigned for special military operations.

choices.text: Do not assign the special use frequency once the flight will operate in airspace assigned for special military operations.
Keep the special use frequency assigned because the nighttime single-pilot formation rule always overrides the special military operations restriction.
Assign the special use frequency only if the formation increases to five or more aircraft before entering the special military operations airspace.
Deny the flight any ARTCC frequency at all because special use frequencies cannot be combined with night or instrument operations.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-15c, do not assign the special use frequency to the aircraft in subparagraph a1 when they will operate in airspace assigned for special military operations.

items.situation: An aircraft's proposed route and intended landing airport are both inside a forecast hazardous-radiation area. A controller wants to wait for the pilot to ask about it before saying anything because the route has not yet been amended.
items.para_context: Under 9-2-16, advise pilots whenever their proposed flight path will traverse a reported or forecast hazardous-radiation area and reroute the aircraft when requested by the pilot. If the intended landing airfield lies within such an area, inform the pilot and request his or her intentions.

choices.text: Advise the pilot about the hazardous-radiation area, request intentions for the landing airport inside the area, and reroute the aircraft if the pilot requests it.
Wait silently until the pilot asks for a reroute because ATC does not mention hazardous-radiation areas unless the pilot raises the issue first.
Reroute the aircraft immediately without pilot request and do not discuss the affected landing airport.
Advise only if the aircraft is already inside the hazardous-radiation area, because forecast areas do not require advance notification.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-16, ATC advises the pilot when the route or intended landing airfield is affected by hazardous radiation and reroutes the aircraft when requested.

items.situation: A USAF aircraft using the call sign 'SAMP' requests an inflight altitude and route change while conducting an aerial sampling mission for hazardous contamination. Another controller wants to deny the request to protect the normal IFR flow even though other IFR traffic could be recleared.
items.para_context: Under 9-2-17, provide special handling to SAMP aircraft engaged in aerial sampling or surveying missions for nuclear, chemical, or hazardous material contamination. Approve inflight clearance requests for altitude and route changes to the maximum extent possible, and other IFR aircraft may be recleared so the SAMP aircraft's requests are approved.

choices.text: Provide special handling and approve the requested altitude and route change to the maximum extent possible, reclearing other IFR aircraft if needed.
Handle the flight routinely because SAMP is only an informational call sign and does not affect IFR priority decisions.
Approve only altitude changes and deny all route changes because contamination missions may not deviate laterally once airborne.
Require the SAMP aircraft to hold until every other IFR aircraft has completed its normal routing.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-17, SAMP aircraft receive special handling, and inflight route or altitude changes are approved to the maximum extent possible, including reclearing other IFR traffic if necessary.

items.situation: An E-3 flight identified in the remarks as an 'AWACS/NORAD Special' mission needs to be expedited to its destination orbit. A controller proposes delaying it in a metering queue, then issuing a large reroute away from the orbit to simplify traffic flow.
items.para_context: Under 9-2-18, do not delay AWACS/NORAD Special flights. Acceptable control actions while expediting them to the destination orbit include en route altitude changes within plus or minus 2,000 feet of the requested flight level and radar vectors or minor route changes that do not impede progress toward the orbit.

choices.text: Do not delay the flight; use only acceptable expediting actions such as plus or minus 2,000 feet or minor vectors and route changes that do not impede progress to the orbit.
Meter the flight with normal delays because AWACS/NORAD Special missions receive no special treatment once airborne.
Issue any major reroute needed to balance traffic, even if it delays arrival at the destination orbit, because route efficiency is secondary.
Keep the requested route and altitude exactly as filed because no deviations of any kind are allowed for AWACS/NORAD Special flights.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-18, AWACS/NORAD Special flights are not delayed, and only minor altitude or route adjustments that do not impede progress to the orbit are used while expediting them.

items.situation: A TEAL aircraft gives you a dropsonde release time. Traffic is estimated to pass below the mission aircraft through the drop area, and another controller says no advisory is needed because the aircraft commander is ultimately responsible for delaying the drop if traffic is a factor.
items.para_context: Under 9-2-19, workload and priorities permitting, when a dropsonde release time is received from a TEAL or NOAA mission aircraft, advise the mission aircraft of any traffic estimated to pass through the drop area below the mission aircraft. The advisory must include altitude, direction of flight, and ETA at the closest point to the drop area or at the fix or intersection where the drop will occur. The aircraft commander then decides whether to delay the release based on the ATC traffic information.

choices.text: Issue the traffic advisory with altitude, direction of flight, and ETA at the closest point to the drop area or fix, then let the aircraft commander decide whether to delay the release.
Do not issue any advisory because dropsonde-delay decisions belong entirely to the aircraft commander.
Issue only the traffic altitude because TEAL and NOAA crews do not need direction or ETA information for dropsonde releases.
Cancel the dropsonde release outright whenever any traffic is within 100 miles, regardless of altitude or ETA.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-19a, controllers advise the mission aircraft of relevant traffic below the drop area, including altitude, direction, and ETA, while the aircraft commander decides whether to delay the release.

items.clearance: CLEARED TO CONDUCT EVASIVE ACTION MANEUVER FROM J42 TO J58, 12 MILES EITHER SIDE OF CENTERLINE, MAINTAIN FL280 THROUGH FL300, COMPLETE MANEUVER AT J58 AT FL290.

choices.text: Cleared to conduct evasive action maneuver from J42 to J58, 12 miles either side of centerline, maintain FL280 through FL300, complete maneuver at J58 at FL290.
Cleared to conduct evasive action maneuver from J42 to J58, 12 miles left of centerline, maintain FL290 only, complete maneuver at J58.
