Cleared to conduct evasive action maneuver from J42 direct J58, 12 miles either side, descend to FL290 at completion.
Cleared evasive maneuver on present route, remain within 12 miles, maintain FL280 through FL300 until further advised.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-20 phraseology, the clearance specifies the route segment, maximum deviation from centerline, altitude block, and the fix and altitude at which the maneuver must be complete.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: CLEARED TO CONDUCT EVASIVE ACTION MANEUVER FROM J42 TO J58.
items.display_text: CLEARED TO BEGIN EVASIVE ACTION MANEUVER FROM J42 TO J58
items.explanation: Per 9-2-20 phraseology, the clearance begins 'CLEARED TO CONDUCT EVASIVE ACTION MANEUVER...'

items.situation: A bomber requests approval for an evasive action maneuver over a route segment, but the current traffic situation would not safely permit the maneuvering block and lateral deviations. Another controller wants to approve it anyway and work out the details later.
items.para_context: Under 9-2-20, approve an evasive action maneuver only on the basis of a permissible traffic situation. When approving it, specify the route segment, maximum route deviation from the centerline, and altitude as necessary.

choices.text: Do not approve the maneuver unless the traffic situation is permissible; if you approve it, specify the route segment, deviation limits, and altitude as needed.
Approve the maneuver first and resolve traffic conflicts afterward because the aircraft will remain within plus or minus 1,000 feet anyway.
Approve the maneuver whenever requested because evasive-action training automatically takes priority over normal traffic considerations.
Deny all evasive-action requests unless they occur inside special use airspace, because route-segment approvals are not authorized elsewhere.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-20, evasive-action maneuvers are approved only when the traffic situation permits them, and the approval includes the required maneuver limits.

items.situation: A nonstandard formation has been approved and will break up in your area. Another controller wants to clear the entire group to the destination as a normal formation and apply separation to each aircraft individually against outside traffic before the breakup.
items.para_context: Under 9-2-21, approved nonstandard formation or cell operations require sufficient altitudes for 500 feet of intra-cell vertical spacing. MARSA applies between the participating aircraft until approved separation is established and acknowledged by ATC. Separation against nonparticipating aircraft is applied to the approved nonstandard formation or cell envelope, and the formation is cleared to the breakup fix as the clearance limit. If the breakup occurs in your area, issue clearances for transition to individual routes or altitudes, or if breakup cannot be approved, clear the flight to continue as a formation.

choices.text: Use the approved nonstandard formation envelope for separation from nonparticipating aircraft, clear the flight to the breakup fix, and issue transition clearances there if the breakup can be approved.
Treat each aircraft as fully independent from the outset and ignore the approved formation envelope once the operation has been approved.
Clear the group directly to destination and leave the breakup entirely to pilot discretion because ATC does not use breakup fixes for nonstandard formations.
Apply MARSA between the formation and all nonparticipating aircraft until landing because no ATC separation is used once the nonstandard operation begins.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-21, separation from outside traffic is applied to the approved formation or cell envelope, MARSA applies only between participating aircraft, and the clearance limit is the breakup fix.

items.situation: An IFR aircraft is approaching an active MOA. The pilot advises that the using agency has already given permission to operate in the airspace, but another controller still wants to apply the section's special-use-airspace avoidance procedures as if no exception exists.
items.para_context: Under 9-3-1, the section's procedures apply unless an exception exists, including when the pilot informs ATC that permission has been obtained from the using agency, the using agency confirms permission, the airspace has been released to the controlling agency, the aircraft is on an approved ALTRV unless the area is an ATCAA, or the operation is offshore or oceanic and Chapter 8 procedures apply.

choices.text: Treat the using-agency permission as an exception to the section's procedures and handle the flight accordingly.
Apply the section's avoidance procedures anyway because pilot-reported using-agency permission has no effect unless restated by the controlling agency.
Deny the operation automatically because pilots may never relay using-agency permission for entry into active special use airspace.
Apply the exception only if the airspace is an ATCAA, because MOAs and restricted areas are never covered by pilot-reported permission.

items.explanation: Per 9-3-1a, if the pilot informs you permission has been obtained from the using agency, the section's general SUA/ATCAA avoidance procedures are not applied as though no exception exists.

items.situation: A stationary ALTRV for a space launch is active and the associated aircraft hazard area has started, but affected facilities have not received real-time start and end notifications from ATO Space Operations. Another controller wants to keep providing ATC services to aircraft at an airport inside the AHA because the launch is already underway.
items.para_context: Under 9-3-2d, for stationary ALTRVs and TFRs issued to protect aircraft hazard areas, do not allow nonparticipating aircraft to operate in an AHA and do not provide ATC services to aircraft at airports within an AHA unless real-time start and end notifications of the AHA are provided to affected facilities via ATO Space Operations coordination.

choices.text: Do not provide ATC services to aircraft at the airport within the AHA until the required real-time start and end notifications have been provided through ATO Space Operations coordination.
Continue providing ATC services because once the launch has begun, real-time ATO Space Operations notifications are no longer required.
Provide services only to VFR aircraft because the notification rule applies solely to IFR operations.
Ignore the AHA restriction if the aircraft remain outside the stationary ALTRV core, because airports inside the AHA are not covered by the notification requirement.

items.explanation: Per 9-3-2d, ATC services are not provided to aircraft at airports within an AHA unless the required real-time notifications have been provided through ATO Space Operations coordination.

items.clearance: MAINTAIN VFR-ON-TOP AT LEAST 500 FEET ABOVE THE UPPER LIMIT OF R-4801 ACROSS R-4801 BETWEEN MACEE AND KENZI.

choices.text: Maintain VFR-on-top at least 500 feet above the upper limit of R-4801 across R-4801 between MACEE and KENZI.
Maintain VFR-on-top exactly 500 feet above R-4801 between MACEE and KENZI.
Maintain IFR-on-top at least 500 feet above the upper limit of R-4801 across R-4801 between MACEE and KENZI.
Maintain VFR-on-top at least 500 feet below the upper limit of R-4801 across R-4801 between MACEE and KENZI.

items.explanation: Per 9-3-3 phraseology, the pilot may be instructed to maintain VFR-on-top at least 500 feet above or below the airspace across the named area between specified fixes.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: MAINTAIN VFR-ON-TOP AT LEAST 500 FEET ABOVE THE UPPER LIMIT OF AIRSPACE.
items.display_text: MAINTAIN VFR-ON-TOP AT MOST 500 FEET ABOVE THE UPPER LIMIT OF AIRSPACE
items.explanation: Per 9-3-3 phraseology, the phrase is 'AT LEAST 500 FEET ABOVE/BELOW,' not 'AT MOST.'

items.situation: An IFR aircraft operating VFR-on-top will cross an active ATCAA. Another controller wants to let it proceed unchanged because the pilot is responsible for VFR-on-top terrain and cloud clearance and already knows the route.
items.para_context: Under 9-3-3, if the route, track, or altitude may cause a VFR-on-top aircraft to enter active special use airspace or an ATCAA, inform the pilot to maintain VFR-on-top at least 500 feet above the upper limit or below the lower limit across the airspace, subject to 7-3-1, or clear the aircraft via a route that provides approved separation from the airspace. If the airspace is an ATCAA, advise that it is ATC-assigned airspace.

choices.text: Issue VFR-on-top instructions to remain at least 500 feet above or below the ATCAA across the area, or reroute the aircraft clear with approved separation from the airspace.
Take no action because VFR-on-top flights may enter active ATCAAs without any special instruction as long as the pilot remains cloud clear.
Require the aircraft to cancel IFR immediately before entering the ATCAA because VFR-on-top operations cannot continue near ATC-assigned airspace.
Apply the 500-foot rule only to MOAs and restricted areas, never to ATCAAs.

items.explanation: Per 9-3-3, ATC either instructs the pilot to maintain VFR-on-top at least 500 feet above or below the airspace or clears the aircraft via a route that provides approved separation from it.

items.situation: A LOA is in place and the using agency has granted permission for two aircraft to transit an active restricted area. Another controller wants to ignore the using agency's transit instructions and use ordinary facility routing instead, even though the transit approval depends on the coordinated procedures.
items.para_context: Under 9-3-4, when a LOA or LOP has been coordinated with the using agency and permission has been granted to transit the area, comply with the instruction or clearances issued by the using agency and provide the applicable separation minima between aircraft when two or more aircraft are transiting the area. If unable to comply with those instructions or clearances, clear the aircraft in accordance with paragraph 9-3-2.

choices.text: Comply with the using agency's instructions or clearances and apply the appropriate aircraft separation; if unable to comply, clear the aircraft in accordance with 9-3-2.
Ignore the using agency instructions because ATC routing always overrides coordinated transit procedures once permission has been granted.
Permit the transit but apply no aircraft separation because the using agency has assumed all separation responsibility inside the area.
Cancel the transit automatically whenever more than one aircraft is involved because LOA-based transits apply only to single-aircraft operations.

items.explanation: Per 9-3-4, coordinated transit approvals are handled using the using agency's instructions or, if that cannot be done, with 9-3-2 separation minima.

items.situation: An aircraft advises it plans to dump fuel. A trainee wants to start vectoring traffic around the area immediately without first gathering any details from the flight.
items.para_context: Under 9-4-1, when information is received that an aircraft plans to dump fuel, determine the route and altitude it will fly and the weather conditions in which the operation will be conducted.

choices.text: Determine the route, altitude, and weather conditions for the planned fuel-dumping operation before proceeding.
Wait until the fuel dump begins before asking for route, altitude, or weather information.
Ask only for the route because altitude and weather are not part of the initial information requirements.
Assume the aircraft will dump fuel on its present route and do not ask for any operating details unless traffic becomes a factor.

items.explanation: Per 9-4-1, once ATC receives information that an aircraft plans to dump fuel, the controller determines the route, altitude, and weather conditions for the operation.

items.situation: An IFR aircraft plans a nonemergency fuel dump. Another controller says routing changes are not allowed because the aircraft is IFR and must remain exactly on its original route during the dump.
items.para_context: Under 9-4-2, except when fuel is being dumped for emergency reasons, an aircraft in either VFR or IFR conditions may be requested to fly a different route.

choices.text: You may request the aircraft to fly a different route because this is a nonemergency fuel dump and the rule applies in either VFR or IFR conditions.
You may not request any route change because IFR fuel-dumping aircraft must stay on their original route regardless of the reason for the dump.
You may request a different route only if the flight is VFR; IFR operations are excluded from 9-4-2.
You may request a different route only when the dump is for emergency reasons, because nonemergency dumps may not be rerouted.

items.explanation: Per 9-4-2, aircraft in either VFR or IFR conditions may be requested to fly a different route except when the fuel dump is for emergency reasons.

items.situation: An aircraft is dumping fuel in IFR conditions. A trainee proposes assigning any convenient altitude above the MEA because the aircraft will only be in the pattern briefly.
items.para_context: Under 9-4-3, if an aircraft is dumping fuel in IFR conditions, assign an altitude at least 2,000 feet above the highest obstacle within 5 miles of the route or pattern being flown.

choices.text: Assign an altitude at least 2,000 feet above the highest obstacle within 5 miles of the route or pattern being flown.
Assign any altitude above the MEA because fuel-dumping operations use normal IFR obstruction clearance only.
Keep the aircraft at its present altitude regardless of nearby terrain because obstacle clearance is the pilot's responsibility during fuel dumping.
Assign an altitude 1,000 feet above the highest obstacle because the dump aircraft is already in controlled airspace.

items.explanation: Per 9-4-3, IFR fuel-dumping operations are assigned an altitude at least 2,000 feet above the highest obstacle within 5 miles of the route or pattern being flown.

items.situation: A radar-identified VFR aircraft is maneuvering near an aircraft dumping fuel. Another controller wants to treat the situation like ordinary VFR traffic advisories and use only 3 miles because the VFR aircraft is being watched on radar.
items.para_context: Under 9-4-4, known IFR aircraft are separated from the aircraft dumping fuel by 1,000 feet above or the greater Chapter 4 vertical minimum, 2,000 feet below, 5 miles radar, or 5 miles laterally. VFR radar-identified aircraft are separated by 5 miles and in accordance with paragraph 5-6-1.

choices.text: Separate the radar-identified VFR aircraft by 5 miles and apply paragraph 5-6-1.
Use only 3 miles because any radar-identified VFR aircraft near fuel dumping may be handled with standard radar traffic advisories.
Apply IFR 1,000-foot-above separation to the VFR aircraft because altitude-only separation always replaces mileage for fuel-dumping operations.
No special separation applies to VFR traffic if the aircraft dumping fuel remains IFR.

items.explanation: Per 9-4-4b, VFR radar-identified aircraft are separated from the aircraft dumping fuel by 5 miles and in accordance with paragraph 5-6-1.

items.clearance: ATTENTION ALL AIRCRAFT. FUEL DUMPING IN PROGRESS OVER BRICK AT FL190 BY B737 EASTBOUND.

choices.text: Attention all aircraft, fuel dumping in progress over BRICK at FL190 by B737 eastbound.
Attention all aircraft, fuel dumping complete over BRICK at FL190 by B737 eastbound.
Attention all aircraft, fuel jettison possible near BRICK by B737 at FL190.
Attention all aircraft, fuel dumping in progress below BRICK by eastbound B737, altitude unavailable.

items.explanation: Per 9-4-5 phraseology, the advisory states 'FUEL DUMPING IN PROGRESS OVER (location) AT (altitude) BY (type aircraft) (flight direction).'

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: FUEL DUMPING OVER BRICK TERMINATED.
items.display_text: FUEL DUMPING OVER BRICK SUSPENDED
items.explanation: Per 9-4-5 phraseology, the terminating advisory is 'FUEL DUMPING OVER (location) TERMINATED.'

items.situation: You are in contact with an aircraft when it starts dumping fuel. Another controller plans to advise only adjacent sectors and skip the frequency broadcasts unless another pilot calls with a concern.
items.para_context: Under 9-4-5, if you are in contact with an aircraft when it starts dumping fuel, inform other controllers and facilities which might be concerned. Facilities concerned must broadcast an advisory on appropriate radio frequencies at 3-minute intervals until the dumping stops, then broadcast a terminating advisory when the operation is completed.

choices.text: Inform other concerned controllers and facilities and ensure advisories are broadcast on appropriate radio frequencies every 3 minutes until the dumping stops, followed by a terminating advisory.
Notify only adjacent sectors because advisory broadcasts are optional unless another aircraft requests them.
Broadcast one advisory at the start and no terminating advisory because pilots are expected to infer when the operation has ended.
Wait until the dumping stops before telling other facilities, then issue only the terminating advisory.

items.explanation: Per 9-4-5, concerned facilities broadcast fuel-dumping advisories every 3 minutes until the operation stops and then issue a terminating advisory.

items.situation: A civil operator flying Air Force-type aircraft requests radar vectors for jettisoning external stores at a local drop area. A controller wants to provide the vectors immediately, but there is no written agreement on file for that operator at your location.
items.para_context: Under 9-5-1, at locations where a drop area has been established for radar assistance in jettisoning external stores, vectoring service may be provided on request to USAF, ANG, and Air Force Reserve aircraft at any time, and to civil operators or civil aircraft only when a written agreement is in effect for the location.

choices.text: Do not provide the vectoring service unless a written agreement is in effect for that civil operator at your location.
Provide the vectoring service because any established drop area automatically authorizes assistance to civil operators.
Provide the vectoring service only if the operator agrees verbally to relieve the FAA of liability.
Deny all vectoring for external-store jettisoning because only military operators may ever use a drop area.

items.explanation: Per 9-5-1, civil operators receive vectoring service for jettisoning external stores only when a written agreement is in effect for the location.

items.clearance: UNMANNED FREE BALLOON ESTIMATED OVER DODGE CITY, MOVING EAST. LAST REPORTED ALTITUDE AT FL280.

choices.text: Unmanned free balloon estimated over Dodge City, moving east. Last reported altitude at FL280.
Unmanned free balloon reported over Dodge City, altitude unknown, moving eastbound.
Derelict balloon estimated over Dodge City, moving east. Last reported altitude FL280.
Unmanned free balloon estimated over Dodge City, moving east. Last reported heading FL280.

items.explanation: Per 9-6-1 phraseology, the advisory states the balloon's known or estimated position, direction of movement, and last reported altitude or altitude unknown.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: ALTITUDE UNKNOWN.
items.display_text: ALTITUDE REPORTED
items.explanation: Per 9-6-1 phraseology, when the altitude is not known, the advisory ends with 'ALTITUDE UNKNOWN.'

items.situation: An unmanned free balloon is in your airspace, but the operator can only provide an approximate position and no accurate altitude. Another controller wants to separate IFR traffic from it vertically because the balloon's climb profile is usually predictable.
items.para_context: Under 9-6-1, provide separation between aircraft and balloons only with pilot concurrence and when the balloon information is sufficiently reliable. Do not attempt vertical separation from the balloon unless you have accurate balloon altitude information. If radar tracking is not possible, tracking should be attempted via chase plane communications, operator contact, pilot reports, or ground observations, and traffic advisories should include position, direction of movement, and altitude as unknown or reported.

choices.text: Do not attempt vertical separation without accurate balloon altitude information; use reliable tracking methods and traffic advisories instead.
Use vertical separation anyway because unmanned balloon climb rates are standardized and accurate altitude is not required.
Ignore the balloon until radar contact is established because chase-plane or operator reports are not acceptable substitutes.
Provide separation from the balloon without pilot concurrence because ATC assumes full responsibility whenever the balloon is in controlled airspace.

items.explanation: Per 9-6-1, balloon information must be sufficiently reliable and vertical separation is not attempted without accurate altitude information.

items.clearance: ADVISORY TO ALL AIRCRAFT. DERELICT BALLOON REPORTED IN THE VICINITY OF GAGE. ALTITUDE/FLIGHT LEVEL UNKNOWN.

choices.text: Advisory to all aircraft. Derelict balloon reported in the vicinity of GAGE. Altitude/flight level unknown.
Advisory to all aircraft. Derelict balloon over GAGE terminated. Altitude unknown.
Advisory to all aircraft. Unmanned free balloon reported in the vicinity of GAGE. Last reported heading unknown.
Advisory to all aircraft. Derelict balloon estimated over GAGE, maintain VFR-on-top.

items.explanation: Per 9-6-2 phraseology, the advisory identifies a derelict balloon's reported or estimated location and gives the last reported altitude or 'ALTITUDE/FLIGHT LEVEL UNKNOWN.'

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: DERELICT BALLOON REPORTED IN THE VICINITY OF GAGE.
items.display_text: DERELICT BALLOON REPORTED ABOVE THE VICINITY OF GAGE
items.explanation: Per 9-6-2 phraseology, the advisory says 'REPORTED IN THE VICINITY OF (location),' not 'ABOVE THE VICINITY OF.'

items.situation: A derelict balloon was in radar contact, but the target has now disappeared. Another controller wants to stop broadcasting after one advisory because the balloon is no longer on radar and no pilot has reported seeing it.
items.para_context: Under 9-6-2, if radar contact with a derelict balloon is lost, broadcast an advisory to all aircraft operating in the affected airspace at 10-minute intervals until the derelict balloon is no longer a factor. Position information from pilot reports or radar returns is forwarded to the supervisor for dissemination, and flight following responsibility is transferred as outlined in 9-6-1e.

choices.text: Continue broadcasting advisories at 10-minute intervals until the derelict balloon is no longer a factor.
Stop broadcasting once radar contact is lost because advisories apply only while the balloon is under radar observation.
Broadcast only when a pilot requests balloon information because derelict-balloon advisories are optional.
Terminate all balloon handling once radar contact is lost and wait for the operator to reestablish control of the balloon.

items.explanation: Per 9-6-2d, lost-radar-contact derelict balloon advisories continue every 10 minutes until the balloon is no longer a factor.

items.situation: A series of parachute jumps begins in your area and will end in an adjacent sector. Another controller wants to wait until the entire jump operation is over before telling the other sector anything.
items.para_context: Under 9-7-1, coordinate any pertinent information prior to and at the end of each parachute jump or series of jumps that begins or ends in your area of jurisdiction with other affected ATC facilities or sectors.

choices.text: Coordinate pertinent information before the jump operation and again at the end with the affected ATC facilities or sectors.
Wait until the jump operation is complete because coordination is required only after the final parachutist is on the ground.
Coordinate only if the jump aircraft enters Class A airspace because routine parachute operations do not require interfacility coordination.
Coordinate only with the next radar sector and not with any other affected ATC facility.

items.explanation: Per 9-7-1, coordination occurs both prior to and at the end of each parachute jump or series of jumps affecting your area.

items.situation: Parachute operations are requested in Class C airspace. Another controller wants to let jumpers drift outside the airspace designated for the jump activity and simply issue traffic advisories to other aircraft in the area.
items.para_context: Under 9-7-2, authorize parachute operations only within airspace designated for the jumping activity. Separate nonparticipating aircraft from the airspace authorized for the jump, and impose any conditions or restrictions necessary to promote safety.

choices.text: Authorize the jump only within the designated airspace, separate nonparticipating aircraft from that airspace, and impose any needed restrictions for safety.
Allow the jumpers to drift outside the designated airspace as long as traffic advisories are issued to nearby aircraft.
Authorize the operation anywhere in Class C airspace because jump authorization automatically applies throughout the controlled area.
Treat the jump aircraft as participating traffic and suspend separation responsibilities for all other aircraft in the vicinity.

items.explanation: Per 9-7-2, the jump is authorized only within designated airspace, nonparticipating aircraft are separated from it, and ATC may impose restrictions needed for safety.

items.situation: A jump operator requests parachute operations into your Class D airspace while a helicopter and a training aircraft are already maneuvering near the drop area. Another controller wants to approve the jump immediately and let the jump pilot work out the traffic visually.
items.para_context: Under 9-7-3, in or into Class D airspace with a functioning control tower, authorize parachute jumping with respect to known or observed traffic and issue advisory information to the jump aircraft and to nonparticipating aircraft as necessary for the safe conduct of the jump operation.

choices.text: Authorize the jump only with respect to the known or observed traffic and issue the needed advisories to both the jump aircraft and nonparticipating aircraft.
Approve the jump immediately because the jump pilot accepts responsibility for visually avoiding all other aircraft in Class D airspace.
Delay all advisories until the first parachutist exits because traffic information is not issued before the jump begins.
Treat the operation as uncontrolled once the jump aircraft reaches the drop altitude because tower responsibilities end at jump altitude.

items.explanation: Per 9-7-3, authorize the jump with respect to known or observed traffic and issue advisory information as necessary for safe conduct of the operation.

items.situation: A jump aircraft calls before a Class E jump, and several VFR aircraft will transit the area during the drop window. Because the frequency is saturated, another controller wants to brief only the jump aircraft and skip advisories to nonparticipating traffic.
items.para_context: Under 9-7-4, issue a traffic advisory to the jump aircraft before the jump including aircraft type, altitude, and direction of flight of all known traffic that will transit the jump airspace. Issue advisories to all known aircraft that will transit that airspace, and when individual transmissions are impractical, those advisories may be broadcast on appropriate control frequencies or, when available, the ATIS broadcast. Advisories to nonparticipating aircraft include the jump location, time, duration, and altitude.

choices.text: Advise the jump aircraft before the jump, advise known nonparticipating aircraft, and if individual calls are impractical broadcast the advisory on appropriate control frequencies or ATIS when available.
Brief only the jump aircraft because nonparticipating traffic advisories are optional in Class E airspace.
Delay all traffic information until the jump begins because advisories may not be issued before the first intended exit altitude is reached.
Issue nonparticipating-aircraft advisories only if each pilot first requests jump information on frequency.

items.explanation: Per 9-7-4, the jump aircraft is advised before the jump, known nonparticipating aircraft are advised, and broadcast methods may be used when individual transmissions are impractical.

items.situation: A pilot and another controller both report unusual airborne lights maneuvering near your sector. A coworker suggests waiting until the end of the shift to mention it because nobody has confirmed what the object was.
items.para_context: Under 9-8-1, inform the operations supervisor/CIC of any reported or observed unidentified anomalous phenomena (UAP)/unexplained phenomena activity.

choices.text: Inform the operations supervisor/CIC of the reported or observed UAP activity right away.
Wait for independent confirmation of the object's identity before notifying the operations supervisor/CIC.
Log the event for later review but do not notify supervision unless a pilot declares an emergency.
Report it only if the object enters Class A airspace because UAP reporting does not apply below that altitude.

items.explanation: Per 9-8-1, any reported or observed UAP or unexplained phenomena activity is reported to the operations supervisor/CIC.

items.clearance: MAYDAY, MAYDAY, MAYDAY, ENGINE FAILURE, REQUEST IMMEDIATE LANDING.

choices.text: Mayday, Mayday, Mayday, engine failure, request immediate landing.
Pan-Pan, Pan-Pan, Pan-Pan, engine failure, request immediate landing.
Mayday, engine failure, request immediate landing.
Engine failure, request immediate landing.

items.explanation: Per 10-1-1, a pilot encountering a Distress condition should begin the initial communication with the word 'Mayday,' preferably repeated three times.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: MAYDAY MAYDAY MAYDAY
items.display_text: MAYDAY PAN-PAN MAYDAY
items.explanation: Per 10-1-1, a Distress condition is declared with 'Mayday,' preferably repeated three times.

items.situation: A pilot reports smoke in the cockpit and requests immediate vectors to land but never says 'Mayday' or 'Pan-Pan.' Another controller says not to treat it as an emergency until the pilot uses the formal emergency words.
items.para_context: Under 10-1-1, if the words 'Mayday' or 'Pan-Pan' are not used but you believe an emergency or urgent situation exists, handle it as though it were an emergency. Because of the infinite variety of emergency situations, specific procedures cannot be prescribed; you should select and pursue the course of action that appears most appropriate under the circumstances and most nearly conforms to the manual.

choices.text: Handle it as an emergency and take the course of action that appears most appropriate under the circumstances.
Wait for the pilot to say 'Mayday' or 'Pan-Pan' before providing emergency handling.
Treat it only as an urgency condition because the pilot did not use the word 'Mayday.'
Continue normal operations because emergency handling applies only after a formal declaration.

items.explanation: Per 10-1-1, if you believe an emergency or urgent situation exists, handle it as an emergency even if the pilot does not use the formal terms.

items.clearance: AIRCRAFT, N123B, PILOT INCAPACITATION, 12 MILES SOUTHWEST OF KOJC, LANDING KIXD AIRPORT. EMERGENCY AUTOLAND IN 13 MINUTES ON RUNWAY 36.

choices.text: Aircraft, N123B, pilot incapacitation, 12 miles southwest of KOJC, landing KIXD airport. Emergency Autoland in 13 minutes on runway 36.
Aircraft, N123B, pilot incapacitation, 12 miles southeast of KOJC, landing KIXD airport. Emergency Autoland in 13 minutes on runway 36.
Aircraft, N123B, pilot incapacitation, 12 miles southwest of KOJC, landing KOJC airport. Emergency Autoland in 13 minutes on runway 36.
Aircraft, N123B, pilot incapacitation, 12 miles southwest of KOJC, landing KIXD airport. Emergency Autoland in 13 minutes on runway 35.

items.explanation: Per 10-1-2b, an Emergency Autoland system may transmit the call sign, activation, position, intended emergency landing airport and runway, and ETE.

items.instruction: One word in this transmission is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: EMERGENCY AUTOLAND IN 13 MINUTES ON RUNWAY 36.
items.display_text: EMERGENCY AUTOLAND IN 13 MINUTES ON RUNWAY 35
items.explanation: Per the 10-1-2 example, the transmission includes the planned landing runway, and the runway number must be correct.

items.situation: A distressed pilot requests the nearest airport and gives only partial information about the problem. You think a different airport may be more beneficial because of weather and equipment, and another controller says you should simply assign that airport without further discussion.
items.para_context: Under 10-1-2, use the information provided or solicit more information as necessary to assist the distressed aircraft. Provide assistance consistent with the pilot's requests. If you believe an alternative course of action may prove more beneficial, transmit your recommendation or recommendations to the pilot.

choices.text: Solicit any additional information needed, assist consistent with the pilot's request, and transmit your recommendation if you believe another course may be more beneficial.
Assign the airport you prefer immediately because the controller, not the pilot, decides the most beneficial course during every emergency.
Refuse further help until the pilot provides every detail you would like to have before offering assistance.
Wait for the pilot to request a recommendation specifically before asking any follow-up questions or offering any alternative.

items.explanation: Per 10-1-2, use or solicit needed information, assist consistent with the pilot's requests, and recommend a better alternative if you believe one exists.

items.situation: An aircraft in distress is losing communications and radar coverage near the edge of your area. Military radar and other emergency services are available, but another controller says you should wait unless the pilot explicitly asks for outside help.
items.para_context: Under 10-1-3, provide maximum assistance to aircraft in distress. Enlist the services of available radar facilities operated by the FAA, the military services, and the Federal Communications Commission, as well as their emergency services and facilities, when the pilot requests or when you deem necessary.

choices.text: Provide maximum assistance and enlist available radar and emergency services when the pilot requests it or when you deem it necessary.
Wait for the pilot to request each outside service individually because ATC may not initiate outside assistance on its own judgment.
Use only FAA facilities because military and FCC resources are outside the scope of distress assistance.
Reduce involvement once the aircraft nears another facility because maximum assistance applies only inside your own airspace.

items.explanation: Per 10-1-3, provide maximum assistance and use available FAA, military, FCC, and emergency-service resources when requested or when you deem necessary.

items.situation: You are in direct communication with an aircraft in distress and have already started coordinating fire, rescue, and adjacent-sector support. The aircraft is approaching another facility's airspace, and another controller says you should transfer responsibility immediately simply because it is nearing the boundary.
items.para_context: Under 10-1-4, if you are in communication with an aircraft in distress, handle the emergency and coordinate and direct the activities of assisting facilities. Transfer that responsibility to another facility only when you feel better handling of the emergency will result. When you receive information about an aircraft in distress, forward detailed data to the center in whose area the emergency exists.

choices.text: Continue handling and coordinating the emergency unless you believe another facility will handle it better, and ensure detailed information is forwarded to the appropriate center.
Transfer responsibility as soon as the aircraft approaches another boundary because proximity alone determines emergency ownership.
Keep responsibility but stop coordinating assisting facilities because each supporting unit manages itself independently.
Wait until the emergency is over before forwarding detailed information to the center in whose area it exists.

items.explanation: Per 10-1-4, the controller in communication handles and coordinates the emergency, transfers responsibility only for better handling, and forwards detailed data to the center where the emergency exists.

items.situation: A flight is believed overdue and contact has not been reestablished. Another controller wants to wait for confirmation that the aircraft is actually in distress before coordinating with other facilities or services.
items.para_context: Under 10-1-5, coordinate efforts to the extent possible to assist any aircraft believed overdue, lost, or in emergency status.

choices.text: Coordinate assistance efforts to the extent possible because the paragraph applies to aircraft believed overdue, lost, or in emergency status.
Wait for positive confirmation of an emergency before coordinating because a belief that the aircraft is overdue is not enough.
Coordinate only if the aircraft is IFR because overdue VFR flights are outside this paragraph.
Limit coordination to the current sector because wider coordination begins only after a center declares an ALNOT.

items.explanation: Per 10-1-5, coordination should begin to the extent possible for any aircraft believed overdue, lost, or in emergency status.

items.situation: A disabled aircraft has stopped on a taxiway, and local fire vehicles need to cross active movement areas to reach it. Another controller wants to keep normal airport traffic moving through the area and let the emergency vehicles sort out their own route.
items.para_context: Under 10-1-6, when an emergency occurs on the airport proper, control other air and ground traffic to avoid conflicts in the area where the emergency is being handled. This also applies when routes within the airport proper are required for movement of local emergency equipment going to or from an emergency outside the airport proper. Workload permitting, monitor the progress of emergency vehicles and provide available information to assist responders in finding the scene if necessary.

choices.text: Control other air and ground traffic to avoid conflicts in the emergency area and along the routes needed by the responding vehicles, and monitor their progress workload permitting.
Keep normal traffic moving through the area because emergency vehicles are responsible for avoiding airport traffic once they enter the movement area.
Protect only aircraft operations because the paragraph does not apply to ground routes used by emergency equipment.
Stop monitoring once the vehicles leave the fire station because responder location assistance is outside ATC's role.

items.explanation: Per 10-1-6, protect the emergency area and the routes used by local emergency equipment, and monitor/respond with available information workload permitting.

items.situation: A military fighter-type aircraft at low altitude reports a possible engine failure. Another controller wants to issue a new transponder code, a frequency change, and several follow-up questions before the aircraft reaches the field.
items.para_context: Under 10-1-7, military fighter-type aircraft place an extremely high workload on the pilot during an inflight emergency. Radio frequency and transponder code changes should be avoided and radio transmissions held to a minimum, especially at low altitude. Pilots may execute a flameout pattern in an emergency, and the pattern may be modified based on actual recovery requirements.

choices.text: Avoid frequency and transponder changes and keep transmissions to a minimum while accommodating the fighter emergency recovery as needed.
Issue the frequency and code changes immediately because standardized ATC housekeeping items take priority during fighter emergencies.
Require the pilot to fly a fixed flameout pattern exactly as published regardless of the actual emergency.
Use normal-length transmissions because minimizing radio traffic applies only after the aircraft climbs to a safe altitude.

items.explanation: Per 10-1-7, avoid frequency and code changes and minimize transmissions during fighter emergencies, especially at low altitude.

items.situation: A low-altitude military fighter reports an emergency and gives aircraft identification, the nature of the problem, and what the pilot wants. Another controller wants to delay assistance until every supplemental information item has been collected first.
items.para_context: Under 10-2-1, start assistance as soon as enough information has been obtained upon which to act. Minimum required information for inflight emergencies is the aircraft identification and type, the nature of the emergency, and the pilot's desires. After initiating action, obtain other pertinent items as necessary. Normally, do not request the additional information from low-altitude military fighter-type aircraft, though request the position if the aircraft is not visually sighted or displayed on radar and the location is not given.

choices.text: Start assistance once you have enough information to act, then obtain additional pertinent items as necessary without overloading the low-altitude fighter pilot.
Delay all assistance until every listed supplemental item has been collected from the pilot.
Treat the flight as nonemergency until fuel, weather, souls on board, and navigation equipment have all been reported.
Avoid asking for the fighter's position even if it is not on radar and the pilot has not provided it.

items.explanation: Per 10-2-1, act once enough information is available, then collect additional information as needed, with special care not to overload low-altitude military fighters.

items.situation: An aircraft in emergency status establishes contact on your working frequency. Another controller wants to move it to 121.5 immediately just because that is an emergency frequency, even though communications are clear where it is.
items.para_context: Under 10-2-2, although 121.5 MHz and 243.0 MHz are emergency frequencies, it might be best to keep the aircraft on the initial contact frequency. Change frequencies only when there is a valid reason.

choices.text: Keep the aircraft on the initial contact frequency unless there is a valid reason to change.
Move it to 121.5 immediately because every emergency aircraft should be transferred to the primary emergency frequency as soon as possible.
Transfer it to 243.0 because military-use emergency frequencies always take priority over the initial contact frequency.
Cycle the aircraft through multiple frequencies to find the clearest audio before beginning assistance.

items.explanation: Per 10-2-2, the initial contact frequency may be the best choice; frequency changes are made only for a valid reason.

items.situation: An aircraft reports being disoriented and unsure of its position. Another controller insists you must use a NAVAID-based solution first, even though radar identification is already solid and another aircraft in the area can also sight the distressed aircraft.
items.para_context: Under 10-2-3, orientate an aircraft by the means most appropriate to the circumstances. Recognized methods include radar, NAVAIDs, pilotage, and sighting by other aircraft.

choices.text: Use the orientation method most appropriate to the circumstances, such as radar if that is the best available means.
Use NAVAIDs first in every case because radar and pilotage are only backups for nonemergency use.
Refuse to orient the aircraft until the pilot identifies a landmark visually.
Wait for another aircraft to sight the distressed aircraft because that is the preferred method over all others.

items.explanation: Per 10-2-3, the controller chooses the orientation method that is most appropriate to the circumstances.

items.situation: You are having difficulty communicating with an aircraft in distress. The weather and terrain would allow a higher altitude, but the aircraft also reports it may be in volcanic ash. Another controller wants to tell the pilot to climb immediately because better radio and radar reception always takes priority.
items.para_context: Under 10-2-4, when necessary and if weather and circumstances permit, recommend that the aircraft maintain or increase altitude to improve communications or radar. The note states that climb while in volcanic ash is to be avoided where terrain permits because engine damage increases as power is increased.

choices.text: Recommend maintaining or increasing altitude only if weather and circumstances permit, and avoid recommending a climb through volcanic ash where terrain permits.
Direct an immediate climb because improved radio reception always outweighs volcanic ash considerations.
Never discuss altitude because communication problems are handled only through frequency changes.
Descend the aircraft automatically because lower altitude always improves reception near the airport.

items.explanation: Per 10-2-4, altitude changes are recommended only when necessary and weather and circumstances permit; the volcanic ash note cautions against climbing in ash where terrain permits.

items.situation: A VFR aircraft stops responding and disappears from radar unexpectedly. Another controller says you should wait for a formal emergency declaration or a 7700 code before notifying anyone because the aircraft never said it was in trouble.
items.para_context: Under 10-2-5, consider that an aircraft emergency exists and inform the RCC or ARTCC if an emergency is declared by specified sources, if there is unexpected loss of radar contact and radio communications with any IFR or VFR aircraft, if reports indicate a forced landing or abandonment is likely or has occurred, if an emergency code is displayed or reported, if intercept or escort services are required, if ground rescue appears likely, or if an ELT signal is heard or reported.

choices.text: Treat it as an emergency situation and inform the RCC or ARTCC because unexpected loss of radar and radio contact with any IFR or VFR aircraft is one of the triggers.
Wait for a pilot declaration or a 7700 code because loss of contact alone does not justify emergency notification.
Notify only after ground rescue has already been dispatched because RCC/ARTCC involvement starts later in the event.
Treat only IFR flights this way because VFR aircraft are excluded from the unexpected-loss-of-contact trigger.

items.explanation: Per 10-2-5, unexpected loss of radar contact and radio communications with any IFR or VFR aircraft is itself a trigger to consider that an emergency exists and notify the RCC or ARTCC.

items.clearance: UNITED 234, VERIFY SQUAWKING SEVEN FIVE ZERO ZERO.

choices.text: United 234, verify squawking seven five zero zero.
United 234, verify squawking seven seven zero zero.
United 234, confirm hijack and squawk seven five zero zero.
United 234, verify transponder emergency code now.

items.explanation: Per 10-2-6b, when the pilot notifies ATC of a hijacking situation by squawking 7500, the verification phraseology is '(Identification)(facility) VERIFY SQUAWKING SEVEN FIVE ZERO ZERO.'

items.instruction: One element in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: VERIFY SQUAWKING SEVEN FIVE ZERO ZERO.
items.display_text: REPORT SQUAWKING SEVEN FIVE ZERO ZERO
items.explanation: Per 10-2-6, the standard verification phraseology begins with 'VERIFY SQUAWKING,' not 'REPORT SQUAWKING.'

items.situation: A pilot verbally advises of a hijacking situation. Another controller wants to ask follow-up questions about the hijack on frequency after assigning a code, and does not think supervision needs to be notified until more details are known.
items.para_context: Under 10-2-6, when a pilot verbally notifies ATC of a hijacking situation, assign code 7500. If the pilot only acknowledges assignment of code 7500 or fails to acknowledge or communicate further, assume the flight is being subject to hijack. No reference to the hijacking is made in subsequent communications unless initiated by the pilot or directed by the DEN Air Traffic Security Coordinator, and the operations manager, supervisor, or CIC is immediately informed.

choices.text: Assign code 7500, avoid referencing the hijacking in subsequent communications unless permitted by the rule, and immediately inform the operations manager, supervisor, or CIC.
Assign 7500 and then ask detailed hijack questions on frequency so the situation can be confirmed before notifying supervision.
Wait for the aircraft to squawk 7500 on its own because verbal hijack notification does not justify code assignment.
Continue normal communications after the code assignment because the hijack reference restriction applies only to military flights.

items.explanation: Per 10-2-6, assign 7500, avoid further reference to the hijacking unless authorized by the rule, and immediately notify the operations manager, supervisor, or CIC.

items.situation: A VFR aircraft reports worsening weather and says it may enter instrument conditions. The best-equipped facility to help is on another frequency. Another controller says to wait until the pilot actually loses communication before making any coordination or relay plan.
items.para_context: Under 10-2-7, determine the facility best able to provide service. If a frequency change is necessary, advise the pilot of the reason for the change, request the pilot contact the appropriate control facility, and inform that facility of the situation. If the aircraft is unable to communicate with the appropriate facility, relay information and clearances.

choices.text: Determine the facility best able to provide service, explain any needed frequency change, coordinate with that facility, and relay information or clearances if the aircraft cannot communicate directly.
Keep the aircraft on the current frequency until communication is lost because early coordination is unnecessary before an actual emergency exists.
Tell the pilot to select 7700 immediately and wait for the next sector to call you back before doing anything else.
Transfer the aircraft without advising the pilot why the frequency change is needed because explanation would delay assistance.

items.explanation: Per 10-2-7, determine the facility best able to provide service, advise the reason for any frequency change, inform the receiving facility, and relay information or clearances if direct communication is not possible.

items.situation: A VFR pilot in deteriorating weather says the aircraft is not IFR capable and refuses to declare an emergency. Another controller says that ends ATC involvement because radar assistance may only be provided after an emergency declaration.
items.para_context: Under 10-2-8, ask whether the pilot is qualified and capable of IFR flight. If so, request an IFR flight plan and issue a clearance. If not, inform the pilot of airports with VFR conditions and other weather information and ask whether the pilot elects to proceed to such an airport. If that is not feasible or the pilot declines, provide radar assistance if the pilot declares an emergency or, if the pilot refuses to declare one, if the controller determines the exact nature of the radar services desired.

choices.text: Offer airports with VFR conditions and other weather information first, and if that is not feasible or the pilot declines, radar assistance may still be provided if you determine the exact nature of the radar services desired.
Terminate assistance because without an emergency declaration the rule prohibits all radar help to a VFR pilot in weather difficulty.
Issue an IFR clearance immediately even though the pilot already said the aircraft is not IFR capable and the pilot is not qualified.
Wait until the aircraft enters IMC and then coordinate only with search and rescue because weather-assistance procedures no longer apply at that point.

items.explanation: Per 10-2-8, first determine IFR qualification/capability, then offer VFR options, and if those are not workable, radar assistance may still be provided when the pilot refuses to declare an emergency if the controller determines the exact radar services needed.

items.situation: A non-IFR-qualified pilot enters clouds while receiving radar assistance. Another controller wants an immediate frequency change, a sharp turn with a simultaneous descent, and a rapid series of maneuvers to get the aircraft lined up for an airport.
items.para_context: Under 10-2-9, avoid frequency changes except when necessary to provide a clear communications channel. Make turns while the aircraft is in VFR conditions so it can fly straight while in IFR conditions. Have the pilot lower the landing gear and slow to approach speed while in VFR conditions. Avoid requiring a climb or descent while in a turn if the pilot is in IFR conditions. Avoid abrupt maneuvers and vector the aircraft to VFR conditions.

choices.text: Minimize frequency changes, avoid abrupt maneuvering, avoid requiring climb or descent while turning in IFR conditions, and use vectors aimed at reaching VFR conditions.
Use any maneuver combination that gets the aircraft to an airport quickly because the rule puts speed ahead of smooth control inputs.
Require the aircraft to keep turning and descending in the clouds because continuous maneuvering is preferred when the pilot is not instrument qualified.
Shift the aircraft to any available frequency first because communication continuity is less important than distributing workload.

items.explanation: Per 10-2-9, keep communications stable when possible, make turns while still in VFR if feasible, avoid abrupt maneuvers, avoid climb/descent while in a turn in IFR conditions, and vector toward VFR conditions.

items.situation: A terminal controller hears a strong ELT signal that appears to be coming from the airport area. Another controller wants to leave the position to search the field personally and says the ARTCC and technical operations can be notified after more precise bearings are collected.
items.para_context: Under 10-2-10, in terminal facilities notify the ARTCC, which coordinates with the RCC, attempt to obtain fixes or bearings, solicit assistance of other aircraft, and forward fixes, bearings, and other information to the ARTCC. If the signal appears to originate from an airport or vicinity, notify technical operations as well. Air traffic personnel must not leave required duty stations to locate an ELT signal source.

choices.text: Notify the ARTCC, obtain and forward fixes or bearings, notify technical operations if the source appears local to the airport, and remain at the duty station rather than leaving to search for the ELT.
Delay notification until enough bearings are collected to identify the source exactly because outside agencies should be called only after local confirmation.
Leave the position briefly to search the airport because locating the source is more important than remaining at the duty station during an ELT event.
Handle the event entirely within the terminal facility unless another aircraft reports hearing the signal too.

items.explanation: Per 10-2-10, terminal facilities notify the ARTCC, gather and forward ELT information, notify technical operations when the source appears to be at the airport or nearby, and ATC personnel must not leave required duty stations to locate the source.

items.situation: A specific airborne aircraft receives a bomb threat while you are in contact with it. Another controller says not to tell the pilot yet, and if the pilot asks for technical help you should tell the crew what to do with the suspected device yourself instead of involving supervisory or explosives-expert support.
items.para_context: Under 10-2-11, notify your supervisor or facility air traffic manager when a bomb threat is received. If the threat is targeted against a specific aircraft and you are in contact with it, advise the pilot of the threat, inform the pilot that technical assistance can be obtained from an FAA aviation explosives expert, ask whether the pilot desires an altitude change to reduce or equalize pressure differential, handle the aircraft as an emergency and/or provide the most expeditious handling possible, and if technical assistance is needed, obtain aircraft type, precise location or description of the device if known, and other pertinent details for the FAA aviation explosives expert. Do not suggest what actions the pilot should take concerning a bomb.

choices.text: Notify supervisory personnel, advise the pilot of the threat, offer access to the FAA aviation explosives expert, handle the aircraft as an emergency or with the most expeditious handling possible, and do not tell the pilot what bomb-handling action to take yourself.
Delay advising the pilot until the threat is confirmed, and if the pilot asks for help, tell the crew how to move or disarm the device before notifying supervision.
Keep the handling routine unless a bomb is actually found, because a reported threat alone does not justify emergency handling or outside technical assistance.
Tell the pilot to continue as planned and have company operations solve the problem because ATC involvement ends once the pilot is notified.

items.explanation: Per 10-2-11, advise supervision, advise the pilot, offer FAA aviation explosives expert support, provide emergency or the most expeditious handling as appropriate, and do not suggest bomb-handling actions to the pilot.

items.situation: An aircraft asks for the nearest explosive-detection K-9 team. Another controller says to guess which airport probably has one, pass that guess to the pilot, and wait to gather the aircraft details until after the flight decides whether to divert.
items.para_context: Under 10-2-12, obtain the aircraft identification and position and advise your supervisor of the pilot request. When you receive the nearest location of the explosive detection K-9 team, relay the information to the pilot. If the aircraft wishes to divert to that airport, obtain an estimated arrival time from the pilot and advise your supervisor.

choices.text: Obtain the aircraft identification and position, advise the supervisor of the request, relay the nearest K-9 location when received, and if the aircraft diverts there obtain the ETA and advise the supervisor.
Give the pilot the airport you think is most likely to have a K-9 team and collect the aircraft details later if the diversion becomes definite.
Tell the pilot ATC cannot assist with K-9 information because only airport operations handles that request.
Wait to notify the supervisor until the aircraft is on final to the diversion airport so the information is not passed prematurely.

items.explanation: Per 10-2-12, get the aircraft identification and position, notify your supervisor, relay the nearest K-9 team location when received, and if the aircraft diverts there obtain and pass the ETA.

items.clearance: ATTENTION EASTERN 417, MANPADS ALERT. EXERCISE EXTREME CAUTION. MANPADS THREAT REPORTED BY TSA, LAGUARDIA VICINITY. SAY INTENTIONS.

choices.text: Attention Eastern 417, MANPADS alert. Exercise extreme caution. MANPADS threat reported by TSA, LaGuardia vicinity. Say intentions.
Attention Eastern 417, MANPADS alert. Delay landing clearance until company approval. Threat reported by TSA, LaGuardia vicinity.
Eastern 417, exercise caution. MANPADS information will be on the next ATIS only.
All aircraft, MANPADS alert. Resume normal operations and advise when ready to copy the ATIS.

items.explanation: Per 10-2-13 phraseology, use 'ATTENTION (aircraft identification), MANPADS ALERT. EXERCISE EXTREME CAUTION...' and, when transmitting to an individual aircraft, add 'SAY INTENTIONS.'

items.instruction: One element in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: ATTENTION EASTERN 417 MANPADS ALERT EXERCISE EXTREME CAUTION MANPADS THREAT REPORTED BY TSA LAGUARDIA VICINITY SAY INTENTIONS
items.display_text: ATTENTION EASTERN 417 MANPADS ALERT EXERCISE MODERATE CAUTION MANPADS THREAT REPORTED BY TSA LAGUARDIA VICINITY SAY INTENTIONS
items.explanation: Per 10-2-13 phraseology, the warning says 'EXERCISE EXTREME CAUTION,' not 'MODERATE CAUTION.'

items.situation: A MANPADS threat near the airport is determined to be real during arrivals. Another controller says to stop issuing landing clearance until the airline decides what it wants to do, and to stop controller-to-pilot advisories as soon as the ATIS is updated even if inbound crews have not yet acknowledged the current ATIS code.
items.para_context: Under 10-2-13, do not withhold landing clearance. Disseminate MANPADS information via the ATIS and/or controller-to-pilot transmissions. Continue controller-to-pilot transmissions until the appropriate MANPADS information is on the ATIS and pilots indicate they have received the appropriate ATIS code. Include the nature and location of the threat or incident, whether observed or reported and by whom, the time if known, and when transmitting to an individual aircraft, request the pilot's intentions. Continue until otherwise directed by the DEN Air Traffic Security Coordinator.

choices.text: Do not withhold landing clearance, keep controller-to-pilot advisories going until the information is on the ATIS and pilots report the appropriate ATIS code, and include the required threat details and a request for intentions when addressing an individual aircraft.
Suspend landing clearance until company approval is received, because operational decisions during a MANPADS event are made by ATC instead of the pilot or operator.
Stop all controller-to-pilot advisories as soon as the ATIS text is changed, even if the arriving aircraft have not yet acknowledged the updated ATIS code.
Provide only the existence of a threat and omit the location, reporting source, and time so the transmission stays short.

items.explanation: Per 10-2-13, landing clearance is not withheld, controller-to-pilot advisories continue until the ATIS information is both broadcast and acknowledged via the appropriate ATIS code, and the required threat details must be included.

items.clearance: UNAUTHORIZED LASER ILLUMINATION EVENT, NORTH OF AIRPORT, THREE THOUSAND FEET.

choices.text: Unauthorized laser illumination event, north of airport, three thousand feet.
Unauthorized light report, use caution somewhere north of the field.
Laser warning, airport vicinity, altitude unknown, continue normal operations.
Authorized laser illumination event, north of airport, three thousand feet.

items.explanation: Per 10-2-14 phraseology, use 'UNAUTHORIZED LASER ILLUMINATION EVENT, (location), (altitude).'

items.instruction: One element in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: UNAUTHORIZED LASER ILLUMINATION EVENT
items.display_text: UNAUTHORIZED RADAR ILLUMINATION EVENT
items.explanation: Per 10-2-14 phraseology, the warning is for an unauthorized LASER illumination event.

items.situation: A pilot reports a green laser north of the airport at three thousand feet. Another controller says one broadcast on a single approach frequency is enough, and the event does not need to go on the ATIS because pilots can ask for it if they care about it.
items.para_context: Under 10-2-14, broadcast on all appropriate frequencies a general caution warning every five minutes for 20 minutes following the last report. Terminal facilities must include reported unauthorized laser illumination events on the ATIS for one hour following the last report and include the time, location, altitude, color, and direction of the laser as reported by the pilot.

choices.text: Broadcast the caution on all appropriate frequencies every five minutes for 20 minutes following the last report and put the event on the ATIS for one hour with the reported time, location, altitude, color, and direction.
Make one advisory on the frequency that received the report and wait to see whether any other aircraft ask for additional information.
Keep the event off the ATIS because laser reports are handled only by direct controller-to-pilot transmissions.
Broadcast the warning for five minutes only because the rule does not permit repeated cautions after the initial report.

items.explanation: Per 10-2-14, warnings go on all appropriate frequencies every five minutes for 20 minutes following the last report, and terminal facilities include the event on the ATIS for one hour with the specified details.

items.situation: A pilot with an engine problem asks for the best emergency airport. An Emergency Autoland advisory identifies a nearby field, but that airport has a closed runway and significant construction. Another controller says to use the automated recommendation as-is because it already chose the nearest suitable airport.
items.para_context: Under 10-2-15, consider remaining fuel in relation to airport distances, weather, airport conditions, NAVAID status, aircraft type, pilot qualifications, and vectoring or homing capability. Information from an automated emergency airport information source must be used together with these factors and with paragraph 10-2-16 guidance. Emergency Autoland may select a suitable airport but does not consider closed runways, equipment on the runway, construction, or other possible airport hazards.

choices.text: Use the automated recommendation only as one input, then evaluate fuel, weather, airport conditions, NAVAID status, aircraft type, pilot qualifications, and guidance capability before recommending the airport.
Accept the automated airport selection without additional review because Emergency Autoland already accounts for runway closures and construction hazards.
Recommend the closest airport solely on mileage because emergency airport recommendation is based only on distance and fuel remaining.
Ignore pilot qualifications and available guidance capability because those factors matter only after the aircraft is established on final.

items.explanation: Per 10-2-15, automated emergency airport information is only one source. Controllers must still consider fuel, weather, airport conditions, NAVAID status, aircraft type, pilot qualifications, and vectoring or homing capability.

items.situation: A distressed pilot needs help reaching the recommended airport. Another controller says the only approved guidance method is radar vectors and that if radar coverage becomes unreliable, ATC has no other guidance options.
items.para_context: Under 10-2-16, when necessary, guidance to the emergency airport may use radar, following another aircraft, NAVAIDs, pilotage by landmarks, or compass headings. These guidance methods must be considered together with paragraph 10-2-15 emergency-airport recommendation factors when using any automated emergency airport information source.

choices.text: Use any suitable listed guidance method, including radar, following another aircraft, NAVAIDs, pilotage, or compass headings, and apply that guidance in conjunction with the emergency-airport recommendation factors.
Use radar only, because the other methods are informal techniques not authorized for emergency guidance.
Stop providing guidance if radar becomes weak, because ATC may not shift to landmarks, headings, or NAVAIDs once an airport has been recommended.
Rely only on the automated emergency airport information source after the airport is chosen, because paragraph 10-2-15 no longer applies at the guidance stage.

items.explanation: Per 10-2-16, controllers may use radar, following another aircraft, NAVAIDs, pilotage by landmarks, or compass headings for guidance to the emergency airport, and that guidance must still be considered with the paragraph 10-2-15 factors.

items.situation: A pilot is descending below safe terrain clearance in deteriorating weather and cannot maintain an appropriate minimum altitude. Another controller wants to start using the EOVM immediately without treating the situation as an emergency and without telling the pilot that the headings are only emergency advisories toward lower terrain.
items.para_context: Under 10-2-17, the EOVM/EAM may be used only when the pilot has declared an emergency or when the controller determines an emergency condition exists or is imminent because of the pilot's inability to maintain an appropriate terrain or obstacle clearance minimum altitude. When providing emergency vectoring service, the controller must advise the pilot that headings issued are emergency advisories intended only to direct the aircraft toward and over an area of lower terrain or obstacle elevation. The map shows actual altitudes and obstacle locations and contains no lateral or vertical obstruction-clearance buffers.

choices.text: Use the EOVM/EAM only once the emergency-use condition is met, and tell the pilot the headings are emergency advisories intended only to direct the aircraft toward lower terrain or obstacle elevation.
Use the EOVM whenever a pilot asks for vectors, even if no emergency exists and safe terrain clearance is not an issue.
Issue EOVM headings as if they guarantee normal obstruction clearance buffers, so there is no need to qualify the service.
Delay all vectors until the pilot can quote the exact MVA or MEA, because controller determination of an imminent emergency is not enough to use the map.

items.explanation: Per 10-2-17, EOVM/EAM use is limited to declared or controller-determined emergency terrain-clearance situations, and the pilot must be told that the headings are emergency advisories only toward lower terrain or obstacle elevation.

items.situation: A turbine-powered aircraft reports that it has entered volcanic ash and the crew says the situation is now distress. Another controller wants to issue an immediate climb and a turn away from the ash without asking the pilot, arguing that any prompt vector is better than waiting.
items.para_context: Under 10-2-18, if volcanic ash is known or forecast, relay all available information and suggest reroutes to avoid it. If an aircraft reports it has entered ash and indicates a distress situation exists, consider the aircraft in an emergency situation, do not initiate climb clearances to turbine-powered aircraft until the aircraft has exited the ash cloud, and do not attempt to provide escape vectors without pilot concurrence.

choices.text: Treat the aircraft as an emergency, avoid initiating climb clearance to the turbine-powered aircraft until it exits the ash cloud, and do not provide escape vectors unless the pilot concurs.
Issue the climb immediately because climbing is always the preferred escape maneuver in volcanic ash, even without pilot concurrence.
Provide an escape vector without consulting the pilot because pilot concurrence is required only when weather radar can see the ash.
Treat the report as routine weather avoidance unless the aircraft actually loses an engine, because volcanic ash alone does not justify emergency handling.

items.explanation: Per 10-2-18, an aircraft in ash reporting distress is handled as an emergency, turbine-powered aircraft are not given climb clearances until out of the ash cloud, and escape vectors are not attempted without pilot concurrence.

items.situation: A pilot reports several ill passengers and requests medical assistance on arrival. Another controller wants to pass the flight to the next sector first and collect only the callsign and destination, saying public-health details can wait until after landing.
items.para_context: Under 10-2-19, when a report of death, illness, or other public health risk is received, obtain the call sign, number of suspected illness cases, nature of illness or public health risk if known, number of persons on board, number of deaths if applicable, pilot's intent, and any request for assistance, then notify the OMIC/OS/CIC as soon as possible. The OMIC/OS/CIC must relay the information to the DEN as soon as possible.

choices.text: Obtain the listed public-health details, including cases, nature of illness, persons on board, deaths if any, pilot intent, and assistance requested, then notify the OMIC/OS/CIC as soon as possible.
Collect only the callsign and destination first, because the rest of the public-health details can wait until the flight lands and taxis in.
Handle the follow-on coordination entirely on ATC frequency with outside agencies so everyone hears the same details immediately.
Wait until the next sector takes handoff responsibility, because only the destination facility is allowed to start public-health reporting.

items.explanation: Per 10-2-19, collect the listed information promptly and notify the OMIC/OS/CIC as soon as possible so the information can be relayed to the DEN.

items.situation: An IFR aircraft receiving radar service loses communications and radar contact at the same time under abnormal circumstances. Another controller says you must still wait 30 minutes before treating it as overdue because the aircraft has not yet reached its ETA or clearance void time.
items.para_context: Under 10-3-1, an aircraft is considered overdue for ALNOT purposes when neither communications nor radar contact can be established and 30 minutes have passed since its ETA over a reporting point or clearance limit, its clearance void time, or its failure to cancel a flight plan after arrival at a nontowered airport. But if there is an abnormal simultaneous loss of radar and communications with an IFR aircraft or a VFR/SVFR aircraft receiving flight following, consider the aircraft to be in emergency status and initiate ALNOT procedures immediately.

choices.text: Treat the aircraft as in emergency status and initiate ALNOT procedures immediately because abnormal simultaneous loss of radar and communications requires immediate action.
Wait the full 30 minutes in every case because ALNOT action cannot begin before the overdue clock expires.
Wait until a supervisor confirms the aircraft was IFR filed all the way to destination before taking any ALNOT-related action.
Treat the event as routine only if the aircraft was receiving radar service, because simultaneous loss becomes emergency status only in nonradar operations.

items.explanation: Per 10-3-1b, abnormal simultaneous loss of radar and communications with an IFR aircraft or a VFR/SVFR aircraft receiving flight following requires immediate emergency-status handling and ALNOT initiation.

items.situation: A terminal facility has an overdue IFR aircraft that may require SAR. Another controller wants to alert the ARTCC with only the callsign and last assigned frequency, saying the rest of the information can be sorted out later if the center asks for it.
items.para_context: Under 10-3-2, the terminal facility must alert the appropriate ARTCC and forward, as available, the flight plan including aircraft color if known, last transmission time/by whom/frequency, last position report and how determined, beacon code, persons on board, fuel status, facility working the aircraft and frequency, last known and estimated present position with maximum range based on remaining fuel and airspeed, nearby aircraft positions when requested, ELT information, and other pertinent information.

choices.text: Alert the ARTCC and forward the available SAR-related information set, not just the callsign and frequency.
Pass only the callsign and current frequency first because the ARTCC does not need details like fuel, persons on board, or last known position until it requests them later.
Wait until the aircraft is officially declared missing by the flight service station before alerting the ARTCC at all.
Call only the nearest tower because terminal facilities do not forward overdue-aircraft information directly to ARTCCs.

items.explanation: Per 10-3-2, the terminal facility alerts the appropriate ARTCC and forwards the available information listed in the paragraph to support overdue-aircraft or SAR action.

items.situation: An ARTCC has an aircraft in emergency status and is alerting the RCC. Another controller wants to pass only the flight plan and last frequency, arguing that details like beacon code, fuel, and last known position can wait until the RCC asks for them later.
items.para_context: Under 10-3-3, when an aircraft is in emergency status or an IFR aircraft is overdue, the ARTCC must alert the RCC and forward, as available, the facility and caller, flight plan including aircraft color if known, last transmission details, last position report, beacon code, action taken and proposed action, persons on board, fuel status, working facility and frequency, last known and estimated present position with maximum range of flight, nearby aircraft positions when requested, ELT information, and other pertinent information.

choices.text: Alert the RCC and forward the available information set, including details such as beacon code, action taken, fuel status, and last known position, instead of waiting to see what the RCC asks for later.
Send only the flight plan and last frequency first because the RCC does not need operational details until a later follow-up call.
Wait until the terminal facility confirms the aircraft is also overdue there before alerting the RCC, because ARTCC notification alone is not enough.
Forward only the last known position and hold back fuel, persons on board, and ELT information because those are search-team issues rather than RCC information.

items.explanation: Per 10-3-3, the ARTCC alerts the RCC and forwards the available information listed in the paragraph, not just a minimal subset.

items.situation: An aircraft is overdue and the center is preparing an ALNOT. Another controller wants to issue it only to the destination center, says the RCC can start SAR before the ALNOT goes out, and does not plan to amend the alert later if new position data is found.
items.para_context: Under 10-3-4, route the ALNOT to the regional office operations center and issue it to all centers and Area B circuits generally 50 miles on either side of the route of flight from last reported position to destination, or farther if the RCC recommends or if you decide to cover the maximum range. An ALNOT must be issued before the RCC can begin search and rescue procedures. Include helpful information such as last known position, time, and beacon code, keep the alert active, respond to INREQ/ALNOT checks within one hour, and amend the ALNOT when new information is obtained.

choices.text: Issue the ALNOT to the required distribution, include the helpful position and beacon details, understand that the ALNOT precedes RCC SAR action, and amend the alert when new information becomes available.
Send the ALNOT only to the destination center because the wider Area B and route distribution is optional unless the aircraft is military.
Let the RCC begin SAR first and issue the ALNOT later, because the alert is only an administrative follow-up once the search starts.
