Retain the original ALNOT unchanged even if better position data arrives, because amendments complicate the search record.

items.explanation: Per 10-3-4, the ALNOT goes to the required route and circuit distribution, precedes RCC SAR action, remains active, and is amended when new information that will help SAR becomes available.

items.situation: The center has been conducting the initial overdue-aircraft search. The aircraft has not been located within one hour after ALNOT issuance. Another controller says the center should keep sole responsibility indefinitely unless fuel exhaustion time has also passed by at least 30 minutes.
items.para_context: Under 10-3-5, transfer responsibility for further search to the RCC when any one of the following occurs: 30 minutes have elapsed after estimated aircraft fuel exhaustion time, the aircraft has not been located within one hour after ALNOT issuance, or the ALNOT search has been completed with negative results.

choices.text: Transfer responsibility for further search to the RCC because any one of the listed triggers, including one hour after ALNOT issuance without locating the aircraft, is enough.
Keep responsibility at the center until all three transfer conditions have occurred, because the RCC takes over only after every threshold is met.
Wait for fuel exhaustion plus 30 minutes in every case, because that is the only transfer trigger that matters.
Transfer responsibility only if the RCC specifically requests it, because the listed triggers are advisory rather than mandatory.

items.explanation: Per 10-3-5, responsibility transfers to the RCC when any one of the listed conditions occurs.

items.situation: Search personnel need the last known position of an aircraft in distress. Another controller wants to delay all reporting until a formal radar-data extraction is complete, even though an approximate latitude and longitude could be produced immediately from other available methods.
items.para_context: Under 10-3-6, provide the most accurate latitude and longitude available using en route and terminal radar sensor data near the aircraft's last known position. If necessary to prevent undue delay, use any available method to determine the initial latitude and longitude, then follow up as soon as possible with a formal latitude and longitude using the appropriate data extraction tools. If available, solicit the assistance of other aircraft near the aircraft in distress and forward the information to the RCC or ARTCC as appropriate.

choices.text: Provide the best available initial latitude and longitude without undue delay, follow up as soon as possible with the formal coordinates, use nearby aircraft assistance if available, and forward the information to the RCC or ARTCC as appropriate.
Wait for the formal extraction every time, because approximate coordinates may never be forwarded during the initial SAR phase.
Use only terminal radar sources and ignore en route data or nearby aircraft because mixed-source position work is not authorized.
Hold the coordinates at the center until the RCC specifically requests them, because unsolicited position forwarding can interfere with SAR planning.

items.explanation: Per 10-3-6, provide the most accurate latitude and longitude available, avoid undue delay by using an initial available method if necessary, follow up with the formal coordinates, and forward the information to the RCC or ARTCC as appropriate.

items.situation: The RCC advises that the overdue aircraft has been found. Another controller wants to cancel the ALNOT immediately with a short message that only says the search is over, without including where the aircraft or wreckage was located.
items.para_context: Under 10-3-7, cancel the ALNOT when directed by the RCC after the aircraft is located or the search is abandoned, and include pertinent information in the cancellation that will aid the RCC, SAR teams, and FAA SAR management, including the location where the aircraft or wreckage was found.

choices.text: Cancel the ALNOT when directed by the RCC and include the pertinent search information, including where the aircraft or wreckage was found.
Cancel the ALNOT immediately on your own authority with a brief note that the search is over, because the RCC already knows the rest.
Leave the ALNOT active until the destination airport closes the flight plan, because RCC direction is not enough to cancel it.
Cancel the ALNOT but omit the location details, because SAR teams do not need that information in the cancellation message.

items.explanation: Per 10-3-7, cancel the ALNOT when directed by the RCC and include pertinent information that will aid SAR, including where the aircraft or wreckage was found.

items.situation: An IFR aircraft becomes overdue after receiving an approach clearance. Another controller says you can keep normal IFR traffic flowing around the destination even though radar separation will not be used, because traffic restrictions only apply once fuel exhaustion time is reached.
items.para_context: Under 10-4-1, IFR traffic that could be affected by an overdue or unreported aircraft must be restricted or suspended unless radar separation is used. The responsible facility applies the restriction or suspension for 30 minutes following the applicable trigger time, such as approach clearance time, EFC time, arrival time over the serving NAVAID, the later current estimate at specified fixes and destination, or release time and any clearance void time.

choices.text: Restrict or suspend the affected IFR traffic for 30 minutes from the applicable trigger time unless radar separation is being used.
Keep all IFR traffic normal because overdue-aircraft restrictions begin only after fuel exhaustion time has passed.
Apply restrictions only if the pilot was VFR or SVFR, because IFR aircraft are protected by the flight plan alone.
Wait until the RCC requests a traffic stop before restricting the airspace, because ATC does not initiate the restriction independently.

items.explanation: Per 10-4-1, the responsible facility must restrict or suspend IFR traffic that could be affected by the overdue or unreported aircraft unless radar separation is used, and the restriction lasts 30 minutes from the applicable listed time.

items.situation: An unreported aircraft is approaching a nontowered airport at night. Another controller wants to wait until the ETA passes before asking anyone to turn on the lights, and says the lights can be shut off as soon as the estimated fuel exhaustion time is reached.
items.para_context: Under 10-4-2, at en route nontower or non-FSS locations, request airport management to light runway, approach, and other required lighting systems at least 30 minutes before the ETA of the unreported aircraft until the aircraft is located or for 30 minutes after its fuel supply is estimated to be exhausted. In terminal environments, operate those lighting systems for the same time period.

choices.text: Have the required airport lighting turned on at least 30 minutes before ETA and keep it on until the aircraft is located or for 30 minutes after estimated fuel exhaustion.
Wait until the ETA has passed before turning on the lights, because the lighting requirement begins only after the aircraft is officially overdue at the airport.
Turn on only the runway edge lights, because approach lights and other required systems are optional during overdue-aircraft events.
Shut the lights off exactly at estimated fuel exhaustion even if the aircraft is still unlocated, because the extra 30-minute period applies only to towered airports.

items.explanation: Per 10-4-2, the required airport lighting is provided beginning at least 30 minutes before ETA and remains on until the aircraft is located or for 30 minutes after estimated fuel exhaustion.

items.situation: The 30-minute traffic suspension period for an overdue aircraft has expired. Another controller wants to resume normal traffic immediately without asking other operators or pilots, and does not intend to keep any record of concurrence once traffic starts moving again.
items.para_context: Under 10-4-3, after the 30-minute traffic suspension period has expired, normal ATC may resume if the operators or pilots of other aircraft concur, and that concurrence must be maintained for 30 minutes after the suspension period has expired.

choices.text: Resume normal ATC only if the operators or pilots of other aircraft concur, and maintain that concurrence for the next 30 minutes.
Resume traffic automatically once the 30-minute suspension period ends, because concurrence is only recommended and does not need to be maintained.
Resume traffic only after the RCC formally cancels all search activity, because pilot or operator concurrence is never enough.
Require concurrence for the entire suspension period before any restriction begins, because the rule does not apply after resumption.

items.explanation: Per 10-4-3, after the 30-minute suspension period expires, normal ATC resumes only with concurrence of other operators or pilots, and that concurrence must continue for 30 minutes after expiration.

items.situation: You lose two-way radio communications with an IFR aircraft under your control jurisdiction. Another controller wants to stop after waiting for Code 7600, saying there is no reason to try emergency frequencies, voice-capable NAVAIDs, relays, or transponder/turn acknowledgments unless the pilot first makes contact.
items.para_context: Under 10-4-4, use all appropriate means available to reestablish communications, including emergency frequencies, voice-capable NAVAIDs, FSS, New York Radio, San Francisco Radio, ACARS/SELCAL when suitable, broadcast clearances through any available means, and attempt acknowledgment through transponder replies or turns. If communications are not reestablished after 5 minutes, consider the activity suspicious and report it to the OS/CIC.

choices.text: Use all appropriate means to reestablish communications, including relays and transponder/turn acknowledgments, and if still not reestablished after 5 minutes, treat the activity as suspicious and report it to the OS/CIC.
Wait for the pilot to select Code 7600 and then take no further action unless the aircraft declares an emergency.
Use only emergency frequency 121.5 because voice NAVAIDs, FSS relays, and radio networks are outside normal ATC tools.
Delay all follow-up action until at least 30 minutes have elapsed, because suspicious-activity reporting does not apply to lost communications.

items.explanation: Per 10-4-4, controllers use all appropriate means to reestablish communications, may use transponder or turn acknowledgments, and report suspicious activity to the OS/CIC if communications have not been reestablished after 5 minutes.

items.situation: A pilot advises an emergency landing will be made with explosive cargo aboard. Another controller wants to clear the aircraft to the first available runway and notify only the crash phone, saying airport management and any military coordination can wait until after landing.
items.para_context: Under 10-5-1, inform the pilot of the safest or least congested airport areas and relay the explosive-cargo information to the emergency equipment crew, airport management, and appropriate military agencies when requested by the pilot.

choices.text: Inform the pilot of the safest or least congested airport areas and relay the explosive-cargo information to the emergency equipment crew, airport management, and any appropriate military agency requested by the pilot.
Advise only the emergency equipment crew, because airport management and military agencies are not part of the required notification chain.
Keep the aircraft away from all airport areas and withhold any location recommendation until the pilot lands on whatever runway is available.
Wait until after touchdown before passing the explosive-cargo information, because prelanding dissemination could distract support personnel.

items.explanation: Per 10-5-1, advise the safest or least congested airport areas and relay the explosive-cargo information to the required responders and agencies.

items.situation: A debris response area has just been activated for a space vehicle mishap. You have airborne aircraft already inside the DRA, more departures ready from an airport under the area, and another controller wants to hold the arrivals outside while continuing normal departures because the debris warning has already been broadcast.
items.para_context: Under 10-5-2, a debris-generating space launch or reentry vehicle mishap is an emergency situation in the NAS. Issue alert broadcasts to affected aircraft, prevent aircraft from entering an activated DRA unless a higher priority duty exists, assist airborne aircraft already inside to exit expeditiously, do not withhold landing or approach clearances, and for airports underlying an active DRA do not issue departure releases or takeoff clearances and, to the extent possible, do not clear aircraft onto the movement area.

choices.text: Keep aircraft from entering the DRA, help aircraft already inside exit expeditiously, do not withhold landing or approach clearances, and stop departure releases and takeoff clearances for airports underlying the active DRA.
Continue normal departures because the alert broadcast satisfies the ATC response, and hold arrivals outside until the DRA is canceled.
Vector all aircraft deeper into the DRA to maintain orderly flow, because debris avoidance is the pilots' responsibility once notified.
Withhold all landing clearances for aircraft already inside the DRA until every departure has been stopped and the area is empty.

items.explanation: Per 10-5-2, a DRA activation requires protecting aircraft from entering, expediting aircraft out of the area, continuing landing/approach clearances as specified, and stopping departures from airports that underlie the active DRA.

items.situation: A domestic sector controller wants to use the procedures in Section 6 for a nonoceanic operation because the emergency checklist looks similar and would be convenient.
items.para_context: Under 10-6-1, the procedures in this section are to be used solely in oceanic airspace.

choices.text: Do not apply the Section 6 procedures there; they are used solely in oceanic airspace.
Use the Section 6 procedures anywhere in the NAS whenever they seem operationally useful.
Apply the Section 6 procedures only in Class A airspace, because oceanic is not the limiting factor.
Use the Section 6 procedures only for VFR aircraft, because IFR flights stay under the domestic emergency rules.

items.explanation: Per 10-6-1, the procedures in Section 6 are used solely in oceanic airspace.

items.situation: An oceanic aircraft has not been heard from for 35 minutes after a report should have been received. No other information suggests fuel exhaustion, forced landing, or severe impairment. Another controller wants to jump immediately to DETRESFA because any missed oceanic report should be treated as grave and imminent danger.
items.para_context: Under 10-6-2, an INCERFA exists when communication has not been received within 30 minutes after it should have been received or when an aircraft fails to arrive within 30 minutes after the latest estimated arrival time. ALERFA and DETRESFA apply only when the higher thresholds or more serious facts in the paragraph are present.

choices.text: Treat the situation as INCERFA because the 30-minute communication threshold has been crossed, but the facts do not yet establish ALERFA or DETRESFA.
Declare DETRESFA immediately because every missed oceanic communication report means grave and imminent danger.
Wait until 60 minutes have passed before recognizing any emergency phase at all.
Treat it as ALERFA solely because communications were missed by more than 30 minutes, regardless of the phase definitions.

items.explanation: Per 10-6-2, the 30-minute missed-communication threshold establishes INCERFA absent the additional facts that would support ALERFA or DETRESFA.

items.situation: An aircraft in oceanic operations becomes subject to alerting service just as it approaches the boundary of the next FIR. Another controller wants the destination FIR to take the entire coordination burden immediately because the destination is already known, while nobody has yet notified the RCC or the operator.
items.para_context: Under 10-6-3, unless otherwise established by LOA, alerting-service coordination rests with the facility serving the FIR or CTA within which the aircraft was flying at last air-ground contact, or which the aircraft was about to enter if last contact was at or close to the boundary. The responsible ACC serves as the control point for collecting information, forwarding it to the RCC, and coordinating with other facilities, and it notifies the listed agencies in the applicable emergency phases.

choices.text: Have the responsible FIR/CTA facility coordinate the alerting service under the paragraph criteria, with the ACC acting as the control point to collect information, notify the RCC and other required parties, and coordinate with affected facilities.
Shift responsibility automatically to the destination FIR the moment destination is known, because boundary and last-contact rules no longer matter once the route is established.
Wait to notify the RCC until the concerned aircraft actually lands or fuel exhaustion is confirmed, because only DETRESFA requires outside notification.
Let each ACC notify only its own internal watch desk, because the paragraph does not assign a single control point for the emergency.

items.explanation: Per 10-6-3, the responsible FIR/CTA facility coordinates the alerting service unless otherwise provided by LOA, and the responsible ACC is the control point for information collection, RCC forwarding, and interfacility coordination.

items.instruction: The controller issued this transmission. Which pilot acknowledgment is correct?
items.clearance: ATC ADVISES N123AB UNABLE TO APPROVE UNRESTRICTED DESCENT. TRAFFIC TWELVE O'CLOCK, ONE FIVE MILES, OPPOSITE DIRECTION, FLIGHT LEVEL TWO NINER ZERO. REQUEST INTENTIONS.

choices.text: Unable unrestricted descent, traffic copied, requesting lower after traffic, N123AB.
Unrestricted descent approved, leaving flight level three five zero now, N123AB.
Traffic behind us, descending immediately to flight level two zero zero, N123AB.
No traffic factor, cancel emergency descent, N123AB.

items.explanation: Per 10-6-4c1, if traffic prevents an unrestricted descent, ATC advises the aircraft it cannot approve the unrestricted descent, gives traffic, and requests intentions.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: ATC ADVISES ALL AIRCRAFT BE ALERT FOR EMERGENCY DESCENT IN THE VICINITY OF FOUR ZERO NORTH FIVE ZERO WEST FROM FLIGHT LEVEL THREE FIVE ZERO TO FLIGHT LEVEL ONE ZERO ZERO.
items.display_text: ATC ADVISES ALL AIRCRAFT BE ALERT FOR EMERGENCY CLIMB IN THE VICINITY OF FOUR ZERO NORTH FIVE ZERO WEST FROM FLIGHT LEVEL THREE FIVE ZERO TO FLIGHT LEVEL ONE ZERO ZERO
items.explanation: Per 10-6-4c2, the emergency broadcast warns other aircraft to be alert for an emergency descent in the vicinity.

items.situation: An aircraft begins an emergency descent through other traffic in oceanic airspace without waiting for a clearance. Another controller wants to wait until the descent is complete before telling anyone else, because the descending aircraft is already committed and broadcasts would only create frequency congestion.
items.para_context: Under 10-6-4, take action immediately to safeguard all aircraft concerned. Advise other aircraft of the emergency descent, broadcast an emergency message to aircraft in the vicinity through available means, include the listed information, provide traffic information if conditions permit, and issue appropriate clearances or instructions as necessary. For ditching, the RCC determines the airspace to be blocked and the ACC blocks it until released.

choices.text: Act immediately to safeguard all aircraft, advise other aircraft of the emergency descent, broadcast the emergency information through available means, provide traffic information if possible, and issue necessary clearances or instructions.
Wait until the descent is complete before alerting nearby aircraft, because the emergency aircraft's maneuvering takes priority over all warnings.
Limit your action to the emergency aircraft only, because surrounding traffic is expected to remain clear on its own in oceanic airspace.
Issue no traffic or emergency broadcast unless the RCC first approves the message content.

items.explanation: Per 10-6-4c2 through c6, controllers immediately protect other aircraft when an emergency descent occurs, using traffic advisories, emergency broadcasts, and necessary clearances or instructions.

items.situation: A SAR aircraft is trying to assist a distressed aircraft in oceanic airspace. Another controller wants to clear the SAR aircraft directly to the distressed aircraft as the clearance limit and continue full IFR separation even after the SAR crew has visual contact and both crews agree they can work visually.
items.para_context: Under 10-6-5, provide IFR separation between the SAR aircraft and the aircraft in distress unless visual or radar contact has been established by the search and rescue aircraft and both pilots concur, in which case IFR separation may be discontinued. Clear the SAR aircraft to a fixed clearance limit rather than to the moving distressed aircraft, issue route clearances consistent with the distressed aircraft, advise the rescue aircraft of adverse factors, and keep agencies, operator, and aircraft informed.

choices.text: Clear the SAR aircraft to a fixed clearance limit, keep its route consistent with the distressed aircraft, and discontinue IFR separation only if the SAR aircraft has visual or radar contact and both pilots concur.
Clear the SAR aircraft directly to the distressed aircraft as the moving clearance limit, because rescue missions are exempt from normal clearance-limit rules.
Maintain IFR separation in every case even after visual or radar contact and pilot concurrence, because rescue operations may never discontinue IFR separation.
Ignore route consistency with the distressed aircraft, because only the RCC determines how the SAR aircraft should navigate.

items.explanation: Per 10-6-5, the SAR aircraft is cleared to a fixed clearance limit and IFR separation may be discontinued only when the stated contact-and-concurrence conditions are met.

items.situation: You receive word of a ground missile emergency affecting a mixed en route and terminal environment. Another controller wants to notify only the local tower and let each adjacent facility discover the rest from the NOTAM once it is published.
items.para_context: Under 10-7-1, notify other concerned facilities and take action to have alerting advisories issued by air carrier company radio stations for VFR company aircraft in the vicinity, by adjacent FSSs, and in terminal environments relay all information to the ARTCC within whose area the emergency exists and disseminate it as a NOTAM.

choices.text: Notify the other concerned facilities and ensure the listed en route and terminal alerting actions occur, including company radio, adjacent FSS, ARTCC relay, and NOTAM dissemination as applicable.
Notify only the local tower because the NOTAM system will automatically alert company radio stations, adjacent FSSs, and the ARTCC without further ATC action.
Limit the notification to IFR aircraft because VFR company aircraft are outside the required alerting advisory chain.
Wait until the emergency is over before relaying it to the ARTCC, because missile-emergency information is handled only after confirmation of impact area size.

items.explanation: Per 10-7-1, controllers notify the other concerned facilities and use the specified relay and alerting channels for ground missile emergencies.

items.situation: An IFR aircraft and an SVFR helicopter are both likely to pass near a ground missile emergency. Another controller wants to keep them on course as long as they remain a few hundred feet clear of the site, because lateral deviation would be more disruptive than vertical spacing.
items.para_context: Under 10-7-2, reroute IFR and SVFR aircraft as necessary to avoid the emergency location by at least 1 mile laterally or 6,000 feet vertically above the surface over the emergency location, or by greater minima when suggested by the notifying official.

choices.text: Reroute the IFR and SVFR aircraft to avoid the emergency location by at least 1 mile laterally or 6,000 feet vertically, or by greater minima if the notifying official suggests it.
Keep them on course as long as they remain a few hundred feet above the site, because missile-emergency routing does not require fixed minima.
Apply the minima only to IFR aircraft, because SVFR flights are handled under the VFR guidance in every case.
Use whichever distance is easiest operationally, even if it is less than 1 mile lateral and less than 6,000 feet vertical.

items.explanation: Per 10-7-2, IFR and SVFR aircraft are rerouted to avoid the emergency location by the stated lateral or vertical minima, or greater minima when directed.

items.situation: Several VFR aircraft are known to be operating near a ground missile emergency. Another controller wants to mention the emergency only if the pilots ask, because the aircraft are not receiving IFR or SVFR handling.
items.para_context: Under 10-7-3, advise all known VFR aircraft that are, or will be, operating in the vicinity of a ground missile emergency to avoid the emergency location by 1 mile laterally or 6,000 feet vertically, or by greater distance or altitude when suggested by the notifying official.

choices.text: Advise all known VFR aircraft in the vicinity to avoid the emergency location by 1 mile laterally or 6,000 feet vertically, or by greater limits if suggested by the notifying official.
Wait for VFR pilots to ask before mentioning the emergency, because avoidance advisories are optional unless the flight is IFR or SVFR.
Advise VFR aircraft only to remain 500 feet from the site because no specific minima apply to visual flights.
Limit the advisory to aircraft already in direct radio contact with the notifying official rather than issuing ATC avoidance guidance.

items.explanation: Per 10-7-3, all known VFR aircraft in the vicinity are advised to avoid the emergency location by the listed minima, or greater limits when specified.

items.situation: Controllers can see a smoke column near the ground missile emergency area. Another controller says there is no need to mention it because the aircraft are already being kept away from the emergency location itself.
items.para_context: Under 10-7-4, advise all aircraft to avoid any observed smoke columns in the vicinity of a ground missile emergency.

choices.text: Advise all aircraft to avoid the observed smoke columns in the vicinity of the ground missile emergency.
Say nothing about the smoke columns because keeping aircraft away from the emergency location automatically covers that hazard.
Advise only IFR aircraft about the smoke columns and leave VFR aircraft to visually avoid them on their own.
Issue the smoke-column advisory only if pilots specifically report reduced visibility first.

items.explanation: Per 10-7-4, controllers advise all aircraft to avoid observed smoke columns near a ground missile emergency.

items.situation: Early reports suggest a ground missile emergency will remain active for an extended period. Another controller wants to avoid any broader notification product and handle the event only with frequency advisories because the local sectors already know about it.
items.para_context: Under 10-7-5, when reports indicate that a ground missile emergency will exist for an extended period of time, a Notice to Airmen may be issued.

choices.text: Consider issuing a NOTAM because extended-duration reports support broader published notification of the emergency.
Avoid issuing a NOTAM because missile-emergency information must stay only on ATC frequencies for as long as the event lasts.
Wait until the emergency ends before deciding whether a NOTAM should have been issued.
Issue a NOTAM only if the event is already affecting IFR aircraft, because duration alone is not a consideration.

items.explanation: Per 10-7-5, when reports indicate the emergency will exist for an extended period, a NOTAM may be issued.

items.situation: Traffic demand is increasing and one supervisor wants to default to a miles-in-trail program even though TBFM tools are available and functioning. The justification is that MIT is simpler and the traffic management mission is mainly to slow demand rather than balance demand with capacity.
items.para_context: Under 11-1-1, the traffic management system mission is to balance air traffic demand with system capacity for maximum efficient utilization of the NAS. TBFM must be used to the maximum extent feasible in preference to miles-in-trail initiatives, and the ATCS is integral to execution of the traffic management mission.

choices.text: Use TBFM to the maximum extent feasible in preference to MIT and apply traffic management in a way that balances demand with capacity for efficient NAS use.
Default to MIT whenever demand rises because TBFM is only optional decision support and the traffic management mission is primarily to reduce traffic volume.
Leave the decision entirely to traffic management personnel because ATCS personnel are not part of the traffic management mission.
Use TBFM only after all MIT options have failed, because MIT is the preferred initiative whenever both are available.

items.explanation: Per 11-1-1, traffic management balances demand with capacity, TBFM is preferred to MIT when feasible, and ATCS personnel are integral to execution of the mission.

items.situation: A day-shift STMCIC says there is no need for the required operational briefing because everyone can read the weather and runway status separately, and the TMU can sort out TBM changes later if congestion develops.
items.para_context: Under 11-1-2, the STMCIC must ensure an operational briefing is conducted at least once during the day and evening shifts with the required participants and operational topics, ensure TBM operations and TMIs are carried out and implemented in a timely manner, and the OS/CIC and air traffic personnel must keep TMU and affected sectors apprised, coordinate TBM/TMIs, and review them continuously.

choices.text: Conduct the required operational briefing with the listed participants and operational topics, and ensure TBM operations or TMIs are coordinated and implemented in a timely manner.
Skip the briefing because traffic management discussions may be handled informally throughout the shift without a required coordinated session.
Wait for congestion to materialize before sharing staffing, runway, equipment, or AAR information, because those items are not part of the required briefing content.
Leave all TMI review and revision decisions to individual sectors because OS/CIC and TMU coordination is not a required part of this paragraph.

items.explanation: Per 11-1-2, the STMCIC conducts the required operational briefing and ensures TBM/TMIs are carried out and implemented promptly, while OS/CIC and air traffic personnel coordinate continuously with TMU.

items.situation: During metering, a sector sees large jumps in the TBFM delay countdown timer for a pair of aircraft. Another controller wants to stop displaying the schedule information and ignore the metering times entirely, arguing that once the countdown becomes unstable there is no need to coordinate with TMU or adjacent sectors.
items.para_context: Under 11-1-3, personnel providing air traffic services must display TBFM schedule information, comply with TBFM-generated metering times within plus or minus 1 minute, and when significant DCT jumps prevent that accuracy for specific aircraft, TMIs such as MIT or MINIT may be used to absorb delay until stability resumes. When compliance is not possible, coordinate with OS/CIC, traffic management personnel, and adjacent facilities or sectors as appropriate.

choices.text: Continue displaying the schedule information, use TMIs such as MIT or MINIT for the affected aircraft if the unstable DCT prevents plus-or-minus-1-minute accuracy, and coordinate with OS/CIC, TMU, and adjacent facilities as needed.
Stop using the TBFM schedule and ignore metering times entirely once the DCT becomes unstable, because no coordination is required after that point.
Apply any amount of metering-time error that seems workable, because the plus-or-minus-1-minute standard is only advisory during all metering periods.
Use MIT or MINIT across the entire stream whenever metering is in effect, because they always replace TBFM-generated metering times rather than support them.

items.explanation: Per 11-1-3, schedule information remains displayed, metering times are complied with within plus or minus 1 minute when possible, TMIs may support specific unstable aircraft, and coordination is required when compliance cannot be achieved.

items.situation: You are working airspace in Canada where control responsibility has been formally delegated to the FAA. Another controller wants to treat the area exactly like domestic FAA airspace and ignore the chapter-specific Canadian procedures because the delegation means only U.S. procedures apply.
items.para_context: Under 12-1-1, where control responsibility within Canadian airspace has been formally delegated to the FAA by Transport Canada Aviation Group, apply basic FAA procedures except for the Canadian procedures contained in Chapter 12.

choices.text: Apply basic FAA procedures, but also use the Canadian procedures contained in Chapter 12 where they apply.
Apply only domestic FAA procedures because delegation removes the need to use any Canadian-specific procedures.
Apply only Canadian procedures and ignore basic FAA procedures while operating in delegated airspace.
Wait for case-by-case approval from Transport Canada before using any FAA procedure in delegated airspace.

items.explanation: Per 12-1-1, FAA controllers use basic FAA procedures in delegated Canadian airspace except for the Canadian procedures specifically contained in Chapter 12.

items.situation: A VFR pilot wants to enter Canadian Class C airspace. Another controller says the pilot only needs to establish two-way communications before entering because that is the standard requirement for all controlled VFR entry in Canada.
items.para_context: Under 12-1-2, in Class C airspace both IFR and VFR flights are permitted, but VFR flights require an ATC clearance to enter. In Class D airspace, VFR flights do not require a clearance, but they must establish two-way communications before entry. In Class B airspace, only IFR and CVFR are permitted and IFR procedures apply to CVFR aircraft.

choices.text: Require the VFR aircraft to obtain an ATC clearance before entering Class C airspace.
Allow entry on two-way communications alone because Class C and Class D have the same VFR entry rule.
Deny VFR entry categorically because only IFR and CVFR flights are permitted in all Canadian controlled airspace.
Treat the request as Class E entry and allow it with no special requirement at all.

items.explanation: Per 12-1-2, VFR aircraft need an ATC clearance to enter Class C, while two-way communications alone apply to VFR entry into Class D.

items.situation: A pilot requests 'at least 1,000 feet-on-top' while planning to continue into Canadian Class B airspace. Another controller wants to approve it because the pilot asked for it and says the pilot is responsible for the weather conditions anyway.
items.para_context: Under 12-1-3, clear an aircraft to maintain at least 1,000 feet-on-top in lieu of VFR-on-top only if the pilot requests it and the pilot will not operate within Class A or Class B airspace. The pilot is responsible for remaining at least 1,000 feet above all cloud, haze, smoke, or other formation with 3 miles or more of visibility.

choices.text: Do not issue the 1,000-feet-on-top clearance if the aircraft will operate within Class A or Class B airspace.
Approve it because the pilot requested it and the pilot's weather responsibility removes the Class B restriction.
Issue it automatically in lieu of any altitude assignment whenever the pilot mentions visibility is acceptable.
Treat it exactly like VFR-on-top and allow it in Class A or B as long as the pilot remains above clouds.

items.explanation: Per 12-1-3, at least 1,000 feet-on-top may be used only when requested by the pilot and only if the pilot will not operate within Class A or Class B airspace.

items.situation: Two aircraft are operating in excellent visual conditions in Canadian airspace, one under an IFR clearance and one under a CVFR clearance. Another controller wants to reduce the standard separation because both crews report the traffic in sight and the weather is good.
items.para_context: Under 12-1-4, apply a lateral, longitudinal, or vertical separation minimum between aircraft operating in accordance with an IFR or CVFR clearance, regardless of the weather conditions.

choices.text: Apply an approved lateral, longitudinal, or vertical separation minimum between the IFR and CVFR aircraft regardless of the weather.
Reduce separation because good weather removes the requirement between IFR and CVFR aircraft.
Apply separation only if both aircraft are IFR, because CVFR is treated as ordinary VFR for separation purposes.
Use only visual avoidance responsibility and no ATC separation whenever one aircraft is CVFR.

items.explanation: Per 12-1-4, separation minima are applied between aircraft operating under IFR or CVFR clearances regardless of the weather conditions.

items.situation: A turbine-powered aircraft loses communications in delegated Canadian airspace after departure and was cleared to a point other than its destination. Another controller wants to hold the aircraft at the clearance-limit altitude indefinitely until the destination is reached because that is how they are treating the radio-failure plan.
items.para_context: Under 12-1-5, unless ATC has issued alternate radio-failure instructions, a turbine-powered aircraft cleared on departure to a point other than the destination proceeds to the destination airport in accordance with the flight plan, maintains the last assigned altitude/flight level or the MEA, whichever is higher, until 10 minutes beyond the clearance limit, then proceeds at the altitude(s) or flight level(s) filed. If the aircraft will enter U.S. airspace within 10 minutes after the clearance limit, the climb to the flight-planned border-crossing altitude begins at the estimated Canada/U.S. boundary crossing time.

choices.text: Plan control actions on the aircraft maintaining the last assigned altitude or MEA, whichever is higher, until 10 minutes beyond the clearance limit and then proceeding at the filed altitude profile, with the border-crossing climb handled as specified if it will enter U.S. airspace within that 10-minute period.
Assume the aircraft will remain indefinitely at the clearance-limit altitude all the way to destination because radio-failure rules do not authorize any later climb or profile change.
Assume the aircraft immediately climbs to all filed altitudes as soon as it passes the clearance limit, because there is no 10-minute hold requirement in Canadian airspace.
Ignore the Canadian rule entirely and apply only domestic U.S. radio-failure expectations because the aircraft will eventually enter U.S. airspace.

items.explanation: Per 12-1-5, the major Canadian difference for turbine departures is the 10-minute continuation beyond the clearance limit before proceeding at filed altitudes, with special handling for border-crossing altitude if the aircraft enters U.S. airspace within that period.

items.situation: A pilot requests parachute-jump approval in delegated Canadian airspace, but no prior permission from the appropriate Canadian authority has been obtained. Another controller wants to approve it locally because the operation appears straightforward and the jump aircraft is already airborne.
items.para_context: Under 12-1-6, do not authorize parachute jumping without prior permission from the appropriate Canadian authority. The note states Canadian regulations require written authority from the Ministry of Transport.

choices.text: Do not authorize the parachute jumping unless prior permission from the appropriate Canadian authority has been obtained.
Approve the jump locally if the pilot accepts responsibility, because Canadian authority is only needed for complex operations.
Authorize the jump verbally and obtain the Canadian permission afterward if no conflicts develop.
Treat the request like a routine U.S. jump operation and ignore the Canadian written-authority requirement.

items.explanation: Per 12-1-6, parachute jumping is not authorized without prior permission from the appropriate Canadian authority.

items.situation: A pilot requests SVFR at night in a Canadian control zone with an airport controller on duty. Another controller says the request should be denied automatically because the pilot is not IFR qualified and the aircraft is not equipped for IFR flight.
items.para_context: Under 12-1-7, pilots do not need to be IFR qualified and the aircraft does not need to be IFR equipped to fly SVFR at night. With an airport controller on duty, approve or refuse SVFR based on current or anticipated IFR traffic only and, if approved, specify the period during which SVFR is permitted. Without an airport controller on duty, base the decision on IFR traffic and official ceiling and visibility reports. Weather minima are 1 mile for aircraft other than helicopters and 1/2 mile for helicopters.

choices.text: Do not deny the request solely for lack of IFR qualification or IFR equipment; with an airport controller on duty, decide on the basis of current or anticipated IFR traffic and specify the approved time period if you authorize SVFR.
Deny the request automatically because night SVFR in Canada requires an IFR-qualified pilot and an IFR-equipped aircraft.
Approve the request solely on the basis of ceiling and visibility, because IFR traffic is not part of the decision when the tower is staffed.
Approve the request without specifying a period of validity, because time limits apply only when no airport controller is on duty.

items.explanation: Per 12-1-7, night SVFR does not require IFR qualification or IFR equipment, and when an airport controller is on duty the approval decision is based on current or anticipated IFR traffic only, with a specified time period if approved.

items.situation: A sector team is evaluating a likely reroute around special use airspace. Another controller says EDST is only useful after the amended clearance has actually been issued to the aircraft, so the team should ignore the projected route for conflict analysis until then.
items.para_context: Under 13-1-1, EDST is an integrated function of ERAM used by the sector team in performing strategic planning responsibilities. It uses current plan data, forecast winds, aircraft performance characteristics, and track data to derive expected aircraft trajectories, predict conflicts between aircraft and with special use or designated airspace, and provide trial planning and enhanced flight data management capabilities. For EDST use, the current plan is what EAS predicts an aircraft will fly and may include clearances not yet issued to the aircraft.

choices.text: Use EDST for the strategic planning analysis because its current plan may include clearances not yet issued, and it is designed to model expected trajectories and detect conflicts with aircraft and airspace.
Ignore EDST until the pilot has been issued every planned amendment, because current plans may only reflect already-issued clearances.
Use EDST only for post-event review, because it is not intended for trajectory prediction or conflict planning.
Limit EDST use to weather depiction and avoid using it for special use airspace or aircraft-to-aircraft conflict analysis.

items.explanation: Per 13-1-1, EDST is a strategic planning tool that models expected trajectories and predicts conflicts using current plans, and those plans may include clearances that have not yet been issued.

items.situation: A conflict probe alert appears for two aircraft. A controller wants to wait until the aircraft are much closer before evaluating it, and another suggests transferring one aircraft with Stop Probe still active because the sector is busy and the alert logic is only advisory anyway.
items.para_context: Under 13-1-2, actively scan EDST information for predicted aircraft-to-aircraft and aircraft-to-airspace alerts. When a conflict probe alert is displayed, evaluate the alert and take appropriate action as early as practical in accordance with duty priorities, prioritizing safe, expeditious, and efficient traffic flow. When determining a solution, consider direct routing, altitude changes, removal of inappropriate flight direction constraints or static restrictions, and the effect on surrounding traffic, complexity, efficiencies, and user preferences. If Stop Probe has been activated for an aircraft, conflict probe must be restarted before transfer of control unless otherwise coordinated.

choices.text: Evaluate the alert as early as practical, work a solution that considers both conflict resolution and surrounding traffic effects, and restart conflict probe before transfer unless otherwise coordinated.
Delay all evaluation until the aircraft are nearly in conflict, because conflict probe is not meant for early controller action.
Transfer the aircraft with Stop Probe still active without coordination, because probe restart is optional when the sector is busy.
Treat the alert as a complete substitute for separation judgment, since conflict probe already accounts for every case requiring greater or reduced separation.

items.explanation: Per 13-1-2, conflict probe alerts must be scanned and acted on early as priorities permit, solutions should be evaluated in operational context, and Stop Probe must be restarted before transfer unless coordinated otherwise.

items.situation: EDST is operational and sector priorities permit. A user requests a direct route and a more efficient altitude, but another controller says trial planning should be saved only for actual conflict alerts and not used to test whether the request or removal of restrictions is feasible.
items.para_context: Under 13-1-3, when EDST is operational at the sector and when sector priorities permit, use the trial plan capability to evaluate solutions to predicted conflicts, the feasibility of granting user requests, the feasibility of removing a flight direction constraint, and the feasibility of removing a static restriction for an aircraft.

choices.text: Use the trial plan capability to evaluate the requested change and the feasibility of removing the relevant direction or static restrictions before deciding whether to approve it.
Do not use trial planning unless an actual conflict probe alert is already active, because user requests and restriction removal are outside its intended use.
Use trial planning only for altitude changes and never for direct-routing requests or removal of static restrictions.
Avoid trial planning whenever sector priorities permit, because it is intended only for low-priority review after the decision has already been made.

items.explanation: Per 13-1-3, when EDST is operational and priorities permit, trial planning is used to evaluate predicted-conflict solutions, user requests, removal of flight direction constraints, and removal of static restrictions.

items.situation: A user requests a route change. The trial plan shows no alerts, but the proposed change would likely complicate operations in the adjacent sector. Another controller says the absence of alerts means the request should be granted automatically with no further sector-impact evaluation.
items.para_context: Under 13-1-4, when the results of a trial plan based on a user request indicate the absence of alerts, every effort should be made to grant the user request unless the change is likely to adversely affect operations at another sector.

choices.text: Make every effort to grant the request, but you may decline or modify it if the change is likely to adversely affect operations at another sector.
Grant the request automatically whenever there are no alerts, regardless of likely effects on another sector.
Deny the request because user-request trial plans are advisory only and do not support granting route or altitude changes.
Ignore the trial plan results and decide solely on pilot preference, because sector impacts are outside the EDST-based clearance rule.

items.explanation: Per 13-1-4, no-alert trial plan results support granting the user's request, but not when the proposed change is likely to adversely affect another sector's operations.

items.situation: A sector team is working several flights with EDST available. One controller is relying on a separate handwritten list instead of the ACL, another sees a Remarks indication on an ACL entry but says the field can wait until later, and a third wants to leave a special mission mixed in with routine traffic rather than grouped anywhere visible.
items.para_context: Under 13-1-5, the ACL is the sector team's primary source of flight data, EDST notifications requiring action must be actively scanned, Remarks indications on the ACL or DL require the Remarks field and any changes to it to be reviewed, highlighting on the ACL or DL indicates the flight requires action or special attention, and the Special Posting Area should be used to group aircraft of special significance. Sector teams must also post strips for nonradar flights, for flight plans not contained in EAS, and whenever needed for safe or efficient operations.

choices.text: Use the ACL as the primary flight data source, review the indicated Remarks field and any changes to it, and use highlighting and the SPA as intended for flights requiring action or special significance.

Treat the ACL as optional if a controller has a working handwritten list, postpone all Remarks review until the aircraft checks on frequency, and leave special-significance flights mixed with routine traffic unless a pilot requests special handling.
Ignore the Remarks indication unless the DL also flags it, and reserve the SPA only for emergencies because sequencing and formation tracking do not belong there.
Use highlighting only after a loss of separation event and keep all flight progress strips off the position whenever ERAM is available for any part of the flight.

items.explanation: Per 13-1-5, ACL use, EDST scanning, Remarks review, highlighting, SPA grouping, and required strip posting are all part of the sector team's normal flight-data-management duties.

items.situation: You must coordinate a flight manually with a facility that has no automated coordination capability. The team uses the Coordination Menu to show the status, then later the flight plan is amended. Another controller wants to leave the old yellow Coordination Indicator in place because it shows there was once manual coordination.
items.para_context: Under 13-1-6, where automated coordination with a facility is not available, use the Coordination Menu or a flight progress strip to annotate manual coordination status in accordance with facility directives. If the Coordination Menu is used and the flight plan is subsequently changed, remove the yellow coding from the Coordination Indicator after appropriate action has been taken.

choices.text: Annotate the manual coordination with the Coordination Menu or a flight progress strip as directed, and after the amendment is handled remove the yellow coding from the Coordination Indicator.
Avoid using the Coordination Menu for manual coordination because it is reserved for automated facilities only, and leave the yellow coding in place permanently once it has been applied.
Delete all coordination evidence immediately when the flight plan changes, even before taking any appropriate follow-up action.
Use only verbal coordination and never annotate status on a strip or menu because manual coordination is not tracked in ERAM procedures.

items.explanation: Per 13-1-6, manual coordination status is annotated with the Coordination Menu or a strip when automation is unavailable, and yellow coordination coding is removed after the appropriate action is taken following a flight-plan change.

items.situation: A flight enters holding and the sector team needs to track the clearance. One controller says the hold only needs to be remembered verbally because EDST already shows the aircraft symbol, while another wants to use a worksheet that has not been approved by the facility.
items.para_context: Under 13-1-7, for flights in hold, use the Hold View, Hold Data Menu, hold message, a flight progress strip, or a facility-approved worksheet to annotate holding instructions, in accordance with facility directives.

choices.text: Annotate the holding instructions using an approved method such as Hold View, Hold Data Menu, a hold message, a flight progress strip, or a facility-approved worksheet, following facility directives.
Rely on memory and the aircraft target alone, because holding instructions do not require a formal annotation once the aircraft is already in the hold.
Use any unofficial worksheet a controller prefers, because facility approval is not relevant once the hold has been issued.
Record holding instructions only by voice on the interphone, because none of the EDST or strip-based tools are authorized for hold annotation.

items.explanation: Per 13-1-7, holding instructions must be annotated using one of the listed approved tools or methods and in accordance with facility directives.

items.situation: A controller enters control information in the ACL Free Text Area, then closes the area to save space and leaves outdated notes in place after the information is no longer relevant. During a handoff, another controller assumes that everything in the Free Text Area will automatically pass with the aircraft and therefore does not coordinate the information separately.
items.para_context: Under 13-1-8, control information not otherwise recorded by automation or voice must be manually recorded using approved methods. When the ACL or DL Free Text Area is used, authorized abbreviations, EDST-equivalent symbols, plain language when helpful, or locally approved abbreviations may be used. The Free Text Area must remain open and visible, outdated information must be updated or deleted when no longer relevant, and information there is for reference only; it does not pass on handoff and must be coordinated if needed.

choices.text: Keep the Free Text Area open and visible while it is being used, update or delete information when it is no longer relevant, and coordinate anything needed because Free Text entries are reference only and do not pass on handoff.
Close the Free Text Area once an entry is made and leave the notes in place indefinitely, because visibility and later cleanup are optional as long as the original controller understands the entry.
Assume all Free Text information transfers automatically with the handoff, so separate coordination is unnecessary.
Avoid manually recording any control information unless it appears in one of the tables, because plain language and locally approved abbreviations are never allowed.

items.explanation: Per 13-1-8, manually recorded control data must use approved methods, Free Text entries must stay visible and current, and that information is reference only and does not automatically pass on handoff.

items.situation: A controller wants to clear the display by removing IAFDOF and EDCT coding before the required coordination and pilot notification are complete. Later, the same controller wants to send ERT coding on a flight the sector has not yet taken track control for, because the route text is already visible on the display.
items.para_context: Under 13-1-9, IAFDOF Manual must remain deselected unless facility directives authorize otherwise, IAFDOF, UTM, and EDCT coding are acknowledged only after the appropriate action has been completed, and ERT or RAN actions may not be sent, acknowledged, or removed unless the sector has track control or has otherwise coordinated as required. The first sector displaying ERT must issue and send or acknowledge the route before handoff unless otherwise coordinated or directed.

choices.text: Leave the coding in place until the required action is complete, and do not send, acknowledge, or remove ERT or RAN coding unless the sector has track control or the action has otherwise been coordinated as required.
Remove coding as soon as the controller understands what action will eventually be taken, because acknowledgement is only a display-management step and does not depend on actual completion.
Send or acknowledge ERT whenever it first appears on the display, even if the sector lacks track control, because visibility of the route text is enough authority to act.
Select IAFDOF Manual as the normal operating mode unless a facility directive specifically requires automatic mode.

items.explanation: Per 13-1-9, automated notification coding is not acknowledged until the required action is done, and ERT or RAN actions require track control or other required coordination before the coding is handled.

items.situation: A controller wants to use the Graphic Plan Display to verify an aircraft's radar identification and to confirm altitude because the trajectory line and predicted altitude look reasonable on the screen.
items.para_context: Under 13-1-13, graphic depictions of flight trajectories may be used only to aid situational awareness and strategic planning. Trajectory-based positions are not a substitute for radar track position, trajectory-based altitude is not used in lieu of Mode C for altitude confirmation, and GPD is not used for radar identification, position information, transfer of radar identification, radar separation, correlation, or point outs.

choices.text: Use the GPD only for situational awareness and strategic planning, and continue to rely on radar track position and Mode C rather than trajectory data for identification or altitude confirmation.
Use the GPD trajectory as a substitute for radar track position whenever it appears stable, because strategic-planning tools may replace normal radar identification methods.
Use the trajectory-based altitude in place of Mode C when it looks reasonable, because the display is part of the same automation suite.
Use the GPD for point outs and correlation but not for radar separation, because only separation is specifically prohibited.

items.explanation: Per 13-1-13, GPD is limited to situational awareness and strategic planning and is not a substitute for radar track position, Mode C, or other radar functions.

items.situation: A controller says ATOP should be treated as a flight-data display only, and that the sector should not rely on it for automated decision support related to separation or airspace conflicts because those functions belong only to local radar automation.
items.para_context: Under 13-2-1, the ATOP ATC System is utilized in designated en route and oceanic airspace. It includes both surveillance and flight data processing and provides automated decision support tools to establish, monitor, and maintain separation between aircraft and between aircraft and airspace or terrain. ATOP capabilities include radar surveillance processing, conflict prediction and reporting, ADS-B, ADS-C, CPDLC, AIDC, weather deviation tools, other situational-awareness decision support tools, and electronic flight data including electronic flight strips.

choices.text: Use ATOP as the integrated surveillance, flight-data, and decision-support system it is designed to be in designated en route and oceanic airspace, including separation-support functions and electronic flight data tools.
Limit ATOP use to static flight-plan display because it does not include surveillance processing or automated decision support for separation.
Treat ATOP as useful only for CPDLC messaging and ignore its conflict prediction, surveillance, and flight-strip capabilities.
Use ATOP only in domestic terminal airspace because oceanic airspace relies on separate non-integrated tools for decision support.

items.explanation: Per 13-2-1, ATOP is the integrated en route and oceanic system that combines surveillance, flight-data processing, and decision-support tools for separation and situational awareness.

items.situation: A red conflict appears for aircraft that will conflict in your sector. Another controller wants to leave it alone because range and bearing tools suggest the aircraft might still work out, and they also want to manually accept another external transfer before entering the coordinated profile and initiating conflict probe on that aircraft.
items.para_context: Under 13-2-2, controllers must use the most accurate information available to initiate, monitor, and maintain separation. When probe is being used, controllers assume supported probe calculations are accurate, evaluate alerts and act as early as practical, and, unless otherwise specified in facility directives, take immediate action to resolve any red conflicts. Prior to manually accepting an external transfer, ensure the coordinated flight profile is accurately entered, conflict probe initiated, and action taken if necessary to resolve conflicts. Additional decision-support tools may support separation when probe is unavailable or the standard is unsupported, but may not be used to override probe when the applicable standard is supported and no override condition applies.

choices.text: Take immediate action on the red conflict unless facility directives specify otherwise, and do not manually accept the transfer until the coordinated profile is entered, conflict probe is initiated, and any needed conflict action has been taken.
Delay action on the red conflict if range and bearing suggest a possible solution, because additional tools may override probe whenever the controller considers them more persuasive.
Manually accept the external transfer first and enter the coordinated profile later, because probe initiation is not required until after the handoff is complete.
Treat supported conflict probe results as optional whenever the sector is busy, because operational pressure is itself a valid reason to override probe.

items.explanation: Per 13-2-2, red conflicts require prompt resolution, manual transfers require an accurate profile and initiated probe first, and additional tools do not override supported probe results unless an authorized override condition exists.

items.situation: Manually entered data on a flight has become stale, nonconformant and error-queue messages are stacking up, and an aircraft that reported degraded navigation capability still does not show the proper reduced-separation flag. On top of that, another window is covering the ASD title bar and a teammate says that can wait because the aircraft targets are still visible.
items.para_context: Under 13-2-3, the sector team must ensure manually entered data is accurate and timely, handle nonconformant messages promptly, and update flight profiles as needed. Data blocks must reflect current flight information and special condition codes require timely action. Electronic flight strips and reduced-separation flags must be kept current, including the Degraded RNP selection when reduced navigation capability affects the applicable minima. Sector and coordination queues, including the error queue, must be managed in accordance with message priority and facility directives. The ASD title bar must not be obscured, and designated windows or lists must remain displayed as required.

choices.text: Update the stale data and flight profile promptly, process the nonconformant and error-queue messages in priority order, apply the appropriate strip flags such as Degraded RNP, and restore the ASD title bar and required windows to proper view.
Leave the manually entered data and queue backlog until workload decreases, because probe accuracy is unaffected as long as the target symbol is still updating.
Skip the Degraded RNP flag unless the pilot specifically asks for it, and ignore the obscured title bar if the aircraft targets remain visible.
Use the error queue only for end-of-shift cleanup, because timely handling of those messages is not part of normal information management duties.

items.explanation: Per 13-2-3, ATOP information management depends on current manually entered data, timely queue handling, accurate strip flags and restrictions, and visible required display elements.

items.situation: A CPDLC-connected aircraft is outside VHF coverage and the controller starts using voice as the primary medium even though CPDLC is available. Later, an automated data link transfer to the next facility fails, and the controller wants to stop protecting a CPDLC clearance immediately even though there is uncertainty whether the flight crew actually received it.
items.para_context: Under 13-2-4, CPDLC must be the primary means of communication for connected aircraft outside VHF coverage when it is available, with a voice backup available. When pilots communicate via CPDLC, the response should be via CPDLC, and CPDLC message-set messages should be used to the extent possible. If automated transfer fails and manual transfer cannot be completed, advise the pilot to log on to the next facility and send EOS. If CPDLC fails, use voice until reestablished. If there is uncertainty that a clearance was delivered via CPDLC, continue to protect the associated airspace until an appropriate operational response is received.

choices.text: Use CPDLC as the primary medium while available outside VHF coverage, maintain voice backup, handle a failed transfer by attempting manual transfer and then advising the pilot to log on to the next facility and send EOS if needed, and continue protecting the clearance until an appropriate response is received.
Use voice as the default primary medium whenever it is available, because CPDLC is only advisory even outside VHF coverage.
Stop protecting the airspace as soon as the message is sent, because delivery via CPDLC is presumed unless the pilot explicitly objects.
If automated transfer fails, wait for the receiving facility to discover the problem rather than instructing the pilot to log on again or send EOS.

items.explanation: Per 13-2-4, CPDLC is primary when available outside VHF coverage, failed transfers require follow-up action, and uncertain CPDLC clearance delivery requires continued protection until the controller receives an appropriate operational response.

items.situation: A flight requests a weather deviation while RNP distance-based longitudinal separation is in use. The deviation monitor window shows a pink advisory with a zero value in the +/- column. Another controller says the advisory is harmless because ATOP already adds all needed spacing automatically, so the deviation can be approved without any further evaluation.
items.para_context: Under 13-2-5, the weather deviation tool adds an additional converging, diverging, and intersecting mileage buffer to the required separation calculated by ATOP when RNP distance-based longitudinal separation is being applied. Pink advisories in the deviation monitor window require further evaluation before approving a weather deviation clearance, and a pink advisory with a zero or positive value indicates the aircraft pair is either at or close to the required spacing.

choices.text: Treat the pink advisory as requiring further evaluation before approving the deviation, especially because a zero or positive value indicates the pair is at or close to the required spacing even with the added buffer applied.
Approve the deviation immediately because a pink advisory shows ATOP has guaranteed adequate additional spacing for the maneuver.
Ignore the weather deviation tool whenever RNP longitudinal separation is in use, because the tool applies only to lateral separation cases.
Treat a zero or positive value as proof that the pair is widely separated and no further review is needed.

items.explanation: Per 13-2-5, the weather deviation tool adds a CDI mileage buffer, but pink advisories still require evaluation before approval, and zero or positive values mean the aircraft are at or close to required spacing.

items.situation: Two adjacent ATOP sectors are working a handoff and one controller assumes the automated coordination alone is fully sufficient, even though the aircraft is in conflict with another aircraft already in the receiving sector. The controller wants to skip any additional coordination because both sectors are on ATOP.
items.para_context: Under 13-2-6, in addition to Chapter 8 oceanic coordination requirements, automated coordination constitutes complete coordination between ATOP sectors internally and across adjacent ATOP facilities except when the aircraft is in conflict with another in the receiving sector or when otherwise specified in facility directives or an LOA.

choices.text: Do not treat automated coordination alone as complete in that case; additional coordination is required because the aircraft is in conflict with another in the receiving sector.
Treat automated coordination as complete in every case between ATOP sectors, because conflict situations do not change the rule.
Ignore Chapter 8 coordination requirements whenever both sectors use ATOP, because 13-2-6 replaces them entirely.
Require additional coordination only when the aircraft is leaving ATOP airspace, not when there is a conflict in the receiving sector.

items.explanation: Per 13-2-6, automated coordination is generally complete between ATOP sectors, but not when the aircraft is in conflict with another in the receiving sector or when directives or LOAs specify otherwise.

items.situation: At a multiple-controller workstation, one team member wants to divide work informally based only on convenience, even if lower-priority tasks get done before higher-priority ones and some locally directed procedures are skipped because everyone at the position already understands the operation.
items.para_context: Under 13-2-7, when operating in a multiple-controller operation at a workstation, ensure all ATC tasks are completed according to their priority of duties, and accomplish the multiple-controller operation according to facility directives.

choices.text: Ensure the team completes all ATC tasks according to their priority of duties and conducts the multiple-controller operation in accordance with facility directives.
Let the team handle tasks in any order they prefer, because once multiple controllers are assigned the formal duty priorities no longer control.
Skip facility-directed workstation procedures whenever the team agrees on a faster local method.
Focus only on the radar controller's tasks, because other ATC tasks at the workstation do not need to follow the priority-of-duties concept.

items.explanation: Per 13-2-7, multiple-person workstation operations still require completion of ATC tasks by duty priority and in accordance with facility directives.

items.situation: A revised route is received for a departure that was going to be sent by PDC. Another controller wants to transmit the amended flight plan through PDC anyway because the system can display the change, and they do not want to call the flight crew unless the route continuity looks broken.
items.para_context: Under 14-1-1, PDC must be used in accordance with this order and local facility directives. Clearances must be reviewed for accuracy, route integrity, completeness, and continuity. PDC does not permit amended or revised flight plans to be transmitted; revised or amended flight plans must be verbally issued to the flight crew. A flight plan initially generated in the tower with a route assigned by automation is not considered revised or amended and may be transmitted.

choices.text: Review the clearance for accuracy and continuity, and verbally issue the revised or amended flight plan instead of transmitting it by PDC.
Transmit the amended route by PDC as long as the route is continuous, because continuity is the only limit on PDC use.
Use PDC for any revision if the automation system generated the display, because automation-generated changes are never considered amended.
Skip the review and send the clearance by PDC first, then correct any route-integrity issue verbally if the pilot questions it.

items.explanation: Per 14-1-1, revised or amended flight plans are not transmitted by PDC and must be verbally issued, after the controller reviews the clearance for accuracy, completeness, and route continuity.

items.situation: A departure needs a revised clearance and the tower has CPDLC DCL available. One controller assumes any revised plan must be handled by voice because that was true for PDC, while another wants to send a CPDLC DCL that still contains ambiguous route information because the data link can be corrected later if needed.
items.para_context: Under 14-1-2, CPDLC DCL must be used in accordance with this order and local facility directives. All clearances must be reviewed for accuracy, route integrity, completeness, and continuity. CPDLC permits amended or revised flight plans to be transmitted, but if a revised or amended flight plan cannot be delivered using CPDLC it must be verbally issued to the flight crew. CPDLC clearance information must be operational in nature.

choices.text: Review the clearance for accuracy and route integrity, and use CPDLC to transmit the revised flight plan only if it can be properly delivered that way; otherwise issue it verbally.
Use voice for every amended or revised flight plan because CPDLC DCL never permits those changes.
Send the CPDLC DCL even if the route is not yet complete or understandable, because the flight crew can request clarification after pushback.
Use CPDLC only for nonoperational advisory text, and issue all actual clearance elements verbally.

items.explanation: Per 14-1-2, CPDLC DCL may transmit amended or revised flight plans, but only when the clearance can be properly delivered via CPDLC after review for accuracy, completeness, and route integrity.

items.situation: A departure has no SID filed or assigned by EAS, no transition is to be assigned, and the clearance will include an initial altitude because there is no vertical guidance from a SID. Another controller wants to leave the SID and Transition fields blank, put the altitude in the CLIMB VIA field, and use the Local Information field for instructions that contradict the rest of the departure clearance.
items.para_context: Under 14-1-3, each DCL selectable field has a specific purpose. If no SID is filed or assigned and none is to be assigned, Field 1 must use the NO SID option. If no Transition is filed or assigned and none is to be assigned, Field 2 must use the ---- option. Field 4 is reserved for CLIMB VIA SID or CLIMB VIA SID EXCEPT MAINTAIN instructions when a SID with vertical guidance is assigned. If no SID is assigned or the assigned SID lacks initial altitude or vertical guidance, Field 5 must contain MAINTAIN (assigned altitude). Field 9 is for local information and must not contradict information elsewhere in the clearance.

choices.text: Use NO SID in Field 1, ---- in Field 2, place MAINTAIN (assigned altitude) in Field 5, and keep Field 9 from contradicting any other part of the departure clearance.
Leave the SID and Transition fields blank, use CLIMB VIA in Field 4 even though no SID with vertical guidance is assigned, and let Field 9 restate conflicting local instructions if needed.
Put the initial altitude in Field 3 only, because Field 5 is never used when there is no SID.
Use any field that has character space available, because the selectable fields are suggestions rather than standardized assignments.

items.explanation: Per 14-1-3, DCL fields are standardized by purpose: SID and Transition fields use specific no-assignment options, Field 4 is only for CLIMB VIA uses tied to a SID, Field 5 carries the maintain altitude instruction when needed, and Field 9 cannot contradict the rest of the clearance.

items.situation: A pilot sends a CPDLC weather-deviation request while the aircraft still has a climb-via clearance and a crossing restriction. Another controller wants to approve it by CPDLC immediately, leave an open trajectory-altering clearance in the system through the handoff, and let a noneligible position terminate the CPDLC connection without first coordinating with the sector that has eligibility.
items.para_context: Under 14-2-1, CPDLC augments voice communications, but controllers should minimize CPDLC during critical phases of flight and should not use it for immediate or expeditious clearances unless voice is not operationally feasible. There must be no open trajectory altering clearances prior to transfer of communication unless otherwise coordinated. Coordination with the sector with eligibility is required before any other position or adapted workstation terminates a CPDLC connection. When responding to a weather-deviation pilot-initiated downlink and the aircraft has a climb-via, descend-via, or crossing restriction, the controller must unable the request and revert to voice communications.

choices.text: Unable the weather-deviation request via CPDLC and revert to voice, ensure there are no open trajectory-altering clearances before transfer unless coordinated otherwise, and coordinate with the sector with eligibility before another position terminates the CPDLC connection.
Approve the deviation by CPDLC anyway because CPDLC may always be used for faster handling than voice, and leave the open trajectory change for the next sector to resolve after transfer.
Terminate the CPDLC connection from any convenient position without coordination, because eligibility matters only for message creation and not for termination.
Use CPDLC for every immediate or expeditious clearance so the voice frequency remains clear, regardless of whether voice is operationally feasible.

items.explanation: Per 14-2-1, certain CPDLC actions require voice handling, open trajectory-altering clearances cannot remain through transfer unless coordinated, and CPDLC termination from another position requires prior coordination with the sector that has eligibility.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: DISREGARD CPDLC ALTITUDE CLEARANCE TO FLIGHT LEVEL THREE FIVE ZERO AND SEND AN UNABLE.
items.display_text: DISREGARD CPDLC ALTITUDE CLEARANCE TO FLIGHT LEVEL THREE FIVE ZERO AND SEND A WILCO
items.explanation: Per 14-2-2, when overriding an open CPDLC clearance by voice, the controller says DISREGARD CPDLC ... CLEARANCE ... AND SEND AN UNABLE.

items.situation: An aircraft shows an IC mismatch indicator in the FDB and ACL. The aircraft is on your voice frequency. At the same time, another controller wants to cancel an open uplink immediately to clear the indication even though the pilot has not yet acknowledged the superseding voice clearance.
