items.para_context: Under 14-2-2, the controller who has the aircraft on their voice frequency must use voice communication to verify the assigned altitude and acknowledge the IC mismatch or CAA time-out indicator. Abnormal CPDLC indications are acknowledged only after required coordination. An open uplink may be canceled only after the pilot has been issued and has acknowledged, by voice, the superseding ATC clearance.

choices.text: Use voice to verify the assigned altitude, complete any required coordination before acknowledging the abnormal indication, and do not cancel the open uplink until the pilot has received and acknowledged the superseding voice clearance.
Acknowledge the abnormal indication immediately and cancel the open uplink first, then verify the altitude later if the pilot has questions.
Use CPDLC to verify the altitude because the mismatch appeared in the data block, and cancel the uplink as soon as the replacement clearance is typed.
Leave the mismatch unresolved until the next sector takes the handoff, because all sectors can see the abnormal indication anyway.

items.explanation: Per 14-2-2, altitude verification for an IC mismatch or CAA time-out is done by voice by the controller working the aircraft, abnormal indications are acknowledged after required coordination, and open uplinks are canceled only after the pilot has acknowledged the superseding voice clearance.

items.situation: A CPDLC shutdown occurs during a busy push. One aircraft also never received an automated altimeter update, and several pilots may still try to send downlinks because they have not heard anything on voice yet.
items.para_context: Under 14-2-3, if the system fails to provide a necessary automated altimeter setting, the controller must issue the altimeter setting in accordance with Chapter 2, Section 7. During a CPDLC shutdown or failure, controllers must use voice to alert pilots that CPDLC is no longer in use, request no downlinks until further advised, and resolve any open or abnormally closed uplinks by voice with each aircraft.

choices.text: Issue the needed altimeter setting by voice, broadcast that CPDLC is no longer in use and that pilots should not downlink until further advised, and resolve any open or abnormally closed uplinks by voice with each affected aircraft.
Wait for the CPDLC system to restore automated altimeter delivery before taking action, because the shutdown message is generated automatically.
Use free text downlinks to tell each aircraft not to downlink anymore, then clean up the open uplinks after the shutdown ends.
Issue the altimeter setting only to aircraft already on voice, but leave open or abnormally closed uplinks unresolved because the system will reconcile them at restart.

items.explanation: Per 14-2-3, controllers must move the affected actions to voice during a shutdown or failure, including the shutdown broadcast, altimeter issuance when automation fails, and resolution of open or abnormally closed uplinks.

items.situation: An aircraft is at FL400 on a procedure with published speed restrictions and later needs further holding instructions. Another controller wants to send the speed change by CPDLC, use advisory free text for a control instruction that requires acknowledgment, and then clear the aircraft out of holding by CPDLC without canceling the climb-via or descend-via clearance first.
items.para_context: Under 14-2-4, control instructions and other messages requiring acknowledgement must not be sent via advisory or free text messages. At or above FL390, a WILCO response to a CPDLC speed assignment satisfies the concurrence requirement, but CPDLC must not be used to issue a speed adjustment to an aircraft established on a route or procedure with published speed restrictions. CPDLC must not be used to clear an aircraft out of holding, and if climb-via or descend-via is in effect, that vertical navigation must be canceled before CPDLC is used to issue holding instructions.

choices.text: Keep acknowledgment-required control instructions out of advisory or free text, do not use CPDLC for the speed change on the published-speed procedure or to clear the aircraft out of holding, and cancel climb-via or descend-via before using CPDLC for any holding instructions.
Use CPDLC for the speed change because the aircraft is above FL390, and use free text for the holding clearance so long as the pilot replies WILCO.
Clear the aircraft out of holding by CPDLC first, then cancel climb-via or descend-via later if the aircraft asks for clarification.
Use advisory free text for any message as long as the intent is obvious, because free text and standard CPDLC messages are operationally interchangeable.

items.explanation: Per 14-2-4, several CPDLC limits apply at once: acknowledgment-required control instructions stay out of advisory/free text, speed changes on published-speed procedures are not issued by CPDLC, aircraft are not cleared out of holding by CPDLC, and climb-via or descend-via must be canceled before CPDLC holding instructions are issued.

items.situation: A CPDLC-connected aircraft is outside VHF coverage. Another controller wants to keep using voice as the primary medium because it is familiar, reply by voice to the pilot's CPDLC downlink, and send a free-text message even though a standard CPDLC message element is available.
items.para_context: Under 14-3-1, when CPDLC is available and the aircraft is outside VHF coverage, CPDLC must be the primary means of communication. Voice may still be used when it offers an operational advantage or workload or equipment limitations demand it, but a voice backup must exist whenever CPDLC is being used. When the pilot communicates via CPDLC, the response should be via CPDLC, and to the extent possible the standard CPDLC message set should be used instead of free text.

choices.text: Use CPDLC as the primary communication method, ensure a voice backup exists, respond via CPDLC to the pilot's CPDLC message, and use the standard CPDLC message set instead of free text when available.
Use voice as the primary method whenever possible outside VHF coverage, because CPDLC is only a backup for oceanic aircraft.
Reply by voice to any CPDLC downlink so the pilot hears an immediate answer, and use free text whenever it is shorter than the standard message set.
Use CPDLC as the primary method only if there is no voice backup available, because the backup requirement applies only when voice is primary.

items.explanation: Per 14-3-1, CPDLC is primary outside VHF coverage when available, but it operates with a voice backup, replies should follow the pilot's CPDLC medium, and standard message elements are preferred over free text.

items.situation: You are transferring communications to the next facility. One receiving sector is CPDLC capable, while another possible receiving facility is not CPDLC capable. A controller wants to handle both transfers the same way without checking facility capability or the local LOA.
items.para_context: Under 14-3-2, when the receiving facility is capable of CPDLC communications, the data link transfer is automatic and is accomplished within facility adapted parameters. When the receiving facility is not CPDLC capable, the transfer of communications must be made in accordance with local directives and Letters of Agreement.

choices.text: Let the transfer occur automatically when the receiving facility is CPDLC capable, but if the receiving facility is not CPDLC capable, make the transfer in accordance with local directives and the applicable LOA.
Handle every transfer manually by voice, because automatic data link transfer is only optional even when both facilities are CPDLC capable.
Assume automatic transfer works for every receiving facility and use local directives only if the pilot reports a failure after transfer.
Treat local directives and LOAs as advisory only, because CPDLC capability alone governs the transfer method.

items.explanation: Per 14-3-2, CPDLC-capable facilities use automatic data link transfer within adapted parameters, while non-CPDLC-capable facilities require transfer procedures from local directives and LOAs.

items.clearance: UNITED 234, CPDLC FAILURE, MAINTAIN FLIGHT LEVEL THREE THREE ZERO.

choices.text: United 234, CPDLC failure, maintain flight level three three zero.
United 234, voice failure, maintain flight level three three zero.
CPDLC failure, maintain flight level three three zero.
United 234, CPDLC failure, maintain flight level three zero zero.

items.explanation: Per 14-3-3e, when making initial voice contact after a CPDLC failure issue, preface the message with '(Call Sign) CPDLC Failure, (message).'

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: UNITED 234 CPDLC FAILURE MAINTAIN FLIGHT LEVEL THREE THREE ZERO.
items.display_text: UNITED 234 VOICE FAILURE MAINTAIN FLIGHT LEVEL THREE THREE ZERO
items.explanation: Per 14-3-3e, the prefatory phrase on initial voice contact is '(Call Sign) CPDLC Failure, (message),' not 'Voice Failure.'

items.situation: An automated data link transfer fails. You cannot complete the transfer manually right away, and there is also uncertainty whether a previously issued CPDLC clearance was actually delivered to the aircraft. Another controller wants to stop protecting the airspace tied to that clearance and wait for the pilot to call if there is a problem.
items.para_context: Under 14-3-3, if any portion of the automated transfer fails, the controller should attempt to initiate the transfer manually. If unable to complete the transfer, the pilot should be advised to log on to the next facility and send an End Service message. If there is uncertainty that a CPDLC clearance was delivered, the controller must continue to protect the airspace associated with the clearance until an appropriate operational response is received and take action to ensure the clearance was received.

choices.text: Attempt the transfer manually, advise the pilot to log on to the next facility and send an End Service message if the transfer still cannot be completed, and continue protecting the airspace associated with the uncertain clearance until an appropriate operational response is received.
Terminate CPDLC immediately, stop protecting the airspace once the transfer automation fails, and wait for the pilot to report whether the clearance was received.
Assume the transfer and clearance both succeeded because the aircraft remains CPDLC capable, so no further action is required unless the next facility rejects the handoff.
Use only voice for future instructions, but do not advise the pilot to log on again or send End Service because the failed transfer will eventually time out on its own.

items.explanation: Per 14-3-3, failed automated transfer requires a manual attempt and possibly pilot logon/EOS instructions, and uncertainty about clearance delivery means the controller continues protecting the affected airspace until operational closure is received.

items.clearance: DEVIATION TWO ZERO DEGREES RIGHT APPROVED, WHEN ABLE PROCEED DIRECT O'NEILL VORTAC AND ADVISE.

choices.text: Deviation twenty degrees right approved, when able proceed direct O'Neill VORTAC and advise, United 234.
Deviation twenty degrees right approved, proceed direct O'Neill now, United 234.
Deviation north of course approved, advise clear of weather, United 234.
Unable requested deviation, fly heading zero niner zero, United 234.

items.explanation: Per 2-6-4i4, when traffic and airspace permit, weather-deviation approval may be combined with a clearance on course using the when able proceed direct phraseology.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: UNABLE REQUESTED DEVIATION, FLY HEADING ZERO NINER ZERO, ADVISE CLEAR OF WEATHER.
items.display_text: UNABLE REQUESTED VECTOR FLY HEADING ZERO NINER ZERO ADVISE CLEAR OF WEATHER
items.explanation: Per 2-6-4j, the phraseology begins UNABLE REQUESTED DEVIATION.

items.situation: An aircraft has already been issued a climb-via clearance and a crossing altitude, then requests a weather deviation. Another controller wants to say only 'deviation approved' and let the crew keep the published vertical path until they are ready to rejoin the procedure.
items.para_context: Under 2-6-4i2, when approving a weather deviation for an aircraft that had previously been issued a crossing altitude, including climb via or descend via clearances, issue an altitude to maintain and, if necessary, a speed along with the clearance to deviate. If you intend to clear the aircraft to resume the procedure, advise the pilot. The note explains that leaving the SID or STAR lateral path cancels the published altitude and speed restrictions.

choices.text: Approve the deviation with an assigned altitude to maintain, add a speed if needed, and advise the pilot if you expect to resume the SID or STAR later.
Approve the deviation without assigning an altitude, because published climb-via or descend-via restrictions remain in effect off the lateral path.
Deny every weather deviation request after a climb-via or descend-via clearance because the FMS cannot accept any amended route guidance.
Issue only a heading and wait until the pilot is clear of weather before deciding whether altitude restrictions still apply.

items.explanation: Per 2-6-4i2, weather deviations off a SID or STAR require an explicit altitude to maintain, and if necessary a speed, because the published altitude and speed restrictions no longer control once the aircraft leaves the lateral path.

items.clearance: ADVISE ON INITIAL CONTACT YOU HAVE DELTA.

choices.text: Boston Tower, United 234 with Delta.
Boston Tower, United 234 request Delta.
Boston Tower, United 234 holding short.
Delta, United 234.

items.explanation: Per 2-9-3p, the pilot acknowledges receipt of the ATIS by informing the controller on initial contact.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: UNAUTHORIZED LASER ILLUMINATION EVENT.
items.display_text: UNAUTHORIZED RADAR ILLUMINATION EVENT
items.explanation: Per 2-9-3c, the ATIS phraseology is UNAUTHORIZED LASER ILLUMINATION EVENT.

items.situation: Construction has shortened Runway 10 to 9,850 feet and the project will continue for weeks. Another controller wants to mention the closure casually in ATIS without using the runway-shortened warning phraseology because pilots already know about the NOTAM.
items.para_context: Under 2-9-3h, when a runway length has been temporarily or permanently shortened, WARNING must preface the runway number and the word shortened must be included in the message. The available runway length must be included in the ATIS broadcast for the duration of the construction project, and for a permanently shortened runway the broadcast continues for at least 30 days or until the Chart Supplement is updated, whichever is longer.

choices.text: Broadcast 'WARNING, RUNWAY ONE ZERO HAS BEEN SHORTENED, 9850 FEET AVAILABLE' and keep the shortened-runway information in ATIS for the required duration.
Mention only that construction is in progress, because the NOTAM is enough and ATIS does not need a runway-shortened warning.
Use the warning phrase only on the first day of construction, then remove it once pilots have likely heard it.
Broadcast the new runway length only when a pilot asks about runway performance data on frequency.

items.explanation: Per 2-9-3h, shortened-runway information stays in ATIS with the required warning phraseology and available length for the prescribed duration.

items.clearance: REPORT ABLE TO RESUME RVSM.

choices.text: Will report able to resume RVSM, American 52.
Unable clearance into RVSM airspace, American 52.
Negative RVSM, American 52.
Resume RVSM now, American 52.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-29e2, REPORT ABLE TO RESUME RVSM is the phraseology used when requesting the pilot to advise when the aircraft can resume RVSM operations.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: POINT OUT BAXTER1 CLIMBING TO FL 360, NEGATIVE RVSM.
items.display_text: POINT OUT BAXTER1 CLIMBING TO FL 360 POSITIVE RVSM
items.explanation: Per 2-1-29d, use negative RVSM in all verbal ground-to-ground communications involving non-RVSM aircraft while cleared within RVSM airspace.

items.situation: A non-RVSM military aircraft requests access to RVSM airspace. Traffic is moderate, and another controller wants to clear it in immediately and let the next sector deal with any additional coordination if the flight later has to be removed from RVSM airspace.
items.para_context: Under 2-1-29a, non-RVSM aircraft are not permitted in RVSM airspace unless they meet the excepted-aircraft criteria and are previously approved by the operations supervisor/CIC on a workload- or traffic-permitting basis. Sector-to-sector coordination is required for all non-RVSM operations in RVSM airspace, and the operations supervisor/CIC must be informed when a non-RVSM exception flight is denied or removed from RVSM airspace.

choices.text: Ensure the aircraft qualifies as an excepted non-RVSM flight, obtain prior operations supervisor/CIC approval, coordinate sector-to-sector, and notify the operations supervisor/CIC if the aircraft is denied or later removed from RVSM airspace.
Clear the aircraft into RVSM airspace immediately because military status alone removes the need for supervisor approval and sector coordination.
Deny all non-RVSM military requests automatically because no exceptions are allowed in RVSM airspace.
Coordinate only if the aircraft later reports an RVSM equipment problem while already in the airspace.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-29, excepted non-RVSM aircraft still require prior supervisor approval, coordination, and supervisor notification if denied or removed.

items.situation: An aircraft under your control reports, "TCAS RA," and begins climbing away from assigned altitude. Another controller wants to keep issuing turns and altitude instructions that conflict with the RA because he is trying to maintain standard separation from nearby traffic immediately.
items.para_context: Under 2-1-28, when an aircraft reports it is responding to a TCAS RA, do not issue control instructions contrary to the RA procedure the crew advises it is executing. Continue safety alerts and traffic advisories as appropriate, and resume approved-separation responsibility only after one of the listed restoration conditions is met.

choices.text: Do not issue instructions contrary to the RA, continue traffic advisories and safety alerts as appropriate, and wait to resume approved-separation responsibility until one of the specified restoration conditions is met.
Override the RA immediately with heading and altitude instructions because ATC separation always takes precedence over a TCAS maneuver.
Stop issuing all traffic advisories because once an RA is reported ATC may no longer mention nearby traffic.
Assume every nearby aircraft is also responding to the RA and stop issuing instructions to all of them until the event ends.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-28, ATC does not issue instructions contrary to the reported RA, continues advisories as appropriate, and does not resume approved-separation responsibility until the specified conditions are met.

items.situation: An IFR arrival is following a heavier aircraft to the runway, and another controller wants to drop wake handling once the aircraft is inside short final because the runway environment is in sight and touchdown is imminent.
items.para_context: Under 2-1-19, apply wake turbulence procedures whenever wake turbulence separation is required. The required separation minima continue to touchdown for all IFR aircraft not making a visual approach or maintaining visual separation.

choices.text: Continue applying the required wake turbulence separation to touchdown unless the aircraft is making a visual approach or maintaining visual separation.
Discontinue wake turbulence handling on short final because touchdown is imminent and runway environment is in sight.
Replace wake turbulence separation with a cautionary advisory only whenever the aircraft is inside the final approach fix.
Apply wake turbulence procedures only to VFR aircraft because IFR aircraft are covered entirely by basic IFR separation.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-19b, wake turbulence separation minima continue to touchdown for IFR aircraft unless they are making a visual approach or maintaining visual separation.

items.situation: A military unit tells you it will be using MARSA inside special use airspace later today and implies ATC should approve or deny the MARSA application before the operation starts. Another controller also wants ATC to verify that all participating military aircraft are authorized for entry into the ATCAA.
items.para_context: Under 2-1-11, MARSA may only be applied to military operations specified in appropriate agreements or documents. ATC facilities do not invoke or deny MARSA; their responsibility is to separate participating MARSA aircraft from other nonparticipating IFR aircraft. DoD ensures pilots have coordinated with the scheduling agency, obtained entry approval, and are familiar with MARSA procedures.

choices.text: Do not attempt to invoke or deny MARSA; provide separation between participating MARSA aircraft and other nonparticipating IFR aircraft, while leaving entry authorization and MARSA-use responsibility to the military side.
Approve or deny the MARSA request yourself because ATC is the controlling authority for when MARSA may be used.
Refuse to separate participating MARSA aircraft from nonparticipating IFR traffic because all separation responsibility transfers to the military once MARSA is mentioned.
Determine which military aircraft are authorized to enter the ATCAA before allowing any operation to begin.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-11, ATC does not invoke or deny MARSA and is not responsible for determining which military aircraft are authorized for SUA or ATCAA entry; ATC's MARSA role is separation from nonparticipating IFR aircraft.

items.situation: You are working a busy sector when an aircraft conflict is developing, a suspicious aircraft report also needs to be relayed, and another pilot is asking for extra services. A trainee wants to answer the extra-service request first because that pilot has been waiting the longest.
items.para_context: Under 2-1-2, first priority is separating aircraft and issuing safety alerts as required. Controllers then use good judgment to prioritize all other provisions based on the situation, while also supporting national security and homeland defense activities, providing or soliciting weather information as required, and furnishing additional services only to the extent possible after higher-priority duties.

choices.text: Handle the separation and any required safety alert first, then work the suspicious-activity reporting and other required duties, and provide additional services only after those higher-priority responsibilities permit.
Answer the additional-service request first because first-come, first-served governs ATC duty priority once all aircraft are in communication.
Relay the suspicious-activity report first and delay separation action because homeland-defense support automatically overrides conflict resolution.
Provide every requested service in the order received because the order does not permit controllers to reprioritize duties based on safety.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-2, separation and required safety alerts take first priority, with other duties handled using judgment based on the situation and additional services provided only after higher-priority responsibilities.

items.situation: An aircraft reports "minimum fuel" before handoff to the next sector. Another controller wants to move it ahead of all other traffic immediately and says there is no need to tell the receiving facility because minimum fuel is not an emergency.
items.para_context: Under 2-1-8, when an aircraft declares minimum fuel, inform any facility to whom control jurisdiction is transferred of the minimum-fuel problem and remain alert for any occurrence that might delay the aircraft en route. A minimum-fuel advisory is not an emergency and does not by itself imply traffic priority, but it signals that undue delay could lead to an emergency.

choices.text: Advise the receiving facility of the minimum-fuel problem and watch closely for any delay, while remembering that minimum fuel alone does not automatically require priority handling.
Move the aircraft ahead of all other traffic automatically because minimum fuel always grants priority handling.
Treat the report as informational only and omit it from the handoff because the aircraft did not declare an emergency.
Instruct the pilot to declare an emergency immediately because controllers may not work an aircraft after hearing the words minimum fuel.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-8, controllers pass the minimum-fuel information forward and remain alert for delay, but minimum fuel by itself is advisory rather than automatic priority.

items.situation: A pilot reports a partial communications failure and says the aircraft also lost GPS navigation capability. Another controller wants to keep working the aircraft normally without asking what kind of help is needed, and plans to let the next sector discover the problem on its own during the handoff.
items.para_context: Under 2-1-7, when a pilot reports an inflight equipment malfunction, determine the nature and extent of any special handling desired, provide the maximum assistance possible consistent with equipment and workload, and relay all pertinent details and special handling to subsequent controllers or facilities.

choices.text: Determine the nature and extent of the assistance desired, provide the maximum help possible consistent with workload and equipment, and relay the pertinent malfunction details to the next controller or facility.
Avoid asking for details because pilots are expected to resolve equipment malfunctions without ATC involvement unless they declare an emergency.
Wait until the malfunction becomes an emergency before changing anything about the service being provided.
Keep the malfunction off the handoff so the next sector is not influenced by earlier controller judgment.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-7, controllers determine what assistance is needed, provide the maximum assistance possible, and relay the pertinent details to anyone subsequently handling the aircraft.

items.situation: Traffic is busy. One pilot has "HOSP" in the flight plan remarks but is using a normal call sign and has not asked for priority. Another aircraft checks in as "MEDEVAC Five Six Alpha" and requests priority handling. A trainee wants to keep working first-come, first-served and says the HOSP remark automatically gives the same operational priority as the verbal MEDEVAC identification.
items.para_context: Under 2-1-4, first-come, first-served applies as circumstances permit, but specified priority exceptions must be honored. Civil air ambulance flights receive priority handling when the pilot verbally identifies the flight by stating MEDEVAC followed by the authorized call sign or full registration. HOSP or AIR EVAC entries in the flight plan are informational only and do not themselves establish operational priority.

choices.text: Give priority handling to the aircraft verbally identifying as MEDEVAC, do not treat the HOSP flight-plan remark by itself as operational priority, and otherwise sequence traffic using good judgment without compromising safety.
Treat the HOSP remark as equal operational priority to the verbal MEDEVAC identification and keep both ahead of all other traffic automatically.
Ignore the MEDEVAC request and keep strict first-come, first-served sequencing because traffic flow considerations remove all priority exceptions.
Require every air ambulance flight to declare an emergency before any priority handling may be provided.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-4, MEDEVAC priority depends on the pilot's verbal identification, while HOSP/AIR EVAC flight-plan entries are informational only.

items.clearance: AMERICAN TWO, REVISED FLIGHT LEVEL, THREE THREE ZERO.

choices.text: American Two, revised flight level, three three zero.
American Two, revised heading, three three zero.
American Two, revised estimate, three three zero.
American Two, flight level unchanged, three three zero.

items.explanation: Per 2-2-11a, amended data are forwarded using revised phraseology, such as revised flight level.

items.instruction: One word in this coordination phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: AMERICAN TWO REVISED FLIGHT LEVEL THREE THREE ZERO.
items.display_text: AMERICAN TWO REVISED HEADING THREE THREE ZERO
items.explanation: Per 2-2-11a, the example phraseology is revised flight level, not revised heading.

items.situation: Within 20 minutes of an aircraft's proposed departure time, you amend the route and altitude. The receiving facility is FDIO-only, and the automated coordination path is temporarily unavailable. Another controller wants to wait for the printed strip to show up and skip the direct call because the amendment was already entered locally.
items.para_context: Under 2-2-11c, when a route or altitude in a previously issued clearance is amended within 30 minutes of proposed departure time, the amending facility must coordinate the amendment with the receiving facility via verbal and automated means to ensure timely passage of the information. If the automated means are unavailable, verbal coordination is sufficient. Under 2-2-11e, when a controller receives a UTM notification to an FDIO-only facility, manual coordination is required and the flight plan data must be verified to the receiving facility within three minutes of the transfer of control point estimate.

choices.text: Coordinate the amendment verbally right away, use automated coordination if available, and because the receiving facility is FDIO-only, ensure the required manual coordination and timely verification are completed.
Rely on the local amendment entry alone, because amendments inside 30 minutes do not need direct facility coordination once they are typed into the system.
Wait for automated coordination to come back before telling the receiving facility, because verbal coordination is never sufficient for route or altitude amendments.
Treat FDIO-only facilities the same as fully automated facilities and skip the manual verification step.

items.explanation: Per 2-2-11, short-notice route or altitude amendments require timely coordination, and FDIO-only facilities require manual coordination with required verification.

items.clearance: THE OMAHA ALTIMETER TWO NINER NINER FOUR MORE THAN ONE HOUR OLD.

choices.text: Omaha altimeter two niner niner four more than one hour old, November 45A.
Omaha altimeter two niner niner four less than one hour old, November 45A.
Destination altimeter two niner niner four, November 45A.
Altimeter received, November 45A.

items.explanation: Per 2-7-2, when the altimeter is more than one hour old, the phraseology includes MORE THAN ONE HOUR OLD and identifies the source facility.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: THE OMAHA ALTIMETER TWO NINER NINER FOUR MORE THAN ONE HOUR OLD.
items.display_text: THE OMAHA ALTIMETER TWO NINER NINER FOUR LESS THAN ONE HOUR OLD
items.explanation: Per 2-7-2, use THE (facility name) ALTIMETER (setting) MORE THAN ONE HOUR OLD when the observation is more than one hour old.

items.situation: An en route aircraft is about to descend below the lowest usable flight level in your area. Another controller wants to wait until the aircraft is already below the flight level and then let the pilot get the altimeter from destination weather later.
items.para_context: Under 2-7-2e, when issuing clearance to descend below the lowest usable flight level, advise the pilot of the altimeter setting of the weather reporting station nearest the point the aircraft will descend below that flight level. Under 2-7-2c1, en route aircraft are issued the altimeter setting for the nearest reporting station along the route of flight.

choices.text: Advise the pilot of the altimeter setting of the weather reporting station nearest the point where the aircraft will descend below the lowest usable flight level.
Wait until the aircraft has leveled below the flight level, because the altimeter is only needed after the descent is complete.
Tell the pilot to use the destination altimeter regardless of location, because the nearest reporting station is only for terminal departures.
Issue no altimeter setting unless the pilot specifically asks for one, because en route altimeters below the lowest usable flight level are pilot responsibility only.

items.explanation: Per 2-7-2e, the controller issues the altimeter setting of the nearest weather reporting station before the aircraft descends below the lowest usable flight level.

items.clearance: ALTIMETER THREE ONE ONE FIVE HIGH BAROMETRIC PRESSURE RESTRICTIONS IN EFFECT, SET THREE ONE ZERO ZERO UNTIL REACHING THE FINAL APPROACH FIX.

choices.text: Altimeter three one one five, high barometric pressure restrictions in effect, set three one zero zero until reaching the final approach fix, United 234.
Altimeter three one one five, high barometric pressure restrictions in effect, set three one zero zero after the final approach fix, United 234.
Altimeter three one one five, low barometric pressure restrictions in effect, set three one zero zero until reaching the final approach fix, United 234.
Altimeter three one one five, maintain current altimeter until touchdown, United 234.

items.explanation: Per 2-7-3a, arrivals are advised that high barometric pressure restrictions are in effect and to set 31.00 until reaching the final approach fix.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: ALTIMETER THREE ONE ONE FIVE HIGH BAROMETRIC PRESSURE RESTRICTIONS IN EFFECT SET THREE ONE ZERO ZERO UNTIL REACHING THE FINAL APPROACH FIX.
items.display_text: ALTIMETER THREE ONE ONE FIVE LOW BAROMETRIC PRESSURE RESTRICTIONS IN EFFECT SET THREE ONE ZERO ZERO UNTIL REACHING THE FINAL APPROACH FIX
items.explanation: Per 2-7-3, the phraseology is HIGH BAROMETRIC PRESSURE RESTRICTIONS IN EFFECT when the barometric pressure exceeds 31.00 inches mercury.

items.situation: Surface pressure is above 31.00 inches mercury. A departure has a mandatory crossing altitude on the SID, and another controller wants to wait until the aircraft passes that restriction before telling the crew to set 31.00.
items.para_context: Under 2-7-3b, for departures, advise pilots that high barometric pressure restrictions are in effect and to set 31.00 prior to reaching any mandatory/crossing altitude or 1,500 feet AGL, whichever is lower.

choices.text: Advise the departure that high barometric pressure restrictions are in effect and to set 31.00 prior to reaching the mandatory or crossing altitude, or 1,500 feet AGL, whichever is lower.
Wait until the aircraft passes the crossing restriction, because the 31.00 setting is only needed after the first mandatory altitude is crossed.
Tell the pilot to keep the local altimeter setting until leaving your sector, because 31.00 is only for arrivals.
Advise the pilot to set 31.00 at top of climb only if the ARTCC has already issued the NOTAM.

items.explanation: Per 2-7-3b, departures set 31.00 before the first limiting altitude point, not after it.

items.clearance: RUNWAY ONE SEVEN DEPARTURE WIND SHEAR ALERT TWO FIVE KNOT GAIN TWO MILE DEPARTURE.

choices.text: Runway one seven departure wind shear alert two five knot gain two mile departure, United 234.
Runway one seven departure wind shear alert two five knot loss two mile departure, United 234.
Runway one seven arrival wind shear alert two five knot gain two mile departure, United 234.
Low level wind shear advisories in effect, United 234.

items.explanation: Per 3-1-8c2a, runway-oriented wind shear or microburst alerts are issued exactly as displayed for the aircraft operation involved.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: RUNWAY ONE SEVEN DEPARTURE WIND SHEAR ALERT TWO FIVE KNOT GAIN TWO MILE DEPARTURE.
items.display_text: RUNWAY ONE SEVEN DEPARTURE WIND SHEAR ALERT TWO FIVE KNOT LOSS TWO MILE DEPARTURE
items.explanation: Per 3-1-8c2a, the alert phraseology must match the displayed gain or loss for the runway operation.

items.situation: A departure reports, 'wind shear escape,' and starts climbing straight ahead instead of following the assigned heading and altitude. Another controller wants to keep issuing turns and altitude instructions to force the aircraft back onto the clearance immediately because there is nearby traffic.
items.para_context: Under 3-1-8d1, if an aircraft informs you it is performing a wind shear escape, do not issue control instructions contrary to pilot actions. ATC should continue to provide safety alerts regarding terrain or obstacles and traffic advisories for the escape aircraft, as appropriate. Under 3-1-8d2, do not presume other nearby aircraft are also escaping unless they advise they are. Continue to provide control instructions, safety alerts, and traffic advisories to other aircraft as appropriate.

choices.text: Do not issue control instructions contrary to the escape maneuver, continue safety alerts and traffic advisories for the escape aircraft as appropriate, and continue to control other aircraft normally unless they also report an escape.
Continue issuing the assigned heading and altitude until the pilot explicitly refuses, because separation responsibility remains unchanged during a wind shear escape.
Assume all nearby aircraft are also escaping and stop issuing control instructions to any of them until the event ends.
Transfer the escaping aircraft to departure control immediately and stop giving any safety alerts because the pilot is now solely responsible for terrain and traffic.

items.explanation: Per 3-1-8d, controllers do not issue instructions contrary to a reported wind shear escape, but they still provide alerts as appropriate and continue handling other aircraft unless those aircraft also report escape procedures.

items.situation: A Cessna calls the tower before entering Class D airspace. You answer with the aircraft call sign and 'standby' because you are busy. Another controller says the aircraft still cannot enter the Delta until you issue an explicit entry approval.
items.para_context: Under 3-1-13, two-way radio communications are established for Class D entry when the controller responds to the pilot with the aircraft call sign followed by 'standby.' If workload or traffic conditions prevent immediate service, instruct the pilot to remain outside the Class D airspace until services can be provided.

choices.text: Treat the standby response as establishing two-way radio communications, but if services still cannot be provided, instruct the pilot to remain outside the Delta airspace and standby.
Keep the aircraft outside the Delta until you issue an explicit entry clearance, because 'standby' does not establish communications for Class D entry.
Assume the pilot may enter Class D regardless of whether you reply with the call sign, because any transmission on the tower frequency is sufficient.
Transfer the aircraft to approach control, because only approach may authorize Class D entry when tower workload is high.

items.explanation: Per 3-1-13, '(call sign) standby' establishes two-way radio communications for Class D entry, but the pilot is told to remain outside the Delta when tower conditions prevent immediate service.

items.situation: An employee who is not on the airport's authorized conditions-reporting list tells you there is a disabled aircraft on the runway. You copy the report, but airport management is not answering. Another controller says you can close the runway yourself and wait for management to call back later.
items.para_context: Under 3-3-1, relay landing-area condition information to airport management or the military operations office, copy the information verbatim, record the submitter's name, and confirm reports received from other than authorized airport or FAA personnel unless that function belongs to military operations. If airport management or the operator cannot be contacted, issue a NOTAM publicizing the unsafe condition and inform management or the operator as soon as practicable. Only airport management or the military operations office can close a runway.

choices.text: Record the report verbatim with the submitter's name, confirm it because it came from an unauthorized source, and if management cannot be reached issue a NOTAM publicizing the unsafe condition and inform management as soon as practicable.
Close the runway yourself and hold the report locally until airport management calls back, because a tower-observed unsafe condition does not require a NOTAM.
Ignore the report because only airport management may mention runway conditions to the tower.
Advise aircraft that the runway is closed and do nothing else, because management notification is optional once pilots have been told.

items.explanation: Per 3-3-1, unauthorized condition reports are confirmed when required, unsafe conditions are publicized by NOTAM if management cannot be reached, and only airport management or military operations can close a runway.

items.situation: A pilot reports braking action as 'fair by the fire station' on the first half of the runway. Another controller wants to pass that wording to other aircraft exactly as received and leave out the aircraft type because the runway is already active.
items.para_context: Under 3-3-4, braking action is issued using the approved terms good, good to medium, medium, medium to poor, poor, or nil. If the pilot uses other terms, ask the pilot to categorize the braking action with the approved terms. Include the type of aircraft from which the report was received, and when the report affects only part of the runway, describe it with clear runway-based terms rather than landmarks.

choices.text: Ask the pilot to restate the report using an approved braking-action term, include the reporting aircraft type, and describe the affected runway portion with runway-based wording rather than a landmark.
Relay 'fair by the fire station' exactly as stated, because the pilot's original wording is always preferred over the approved braking-action terms.
Convert the report to an approved term yourself but omit the aircraft type, because the type is not needed once the runway is active.
Issue the report only to military aircraft, because civil aircraft do not receive braking-action advisories unless they ask first.

items.explanation: Per 3-3-4, the controller uses approved braking-action terminology, includes the reporting aircraft type, and describes the affected runway portion in terms other pilots can easily understand.

items.clearance: FALCON TWO ONE, CABLE INDICATES UP. CLEARED TO LAND.

choices.text: Falcon Two One, cable indicates up, cleared to land.
Falcon Two One, cable indicates down, cleared to land.
Falcon Two One, barrier indicates up, continue approach.
Cleared to land, Falcon Two One.

items.explanation: Per 3-3-6e, when arresting system operation has been requested, inform the pilot of the indicated barrier or cable position with the associated landing or takeoff clearance.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: FALCON TWO ONE CABLE INDICATES UP CLEARED TO LAND.
items.display_text: FALCON TWO ONE CABLE INDICATES DOWN CLEARED TO LAND
items.explanation: Per 3-3-6e, the controller tells the pilot the indicated barrier or cable position, such as CABLE INDICATES UP.

items.situation: A civil aircraft is landing Runway 14. A rubber-supported arresting cable is in place 1,000 feet from the threshold, and the information is not published in Domestic Notices, International Notices, or the DoD FLIP. Another controller wants to omit the advisory because the arriving aircraft is not military.
items.para_context: Under 3-3-6d, inform civil and U.S. Army aircraft whenever rubber supported cables are in place at the approach end of the landing runway, and include the distance of the cables from the threshold. This information may be omitted only if it is published in the Domestic Notices webpage, International Notices webpage, or the DoD FLIP.

choices.text: Advise the aircraft of the arresting cable and include the distance from the threshold, because the information is not published and must be given to civil aircraft.
Omit the advisory because cable information is only required for military aircraft that may engage the arresting system.
Wait until the pilot asks about runway condition details before mentioning the cable, because publication status does not matter.
Issue only 'cable in place' without the threshold distance, because distance is required only for approach-end barriers, not cables.

items.explanation: Per 3-3-6d, civil and U.S. Army aircraft are advised when rubber-supported cables are in place at the approach end, including the cable distance from the threshold, unless the information is already published.

items.situation: An inbound aircraft has declared an emergency. Another controller wants to wait until the aircraft is on short final before bringing up any additional airport lighting so maintenance can avoid unnecessary activations if the situation improves.
items.para_context: Under 3-4-1, whenever you become aware that an emergency has or will occur, take action to provide for the operation of all appropriate airport lighting aids as required.

choices.text: Take action to provide for the operation of all appropriate airport lighting aids as soon as you become aware of the emergency.
Wait until the aircraft is established on final, because emergency lighting changes are only required once the landing runway is assigned.
Activate only the runway edge lights and leave all other aids unchanged unless the pilot specifically asks for them.
Leave the airport lighting configuration alone unless the supervisor declares a full airport emergency.

items.explanation: Per 3-4-1, once the controller is aware an emergency has or will occur, appropriate airport lighting aids are operated as required.

items.situation: It is night IMC and a pilot requests a brighter ALS setting. Another controller says step 4 or 5 should never be used at night because the table already covers normal nighttime operation.
items.para_context: Under 3-4-6, when operating ALS as prescribed in 3-4-5, intensity controls are operated in accordance with the table except when facility directives specify other settings, as requested by the pilot, or as you deem necessary if not contrary to the pilot's request. The note states that at night, use step 4 or 5 only when requested by a pilot.

choices.text: Honor the pilot's request and use the requested higher ALS intensity setting, because step 4 or 5 may be used at night when requested by a pilot.
Refuse any request for step 4 or 5 at night, because nighttime ALS intensity must always remain at the table's normal value.
Use any ALS intensity you prefer regardless of the pilot's request, because the controller may always override the pilot on lighting intensity.
Wait until the aircraft reaches the runway threshold before changing the ALS intensity so the pilot is not distracted during the approach.

items.explanation: Per 3-4-6, pilot request is a valid exception to the table, and at night steps 4 or 5 are used when requested by the pilot.

items.instruction: Which readback correctly acknowledges the movement-area instruction?
items.clearance: PROCEED ON RUNWAY TWO-SIX RIGHT, HOLD SHORT OF RUNWAY ONE-EIGHT LEFT.

choices.text: Proceed on Runway Two-Six Right, hold short of Runway One-Eight Left, Airport One.
Cleared on Runway Two-Six Right, hold short of Runway One-Eight Left, Airport One.
Proceed across Runway One-Eight Left, Airport One.
The field is ours, Airport One.

items.explanation: Per 3-7-1, movement-area instructions use specific proceed and hold short language, not blanket or ambiguous authorization.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: PROCEED ON RUNWAY TWO-SIX RIGHT, HOLD SHORT OF RUNWAY ONE-EIGHT LEFT.
items.display_text: CLEARED ON RUNWAY TWO-SIX RIGHT HOLD SHORT OF RUNWAY ONE-EIGHT LEFT
items.explanation: Per 3-7-1c, do not use the word CLEARED for taxi or equipment, vehicle, or personnel movement authorization. Use PROCEED, TAXI, or HOLD as appropriate.

items.situation: Airport operations requests movement-area access to inspect pavement on an active runway complex. Another controller suggests saying, "The field is yours," and letting the vehicle work wherever needed until it calls back clear.
items.para_context: Under 3-7-1, issue specific instructions approving or disapproving movement on the movement area. Do not use blanket unconditional language such as "the field is yours," and ensure positive control with proceed and hold short instructions as necessary.

choices.text: Issue specific proceed and hold short instructions for the exact runway or taxiway movement needed and maintain positive control of the vehicle's route.
Approve the request with a blanket "field is yours" authorization because airport operations is responsible for its own movement once on the airfield.
Use the word CLEARED to show the vehicle has movement priority over aircraft on the surface.
Wait until the vehicle reaches the runway before deciding whether any hold short instructions are necessary.

items.explanation: Per 3-7-1a through c, movement-area instructions must be specific, must not depend on ambiguous conditional runway movements, and must not use blanket phrases or the word CLEARED for surface authorization.

items.instruction: Which readback correctly acknowledges the hold instruction?
items.clearance: HOLD SHORT OF RUNWAY TWO-SEVEN ILS CRITICAL AREA.

choices.text: Hold short of Runway Two-Seven ILS critical area, Cessna Five One Two.
Cross the ILS critical area after the arrival passes, Cessna Five One Two.
Hold short of Runway Two-Seven safety area, Cessna Five One Two.
ILS not protected, Cessna Five One Two.

items.explanation: Per 3-7-5, the standard hold instruction is HOLD SHORT OF (runway) ILS CRITICAL AREA.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: HOLD SHORT OF RUNWAY TWO-SEVEN ILS CRITICAL AREA.
items.display_text: HOLD SHORT OF RUNWAY TWO-SEVEN ILS SAFETY AREA
items.explanation: Per 3-7-5, the protected area is the ILS CRITICAL AREA, not a safety area.

items.situation: The weather is 500 overcast with 1 mile visibility. An arrival is inside the outer marker on the ILS, and a vehicle asks to operate through the localizer critical area so it can reposition before the aircraft lands.
items.para_context: Under 3-7-5, aircraft and vehicle access to the ILS critical area must be controlled whenever the official weather observation is a ceiling of less than 800 feet or visibility less than 2 miles. Unless an exception applies, do not authorize operations in or over the localizer critical area once the arriving aircraft is inside the outer marker or fix used in lieu of the outer marker.

choices.text: Do not authorize the vehicle into the critical area and keep it holding short unless one of the listed exceptions applies.
Approve the movement because the vehicle will be clear before touchdown, and the critical area matters only inside the middle marker.
Approve the movement because the weather restriction applies only when visibility is below one-half mile.
Ignore the localizer critical area and protect only the glideslope area in these conditions.

items.explanation: Per 3-7-5a1, when ceiling is less than 800 feet or visibility is less than 2 miles, localizer critical area protection applies once the aircraft is inside the outer marker or equivalent fix, subject only to the listed exceptions.

items.instruction: Which pilot readback correctly acknowledges the traffic advisory?
items.clearance: UNITED SIX TWENTY-THREE, IN THE EVENT OF MISSED APPROACH, TAXIING AIRCRAFT RIGHT OF RUNWAY.

choices.text: In the event of missed approach, taxiing aircraft right of runway, United Six Twenty-Three.
Runway is clear for missed approach, United Six Twenty-Three.
Vehicle left of runway, United Six Twenty-Three.
Missed approach approved now, United Six Twenty-Three.

items.explanation: Per 3-7-6c, when the POFZ or OCS cannot be cleared in time, issue traffic to the landing aircraft using the IN THE EVENT OF MISSED APPROACH traffic advisory.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: IN THE EVENT OF MISSED APPROACH, VEHICLE LEFT OF RUNWAY.
items.display_text: IN THE EVENT OF FAILED APPROACH VEHICLE LEFT OF RUNWAY
items.explanation: Per 3-7-6c, the advisory is IN THE EVENT OF MISSED APPROACH, followed by the traffic information.

items.situation: A vertically guided arrival is 1.8 miles from the runway threshold. The weather is 200 overcast and one-half mile visibility, and a taxiing aircraft will still be inside the protected missed-approach surface when the arrival reaches the threshold area.
items.para_context: Under 3-7-6, ensure the POFZ is clear within 2 miles when the ceiling is below 300 feet or visibility is less than three-quarters statute mile, and ensure the final approach OCS is clear within 2 miles when the ceiling is below 800 feet or visibility is less than 2 miles. If the area cannot be cleared in time, issue traffic to the landing aircraft and clear the area as soon as practical.

choices.text: Issue the missed-approach traffic advisory to the landing aircraft and clear the protected area as soon as practical.
Say nothing because the aircraft is already inside 2 miles and traffic advisories are no longer useful.
Continue normal operations because only the glideslope area, not the missed-approach surface, matters once the aircraft is on short final.
Cancel the arrival clearance immediately because the rule requires an automatic missed approach whenever the area is not clear.

items.explanation: Per 3-7-6c, if the POFZ or OCS cannot be cleared before the aircraft reaches 2 miles and the weather is below the threshold, issue traffic to the landing aircraft and clear the area as soon as practical.

items.instruction: Which pilot readback correctly acknowledges the sequencing instruction?
items.clearance: FOLLOW THE CESSNA ON LEFT BASE, NUMBER TWO.

choices.text: Follow the Cessna on left base, number two, Warrior Three Five Mike.
Cleared to land behind the Cessna, Warrior Three Five Mike.
Make short approach, Warrior Three Five Mike.
Number one, Warrior Three Five Mike.

items.explanation: Per 3-8-1, sequencing may be established with instructions such as FOLLOW traffic and NUMBER (landing sequence number).

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: MAKE SHORT APPROACH.
items.display_text: MAKE LONG APPROACH
items.explanation: Per 3-8-1, MAKE SHORT APPROACH is approved sequencing phraseology.

items.situation: Two aircraft are inbound to the same runway and spacing is compressing in the pattern. One is on downwind and the other is turning base. You need to restore proper sequence without canceling the arrival flow.
items.para_context: Under 3-8-1, establish the sequence of arriving and departing aircraft by requiring them to adjust flight or ground operation as necessary to achieve proper spacing, using instructions such as extend downwind, make short approach, follow traffic, or make a 360 when appropriate.

choices.text: Use an approved spacing instruction, such as extend downwind, follow traffic, short approach, or a 360, as needed to re-establish proper sequence.
Avoid intervening because sequencing is the pilots' responsibility once both aircraft are in the pattern.
Issue landing clearance to both aircraft and let them sort out their relative spacing visually.
Instruct the trailing aircraft to taxi off the runway after landing so spacing will improve.

items.explanation: Per 3-8-1, controllers establish proper sequence and spacing by requiring aircraft to adjust flight or ground operation as needed.

items.instruction: Which pilot readback correctly acknowledges the clearance?
items.clearance: CLEARED LOW APPROACH AT OR ABOVE SEVEN HUNDRED FEET. TRAFFIC TAXIING AIRCRAFT LEFT OF RUNWAY.

choices.text: Cleared low approach at or above seven hundred feet, traffic taxiing aircraft left of runway, Navy Seven One.
Cleared low approach below seven hundred feet, Navy Seven One.
Cleared to overfly the aircraft in position, Navy Seven One.
Low approach approved with no altitude restriction, Navy Seven One.

items.explanation: Per 3-10-10, an altitude restricted low approach uses CLEARED LOW APPROACH AT OR ABOVE (altitude) with applicable traffic information.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: CLEARED LOW APPROACH AT OR ABOVE FIVE HUNDRED FEET. TRAFFIC VEHICLE RIGHT OF RUNWAY.
items.display_text: CLEARED LOW APPROACH AT OR BELOW FIVE HUNDRED FEET TRAFFIC VEHICLE RIGHT OF RUNWAY
items.explanation: Per 3-10-10, the phraseology is CLEARED LOW APPROACH AT OR ABOVE (altitude).

items.situation: A pilot requests a restricted low approach at 400 feet above the airport while another aircraft is holding in position on the runway. Another controller wants to approve it because the low approach aircraft will stay fast and well above the departure point.
items.para_context: Under 3-10-10, a restricted low approach must be no less than 500 feet above the airport and may not be authorized over an aircraft holding in position or a departing aircraft. The approaching aircraft must also be advised of applicable ground traffic, personnel, or equipment.

choices.text: Do not approve that restricted low approach as requested; it is below the 500-foot minimum and may not be conducted over an aircraft holding in position.
Approve it as long as the pilot accepts responsibility for the reduced altitude and the aircraft in position remains stopped.
Approve it if you add cautionary traffic only, because traffic information substitutes for the restriction.
Convert it to a normal low approach and allow any altitude the pilot requests as long as it is above runway level.

items.explanation: Per 3-10-10, altitude restricted low approaches are minimum 500 feet above the airport and are not authorized over aircraft holding in position or departing aircraft.

items.instruction: Which pilot readback correctly acknowledges the helicopter movement instruction?
items.clearance: HOVER-TAXI TO HELIPAD TWO, CAUTION LOOSE DEBRIS.

choices.text: Hover-taxi to Helipad Two, caution loose debris, Helicopter Four Six Hotel.
Air-taxi to Helipad Two above one hundred feet, Helicopter Four Six Hotel.
Taxi to Helipad Two on the surface, Helicopter Four Six Hotel.
Proceed direct to Helipad Two no caution, Helicopter Four Six Hotel.

items.explanation: Per 3-11-1b, HOVER-TAXI is used for helicopter or powered-lift movement at slow speed above the surface, supplemented with caution information as appropriate.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: AIR-TAXI VIA TAXIWAY ALPHA TO HELIPAD TWO. REMAIN AT OR BELOW ONE HUNDRED FEET.
items.display_text: AIR-TAXI VIA TAXIWAY ALPHA TO HELIPAD TWO REMAIN AT OR ABOVE ONE HUNDRED FEET
items.explanation: Per 3-11-1c, AIR-TAXI may be supplemented with REMAIN AT OR BELOW (altitude).

items.situation: A helicopter needs to move quickly from one side of the airport to another, normally above 20 knots and below 100 feet AGL. Another controller wants to issue a normal taxi clearance because the route is entirely on the airport.
items.para_context: Under 3-11-1c, when a helicopter or powered-lift aircraft is to proceed expeditiously from one point to another, normally below 100 feet AGL and above 20 knots, use AIR-TAXI and supplement it with route, avoidance, altitude, and caution instructions as appropriate.

choices.text: Use AIR-TAXI with the route or destination and add avoid, remain at or below, or caution information as needed.
Use only normal taxi phraseology because anything on the airport surface must be handled as ground taxi.
Use HOVER-TAXI because all helicopter movement above the surface is hover-taxi regardless of speed.
Issue takeoff clearance instead, because movement above 20 knots is treated as departure from the airport.

items.explanation: Per 3-11-1c, AIR-TAXI is the correct phraseology for expeditious helicopter or powered-lift movement above 20 knots and normally below 100 feet AGL.

items.instruction: Which pilot response correctly acknowledges the controller's terrain-clearance question?
items.clearance: NOVEMBER EIGHT SEVEN SIX, ARE YOU ABLE TO MAINTAIN YOUR OWN TERRAIN AND OBSTRUCTION CLEARANCE UNTIL REACHING SIX THOUSAND?

choices.text: Able to maintain my own terrain and obstruction clearance until reaching six thousand, November Eight Seven Six.
Able to maintain my own traffic and obstruction clearance, November Eight Seven Six.
Request radar vectors below minimum IFR altitude, November Eight Seven Six.
Unable to maintain VFR but issue the IFR clearance anyway, November Eight Seven Six.

items.explanation: Per 4-2-8d1, before issuing the IFR clearance in this situation, ask whether the pilot can maintain terrain and obstruction clearance until reaching the appropriate minimum altitude.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: ARE YOU ABLE TO MAINTAIN YOUR OWN TERRAIN AND OBSTRUCTION CLEARANCE UNTIL REACHING SIX THOUSAND?
items.display_text: ARE YOU ABLE TO MAINTAIN YOUR OWN TRAFFIC AND OBSTRUCTION CLEARANCE UNTIL REACHING SIX THOUSAND
items.explanation: Per 4-2-8d1, the controller asks whether the pilot can maintain terrain and obstruction clearance until reaching the applicable minimum IFR altitude.

items.situation: A VFR aircraft below the minimum IFR altitude requests a pop-up IFR clearance and tells you it cannot climb in VFR conditions to the minimum IFR altitude. After you ask the required question, the pilot says it cannot maintain its own terrain and obstruction clearance during the climb.
items.para_context: Under 4-2-8d, if the pilot is unable to maintain terrain and obstruction clearance, instruct the pilot to maintain VFR and state intentions. If appropriate, apply VFR weather difficulty or radar assistance provisions.

choices.text: Instruct the pilot to maintain VFR and state intentions, and use weather-difficulty or radar-assistance provisions if appropriate.
Issue the IFR clearance immediately and provide course guidance below the minimum IFR altitude until the aircraft reaches it.
Assign a beacon code and treat the aircraft as IFR even though it cannot maintain terrain and obstruction clearance.
Clear the aircraft direct to a fix and let the pilot decide when to accept IFR responsibility.

items.explanation: Per 4-2-8d3, if the pilot states they are unable to maintain terrain and obstruction clearance, instruct them to maintain VFR and state intentions.

items.instruction: Which pilot readback correctly acknowledges the departure procedure clearance?
items.clearance: DEPART VIA THE METRO TWO OBSTACLE DEPARTURE PROCEDURE. REMAIN WITHIN THREE MILES OF METRO DURING VISUAL CLIMB.

choices.text: Depart via the Metro Two obstacle departure procedure, remain within three miles of Metro during visual climb, N123AB.
Depart runway heading and expect obstacle departure later, N123AB.
Climb via SID and remain within ten miles, N123AB.
Proceed on course after takeoff, N123AB.

items.explanation: Per 4-3-2c2, when an ODP is included in the clearance and VCOA applies, include the obstacle departure procedure and the remain-within mileage instruction for the visual climb.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: CLIMB VIA SID EXCEPT MAINTAIN FLIGHT LEVEL ONE EIGHT ZERO.
items.display_text: DESCEND VIA SID EXCEPT MAINTAIN FLIGHT LEVEL ONE EIGHT ZERO
items.explanation: Per 4-3-2e4, when the SID contains crossing restrictions and an interim altitude is required, the phraseology is CLIMB VIA SID EXCEPT MAINTAIN (altitude).

items.situation: A published ODP is necessary to ensure separation after departure, and the pilot will use the VCOA option. Another controller wants to issue only an initial heading and clean up the rest after takeoff to save time on frequency.
items.para_context: Under 4-3-2c2, when an ODP has been published and pilot compliance is necessary to ensure separation, include the procedure as part of the ATC clearance. If the VCOA is requested by the pilot or assigned by ATC when it is the only procedure published, include the instruction to remain within the published visibility or mileage during visual climb.

choices.text: Include the ODP in the clearance and add the remain-within visual-climb instruction that applies to the VCOA.
Issue only an initial heading because pilots are automatically responsible for obstacle procedures once airborne.
Avoid mentioning the ODP unless the pilot specifically quotes it back first.
Replace the ODP with a DVA heading even when a SID is already being assigned in the clearance.

items.explanation: Per 4-3-2c2, if separation depends on pilot compliance with a published ODP, the procedure must be included in the ATC clearance, and VCOA remain-within instructions are added when applicable.

items.instruction: Which pilot readback correctly acknowledges the abbreviated departure clearance?
items.clearance: CLEARED TO REYNOLDS AIRPORT, DAVID TWO DEPARTURE, KINGHAM TRANSITION, THEN AS FILED. CLIMB VIA SID EXCEPT MAINTAIN FLIGHT LEVEL TWO FOUR ZERO.

choices.text: Cleared to Reynolds Airport, David Two Departure, Kingham Transition, then as filed. Climb via SID except maintain flight level two four zero, N123AB.
Cleared to Reynolds as filed, climb unrestricted, N123AB.
Cleared to Reynolds Airport, David Two Departure, maintain flight level two four zero and disregard the SID, N123AB.
Cleared to destination as amended, N123AB.

items.explanation: Per 4-3-3, abbreviated departure clearances still specify the destination airport and the correct SID / then-as-filed / climb-via phraseology.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: CLEARED TO REYNOLDS AIRPORT, DAVID TWO DEPARTURE, KINGHAM TRANSITION, THEN AS FILED.
items.display_text: CLEARED TO REYNOLDS AIRPORT DAVID TWO DEPARTURE KINGHAM TRANSITION THEN AS AMENDED
items.explanation: Per 4-3-3d, when no changes are required in the filed route, the abbreviated clearance uses THEN AS FILED.

items.situation: The pilot's route was revised before departure and the strip now shows FRC. Another controller wants to keep using abbreviated departure phraseology to save frequency time and let the pilot sort out the route change after takeoff.
items.para_context: Under 4-3-3b, when FRC or FRC/(fix) appears, the controller issuing the clearance must issue a full route clearance to the specified fix or entire route if no fix is specified. Abbreviated departure procedures do not apply in that case.

choices.text: Issue the required full route clearance rather than an abbreviated departure clearance.
Use abbreviated clearance phraseology because the route change can be handled by downstream facilities after departure.
Issue only the destination and altitude, because FRC means the route is already understood by the pilot.
Tell the pilot to depart VFR and pick up the route correction later in the air.

items.explanation: Per 4-3-3b, FRC requires the controller to issue a full route clearance, not abbreviated phraseology.

items.instruction: Which pilot readback correctly acknowledges the release?
items.clearance: VFR DEPARTURE AUTHORIZED. CONTACT DEPARTURE ON ONE TWO FOUR POINT SIX AT OR ABOVE TWO THOUSAND FOR CLEARANCE.

choices.text: VFR departure authorized. Contact Departure on one two four point six at or above two thousand for clearance, N123AB.
IFR departure authorized on one two four point six, N123AB.
VFR departure authorized, clearance void if not off in five minutes, N123AB.
Contact tower after departure for IFR release, N123AB.

items.explanation: Per 4-3-9a, the phraseology is VFR DEPARTURE AUTHORIZED. CONTACT (facility) ON (frequency) AT (location or time if required) FOR CLEARANCE.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: VFR DEPARTURE AUTHORIZED. CONTACT DEPARTURE ON ONE TWO FOUR POINT SIX FOR CLEARANCE.
items.display_text: IFR DEPARTURE AUTHORIZED CONTACT DEPARTURE ON ONE TWO FOUR POINT SIX FOR CLEARANCE
items.explanation: Per 4-3-9a, the aircraft is being authorized for a VFR departure while waiting to obtain the IFR clearance.

items.situation: The facility responsible for issuing the IFR clearance cannot issue it yet. The pilot insists on departing VFR and picking up the IFR clearance airborne instead of waiting on the ground.
items.para_context: Under 4-3-9b, inform the pilot the facility is unable to issue the clearance and suggest the delay be taken on the ground. If the pilot insists on departing VFR, inform the facility or sector holding the flight plan of the pilot's intentions and, if possible, the VFR departure time.

choices.text: Advise the pilot the IFR clearance is unavailable, suggest the delay be taken on the ground, and if the pilot still departs VFR, notify the facility holding the flight plan of the pilot's intentions and departure time if possible.
Cancel the IFR flight plan immediately because VFR departure voids any later IFR pickup.
Issue the IFR clearance anyway because pilot insistence substitutes for facility approval.
Tell the pilot to depart VFR without notifying the facility holding the IFR flight plan.

items.explanation: Per 4-3-9b, if the clearance cannot be issued and the pilot still insists on departing VFR, the facility holding the flight plan must be informed.

items.instruction: Which pilot readback correctly acknowledges the holding clearance?
items.clearance: CLEARED TO WALLY, HOLD NORTH, AS PUBLISHED, DELAY INDEFINITE, SNOW REMOVAL IN PROGRESS, EXPECT FURTHER CLEARANCE ONE ONE THREE ZERO.

choices.text: Cleared to Wally, hold north as published, delay indefinite, snow removal in progress, expect further clearance one one three zero, N123AB.
Cleared to Wally, no delay expected, N123AB.
Cleared to Wally, hold south as published, N123AB.
Expect further clearance at Wally only if I lose communications, N123AB.

items.explanation: Per 4-6-1, when delay is expected the clearance may include the clearance limit, holding instructions, reason if known, and an expect further clearance time.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: CLEARED TO WALLY, NO DELAY EXPECTED.
items.display_text: CLEARED TO WALLY NO HOLD EXPECTED
items.explanation: Per 4-6-1b, the approved phraseology is CLEARED TO (fix), NO DELAY EXPECTED.

items.situation: You know an aircraft will need holding at the clearance limit and it is about six minutes from the fix. Another controller wants to wait until the aircraft reaches the fix before issuing the hold so the frequency stays clear until the last possible moment.
items.para_context: Under 4-6-1c4, when delay is expected, issue the clearance limit, holding instructions, and EFC at least 5 minutes before the aircraft is estimated to reach the clearance limit. If the aircraft is less than 5 minutes from the fix, issue them immediately.

choices.text: Issue the hold items now, because the aircraft is within the point where the clearance limit, holding instructions, and EFC should already be out at least 5 minutes before the fix.
Wait until the aircraft reaches the fix because holding instructions should not be issued before arrival at the clearance limit.
Issue only the EFC and omit the holding instructions until the pilot requests them.
Use delay indefinite without an EFC because all holding clearances may omit the expect-further-clearance time.

items.explanation: Per 4-6-1c4, when delay is expected the required holding items are issued at least 5 minutes before the aircraft reaches the clearance limit, or immediately if already closer than that.

items.instruction: Which pilot readback correctly acknowledges the holding instructions?
items.clearance: HOLD EAST OF SIDNEY V-O-R ON VICTOR TWO-TWO-FIVE, SEVEN MILE LEG, LEFT TURNS.

choices.text: Hold east of Sidney V-O-R on Victor Two-Two-Five, seven mile leg, left turns, N123AB.
Hold east of Sidney V-O-R on Victor Two-Two-Five, seven minute leg, right turns, N123AB.
Hold west of Sidney V-O-R as published, N123AB.

items.situation: A pilot calls airborne and wants to airfile IFR to the destination airport. The aircraft is still outside your area of jurisdiction, and no prior coordination has been completed. items.situation: An aircraft in your sector is part of a known traffic management initiative, and another controller wants to shortcut the flight with a direct clearance that would take it off its flight plan route through a protected segment without any TMU coordination because the route looks open right now.

items.situation: Your facility is switching the active ILS from one runway to another at an airport with multiple ILS systems that are not used simultaneously. items.situation: At an airport with both a SID and a DVA, an aircraft has already been assigned a SID. Another controller wants to use the DVA instead and says you can just vector the aircraft after takeoff without amending the departure clearance until the pilot is already on the runway.
items.para_context: Under 5-8-2a and b, concurrent use of both a SID and DVA is not permitted. To cancel a previously assigned SID and subsequently utilize a DVA, or vice versa, an amended departure clearance is required and must be provided in time for the pilot to brief the changes before entering the runway.

choices.text: Issue an amended departure clearance in time for the pilot to brief the change before entering the runway; do not use the SID and DVA concurrently.
Use the SID for obstacle protection and the DVA for the initial vector, because both may be used together if the runway is the same.
Wait until the aircraft is rolling before amending the clearance, because the pilot can brief the DVA after takeoff.
Cancel the SID verbally after liftoff without amending the departure clearance, because the initial heading replaces the departure procedure automatically.

items.explanation: Per 5-8-2, switching between a SID and a DVA requires an amended clearance provided in a timely manner, and the two are not used concurrently.

items.clearance: TRAFFIC ALERT, N123AB, TURN RIGHT IMMEDIATELY HEADING ZERO NINER ZERO, CLIMB AND MAINTAIN THREE THOUSAND.

choices.text: Traffic alert, turning right immediately heading zero niner zero, climb and maintain three thousand, N123AB.
Traffic alert, turning left immediately heading zero niner zero, climb and maintain three thousand, N123AB.
Traffic alert, turning right immediately heading zero niner zero, descend and maintain three thousand, N123AB.
Traffic alert, remaining on final, N123AB.

items.explanation: Per 5-9-7, breakout instructions for simultaneous independent approaches use immediate turn instructions and may include a climb or descent to maintain separation.

items.situation: During simultaneous independent parallel approaches, you observe one aircraft continuing toward the NTZ. Another controller wants to wait another sweep to see whether the aircraft corrects back on its own before issuing any breakout instructions.
items.para_context: Under 5-9-7, when an aircraft is observed penetrating, or in your judgment will penetrate, the NTZ, issue the required breakout instructions immediately and alter the course of aircraft on the adjacent final approach course as necessary to avoid the deviating aircraft.

choices.text: Issue the breakout instructions immediately and, if necessary, alter the course of aircraft on the adjacent final approach course to avoid the deviating aircraft.
Wait to see whether the aircraft corrects on its own, because breakout instructions should only be used after an actual NTZ penetration is complete.
Continue monitoring without intervening unless both aircraft are at the same altitude.
Transfer both aircraft to tower and let tower handle the deviation visually.

items.explanation: Per 5-9-7, breakout action is taken when the aircraft is observed penetrating, or in your judgment will penetrate, the NTZ.

items.clearance: YOU HAVE CROSSED THE FINAL APPROACH COURSE. TURN RIGHT IMMEDIATELY AND RETURN TO FINAL APPROACH COURSE.

choices.text: You have crossed the final approach course, turning right immediately and returning to final approach course, N123AB.
You have crossed the final approach course, turning left immediately and returning to final approach course, N123AB.

Traffic in sight, continuing visually, N123AB.

items.explanation: Per 5-9-9, when an aircraft overshoots the turn-on or continues toward the NTZ, instruct it to return immediately to the correct final approach course.

items.explanation: Per 5-9-9, the monitor controller's corrective instruction must contain the correct immediate turn direction.

items.situation: During the visual segment of a SOIA, the trailing offset aircraft reports that the lead straight-in traffic is no longer in sight. Another controller wants the aircraft to continue because the runway environment is still visible.
items.para_context: Under 5-9-9f, if the pilot of the trailing offset aircraft loses visual contact with the lead straight-in traffic in the visual segment between the offset approach MAP and the runway threshold, the pilot must advise ATC as soon as practical and follow the published missed approach procedure. If necessary, issue alternate missed approach instructions.

choices.text: Have the aircraft follow the published missed approach and issue alternate missed approach instructions if necessary.
Allow the aircraft to continue visually as long as the runway environment remains in sight.
Keep the aircraft on the offset course to touchdown and let tower resolve the traffic visually.
Tell the aircraft to continue only if it can maintain wake turbulence avoidance, because loss of traffic sight alone does not affect SOIA continuation.

items.explanation: Per 5-9-9f, loss of visual contact with the lead straight-in traffic requires the trailing offset aircraft to advise ATC and follow the published missed approach procedure.

Pattern altitude six thousand, left turns, report initial, Air Force Three Six Eight.
Pattern altitude five thousand, right turns, report initial, Air Force Three Six Eight.
