items.situation: A military aircraft requests the overhead maneuver, but the overhead pattern would extend into the path of a departure and a possible missed approach. Another controller wants to approve the overhead anyway because the airport has a standard overhead pattern.
items.para_context: Under 3-10-12d and e, the existence of a standard overhead maneuver pattern does not eliminate the possible requirement for an aircraft to conform to a conventional rectangular pattern if an overhead maneuver cannot be approved. Timely and positive controller action is required to prevent a conflict when an overhead pattern could extend into the path of a departing or missed approach aircraft.

choices.text: Take timely action to prevent the conflict and require the aircraft to use a conventional rectangular pattern if the overhead maneuver cannot be approved safely.
Approve the overhead anyway, because a published standard overhead pattern automatically protects against departure and missed approach conflicts.
Approve the overhead and let the pilot resolve any conflict visually after reaching the initial point.
Deny the arrival entirely until the departure path is no longer used at the airport.

items.explanation: Per 3-10-12, standard overhead patterns do not override conflict prevention requirements, and conventional rectangular patterns may be required if an overhead maneuver cannot be approved.

items.situation: A helicopter requests takeoff from a non-movement area off the airport boundary. The operation appears reasonable. Another controller wants to issue a normal takeoff clearance from that location to keep the phraseology simple.
items.para_context: Under 3-11-2b, if takeoff is requested from a non-movement area, an area not authorized for helicopter or powered-lift use, or an area off the airport, and the operation appears reasonable, use the phraseology that departure from the requested location will be at the pilot's own risk, with additional instructions and caution as necessary.

choices.text: Use the own-risk departure phraseology, with any additional instructions or caution needed.
Issue a normal takeoff clearance from the off-airport location, because the pilot already accepted the risk by asking.
Deny the operation automatically, because off-airport helicopter departures are never allowed.
Issue hover-taxi instructions only and wait for the pilot to move onto a runway before using any takeoff phraseology.

items.explanation: Per 3-11-2b, reasonable helicopter or powered-lift departures from non-movement or off-airport locations use the own-risk departure phraseology instead of the normal takeoff clearance.

items.situation: ASDE shows an unidentified target or track on the runway. A departure is ready and another controller says to let it roll because the pilot reports the runway looks clear from the cockpit.
items.para_context: Under 3-6-3c, do not allow an aircraft to begin departure roll or cross the landing threshold whenever there is an unidentified target or track displayed on the runway.

choices.text: Do not allow the departure to begin takeoff roll or cross the landing threshold while the unidentified target or track is displayed on the runway.
Issue takeoff clearance if the pilot reports the runway appears clear visually, because ASDE targets are advisory only.
Permit the departure to line up and begin the roll, but instruct the pilot to reject if the target becomes visible.
Ignore the unidentified target if no vehicles or aircraft were expected on the runway.

items.explanation: Per 3-6-3c, an unidentified runway target or track bars allowing a departure roll or a crossing of the landing threshold.

items.situation: A surveillance approach will terminate in a circle-to-land maneuver. Another controller wants to issue final descent first and ask the pilot's approach category later if needed.
items.para_context: Under 5-11-3b, when it is determined that the surveillance approach will terminate in a circle-to-land maneuver, request the aircraft approach category from the pilot and, after receiving it, provide the applicable circling MDA prior to issuing final descent for the approach.

choices.text: Request the aircraft approach category and issue the applicable circling MDA before issuing final descent.

Use the straight-in MDA for all surveillance approaches until the pilot reports the runway in sight.
Let the pilot choose the circling MDA without providing it from ATC, because the chart contains the necessary information.

items.situation: A helicopter requests landing at a non-movement area off the airport. The operation appears reasonable. Another controller wants to issue a normal landing clearance from that location because the pilot already requested it.
items.para_context: Under 3-11-6b, if landing is requested to a non-movement area, an unauthorized area, or off the airport, and the operation appears reasonable, use the phraseology that landing at the requested location will be at the pilot's own risk, with additional instructions and caution as necessary.

choices.text: Use the own-risk landing phraseology, with any additional instructions or caution required.
Issue a normal landing clearance from the off-airport location because the pilot's request itself authorizes it.
Deny the landing automatically because helicopters may never land off the airport under ATC control.
Issue hover-taxi instructions until the helicopter reaches a runway, then clear it to land there instead.

items.explanation: Per 3-11-6b, reasonable off-airport or non-movement-area landing requests use the own-risk landing phraseology rather than the normal landing clearance.

items.situation: A military aircraft requests a simulated flameout approach. Another controller wants to approve it immediately because the tower has handled these before, even though no one has confirmed the high-key altitude and the applicable procedure details for today.
items.para_context: Under 3-10-13a, SFO, ELP, and practice precautionary approaches are authorized only when the required agreement or local operating procedure is in effect, required coordination and traffic-information provisions are met, and the high-key altitude or practice precautionary approach maneuvering altitudes are obtained prior to approving the approach.

choices.text: Do not approve the approach until the required procedure is in effect, the coordination requirements are met, and the high-key or maneuvering altitude information has been obtained.
Approve the approach now and obtain the high-key altitude later, because the pilot will provide it by low key if it matters.
Approve the approach without the agreement or procedure as long as the aircraft remains in VFR conditions.
Treat the request as a standard overhead maneuver and ignore the special SFO and ELP coordination requirements.

items.explanation: Per 3-10-13a, the required agreement, coordination, traffic information, and high-key or maneuvering-altitude information must be in place before the approach is approved.

items.situation: The FFM remote status unit alarms while an arriving aircraft is still outside the middle marker. Another controller says to ignore it unless the aircraft reports any localizer problem, because false alarms sometimes happen.
items.para_context: Under 3-3-7e, when the remote status unit indicates localizer alarm and the aircraft is outside the middle marker, or 1/2 mile final where no middle marker exists, check for visible critical-area encroachment. If the alarm has not cleared prior to the aircraft reaching the middle marker or 1/2 mile final, immediately issue the advisory that the localizer is unreliable.

choices.text: Check for visible critical-area encroachment, and if the alarm has not cleared before the aircraft reaches the middle marker or 1/2 mile final, immediately issue the localizer-unreliable advisory.
Ignore the alarm unless the pilot reports a localizer problem, because false alarms remove the ATC action requirement.
Wait until the aircraft passes the middle marker before deciding whether to issue any advisory, because earlier notification is unnecessary.
Cancel the approach immediately whenever any FFM alarm occurs, regardless of aircraft position or whether the alarm clears.

items.explanation: Per 3-3-7e, the controller checks for possible causes and issues the unreliable-localizer advisory if the alarm remains before the specified point on final.

items.situation: An aircraft is nearing its clearance limit and no delay is expected. Another controller wants to wait until the aircraft reaches the fix before issuing clearance beyond the limit because the route is simple and the pilot can hold if needed.
items.para_context: Under 4-6-2a, if no delay is expected, issue a clearance beyond the clearance limit as soon as possible and, whenever possible, at least 5 minutes before the aircraft reaches the fix.

choices.text: Issue the clearance beyond the clearance limit as soon as possible and, when possible, at least 5 minutes before the aircraft reaches the fix.

Let the aircraft hold automatically and issue the onward clearance only after the pilot requests it.
Issue only the new altitude and wait to provide the route beyond the clearance limit after the aircraft crosses the fix.

items.situation: A pilot reports the localizer is unreliable. A second aircraft then reports the same problem, and the standby equipment is available.
items.para_context: Under 2-1-10a, if a second aircraft confirms the malfunction, or if no second report is available, activate the standby equipment or request the monitor facility to activate it. If the standby equipment operates normally, continue use, record the incident, and notify Technical Operations. If the standby equipment is not available or also malfunctions, inform Technical Operations and request further instructions.

choices.text: Activate the standby equipment or have the monitor facility do so, continue use only if it operates normally, record the incident, and notify Technical Operations.
Keep using the primary localizer without change because one confirmation does not justify any equipment action.
Immediately shut the NAVAID down permanently and stop all approaches until headquarters issues written guidance.
Ignore the second report unless the pilot also reports the exact cause of the malfunction.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-10a, confirmation by a second aircraft triggers standby activation or monitor-facility action, followed by recording the incident and notifying Technical Operations.

items.situation: A controller working a local position sees an aircraft on an uncertified tower display and wants to issue a radar traffic advisory based solely on that display.
items.para_context: Under 3-1-9a, uncertified tower display workstations may only be used as an aid for visually locating aircraft or determining spatial relationship. Do not provide radar services or traffic advisories using an uncertified tower display workstation.

items.para_context: Under 4-4-1c1, GNSS-equipped RNAV aircraft on random RNAV routes, when not radar monitored, must be cleared via or reported established on a point-to-point route. The points must be published NAVAIDs, waypoints, fixes, or airports recallable from the aircraft navigation database, displayed on controller video maps or depicted on the controller chart, and when applying nonradar separation the maximum distance between points must not exceed 500 miles.

choices.text: Do not use that requested segment as filed; clear the aircraft via a point-to-point route using published recallable points shown on your controller display, with no more than 500 miles between points when applying nonradar separation.
Approve the request because any RNAV-equipped aircraft may use unpublished user waypoints of any length when not radar monitored.
Approve the route if the pilot verbally confirms the waypoint is in the aircraft database, even if the point is not displayed to the controller.
Convert the route to direct-to navigation between the same points because distance limits only apply on ATS routes.

items.explanation: Per 4-4-1c1, nonradar random RNAV routing uses point-to-point routing with published, recallable, controller-displayed points, and the distance between points may not exceed 500 miles when nonradar separation is applied.

items.clearance: CLEARED TO GRAND RAPIDS VOR.

choices.text: Cleared to Grand Rapids VOR, N123AB.
Cleared to Grand Rapids radial, N123AB.
Cleared to Grand Rapids airport, N123AB.
Direct Grand Rapids VORTAC, N123AB.

items.explanation: Per 2-5-2, when a NAVAID is used as a clearance limit, state the name of the NAVAID followed by the type if known.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: APPLETON ZERO FIVE ZERO RADIAL.
items.display_text: APPLETON ZERO FIVE ZERO BEARING
items.explanation: Per 2-5-2, VOR, VORTAC, TACAN, and GPS waypoint descriptions use the word radial, azimuth, or bearing as appropriate. This example is a radial.

items.clearance: VERIFY ASSIGNED FLIGHT LEVEL TWO THREE ZERO.

choices.text: Verify assigned flight level two three zero, N123AB.
Verify assigned altitude two three zero, N123AB.
Affirmative flight level two three zero, N123AB.
Maintain flight level two three zero, N123AB.

items.explanation: Per 4-5-9, when an aircraft has been assigned a flight level at or above the lowest usable flight level, the phraseology is VERIFY ASSIGNED FLIGHT LEVEL.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: NEGATIVE. MAINTAIN ONE ONE THOUSAND.
items.display_text: AFFIRMATIVE MAINTAIN ONE ONE THOUSAND
items.explanation: Per 4-5-9, when an altitude readback is not correct, the controller responds with NEGATIVE and restates the assigned altitude.

items.situation: A succeeding arrival is on final and another aircraft is line up and wait on the runway. A controller wants to use anticipating separation and issue the landing clearance now because the departure should be rolling soon.
items.para_context: Under 3-10-6, anticipating separation may be used when the controller observes the aircraft positions and determines prescribed runway separation will exist when the succeeding aircraft crosses the landing threshold. It must not be applied during LUAW operations except as specifically authorized in 3-10-5e2, and applicable traffic information must be issued when using the provision.

choices.text: Do not apply anticipating separation in this LUAW situation unless the specific authorization in 3-10-5e2 applies.
Use anticipating separation whenever a departure is expected to move before the arrival reaches the threshold, even during LUAW.
Issue the landing clearance and omit traffic information because LUAW traffic is already visible on the runway.
Cancel the departure's LUAW and then automatically clear the arrival to land without considering threshold separation.

items.explanation: Per 3-10-6b, anticipating separation is not used during LUAW operations except as specifically authorized.

items.clearance: NOT IN SIGHT, RUNWAY THREE SIX CLEARED TO LAND.

choices.text: Not in sight, runway three six cleared to land, N123AB.
In sight, runway three six cleared to land, N123AB.
Not in sight, runway three six cleared to takeoff, N123AB.
Runway three six not in sight, going around, N123AB.

items.explanation: Per 3-10-7, when the aircraft should be seen but is not visually observed, advise that it is not in sight as part of the landing clearance and restate the landing runway.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: NOT IN SIGHT, RUNWAY THREE SIX CLEARED TO LAND.
items.display_text: NOT IN SIGHT RUNWAY THREE SIX CLEARED TO TAKEOFF
items.explanation: Per 3-10-7, the restated runway clearance is a landing clearance, not a takeoff clearance.

items.clearance: NO BRAKING ACTION REPORTS RECEIVED FOR RUNWAY TWO SEVEN.

choices.text: No braking action reports received for runway two seven, N123AB.
Braking action medium reported for runway two seven, N123AB.
No braking action reports received for taxiway two seven, N123AB.
Braking action advisories no longer in effect, N123AB.

items.explanation: Per 3-3-5, if no braking action report has been received for the runway of intended use, issue the advisory that no braking action reports have been received for that runway.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: BRAKING ACTION ADVISORIES ARE IN EFFECT.
items.display_text: BRAKING ACTION REPORTS ARE IN EFFECT
items.explanation: Per 3-3-5, the ATIS statement is Braking Action Advisories are in effect.

items.clearance: GATE HOLD PROCEDURES ARE IN EFFECT. ALL AIRCRAFT CONTACT GROUND ON ONE TWO ONE POINT SEVEN FOR ENGINE START TIME. EXPECT ENGINE START/TAXI AT ONE FOUR THREE ZERO.

choices.text: Gate hold procedures are in effect. Contact ground on one two one point seven for engine start time. Expect engine start and taxi at one four three zero, N123AB.
Gate hold procedures are no longer in effect. Contact ground on one two one point seven for engine start time, N123AB.
Start engines now and contact tower when ready to taxi, N123AB.
Expect engine shutdown at one four three zero, N123AB.

items.explanation: Per 3-9-2, when gate hold procedures are in effect, advise aircraft of the engine start contact position and frequency and the expected engine start or taxi time as appropriate.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: ADVISE WHEN READY TO TAXI.
items.display_text: ADVISE WHEN READY TO FLY
items.explanation: Per 3-9-2, the gate-hold phraseology is ADVISE WHEN READY TO TAXI.

items.clearance: N13WA REPORT CANCELLATION OF IFR THIS FREQUENCY OR WITH MC ALESTER RADIO.

choices.text: Report cancellation of IFR this frequency or with Mc Alester Radio, N13WA.
Report cancellation of VFR this frequency or with Mc Alester Radio, N13WA.
IFR cancellation received, N13WA.
Remain IFR this frequency, N13WA.

items.explanation: Per 4-2-10, at airports without an air/ground communications station, the pilot may be instructed to report cancellation of IFR on the frequency in use or with Flight Service or the named FSS or ATC controlling facility.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: N13WA IFR CANCELLATION RECEIVED.
items.display_text: N13WA VFR CANCELLATION RECEIVED
items.explanation: Per 4-2-10, the acknowledgment phraseology is IFR CANCELLATION RECEIVED.

items.clearance: THIS WILL BE A NO-GYRO SURVEILLANCE APPROACH. TURN LEFT. STOP TURN.

choices.text: This will be a no-gyro surveillance approach. Turn left, stop turn, N123AB.
This will be a no-gyro P-A-R approach. Turn right, stop turn, N123AB.
This will be a standard surveillance approach. Continue present heading, N123AB.
Make half-standard rate turns now, N123AB.

items.explanation: Per 5-10-3, the aircraft is informed of the type of no-gyro approach before vectors are issued, and the controller instructs when to start and stop turn.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: MAKE HALF-STANDARD RATE TURNS.
items.display_text: MAKE FULL-STANDARD RATE TURNS
items.explanation: Per 5-10-3, after turn on to final and before the approach gate, the controller instructs the aircraft to make half-standard rate turns.

items.clearance: CONTACT OMAHA FINAL CONTROLLER ON ONE TWO FIVE POINT THREE.

choices.text: Contact Omaha Final Controller on one two five point three, N123AB.
Contact Omaha Tower on one two five point three, N123AB.
Contact Omaha Final Controller on one two three point five, N123AB.
Remain this frequency for final guidance, N123AB.

items.explanation: Per 5-10-8, when changing frequency for final approach guidance, include the name of the facility in the transmission.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: CONTACT OMAHA FINAL CONTROLLER ON ONE TWO FIVE POINT THREE.
items.display_text: CONTACT OMAHA LOCAL CONTROLLER ON ONE TWO FIVE POINT THREE
items.explanation: Per 5-10-8, the phraseology identifies the final controller when instructing the aircraft to change frequency for final approach guidance.

items.clearance: FIVE MILES FROM RUNWAY. DESCEND TO YOUR MINIMUM DESCENT ALTITUDE.

choices.text: Five miles from runway, descend to your minimum descent altitude, N123AB.
Five miles from runway, maintain present altitude, N123AB.
Five miles from airport, climb to your minimum descent altitude, N123AB.
Descend and maintain glidepath, N123AB.

items.explanation: Per 5-11-4, when the aircraft reaches the descent point and no descent restriction exists, advise the aircraft to descend to minimum descent altitude.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: DESCEND TO YOUR MINIMUM DESCENT ALTITUDE.
items.display_text: CLIMB TO YOUR MINIMUM DESCENT ALTITUDE
items.explanation: Per 5-11-4, at the descent point the controller instructs the aircraft to descend, not climb, to the MDA.

items.clearance: NO GLIDEPATH INFORMATION AVAILABLE. IF RUNWAY LIGHTS NOT IN SIGHT, EXECUTE MISSED APPROACH.

choices.text: No glidepath information available. If runway lights not in sight, execute missed approach, N123AB.
No localizer information available. If runway lights not in sight, execute missed approach, N123AB.
No glidepath information available. If runway lights not in sight, continue visually, N123AB.
Glidepath available. Execute missed approach, N123AB.

items.explanation: Per 5-12-10, if the elevation portion of PAR fails during a precision approach, discontinue PAR instructions and tell the aircraft to take over visually or, if unable, execute a missed approach.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: NO GLIDEPATH INFORMATION AVAILABLE.
items.display_text: NO TOUCHDOWN INFORMATION AVAILABLE
items.explanation: Per 5-12-10, the failed element is the glidepath or elevation portion, and the phraseology says no glidepath information available.

items.situation: During a PAR approach, the pilot reports the approach lights in sight but does not request to proceed visually. Another controller says to terminate PAR guidance immediately because any report of the lights in sight ends the approach.
items.para_context: Under 5-12-8, discontinue precision approach guidance if requested by the pilot, if continuation is questionable, when the aircraft passes the landing threshold, or when the pilot reports runway or approach lights in sight and requests to or advises that he or she will proceed visually. A pilot report of runway in sight or visual alone is not a request to proceed visually. When guidance is discontinued, advise position and to proceed visually.

choices.text: Continue PAR guidance until the pilot requests to or advises that the aircraft will proceed visually, because a report of the lights in sight alone does not end the approach.
Terminate precision guidance immediately upon any report of approach lights in sight, even without a request to proceed visually.
Switch the aircraft to tower automatically and stop monitoring the final controller frequency as soon as the pilot sees the lights.
Cancel the landing clearance and instruct the aircraft to go missed because reports of the lights in sight are not allowed on PAR approaches.

items.explanation: Per 5-12-8, a report of runway in sight or visual is not itself a request to proceed visually. PAR procedures are discontinued only after the pilot requests or advises the intention to proceed visually and is instructed accordingly, or under the other listed conditions.

items.situation: You are providing a surveillance approach. The aircraft is approaching the missed approach point, the pilot has not reported the runway or approach lights in sight, and you no longer believe continuation to the MAP can be kept safe.
items.para_context: Under 5-11-6, discontinue surveillance approach guidance when the pilot requests it, when safe continuation to the MAP is questionable, or when the aircraft is over the MAP. If the runway or approach lights are not in sight when guidance is discontinued, advise the aircraft of its position and instruct it to execute a missed approach unless it can proceed visually.

choices.text: Discontinue guidance, advise the aircraft of its position, and instruct it to execute the missed approach unless the runway or approach lights are in sight or the aircraft can proceed visually on a point-in-space procedure.
Continue radar guidance to touchdown because surveillance approach guidance must continue once the aircraft is inside the final segment.
Tell the pilot to decide whether to continue and withhold position information until the aircraft reports a missed approach.
Cancel the approach clearance and vector the aircraft visually around the airport without issuing missed approach instructions.

items.explanation: Per 5-11-6, when surveillance approach guidance is discontinued and the runway or approach lights are not in sight, advise position and require the missed approach unless the pilot can proceed visually under the stated conditions.

items.situation: A pilot specifically requests to be vectored inside the approach gate for training, and another controller plans to turn the aircraft onto final inside the gate about a mile before the final approach fix.
items.para_context: Under 5-9-1a2, if specifically requested by the pilot, an aircraft may be vectored to intercept the final approach course inside the approach gate, but no closer than the final approach fix.

choices.text: You may vector inside the approach gate because the pilot requested it, but not closer than the final approach fix.
You may vector the aircraft anywhere inside the gate, including inside the final approach fix, because the pilot requested it.
You may never vector inside the approach gate under any circumstances.
You may vector inside the final approach fix if the weather is VFR and the aircraft is established before crossing the runway threshold.

items.explanation: Per 5-9-1a2, a pilot-requested inside-the-gate intercept is allowed, but not closer than the final approach fix.

items.situation: An aircraft has already intercepted the final approach course, but inside the approach gate you observe it drifting left of course. Another controller suggests issuing another aggressive vector to force the aircraft back onto final immediately.
items.para_context: Under 5-9-2b2, when deviations from final are observed inside the approach gate, inform the pilot of the aircraft's observed position and ask intentions. Outside the approach gate, you may vector for another approach if necessary.

choices.text: Inform the pilot of the aircraft's observed position relative to the airport and course, then ask intentions.
Issue another large intercept vector inside the approach gate because the aircraft must always be forced back to course before asking intentions.
Clear the aircraft for a visual approach immediately and stop providing any further information about its position.
Terminate radar service because any deviation inside the approach gate automatically cancels the approach clearance.

items.explanation: Per 5-9-2b2, inside the approach gate the controller informs the pilot of the aircraft's position and asks intentions rather than issuing a new intercept vector as the default response.

items.situation: An aircraft under your control reports it is responding to a TAWS alert and begins an immediate climb. Another controller wants you to issue a descent to keep it clear of other traffic.
items.para_context: Under 2-1-30, do not issue control instructions contrary to the TAWS procedure the crew advises they are executing. Provide safety alerts and traffic advisories as appropriate. Once the TAWS maneuver has begun, the controller is not responsible for approved separation until one of the listed restoration conditions is met.

choices.text: Do not issue instructions contrary to the TAWS response; provide appropriate safety alerts and traffic advisories, and resume approved-separation responsibility only after one of the listed conditions is met.
Immediately override the TAWS maneuver with a descent clearance because ATC separation always takes priority over on-board terrain warnings.
Stop issuing any traffic or terrain information because the crew is now fully responsible for everything around the aircraft.
Treat the aircraft as NORDO and begin overdue-aircraft procedures as soon as the TAWS maneuver starts.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-30, controllers do not issue instructions contrary to the TAWS maneuver and continue to provide safety alerts and traffic advisories as appropriate.

items.situation: Traffic is developing quickly and you need a climbing aircraft to comply at once to avoid an imminent conflict. A trainee suggests using the word 'expedite' because it sounds less forceful than 'immediately.'
items.para_context: Under 2-1-5, use 'immediately' only when expeditious compliance is required to avoid an imminent situation. Use 'expedite' only when prompt compliance is required to avoid the development of an imminent situation. If time permits, include the reason.

choices.text: Use 'immediately' because compliance is required now to avoid an imminent situation, and include the reason if time permits.
Use 'expedite' because it means the same thing as 'immediately' in every case.
Avoid either term because controllers should never add urgency words to altitude instructions.
Issue only the new altitude and wait to see whether the pilot climbs fast enough before adding urgency language.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-5, 'immediately' is reserved for avoiding an imminent situation, while 'expedite' is used when prompt compliance is needed to avoid the development of one.

items.situation: An en route sector is staffed with a Radar Position and a Radar Coordinator Position. The Radar Coordinator starts directing traffic-flow actions and then tells the Radar Position that the coordinator now owns aircraft separation for the sector because the coordinator position is staffed.
items.para_context: Under 2-10-1, the sector team as a whole is responsible for safe and efficient operation. When the Radar Coordinator Position is staffed, it assumes responsibility for managing traffic flows, but the Radar Position retains responsibility for aircraft separation.

choices.text: Continue to let the Radar Coordinator manage traffic flows, but keep aircraft-separation responsibility with the Radar Position.
Transfer aircraft-separation responsibility to the Radar Coordinator Position whenever that position is staffed.
Stop all coordination because staffing an RC position removes the need for team responsibility.
Assign all radios and interphone work to the Radar Position and prohibit the other team members from assisting.

items.explanation: Per 2-10-1, the Radar Coordinator manages traffic flows when staffed, but the Radar Position retains responsibility for aircraft separation.

items.situation: A terminal radar team is busy. The Radar Associate accepts an automated handoff that is necessary to keep traffic moving but does not tell the Radar Position because the associate assumes the automation is enough notice.
items.para_context: Under 2-10-2, the Radar Associate may assist by accepting or initiating automated handoffs necessary for the smooth operation of the facility or sector, but must ensure the Radar Position is made immediately aware of any action taken.

choices.text: Ensure the Radar Position is made immediately aware of the automated handoff action taken by the Radar Associate.
Let the automation stand on its own because the Radar Position does not need to be told about associate handoff actions.
Require the Radar Associate to stop using automated handoffs entirely and do only interphone coordination.
Transfer separation responsibility to the Radar Associate as soon as the automated handoff is accepted.

items.explanation: Per 2-10-2c2f, the Radar Associate can take necessary automated handoff actions but must ensure the Radar Position is immediately aware of them.

items.situation: A tower cab is staffed with tower positions and a Tower Coordinator Position. The coordinator begins managing traffic flows and then tells the local controller that the coordinator now owns aircraft separation because the coordinator position is staffed.
items.para_context: Under 2-10-3, the Tower Positions retain responsibility for aircraft separation and traffic flows. When the Tower Coordinator Position is staffed, it assumes responsibility for managing traffic flows, but the Tower Positions retain responsibility for aircraft separation.

choices.text: Let the Tower Coordinator manage traffic flows, but keep aircraft-separation responsibility with the Tower Positions.
Transfer aircraft-separation responsibility to the Tower Coordinator whenever that position is staffed.
Suspend all coordination because staffing the coordinator position removes the need for team roles.
Move all strip-marking and flight-plan processing to the local controller because tower associates and flight data can no longer assist.

items.explanation: Per 2-10-3, the coordinator manages traffic flows when staffed, but the Tower Positions retain aircraft-separation responsibility.

items.situation: A controller wants to use a locally approved identifier on an interphone coordination line to save time, and another controller says it is acceptable because the identifier is already authorized inside the facility.
items.para_context: Under 2-3-10, locally approved identifiers may be used only within the facility and not on teletypewriter or interphone circuits. Authorized control and clearance symbols or abbreviations must be used for recording clearances, reports, and instructions, and aircraft control status must always be current.

choices.text: Do not use the local identifier on the interphone circuit; keep local identifiers inside the facility only and use the authorized notation methods instead.
Use the local identifier on any interphone or teletype circuit as long as everyone on the watch understands it.
Stop using control abbreviations entirely and switch every strip entry to full plain-language phrases only.
Leave the strip unchanged because control status does not need to be current if a verbal coordination was completed.

items.explanation: Per 2-3-10, locally approved identifiers stay within the facility and may not be used on teletypewriter or interphone circuits.

items.situation: An en route controller wants to put a random RNAV latitude/longitude waypoint into a domestic strip, but another controller says only adapted NAVAIDs can appear in space 25. The route request cannot be recorded clearly any other way.
items.para_context: Under 2-3-2b, latitude/longitude coordinates may be used to define waypoints and may be substituted for nonadapted NAVAIDs in space 25 of domestic en route flight progress strips when necessary to accommodate a random RNAV or GNSS route request.

choices.text: Use the latitude/longitude waypoint in space 25 if it is needed to accommodate the random RNAV or GNSS route request.
Refuse to record the waypoint because space 25 can only contain adapted NAVAIDs under all circumstances.
Move the waypoint into block 27 with the beacon code because space 25 cannot be used for routing information.
Leave the strip unchanged and rely only on verbal coordination because random RNAV routes are never written on the strip.

items.explanation: Per 2-3-2b, latitude/longitude waypoints may be substituted for nonadapted NAVAIDs in space 25 when needed to accommodate a random RNAV or GNSS route request.

items.situation: After a catastrophic ATOP failure, the printed oceanic strip shows reduced-separation flags. A trainee assumes the strip is wrong because reduced Mach number technique and RVSM indicators are supposed to appear only on the screen, not on the strip.
items.para_context: Under 2-3-3, ATOP displays information on electronic flight progress strips and, in a catastrophic system failure, prints flight progress strips with the same information in corresponding numbered spaces. Reduced separation flags such as M, R, D/3, and W are part of the recorded strip information.

choices.text: Accept the printed reduced-separation flags as valid strip information because ATOP prints the corresponding numbered data in a catastrophic failure.
Ignore all reduced-separation flags on printed strips because they are display-only items and never belong on a paper strip.
Erase the flags and rewrite the strip from memory because printed ATOP strips cannot be used after a catastrophic failure.
Move the flags into field 22 annotations because field 4 is reserved only for aircraft type and beacon data.

items.explanation: Per 2-3-3a, ATOP prints the corresponding numbered strip information in a catastrophic failure, including reduced-separation flags.

items.situation: A terminal facility wants to omit several strip spaces on arrival and departure strips to simplify marking. The proposal would not create misunderstanding, but the omissions have not been documented in any facility directive.
items.para_context: Under 2-3-4, facility managers may authorize omissions or optional use of certain spaces on terminal strips only when no misunderstanding will result, and those omissions or optional uses must be specified in a facility directive.

choices.text: Do not make the omissions until they are authorized and specified in a facility directive.
Allow the omissions immediately because a verbal briefing is enough whenever controllers agree there is no misunderstanding.
Omit any spaces the local controller prefers, because terminal strip entries are never standardized.
Use the omitted spaces only on overflight strips, because arrivals and departures must always contain every block with no exceptions.

items.explanation: Per 2-3-4, authorized omissions and optional uses must be specified in a facility directive and cannot be made ad hoc.

items.situation: An en route controller is talking to a terminal facility about a heavy aircraft and leaves off the word 'heavy' because the center usually omits weight designators in en route communications. A second controller points out that the aircraft will be followed by traffic with reduced spacing concerns.
items.para_context: Under 2-4-14, en route controllers may omit super or heavy in some communications, but not when communicating with a terminal facility about super or heavy aircraft operations, when the center is providing approach control service, when separation from a following aircraft may become less than 5 miles by approved procedure, or when issuing traffic advisories.

choices.text: Include the heavy designator because the communication involves a terminal facility and reduced-following-aircraft separation could become a factor.
Omit the heavy designator because en route facilities may always leave it off once the aircraft has checked in.
Replace 'heavy' with 'super' because either term may be used for any large aircraft.
Add 'heavy' only if the aircraft is Air Force One or Air Force Two.

items.explanation: Per 2-4-14, en route omission of super or heavy is limited, and one exception is communications with a terminal facility about heavy aircraft operations.

items.situation: A pilot requests ride conditions along the route while moderate turbulence and low ceilings are forecast in your area of jurisdiction. Another controller suggests waiting for unsolicited reports instead of asking for a PIREP because no urgent weather has been reported yet.
items.para_context: Under 2-6-2, solicit PIREPs when requested, when deemed necessary, or when listed conditions exist or are forecast for the area. Controllers must provide PIREP information in sufficient detail to assist pilots in making flight-safety decisions.

choices.text: Solicit the PIREP and ask for the relevant flight conditions or specific conditions over the fix, along the route, or between fixes as appropriate.
Wait for the pilot to volunteer a report because controllers only solicit PIREPs after an urgent report has already been received.
Provide only the forecast and avoid requesting any actual ride or cloud information from the pilot.
Relay the request to another aircraft and tell the original pilot to monitor for secondhand reports.

items.explanation: Per 2-6-2, controllers solicit PIREPs when requested, when necessary, or when listed weather conditions exist or are forecast.

items.situation: From the tower you can plainly see visibility lowering to the south, but you are not the official weather observer and have not yet received a verified ceiling value from the weather station. A pilot asks for the latest weather.
items.para_context: Under 2-6-5, general weather information without specific values may be transmitted without consulting the weather reporting station. Specific values such as ceiling and visibility may be transmitted only if obtained through one of the approved means.

choices.text: You may transmit the general weather information you observe, but do not state specific ceiling or visibility values unless they come from an approved source.
Transmit any specific ceiling and visibility values you estimate from the tower because tower observations are always official weather values.
Do not transmit any weather information at all unless the weather station first calls the tower.
Issue the tower estimate as official weather and skip notifying the weather observer about any difference.

items.explanation: Per 2-6-5, general non-specific weather information may be transmitted directly, but specific values require an approved source.

items.situation: Touchdown RVR is above 2,000 feet, but the midpoint RVR has dropped below 2,000 feet for the runway in use. Local control is about to clear an aircraft for departure and plans to issue only the touchdown RVR because that is the primary sensor.
items.para_context: Under 2-8-2, issue both midpoint and rollout RVR when either is less than 2,000 feet and the touchdown RVR is greater than the midpoint or rollout value. Local control must issue the current RVR before landing or departure as required.

choices.text: Issue both the touchdown RVR and the lower midpoint or rollout RVR before the departure or landing clearance proceeds.
Issue only the touchdown RVR because midpoint and rollout values are never required unless touchdown RVR is also below 2,000 feet.
Withhold all RVR because mixed sensor values automatically make the entire RVR report unusable.
Wait until the aircraft is airborne to advise the lower midpoint or rollout RVR so the departure is not delayed.

items.explanation: Per 2-8-2b and c, local control must issue both values when midpoint or rollout is below 2,000 feet and touchdown is higher.

items.situation: A Category I floatplane is ready for departure from a sea lane behind a preceding Category II departure. The other aircraft is airborne, but landmark reference shows only about 2,000 feet of separation. The local controller wants to release the second aircraft immediately because both are already on the step.
items.para_context: Under 3-12-2, a succeeding Category I aircraft behind a Category II aircraft on the same sea lane needs at least 3,000 feet if using landmark distance determination. Departure may not begin until the required condition or minimum distance exists.

choices.text: Do not allow the takeoff yet; wait until the required 3,000-foot minimum exists or the other aircraft has otherwise crossed the end of the sea lane or turned to avert conflict.
Authorize the takeoff because any airborne preceding aircraft is automatically enough for a Category I follower.
Authorize the takeoff because 2,000 feet exceeds the 1,500-foot minimum used for all sea-lane departures.
Line the aircraft up and wait indefinitely because sea-lane departures may not be cleared until the preceding aircraft lands.

items.explanation: Per 3-12-2a2, a Category I aircraft preceded by a Category II aircraft requires 3,000 feet if using landmark distance determination.

items.situation: Between sunrise and sunset, a Category II seaplane is arriving behind a Category I aircraft that has landed on the same sea lane but has not taxied clear. Using landmarks, you determine the preceding aircraft is only about 2,100 feet from the landing threshold.
items.para_context: Under 3-12-3a, a Category II aircraft landing behind a Category I or II aircraft needs 2,500 feet from the landing threshold if the preceding aircraft has landed but is not yet clear of the sea lane and landmark distance is being used.

choices.text: Do not allow the arriving aircraft to cross the landing threshold yet; wait until the required 2,500-foot minimum exists or the sea lane is clear.
Allow the aircraft to cross the threshold because any landed preceding aircraft is sufficient during daylight if landmarks are available.
Allow the aircraft to land because 2,100 feet exceeds the 2,000-foot minimum used for every sea-lane arrival.
Cancel the approach because sea-lane arrivals may never follow another aircraft until it has taxied completely out of the water area.

items.explanation: Per 3-12-3a2, a Category II aircraft behind a Category I or II arrival needs 2,500 feet from the threshold when using landmark distance.

items.situation: Construction equipment is working just off the movement area, a portion of the lighting system is malfunctioning, and braking action has degraded. A controller suggests waiting until the aircraft is short final to pass the information so the update stays current.
items.para_context: Under 3-3-3, issue airport condition information necessary for safe operation in time for it to be useful to the pilot. This includes construction, rough areas, braking conditions, irregular lighting, volcanic ash, and other pertinent conditions.

choices.text: Issue the pertinent airport condition information in time for it to be useful to the pilot, including the construction, lighting, and braking information as appropriate.
Wait until the aircraft is short final because airport condition information should be delayed until the latest possible moment.
Issue only the braking action because construction and lighting information are not airport condition items.
Pass the information only if the pilot specifically asks for airport conditions.

items.explanation: Per 3-3-3, airport condition information must be issued in time to be useful and must include the listed pertinent conditions.

items.situation: It is daytime and surface visibility has dropped below 2 miles. A departure is about to taxi onto the runway in use, but another controller says runway edge lights are only a night requirement unless a pilot asks for them.
items.para_context: Under 3-4-10b, between sunrise and sunset, runway edge lights are turned on as specified for arrivals and departures when surface visibility is less than 2 miles. For departures, lights are turned on before the aircraft taxies onto the runway.

choices.text: Turn the runway edge lights on before the aircraft taxies onto the runway because daytime visibility is below 2 miles.
Leave the lights off unless the pilot specifically requests them because runway edge lights are a night-only requirement.
Wait until the aircraft begins the takeoff roll, then turn the lights on if needed.
Turn the lights on only after the aircraft leaves the surface area.

items.explanation: Per 3-4-10b and a1, daytime operations with surface visibility below 2 miles require runway edge lights to be on before the departure taxis onto the runway.

items.situation: A pilot says a portion of the runway status lights is illuminated and the aircraft cannot accept the takeoff clearance. You can see the entire runway from the tower and verify that it is clear, but the lights remain on.
items.para_context: Under 3-4-19a1, if the runway is observed to be clear and the runway status lights are still illuminated, the lights must be turned off and the clearance re-issued. Once turned off, they remain off until returned to service by technical operations personnel.

choices.text: Turn the runway status lights off and then re-issue the clearance, since the runway is clear and the lights remain illuminated.
Keep the lights on and require the pilot to depart anyway because ATC clearance overrides runway status lights.
Cancel all runway operations for the day because any false illumination automatically closes the runway.
Ignore the report and issue a different runway because ATC may not turn runway status lights off.

items.explanation: Per 3-4-19a1, if the runway is clear and the lights remain illuminated, ATC turns the lights off and re-issues the clearance.

items.situation: It is daytime, the ceiling is 800 feet, and approaches are being made to a runway served by the approach light system. Another controller says the lights can stay off because it is not yet sunset and no pilot has asked for them.
items.para_context: Under 3-4-5, approach lights are operated between sunrise and sunset when the ceiling is less than 1,000 feet or prevailing visibility is 5 miles or less and approaches are being made to the runway or airport as listed.

choices.text: Turn the approach lights on because daytime operations with a ceiling below 1,000 feet meet the requirement when approaches are being made.
Leave the lights off until sunset because approach lights are only required at night unless a pilot requests them.
Turn the lights on only after the aircraft crosses the threshold.
Keep the lights off because the airport lighting decision belongs only to the arriving pilot.

items.explanation: Per 3-4-5b, approach lights are used between sunrise and sunset when the ceiling is under 1,000 feet or visibility is 5 miles or less and approaches are being made to the applicable runway or airport.

items.situation: Wind is 7 knots favoring Runway 18, but another runway would slightly reduce delay. A controller suggests using the alternate runway because capacity should take priority over wind considerations.
items.para_context: Under 3-5-1, assign the runway most nearly aligned with the wind when the wind is 5 knots or more unless another runway is operationally advantageous or a Runway Use Program is in effect. Tailwind and crosswind considerations take precedence over delay, capacity, and noise-abatement considerations.

choices.text: Use the runway most nearly aligned with the wind unless another runway is operationally advantageous within the limits of the stated tailwind and crosswind priority.
Choose the runway that reduces delay even if it creates the less favorable wind condition, because capacity always comes first.
Treat 7 knots as calm wind and use any runway the controller prefers.
Honor any pilot-requested runway immediately even if it is not operationally practicable.

items.explanation: Per 3-5-1, wind alignment governs at 5 knots or more, and tailwind/crosswind considerations take precedence over delay or capacity.

items.situation: Two Category I aircraft want to operate simultaneously in the same direction on parallel runways whose centerlines are only 250 feet apart. Weather is VFR and radio communication is established with both aircraft.
items.para_context: Under 3-8-3, same-direction simultaneous operations require VFR conditions unless visual separation is applied, radio communication, traffic information, and runway or strip spacing that meets Table 3-8-1. For Category I or II aircraft, runway centerlines must be at least 300 feet apart.

choices.text: Do not authorize the simultaneous same-direction operation because the runway centerline spacing does not meet the required minimum.
Authorize it because VFR and two-way radio communication alone are enough for Category I aircraft.
Authorize it because the spacing minimum applies only to heavy aircraft.
Authorize it if both pilots say they accept visual separation, regardless of runway spacing.

items.explanation: Per 3-8-3 and Table 3-8-1, Category I or II operations need at least 300 feet between runway centerlines for SSDO.

items.situation: The ATIS is out of service and the runway available for departure has been temporarily shortened. A departure is taxiing out, and another controller wants to give only the wind and altimeter because the pilot already has the route clearance.
items.para_context: Under 3-9-1, when ATIS is unavailable and runway length available for departure has been temporarily shortened, controllers must ensure pilots receive the runway number combined with a shortened announcement. Departure information also includes current runway, wind, altimeter, and other applicable items.

choices.text: Ensure the pilot receives the runway number with the shortened announcement, along with the applicable departure information not otherwise omitted.
Omit the shortened announcement because route clearance already implies the runway available for departure.
Wait until after takeoff to mention the shortened runway if the pilot asks for time or weather.
Broadcast the shortened information only on ATIS even though ATIS is unavailable.

items.explanation: Per 3-9-1j, when ATIS is unavailable and runway length is temporarily shortened, the controller must ensure the pilot receives the runway number plus the shortened announcement.

items.situation: A pilot checks in for arrival without stating the ATIS code. The pilot then says the ATIS is unreadable due to radio issues, and rapidly changing wind and braking-action information is current.
items.para_context: Under 2-9-2, ask a pilot to confirm receipt of the current ATIS when the proper code is not stated. If the pilot is unable to receive ATIS, issue the current weather, runway in use, approach/departure information, pertinent NOTAMs, and airport conditions.

choices.text: Verify whether the pilot has the current ATIS, and if not, issue the current weather, runway, approach or departure information, pertinent NOTAMs, and airport conditions.
Assume the pilot has the current information because ATIS is advisory and need not be confirmed.
Delay all weather and runway information until the aircraft reaches short final so the briefing stays current.
Give only the ATIS code change and require the pilot to obtain the rest from another aircraft on frequency.

items.explanation: Per 2-9-2, controllers ensure the pilot has current ATIS information or issue the full current information directly when ATIS cannot be received.

items.situation: An aircraft is being vectored toward the localizer and is about to reach the approach gate. Another controller wants to issue the approach clearance immediately without position information or an altitude-to-maintain-until-established instruction because the intercept looks easy on radar.
items.para_context: Under 5-9-4, before the aircraft reaches the approach gate, issue position relative to a fix, vector to intercept if required, and approach clearance only after the aircraft is established on a published segment or assigned an altitude to maintain until established. Also issue the appropriate monitoring or contact instructions.

choices.text: Issue the aircraft's position relative to the fix, the intercept vector if needed, an altitude to maintain until established or the approach clearance when authorized, and the proper frequency or monitoring instructions before the approach gate.
Issue only the approach clearance because position information and tower contact can wait until after the final approach fix.
Wait until the aircraft crosses the final approach fix to issue all arrival instructions at once.
Turn the aircraft inbound and let the pilot choose when to switch to tower because arrival instructions are optional before the approach gate.

items.explanation: Per 5-9-4, the controller issues the required arrival information, including position, vector or altitude restrictions, approach clearance when authorized, and communications instructions before the aircraft reaches the approach gate.

items.situation: You are setting up simultaneous dependent approaches to adjacent runways separated by 3,000 feet. Aircraft are established on parallel finals and straight-in landings are planned, but another controller wants to apply 1 mile diagonal separation before the aircraft are established because the geometry already looks stable.
items.para_context: Under 5-9-6, apply the diagonal adjacent-final minimum only after aircraft are established on the parallel final approach course. For runway centerlines at least 2,500 feet but no more than 3,600 feet apart, the diagonal minimum is 1 mile. Additional required conditions include straight-in landings, nonconflicting missed approaches, both runways in use, and direct interphone capability when approach control retains separation responsibility.

choices.text: Wait until the aircraft are established on the parallel finals, then apply the 1-mile diagonal adjacent-final minimum if the other required conditions are met.
Apply the 1-mile diagonal minimum as soon as the turn-on begins, because establishment on final is not required once the runways are only 3,000 feet apart.
Use 3 miles between all aircraft on adjacent finals because simultaneous dependent approaches never use reduced diagonal spacing.
Cancel simultaneous dependent approaches unless the runway centerlines are more than 8,300 feet apart.

items.explanation: Per 5-9-6, the diagonal minimum depends on runway spacing and is applied only after aircraft are established on the adjacent parallel finals and the other procedural conditions are satisfied.

items.situation: A military pilot says the aircraft is 'going tactical' and will leave ATC frequency while claiming that this automatically means the flight is operating with due regard. No monitoring agency has been arranged and none of the listed due-regard conditions have been confirmed.
items.para_context: Under 1-2-1o, due regard or operational flight options impose specific responsibilities and conditions. The note states that a pilot's use of the phrase 'Going Tactical' does not indicate Due Regard. Before the aircraft leaves ATC frequency, an understanding regarding the pilot's intent and the flight status should be reached.

choices.text: Do not treat 'going tactical' as due regard by itself; ensure the required understanding is reached and that the stated due-regard conditions and responsibilities are actually satisfied before the aircraft leaves ATC frequency.
Assume due regard automatically applies whenever a pilot says 'going tactical,' and let the aircraft leave frequency without further clarification.
Convert the flight to VFR immediately because due regard only exists in visual conditions.
Continue to provide normal IFR separation after the aircraft leaves frequency because due regard transfers all separation responsibility back to ATC.

items.explanation: Per 1-2-1, due regard has specific requirements, and the phrase 'going tactical' alone does not establish it.

items.situation: Two facilities have been using a jointly applied arrival procedure based on a verbal understanding, and one supervisor wants to add reduced separation language locally without documenting it because both facilities already know what to do.
items.para_context: Under 1-1-10, procedures or minima applied jointly or otherwise requiring the cooperation or concurrence of more than one facility or organization must be documented in a letter of agreement. LOAs only supplement this order, and any minima they specify must not be less than the minima in the order unless appropriate military authority has authorized reduced separation between military aircraft.

choices.text: Document the jointly applied procedure in an LOA and do not use minima below the order unless the appropriate military authority has specifically authorized reduced separation between military aircraft.
Continue using the verbal procedure indefinitely because joint facility familiarity removes the need for an LOA.
Put the reduced separation in a local memo and treat it as replacing the order for all aircraft operations.
Use the reduced minima immediately and document the LOA only if a pilot or auditor later asks to see it.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-10, jointly applied procedures or minima requiring cooperation between facilities must be documented in an LOA, and the LOA cannot reduce minima below the order except under the stated military authority exception.

items.situation: Two aircraft will cross with an angular difference of about 100 degrees, and their protected airspaces intersect. Another controller calls them opposite courses and wants to apply separation based on reciprocal-course logic.
items.para_context: Under 1-2-2, crossing courses are intersecting courses whose angular difference is 45 through 135 degrees inclusive. Same courses are less than 45 degrees, and opposite or reciprocal courses are greater than 135 through 180 degrees inclusive, when the protected airspaces are coincident, overlap, or intersect as applicable.

choices.text: Treat the aircraft as on crossing courses because the angular difference is within 45 through 135 degrees and the protected airspaces intersect.
Treat them as opposite courses because any intersecting protected airspaces automatically make the courses reciprocal.
Treat them as same courses because any angle under 180 degrees is considered the same course for separation purposes.
Disregard course category entirely because protected-airspace overlap matters only for parallel tracks.

items.explanation: Per 1-2-2b, intersecting courses with an angular difference of 45 through 135 degrees inclusive are crossing courses.

items.clearance: HOLD FOR WAKE TURBULENCE.

choices.text: Holding for wake turbulence, Cessna 3AB.
Holding for traffic, Cessna 3AB.
Cessna 3AB, cleared for takeoff.
Wake turbulence, holding.

items.explanation: Per 3-9-6l and n, when the aircraft must wait for the required interval, use HOLD FOR WAKE TURBULENCE and do not launch it early.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: HOLD FOR WAKE TURBULENCE.
items.display_text: HOLD FOR WIND TURBULENCE
items.explanation: Per 3-9-6l, the phraseology is HOLD FOR WAKE TURBULENCE.

items.situation: A Category I aircraft is ready to depart behind traffic that still requires a same-runway wake interval. Only part of the required interval has elapsed, but the pilot says they will accept the wake and wants an immediate takeoff.
items.para_context: Under 3-9-6, do not approve pilot requests to deviate from the required wake intervals in subparagraphs f through k. When it is necessary for the aircraft to hold to provide the required interval, inform the aircraft. The associated phraseology is HOLD FOR WAKE TURBULENCE.

choices.text: Do not approve the request to deviate from the interval, and advise the aircraft to hold for wake turbulence until the required interval has elapsed.
Approve the immediate takeoff if the pilot accepts the wake, because the required interval may be waived at pilot discretion.
Issue line up and wait behind the preceding super or heavy aircraft so the interval can be absorbed on the runway.
Let the aircraft begin the takeoff roll once the runway is physically clear, because wake intervals apply only to arrivals.

items.explanation: Per 3-9-6, the required wake intervals are not waived on controller approval, and the aircraft must be held when necessary to provide the interval.

items.clearance: HOLD FOR WAKE TURBULENCE.

choices.text: Holding for wake turbulence, Cherokee 92K.
Holding for traffic, Cherokee 92K.
Cherokee 92K, immediate takeoff approved.
Wake turbulence only, Cherokee 92K.

items.explanation: Per 3-9-7, when an intersection departure must wait for the wake interval, the aircraft is informed accordingly.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: HOLD FOR WAKE TURBULENCE.
items.display_text: HOLD FOR RUNWAY TURBULENCE
items.explanation: Per 3-9-7, the wake-delay phraseology remains HOLD FOR WAKE TURBULENCE.

items.situation: A Category I aircraft is departing from an intersection on the same runway behind a departing Category G aircraft. Two minutes have elapsed since the preceding aircraft took off, and another controller wants to launch the intersection departure now to keep the sequence moving.
items.para_context: Under 3-9-7a1, a Category I aircraft taking off from an intersection on the same runway behind a departing Category F, G, or H aircraft must not begin takeoff roll until at least 3 minutes after the preceding aircraft has taken off. Inform aircraft when it is necessary to hold in order to provide the required time interval.

choices.text: Continue to hold the Category I aircraft until at least 3 minutes have elapsed since the preceding takeoff, and inform the aircraft that the hold is for the required wake interval.
Launch the aircraft now because intersection departures may use a shorter interval when the runway is long and the pilot is ready.
Issue an immediate takeoff as long as the aircraft can depart before the preceding aircraft crosses the runway end.
Apply only the same-runway clear-of-runway rule, because intersection departures do not have separate wake intervals.

items.explanation: Per 3-9-7a1, a Category I intersection departure behind a Category F, G, or H aircraft waits 3 minutes after the preceding takeoff.

items.clearance: MAINTAIN TWO TWO ZERO KNOTS UNTIL BALTR THEN RESUME PUBLISHED SPEED.

choices.text: Maintain two two zero knots until BALTR then resume published speed, United 234.
Maintain two two zero knots and disregard published speed, United 234.
Descend via and resume published speed now, United 234.
Maintain two five zero knots until BALTR then resume published speed, United 234.

items.explanation: Per 5-7-2e, when you assign a speed on a procedure with published restrictions, you must state where that ATC speed ends or changes the published restriction.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: REDUCE SPEED TO ONE NINER ZERO IF UNABLE ADVISE.
items.display_text: REDUCE SPEED TO ONE NINER ZERO IF ABLE ADVISE
items.explanation: Per 5-7-2b, speed adjustments at or above FL390 use the phraseology '(speed adjustment), IF UNABLE ADVISE.'

items.situation: A turbojet at FL400 needs a speed reduction and a descent, and it is also on a procedure with published speed restrictions. Another controller wants to say only 'descend and reduce speed to two two zero' and later 'maintain two two zero knots' without saying where the assignment begins or ends.
items.para_context: Under 5-7-2, when combining a descent with a speed reduction, specify which action is expected first. At or above FL390, use '(speed adjustment), IF UNABLE ADVISE' to obtain pilot concurrence. When issuing speed adjustments on a procedure with published speed restrictions, specify the point at which the issued restriction begins, ends, or changes the published restrictions.

choices.text: State which action is first, use IF UNABLE ADVISE for the FL390 speed adjustment, and specify where the assigned speed begins, ends, or changes the published procedure speed restrictions.
Leave the sequence of descent and speed reduction to pilot judgment and let the published restrictions sort themselves out after the aircraft slows.
Use IF UNABLE ADVISE only below FL390 and omit any start or end point for the assigned speed when the aircraft is on a published procedure.
Cancel all published speed restrictions whenever you assign any speed, even if you do not say so.

items.explanation: Per 5-7-2, speed control phraseology must remove ambiguity: specify which action occurs first, use the FL390 concurrence phraseology, and tell the pilot where an assigned speed affects published restrictions.

items.clearance: TRAFFIC ALERT, DELTA 52, TURN RIGHT IMMEDIATELY HEADING ONE EIGHT ZERO, CLIMB AND MAINTAIN THREE THOUSAND.

choices.text: Traffic alert, Delta 52, turning right immediately heading one eight zero, climb and maintain three thousand.
Traffic alert, Delta 52, turning right immediately heading one eight zero, descend and maintain three thousand.
Delta 52, returning to final approach course.
Traffic alert, Delta 52, heading one seven zero, climb and maintain three thousand.

items.explanation: Per 5-9-10d2, when an aircraft on the adjacent final must avoid a deviating aircraft, issue TRAFFIC ALERT, a turn, a heading, and CLIMB AND MAINTAIN.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: TRAFFIC ALERT TURN RIGHT IMMEDIATELY HEADING ONE EIGHT ZERO CLIMB AND MAINTAIN THREE THOUSAND.
items.display_text: TRAFFIC ALERT TURN RIGHT IMMEDIATELY HEADING ONE EIGHT ZERO DESCEND AND MAINTAIN THREE THOUSAND
items.explanation: Per 5-9-10d2, the breakout instruction for the aircraft on the adjacent final uses CLIMB AND MAINTAIN.

items.situation: During simultaneous independent approaches to widely-spaced parallel runways without final monitors, one aircraft overshoots the turn-on and continues toward the adjacent final approach course. Another controller wants to delay intervention because no NTZ is required for this operation.
items.para_context: Under 5-9-10d, if an aircraft crosses or continues to deviate toward the adjacent final approach course, instruct that aircraft to return to the correct final approach course. Instruct aircraft on the adjacent final to alter course to avoid the deviating aircraft when the deviation is observed or, in the controller's judgment, exists. The note specifies there is no requirement for establishment of an NTZ.

choices.text: Issue the appropriate return-to-final instruction to the deviating aircraft and, if needed, issue the adjacent-final traffic-alert turn and climb instruction to keep the aircraft separated.
Wait to see whether the aircraft corrects on its own, because without an NTZ there is no immediate controller action requirement.
Transfer both aircraft to the tower immediately so the tower can resolve the overshoot locally.
Cancel simultaneous approaches for all aircraft first, then issue no specific correction to the deviating aircraft until the supervisor arrives.

items.explanation: Per 5-9-10d, the absence of an NTZ requirement does not remove the controller's responsibility to correct the deviating aircraft and protect the adjacent final.

items.question_text: Under 3-10-1, when may landing information contained in the ATIS broadcast be omitted?
items.explanation: Per 3-10-1, landing information contained in the ATIS broadcast may be omitted if the pilot states the appropriate ATIS code.

choices.text: When the pilot states the appropriate ATIS code.
Whenever the controller believes the pilot probably heard the ATIS.
Only when the aircraft is on a straight-in approach.
Only after the landing clearance has been issued.

items.question_text: Under 3-10-1, which items may be omitted when a pilot uses the phrase 'have numbers'?
items.explanation: Per 3-10-1, runway, wind, and altimeter may be omitted if the pilot uses the phrase 'have numbers.'

choices.text: Runway, wind, and altimeter.
Traffic pattern information and supplementary information only.
Braking action and wind shear advisories only.
All landing information including position reports and landing clearance.

items.question_text: Under 3-10-1, a pilot's use of the phrase 'have numbers' does not by itself indicate receipt of the ATIS broadcast.
items.explanation: The note to 3-10-1 states that 'have numbers' does not indicate receipt of the ATIS broadcast.

False

items.question_text: Under 3-9-11, once an aircraft has started the takeoff roll, for what purpose may the takeoff clearance be canceled?
items.explanation: Per 3-9-11, after takeoff roll has begun, cancel the takeoff clearance only for the purpose of safety.

choices.text: Only for the purpose of safety.
For safety or to meet traffic-management requirements.
Whenever the pilot has not yet rotated.
Only when the supervisor requests it for EDCT compliance.

items.question_text: Under 3-9-11, what should the pilot be told when a previously issued takeoff clearance is canceled and circumstances require additional information?
items.explanation: Per 3-9-11, inform the pilot of the reason if circumstances require when canceling a previously issued takeoff clearance.

choices.text: The reason for the cancellation.
Only the new departure frequency.
Only the expected wheels-up time.
Nothing further, because reasons are never included with a cancellation.

items.question_text: Under 3-9-11, a takeoff clearance may be canceled after the aircraft has started its takeoff roll solely to meet traffic management requirements or EDCT.
items.explanation: The note to 3-9-11 says this must not be done.

False

items.question_text: Complete the phraseology from 3-9-11: "CANCEL TAKEOFF _____ (reason)."
items.explanation: Per 3-9-11, the phraseology is 'CANCEL TAKEOFF CLEARANCE (reason).'

choices.text: CLEARANCE

items.question_text: Under 3-9-3, when may the departure control frequency be omitted before takeoff?
items.explanation: Per 3-9-3a1, the departure control frequency may be omitted if a SID has been or will be assigned and the departure control frequency is published on the SID.

choices.text: When a SID has been or will be assigned and the departure control frequency is published on that SID.
Whenever the aircraft is IFR and already has an assigned beacon code.
Only when the aircraft is VFR and receiving radar service.
Whenever the pilot has the ATIS code.

items.question_text: Under 3-9-3, before what altitude should controllers avoid requesting departing military turboprop or turbojet aircraft, except transport and cargo types, to make radio frequency or radar beacon changes after takeoff?
items.explanation: Per 3-9-3b2, do not request those military aircraft to make radio frequency or radar beacon changes before the aircraft reaches 2,500 feet above the surface.

choices.text: Before the aircraft reaches 2,500 feet above the surface.
Before the aircraft reaches 1,000 feet above the surface.
Before the aircraft reaches 5 miles from the runway.
Before the aircraft checks in with departure control.

items.question_text: Under 3-9-3, when no further communication with local control is required, civil aircraft and military transport or cargo types are instructed to contact departure control when the aircraft is about one-half mile beyond the runway end.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 3-9-3b1.

items.question_text: Under 4-2-7, what phrase is used if the aircraft will operate in an approved ALTRV?
items.explanation: Per 4-2-7, use the phrase 'via approved altitude reservation (mission name) flight plan.'

choices.text: Via approved altitude reservation (mission name) flight plan.
Via approved special-use mission route.
Via prearranged military reservation clearance.
Via mission route as filed.

items.question_text: Under 4-2-7, an ALTRV normally includes which phases of flight before ATC begins providing separation between aircraft or until the holding point or designated point/time?
items.explanation: Per the note to 4-2-7, an ALTRV normally includes departure, climb, cruise, and arrival phases up to and including the holding pattern or the point and time at which ATC provides separation between aircraft.

choices.text: Departure, climb, cruise, and arrival phases of flight.

Departure and climb only.
Arrival and holding only.

items.question_text: Under 3-1-13, when are two-way radio communications established for a pilot entering Class D airspace?
items.explanation: Per 3-1-13, if the controller responds to the pilot with the aircraft call sign followed by 'standby,' two-way radio communications have been established.

choices.text: When the controller responds with the aircraft call sign followed by 'standby.'
Only when the controller explicitly says the aircraft is cleared to enter the Delta.
Only after the pilot reads back the assigned runway and pattern instructions.
When the pilot first makes a radio call on the tower frequency, even if the tower does not answer.

items.question_text: Under 3-1-13, what should the controller tell a pilot when workload or traffic conditions prevent immediate provision of airport traffic control services?
items.explanation: Per 3-1-13, the pilot is told to remain outside the Class D airspace until conditions permit the services to be provided.

choices.text: Remain outside the Delta airspace and standby until conditions permit service.
Enter the Delta and maintain VFR while waiting for further service.
Orbit over the airport until the tower can provide service.
Contact the overlying approach control for Class D entry approval.

items.question_text: Under 3-1-13, if the controller responds with the aircraft call sign and 'standby,' radio communications have been established and the pilot may enter Class D airspace.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 3-1-13.

items.question_text: Under 3-3-1, what should be done with condition information received from someone other than authorized airport or FAA personnel, unless that responsibility belongs to the military operations office?
items.explanation: Per 3-3-1, information received from other than authorized airport or FAA personnel is confirmed unless that function is the responsibility of the military operations office.

items.explanation: Per 3-3-1, issue a NOTAM publicizing the unsafe condition and inform airport management or the operator as soon as practicable.

items.question_text: Under 2-1-6, when must a controller issue a safety alert?
items.explanation: Per 2-1-6, issue a safety alert when you are aware the aircraft is in unsafe proximity to terrain, obstructions, or other aircraft.
