items.question_text: When may a terminal facility use secondary radar only to conduct an ASR final approach under 5-1-2?
items.explanation: Per 5-1-2, terminal facilities do not use secondary radar only to conduct ASR final approaches unless an emergency exists and the pilot concurs.

choices.text: Only when an emergency exists and the pilot concurs.
Whenever the primary radar is temporarily unusable, even if there is no emergency.
Whenever the pilot requests an ASR approach.
At any terminal facility operating in STARS Fusion mode.

items.question_text: When operating in Fusion mode, the provisions of 5-1-2a are not applicable unless required by facility directive.
items.explanation: Per the opening sentence of 5-1-2, that is the stated Fusion-mode exception.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: To which aircraft do merging target procedures apply under 5-1-4, except while established in a holding pattern?
items.explanation: Per 5-1-4, merging target procedures apply to radar-identified aircraft at 10,000 feet and above, turbojet aircraft regardless of altitude, and presidential aircraft regardless of altitude.

choices.text: Radar-identified aircraft at 10,000 feet and above, turbojet aircraft regardless of altitude, and presidential aircraft regardless of altitude.
Only turbojet aircraft at or above FL180.
All radar-identified aircraft regardless of altitude, type, or status.
Only aircraft in holding patterns because closure rates are lower and easier to monitor.

items.question_text: When one pilot in RVSM airspace reports unable RVSM due to turbulence or mountain wave and the aircraft are vertically separated by 1,000 feet, what must be done under 5-1-4?
items.explanation: Per 5-1-4, use vectors to prevent the targets from merging when the specified unable-RVSM condition exists in RVSM airspace.

choices.text: Use vectors to prevent the targets from merging.
Rely on the existing 1,000-foot vertical separation and take no further action.
Terminate RVSM procedures for the entire sector until both aircraft have landed.
Issue a speed reduction only, because vectors are not authorized for merging target procedures.

items.question_text: If unable to provide vector service to avoid merging targets after a pilot request under 5-1-4, the controller must inform the pilot.
items.explanation: Per 5-1-4e, if unable to provide vector service, inform the pilot.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: When should a controller issue a deviation advisory under 5-1-6?
items.explanation: Per 5-1-6, inform an aircraft when it is observed in a position and on a track that will obviously cause it to deviate from its protected airspace area.

choices.text: When the aircraft is observed in a position and on a track that will obviously cause it to deviate from protected airspace.
Only after the aircraft has already crossed outside protected airspace.
Only if another aircraft reports the deviation first.
Only for aircraft operating on conventional airways, not RNAV ATS routes.

items.question_text: If necessary under 5-1-6, what assistance should the controller provide?
items.explanation: Per 5-1-6, help the aircraft return to the assigned protected airspace if necessary.

choices.text: Help the aircraft return to the assigned protected airspace.
Terminate radar service and leave the aircraft to recover on its own.
Issue only traffic information because route assistance is not authorized.
Require the pilot to declare an emergency before any corrective assistance may be given.

items.question_text: Aircraft approaching the RNAV ATS route performance limit discussed in 5-1-6 may be experiencing a navigation system error or failure.
items.explanation: Per the note in 5-1-6, aircraft approaching the 2-mile performance limit may be experiencing a navigation system error or failure.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: What must a controller normally do when radar service is terminated under 5-1-9?
items.explanation: Per 5-1-9a, inform aircraft when radar service is terminated.

choices.text: Inform the aircraft that radar service is terminated.
Assume the pilot will infer termination from the next frequency change.
Wait until the pilot asks whether radar service is still being provided.
Advise only IFR aircraft and never VFR aircraft.

items.question_text: Which situation is listed in 5-1-9 as one where radar service is automatically terminated and the aircraft need not be advised of termination?
items.explanation: Per 5-1-9b, one listed case is when an aircraft conducting an instrument, visual, or contact approach has landed or has been instructed to change to advisory frequency.

choices.text: An aircraft conducting an instrument, visual, or contact approach has landed or has been instructed to change to advisory frequency.
Any time an aircraft changes from center to approach frequency.
Whenever an arriving aircraft is inbound to a tower-controlled airport, regardless of radar coverage.
Whenever an IFR aircraft cancels IFR inside Class B airspace.

items.question_text: At tower-controlled airports where radar coverage does not exist to within one-half mile of the end of the runway, arriving aircraft must be informed when radar service is terminated.
items.explanation: Per 5-1-9b3, that notification is required in this specific case.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: When is Code 7700 assigned under 5-2-3?
items.explanation: Per 5-2-3a, assign Code 7700 when the pilot declares an emergency and the aircraft is not radar identified.

choices.text: When the pilot declares an emergency and the aircraft is not radar identified.
Only after radar contact has been established and the aircraft accepts a discrete code change.
Whenever any aircraft requests priority handling, regardless of emergency status.
Only for VFR emergency aircraft below Class A airspace.

items.question_text: After radio and radar contact have been established, which emergency aircraft may the controller request to change from Code 7700 to a computer-assigned discrete code under 5-2-3?
items.explanation: Per 5-2-3b, controllers may request other than single-piloted helicopters and single-piloted turbojet aircraft to change from 7700 to a discrete code after radio and radar contact are established.

choices.text: Other than single-piloted helicopters and single-piloted turbojet aircraft, if feasible.
Only single-piloted helicopters and single-piloted turbojet aircraft because they need the most automation support.
Any emergency aircraft without regard to pilot concurrence or flight conditions.
No emergency aircraft; Code 7700 must remain set until landing.

items.question_text: For a Mode C equipped VFR aircraft in emergency that no longer requires Code 7700, a terminal facility must assign a beacon code that permits MSAW alarm processing.
items.explanation: Per 5-2-3c1, terminal facilities assign a beacon code that permits MSAW processing when the VFR emergency aircraft no longer requires Code 7700.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: After which events must Mode C altitude readouts be validated under 5-2-15?
items.explanation: Per 5-2-15a, validation is required after initial track start, track start from coast or frozen status, during and after unreliable Mode C readouts, and after accepting an interfacility handoff except for the listed exceptions.

choices.text: After initial track start, track start from coast or frozen status, during and after an unreliable Mode C readout, and after accepting an interfacility handoff except for the stated exceptions.
Only after the pilot volunteers an altitude that seems different from the data block.
Only after accepting an interfacility handoff, with no exceptions.
Only for aircraft at or above FL180.

items.question_text: Which condition makes an altitude readout valid under 5-2-15?
items.explanation: Per 5-2-15b, an altitude readout is valid when it varies by less than 300 feet from the pilot reported altitude, when a continuous airport readout varies by less than 300 feet from field elevation, or when it exactly matches coordinated validated information from another facility.

choices.text: It varies by less than 300 feet from the pilot reported altitude.
It varies by less than 500 feet from the pilot reported altitude.
It differs from another facility's validated readout by up to 300 feet.
It displays continuously even if it is more than 300 feet off the reported altitude.

items.question_text: When unable to validate a Mode C altitude readout under 5-2-15, the controller must not use that Mode C altitude information for separation.
items.explanation: Per 5-2-15c, unvalidated Mode C altitude information is not used for separation.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: When may a controller identify a departing target by observation under 5-3-2?
items.explanation: Per 5-3-2a, the target may be identified by observing a departing aircraft within one mile of the takeoff runway end at a towered airport if the required verbal or nonverbal rolling or boundary notification is accomplished.

choices.text: When the target is observed within one mile of the takeoff runway end at a towered airport and the required rolling or boundary notification has been accomplished.
Only after the aircraft reports level at cruise altitude.
Whenever the target is observed departing, even if no coordination has occurred.
Only if the target is more than one mile from the departure end of the runway.

items.question_text: What condition must be met before using identifying turns under 5-3-2?
items.explanation: Per 5-3-2c, one required condition is that only one aircraft is observed making the identifying turn or turns of 30 degrees or more, along with the other listed safeguards.

choices.text: Only one aircraft is observed making the identifying turn or turns of 30 degrees or more.
Any number of aircraft may make similar turns as long as the controller suspects which one is correct.
The aircraft must be flying a normal IFR route or known VFR path to reduce misidentification risk.
No pilot position report is needed before using identifying turns.

items.question_text: If a TACAN or VORTAC is located within 6,000 feet of the radar antenna, it may be used as a reference fix for radar identification even if it is not displayed on the video map or map overlay.
items.explanation: Per 5-3-2b, a TACAN or VORTAC within 6,000 feet of the radar antenna may be used as a reference fix without being displayed on the map or overlay.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: When should a controller use more than one method of identification under 5-3-5?
items.explanation: Per 5-3-5a, use more than one method when proximity of targets, duplication of observed action, or any other circumstance causes doubt as to target identification.

choices.text: When proximity of targets, duplication of observed action, or any other circumstance causes doubt as to target identification.
Only after radar service has already been terminated.
Only when the pilot requests an additional identification method.
Only for primary radar targets and never for beacon or ADS-B targets.

items.question_text: If identification remains questionable for any reason under 5-3-5, what immediate action is required?
items.explanation: Per 5-3-5b, take immediate action to re-identify the aircraft or terminate radar service.

choices.text: Re-identify the aircraft immediately or terminate radar service.
Continue radar service and wait for the next handoff to resolve the doubt.
Transfer the aircraft to another frequency and let the next controller decide whether it is identified.
Suppress all primary targets from the display and continue using only beacon targets.

items.question_text: In en route operations, all primary targets must be displayed when radar identification is lost or is questionable.
items.explanation: Per 5-3-5b2, en route facilities ensure that all primary targets are displayed when radar identification is lost or questionable.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 5-2-16, when is altitude confirmation on initial contact not required for a Mode C aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 5-2-16, initial-contact altitude confirmation is not required when one of the listed exceptions applies, including the pilot stating the assigned altitude, a new altitude assignment, a valid Mode C readout showing the aircraft established at the assigned altitude, or a terminal intrafacility transfer.

choices.text: When one of the listed exceptions applies, such as the pilot stating the assigned altitude or a valid Mode C readout showing the aircraft established at the assigned altitude.
Only when the pilot is level at or above FL180.
Only when the aircraft was handed off from another facility, with no other exceptions.
Never; all Mode C aircraft must always confirm altitude on every initial contact.

items.question_text: Which exception is unique to terminal operations in 5-2-16?
items.explanation: Per 5-2-16d, terminal intrafacility transfers are an exception to initial-contact altitude confirmation.

choices.text: The aircraft was transferred to you from another sector or position within your facility.
The pilot is using standard pressure above the lowest usable flight level.
The pilot reports any altitude within 500 feet of the assigned altitude.
The aircraft is climbing or descending through the assigned altitude.

items.question_text: Under 5-2-16, 'initial contact' means the pilot's first radio contact with each sector or position.
items.explanation: Per the note in 5-2-16, that is the defined meaning of initial contact.

False

items.question_text: What additional requirement applies in the USA under 5-2-17 for non-Mode C aircraft?

choices.text: Reconfirm all pilot altitude readbacks.
Treat all pilot altitude readbacks as automatically correct unless the pilot sounds unsure.

choices.text: Negative. Maintain (altitude), or Negative. Climb/descend and maintain (altitude).
Radar contact. Verify altitude.
Stand by altitude correction.
Stop altitude squawk. Altitude differs by (number of feet).

items.explanation: Per 5-2-17a3, terminal intrafacility transfer is one of the exceptions.

False

items.question_text: When an aircraft's ADS-B transmitter appears inoperative or malfunctioning under 5-2-22, what information must be given to the OS/CIC?

choices.text: The aircraft call sign, location, and time of the occurrence in UTC.
Only the aircraft type and destination airport.

choices.text: When the malfunctioning ADS-B transmitter is jeopardizing the safe execution of air traffic control functions.

Only after the pilot requests to disable ADS-B.

items.question_text: What triggers a Call Sign Mis-Match alert under 5-2-23?

items.question_text: Who must authorize ADS-B Out OFF operations under 5-2-24?

choices.text: Do not approve the request and notify the OS/CIC of the aircraft call sign and location.

Instruct the pilot to stop ADS-B transmissions immediately without involving the OS/CIC.

items.question_text: Which is an approved identification method under 5-3-3 when using only Mode 3/A radar beacon or ADS-B?

items.question_text: Under 5-3-3, the standby method requires observing the target disappear for sufficient scans and then reappear after the pilot returns the transponder to normal operation.

items.question_text: When must a controller advise an aircraft of radar contact under 5-3-7?

items.question_text: What phraseology is used when radar contact is lost under 5-3-7?

items.question_text: Under 5-2-14, what is a valid basis to disapprove or withdraw approval to operate with a failed transponder or ADS-B Out transmitter?

items.question_text: What phraseology is used under 5-2-18 when the controller wants an aircraft to turn on automatic altitude reporting?

items.question_text: Except in an emergency, how are inflight deviation requests from aircraft without transponder equipment installed handled under 5-2-19?

items.question_text: When must position information be given under 5-3-6?

items.question_text: How must the upper altitude filter limit be set under 5-2-21 in terminal operations?

choices.text: No lower than 1,000 feet above the highest altitude for which the controller is responsible.
Exactly at the highest altitude for which the controller is responsible.
At least 1,000 feet below the highest altitude for which the controller is responsible.
Only high enough to encompass the next higher sector's airspace.

items.question_text: When the position's area of responsibility includes down to airport field elevation, how is the lower altitude filter normally set under 5-2-21?
items.explanation: Per 5-2-21, the facility normally sets the lower altitude filter to encompass the field elevation so certain safety-alert and validation provisions may be applied.

choices.text: To encompass the field elevation.
Exactly 1,000 feet above the field elevation.
At the minimum vectoring altitude, regardless of airport elevation.
Above the field elevation whenever any target clutter exists.

items.question_text: Under 5-2-21, air traffic managers may authorize temporary suspension of the normal field-elevation filter requirement when target clutter is excessive.
items.explanation: Per 5-2-21, temporary suspension may be authorized when target clutter is excessive.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: When may a controller instruct an aircraft on an assigned code to squawk standby under 5-2-10?
items.explanation: Per 5-2-10, standby may be used when approximately 15 miles from destination and operation is no longer desired, or when needed to reduce clutter in a multi-target area.

choices.text: When the aircraft is about 15 miles from destination and operation is no longer desired, or when needed to reduce clutter in a multi-target area.
Only after radar contact has been lost.
Only when the pilot requests deactivation of the transponder.
Only when the aircraft has exited your sector.

items.question_text: What must the controller do after using standby to reduce clutter under 5-2-10?
items.explanation: Per 5-2-10b, when standby is used to reduce clutter in a multi-target area, the pilot is instructed to return to normal as soon as possible thereafter.

choices.text: Instruct the pilot to return the transponder or ADS-B to normal as soon as possible thereafter.
Leave the aircraft on standby until landing without further instruction.
Tell the pilot to stop squawk permanently for the remainder of the flight.
Transfer the decision to the next controller because only the receiving sector may restore normal operation.

items.question_text: Under 5-2-10, a controller may instruct an aircraft approximately 15 miles from destination to squawk standby when operation of the transponder or ADS-B is no longer desired.
items.explanation: Per 5-2-10a, that is an authorized use of standby operation.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Which additional codes must be monitored under 5-2-11 unless the area of responsibility includes only Class A airspace?
items.explanation: Per 5-2-11a, in addition to assigned codes, monitor Codes 1200, 1202, 1203, 1255, and 1277 unless the area of responsibility includes only Class A airspace.

choices.text: Only Code 4000 and Code 7700.
Only whatever discrete codes the controller assigned during the current shift.
No additional codes; only assigned IFR codes are monitored.

items.question_text: When must Code 4000 be monitored under 5-2-11?
items.explanation: Per 5-2-11b, monitor Code 4000 and any substitute code when the area of responsibility contains or is immediately adjacent to a restricted area, warning area, VR route, or other category where Code 4000 is appropriate.

choices.text: When the area of responsibility contains or is immediately adjacent to a restricted area, warning area, VR route, or another category where Code 4000 is appropriate.
Only when the area includes only Class A airspace.
Only after a military aircraft reports using Code 4000 on frequency.
Only when Code 1200 monitoring has already been discontinued.

items.question_text: Under 5-2-11, monitoring of the listed VFR beacon codes may be temporarily discontinued when excessive VFR target presentations derogate the separation of IFR traffic.
items.explanation: Per 5-2-11a, temporary discontinuation of the listed VFR-code monitoring is permitted when excessive VFR target presentations derogate IFR separation.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: How long is an en route data block retained under 5-3-8?
items.explanation: Per 5-3-8, retain the data block until the aircraft has exited the sector or delegated airspace and all potential conflicts have been resolved, including an aircraft that is a point out.

choices.text: Until the aircraft has exited the sector or delegated airspace and all potential conflicts have been resolved, including point-outs.
Only until the aircraft is radar identified for the first time.
Only until the aircraft enters delegated airspace, even if conflicts remain.
Until the next controller verbally acknowledges seeing the target symbol.

items.question_text: What minimum information must an en route data block display under 5-3-8?
items.explanation: Per 5-3-8, the data block must display flight identification and altitude information at a minimum, and the altitude may be assigned, interim, or reported.

choices.text: Flight identification and altitude information, at a minimum.
Only a target symbol and speed readout.
Only flight identification if altitude is available elsewhere.
Only altitude, because call sign retention is optional in en route operations.

items.question_text: In ERAM, when you have separation responsibility for an aircraft and a paired track exists, you display a full data block.
items.explanation: Per 5-3-8, that full data block display is required in ERAM when a paired track exists and the controller has separation responsibility.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: How long are terminal data blocks retained under 5-3-9?
items.explanation: Per 5-3-9a, retain terminal data blocks associated with the correct target symbol until the aircraft has exited the sector or delegated airspace and all potential conflicts have been resolved, including an aircraft that is a point out.

choices.text: Until the aircraft has exited the sector or delegated airspace and all potential conflicts have been resolved, including point-outs.
Only until the aircraft leaves Class B or Class C airspace, even if conflicts remain.
Only until the next controller accepts a handoff, regardless of delegated airspace or conflict status.
Until the aircraft reports the airport in sight.

items.question_text: During prearranged coordination under 5-3-9, what minimum information must be displayed for another controller's aircraft when penetrating that controller's airspace?
items.explanation: Per 5-3-9b, the penetrating controller displays that controller's aircraft data block information containing, at a minimum, the position symbol and altitude information.

choices.text: At least the position symbol and altitude information.
Only flight identification.
Only a conflict alert indicator.
No display is required if the coordination is prearranged.

items.question_text: Where delegated airspace extends beyond Class B and or Class C airspace, retention of the data block is no longer required for a VFR aircraft once it is clear of that airspace and radar services have been terminated.
items.explanation: Per the note in 5-3-9, that is the stated exception for certain VFR aircraft clear of Class B or Class C after radar service termination.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: What action is required under 5-2-4 when a Code 7600 display is observed?
items.explanation: Per 5-2-4, apply the procedures in paragraph 10-4-4, Communications Failure.

choices.text: Apply the procedures in paragraph 10-4-4, Communications Failure.
Apply the procedures in paragraph 10-2-6, Hijacked Aircraft.
Treat the aircraft as a routine VFR advisory target and continue normal service.
Instruct the aircraft to squawk VFR immediately.

items.question_text: How may a radio-failure squawk appear in ERAM and STARS or MEARTS under 5-2-4?
items.explanation: Per the note in 5-2-4, ERAM may display RDOF and STARS/MEARTS may display RF instead of 7600.

choices.text: ERAM may display RDOF and STARS/MEARTS may display RF.
ERAM may display HIJK and STARS/MEARTS may display HJ.
ERAM may display LLNK and STARS/MEARTS may display LL.
ERAM may display STBY and STARS/MEARTS may display SB.

items.question_text: Under 5-2-4, an aircraft that loses two-way radio communications capability can be expected to squawk Code 7600.
items.explanation: Per the note in 5-2-4, Code 7600 is the expected radio-failure squawk.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: What action is required under 5-2-5 when a Code 7500 display is observed?
items.explanation: Per 5-2-5, apply the procedures in paragraph 10-2-6, Hijacked Aircraft.

choices.text: Apply the procedures in paragraph 10-2-6, Hijacked Aircraft.
Apply the procedures in paragraph 10-4-4, Communications Failure.
Treat the aircraft as a lost-link UAS event.
Instruct the pilot to squawk 1200 and continue VFR.

items.question_text: How may a hijack or unlawful interference display appear in ERAM and STARS or MEARTS under 5-2-5?
items.explanation: Per the note in 5-2-5, ERAM may display HIJK and STARS/MEARTS may display HJ instead of 7500.

choices.text: ERAM may display HIJK and STARS/MEARTS may display HJ.
ERAM may display RDOF and STARS/MEARTS may display RF.
ERAM may display LLNK and STARS/MEARTS may display LL.
ERAM may display ULAW and STARS/MEARTS may display UI.

items.question_text: Under 5-2-5, a Code 7500 display requires application of the hijacked-aircraft procedures.
items.explanation: Per 5-2-5, Code 7500 is handled under the hijacked-aircraft procedures.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: What should a controller determine first under 5-2-6 when Code 7400 is observed for a UAS?
items.explanation: Per 5-2-6a, determine the lost-link procedure as outlined in the Special Airworthiness Certificate or Certificate of Waiver or Authorization.

choices.text: The lost-link procedure outlined in the Special Airworthiness Certificate or COA.
Whether the pilot wants to cancel IFR and continue VFR.
Whether the UAS should be reassigned Code 1200 immediately.
Which runway the UAS will land on after the anomaly.

items.question_text: What additional action is required if the UAS deviates from the programmed lost-link procedure or another anomaly occurs under 5-2-6?
items.explanation: Per 5-2-6d, if the UAS deviates from the programmed lost-link procedure or another anomaly occurs, treat the situation in accordance with Chapter 10, paragraph 10-1-1c.

choices.text: Treat the situation in accordance with Chapter 10, paragraph 10-1-1c.
Assume the event has ended because the original lost-link procedure is still available in the COA.
Ignore the anomaly unless the UAS also displays Code 7500.
Reassign the UAS a VFR code and terminate all coordination.

items.question_text: Under 5-2-6, the OS/CIC is advised when feasible so the UAS lost-link event can be documented.
items.explanation: Per 5-2-6c, the OS/CIC is advised when feasible so the event can be documented.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: What code is issued to a VFR aircraft receiving radar advisories under 5-2-7?
items.explanation: Per 5-2-7a, issue a computer-assigned beacon code to VFR aircraft receiving radar advisories.

choices.text: A computer-assigned beacon code.
Always Code 1200.
Always Code 4000.
Code 7600 unless the aircraft requests advisories only.

items.question_text: When an IFR aircraft cancels its IFR flight plan and is not requesting radar advisory service, or when VFR radar advisory service is being terminated, what should the aircraft be told under 5-2-7?
items.explanation: Per 5-2-7b, instruct the aircraft to squawk VFR or squawk 1200.

choices.text: SQUAWK VFR or SQUAWK 1200.
SQUAWK STANDBY.
STOP SQUAWK.
SQUAWK 7600.

items.question_text: Under 5-2-7, safety alerts or traffic advisories may be issued before coordination when an imminent situation may be averted, but coordination should follow as soon as possible.
items.explanation: Per the note in 5-2-7, alerts or traffic advisories may precede coordination in an imminent situation, with coordination accomplished as soon as possible thereafter.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: What is designated as the common military and civil mode for air traffic control use under 5-2-1?
items.explanation: Per 5-2-1a1, Mode 3/A is designated as the common military and civil mode for ATC use.

choices.text: Mode 3/A.
Mode C.
Mode S only.
ADS-B Out only.

items.question_text: When should Code 4000 be assigned under 5-2-1?
items.explanation: Per 5-2-1c, Code 4000 is assigned when aircraft operate on a flight plan with frequent or rapid assigned altitude changes in more than one stratum or another category not compatible with a discrete code assignment.

choices.text: When the flight requires frequent or rapid assigned altitude changes in more than one stratum or another category not compatible with a discrete code assignment.
Whenever a VFR aircraft requests radar advisories.
Whenever an aircraft cancels IFR and is squawking VFR.
Only when the transponder fails in flight.

items.question_text: Under 5-2-1, beacon code assignments are made only to ADS-B and or transponder-equipped aircraft.
items.explanation: Per 5-2-1a2, make beacon code assignments only to ADS-B and or transponder-equipped aircraft.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: When may a receiving facility normally request an aircraft to change from the code it was squawking in the transferring facility's area under 5-2-2?
items.explanation: Per 5-2-2, unless otherwise specified or coordinated, do not request the change until the aircraft is within your area of responsibility.

choices.text: When the aircraft is within your area of responsibility.
Immediately upon handoff acceptance, regardless of location.
Only after the aircraft leaves the previous controller's frequency and reports on yours.
Only after the aircraft lands at destination.

items.question_text: Which condition allows a code-change request before the aircraft is within your area of responsibility under 5-2-2?
items.explanation: Per 5-2-2, earlier code changes are allowed when otherwise specified in a directive, letter of agreement, or coordinated at the time of handoff.

choices.text: A directive, letter of agreement, or specific coordination at the time of handoff authorizes it.
The aircraft is still in the transferring facility's area but is descending toward your airspace.
The pilot volunteers to change codes before being asked.
The receiving controller would prefer to see a different code earlier on the display.

items.question_text: Under 5-2-2, absent other specification or coordination, you do not request an aircraft to change from the code it was squawking in the transferring facility's area until the aircraft is within your area of responsibility.
items.explanation: Per 5-2-2, that is the normal rule for radar beacon code changes between facilities.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: What is the normal beacon-code rule under 5-2-8 for pressure suit flights and flights above FL 600 when a special-use code is filed?
items.explanation: Per 5-2-8a, ensure these flights remain on one of the special-use codes if filed in the flight plan, except for the stated conflict cases.

choices.text: Ensure the flight remains on one of the filed special-use codes.
Always assign an ordinary discrete ARTCC code after takeoff.
Tell the pilot to squawk VFR below FL 600 and resume the special-use code only above FL 600.
Use Code 4000 for all pressure suit flights regardless of what is filed.

items.question_text: When may a controller request a code change, squawk standby, or stop squawk under 5-2-8?
items.explanation: Per 5-2-8b, if unforeseen events place more than one aircraft in the same or adjacent ARTCC's airspace at the same time on the same special-use discrete code, the controller may request a change, standby, or stop squawk as appropriate.

choices.text: When unforeseen events put more than one aircraft in the same or adjacent ARTCC airspace at the same time on the same special-use discrete code and action is necessary.
Whenever a pilot asks for a more convenient code, regardless of mission status.
Only when the aircraft descends below FL 600.
Whenever automation shows multiple targets on one code, because a code change is always mandatory.

items.question_text: Under 5-2-8, in an emergency Code 7700 can still be activated, and ERAM may display EMRG while STARS or MEARTS may display EM.
items.explanation: Per note 3 in 5-2-8, Code 7700 remains available for emergency use, with EMRG and EM as the automation labels.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: What must an en route controller ensure for exercise FAKER aircraft under 5-2-9?
items.explanation: Per 5-2-9, ensure exercise FAKER aircraft remain on the exercise flight plan filed discrete beacon code.

choices.text: Ensure the aircraft remains on the exercise flight plan filed discrete beacon code.
Assign an ordinary ARTCC computer code after handoff acceptance.
Instruct the aircraft to squawk VFR outside military areas.
Place the aircraft on standby until NORAD revalidates the plan.

items.question_text: What does NORAD ensure regarding exercise FAKER aircraft that are assigned the same discrete beacon code under 5-2-9?
items.explanation: Per note 2 in 5-2-9, NORAD ensures those aircraft are not flight planned in the same or adjacent ARTCC airspace at the same time, except when operational requirements necessitate simultaneous assignment.

choices.text: They are not flight planned in the same or any adjacent ARTCC's airspace at the same time unless operational requirements necessitate it.
They always remain in the same ARTCC together so the code can be monitored more easily.
They are automatically reassigned Code 4000 any time they enter adjacent ARTCC airspace.
They must never use the same discrete code under any circumstances.

items.question_text: Under 5-2-9, simultaneous assignment of the same discrete beacon code to exercise FAKER aircraft will occur only when operational requirements necessitate it.
items.explanation: Per note 2 in 5-2-9, simultaneous assignment occurs only when operational requirements necessitate it.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 5-4-2, what is a handoff?
items.explanation: Per 5-4-2a, a handoff transfers radar identification to another controller if the aircraft will enter the receiving controller's airspace and radio communications will be transferred.

choices.text: An action to transfer radar identification to another controller when the aircraft will enter that controller's airspace and communications will be transferred.
An action to transfer radar identification without any communications transfer.
A term used only to coordinate traffic restrictions with no airspace entry approval.
A pilot readback used to acknowledge radar service.

items.question_text: What does the term 'Point Out Approved' mean under 5-4-2?
items.explanation: Per 5-4-2d, it means the aircraft is identified and approval is granted for it to enter the receiving controller's airspace as coordinated without a communications transfer or the appropriate automated response.

choices.text: The aircraft is identified and approved to enter the receiving controller's airspace as coordinated without a communications transfer.
The handoff is complete and the pilot must immediately change frequency.
Traffic separation restrictions are understood and will be complied with.
Radar contact is terminated and responsibility returns to the transferring controller.

items.question_text: Under 5-4-2, a point out transfers radar identification without transferring radio communications.
items.explanation: Per 5-4-2c, that is the definition of a point out.

items.question_text: What does 5-4-1 require regarding interfacility and intrafacility transfers of radar identification?
items.explanation: Per 5-4-1, such transfers must be accomplished in all areas of radar surveillance except where it is not operationally feasible.

choices.text: They must be accomplished in all areas of radar surveillance except where not operationally feasible.
They are optional whenever both controllers can see the target on their scopes.
They are required only for interfacility transfers, not intrafacility transfers.
They may be omitted any time communications are transferred before the boundary.

items.question_text: When radar handoff-based control is not operationally feasible under 5-4-1, how must that constraint be handled?
items.explanation: Per 5-4-1, the constraint must be covered in an LOA stating that control will not be based on a radar handoff, or coordinated by the transferring and receiving controllers for a specified period of time.

choices.text: By an LOA clearly stating control will not be based on a radar handoff, or by controller coordination for the specified period.
By informal practice at the position, without any written or verbal coordination.
By transferring communications first and determining control responsibility later.
By suspending radar service until traffic demand decreases.

items.question_text: If radar-handoff-based control is not operationally feasible, controllers may omit the transfer of radar identification without an LOA or specific coordination.
items.explanation: Per 5-4-1, any such constraint must be handled by LOA or coordination for a specified period; it is not simply omitted without coordination.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Which of the following is an authorized method to transfer radar identification under 5-4-3?
items.explanation: Per 5-4-3a, radar identification may be transferred by physically pointing to the target, landline voice communications, or automation capabilities, with the terminal Modify/Quick Look limitation stated separately.

choices.text: Physically point to the target on the receiving controller's display.
Instruct the pilot to ident and let the receiving controller assume the correct target automatically.
Transfer communications and wait for the receiving controller to call back if the target is uncertain.
Use Quick Look between any terminal positions without a facility directive.

items.question_text: When making a handoff, point out, or issuing traffic restrictions under 5-4-3, what information is normally relayed immediately after the aircraft identification?
items.explanation: Per 5-4-3b, after the aircraft identification, relay the assigned altitude, applicable restrictions, and whether the aircraft is climbing or descending if applicable, except when directives ensure that altitude information will already be known.

choices.text: Assigned altitude, applicable restrictions, and climb or descent information if applicable.
The next frequency and communications transfer point.
The receiving controller's preferred coordination method.
Only the route of flight, because altitude is inferred from the data block in all cases.

items.question_text: When physically pointing to the target during a handoff or point out, you still must state the aircraft position.
items.explanation: Per the note in 5-4-3b4, when physically pointing to the target, you do not have to state the aircraft position.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 5-4-5, when must the transferring controller complete a handoff unless otherwise coordinated or specified?
items.explanation: Per 5-4-5a, the handoff must be completed before the aircraft enters the airspace delegated to the receiving controller.

choices.text: Before the aircraft enters the airspace delegated to the receiving controller.
Only after the aircraft checks in on the receiving controller's frequency.
Any time before the aircraft exits the receiving controller's sector.
After the aircraft is established in the receiving controller's airspace and separation is assured.

items.question_text: What must the transferring controller do before changing an aircraft's flight path, altitude, speed, or data block information while a handoff is being initiated or after acceptance under 5-4-5?
items.explanation: Per 5-4-5b, verbal approval from the receiving controller is required unless otherwise coordinated or specified in an LOA or facility directive.

choices.text: Verbally obtain the receiving controller's approval first.
Make the change first and include it in the next automated update.
Wait until communications are transferred, then issue the change without further coordination.
Cancel the handoff and immediately reinitiate it with no verbal coordination.

items.question_text: Under 5-4-5, the transferring controller must advise the receiving controller if radar monitoring is required.
items.explanation: Per 5-4-5f, the transferring controller must advise the receiving controller if radar monitoring is required.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Before accepting a handoff under 5-4-6, what must the receiving controller ensure?
items.explanation: Per 5-4-6a, ensure the target position matches the transferring controller's information or that there is an appropriate association between the automated data block and the target being transferred.

choices.text: That the target position corresponds with the transferring controller's information or that the automated association is appropriate.
That the pilot has already been transferred to your frequency.
That the data block shows a full route of flight and requested altitude.
That the aircraft has crossed the sector boundary before you accept the handoff.

items.question_text: What action is required under 5-4-6 when 'DATA,' 'CST,' 'NONE,' or 'OLD' is displayed in the data block before accepting control of a track?
items.explanation: Per 5-4-6f3, the receiving controller must initiate verbal coordination.

choices.text: Initiate verbal coordination.
Accept control and wait to see whether the indication clears automatically.
Delete the data block and rely on primary radar only.
Tell the pilot to recycle the transponder before accepting the handoff.

items.question_text: Under 5-4-6, if a displayed discrete code is automatically removed from the data block when the aircraft changes to the computer assigned code, that observation satisfies the beacon target confirmation requirement.
items.explanation: Per the note under 5-4-6e2, observation of the code removal from the data block satisfies confirmation requirements when the aircraft changes to the computer assigned code.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Before permitting an aircraft to enter the receiving controller's delegated airspace under 5-4-7, what must the transferring controller obtain?
items.explanation: Per 5-4-7a1, the transferring controller must obtain approval before permitting the aircraft to enter the receiving controller's delegated airspace.

choices.text: Approval from the receiving controller.
A pilot readback confirming the next frequency.
A new beacon code assignment for the aircraft.
A supervisor's approval to begin communications transfer.

items.question_text: If an en route automated point out receives no action from the receiving controller under 5-4-7, what must the transferring controller do?
items.explanation: Per 5-4-7a1(a), if the receiving controller takes no action on an automated point out, revert to verbal procedures.

choices.text: Revert to verbal procedures.
Treat the lack of response as automatic approval.
Transfer communications and wait for the receiving controller to respond after check-in.
Convert the point out into prearranged coordination without further action.

items.question_text: Under 5-4-7, once a point out has been approved, the transferring controller must obtain the receiving controller's approval before changing the aircraft's flight path, altitude, speed, or data block information.
items.explanation: Per 5-4-7a2, approval is required before making those changes after the point out has been approved.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 5-4-8, when may automated information transfer be used without verbal coordination?
items.explanation: Per 5-4-8, AIT may be used without verbal coordination only during radar handoff, via information displayed in full data blocks, and when following procedures specified in a facility AIT directive and or LOA.

choices.text: During radar handoff, via information displayed in full data blocks, and while following procedures in a facility AIT directive or LOA.
Any time both controllers can see the target, even if no AIT directive or LOA exists.
Only after the pilot has checked in on the receiving controller's frequency.
Only when the transferring controller also sends verbal coordination as backup.

items.question_text: Which display requirement is specifically stated in 5-4-8 for using automated information transfer without verbal coordination?
items.explanation: Per 5-4-8b, the transferred information must be displayed in full data blocks.

choices.text: The information must be displayed in full data blocks.
The information may be displayed in limited data blocks so long as the target is correlated.
The information must be written in free text on the fourth line.
The information must be displayed only on the transferring controller's scope.

items.question_text: Under 5-4-8, automated information transfer may be used without verbal coordination even if no facility AIT directive or LOA covers the procedure.
items.explanation: Per 5-4-8c, the procedure must follow the facility AIT directive and or LOA.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: When may prearranged coordination be approved under 5-4-9?
items.explanation: Per 5-4-9, prearranged coordination may be approved only when procedures are established and published in a facility directive in accordance with FAA Order JO 7210.3.

choices.text: Only when procedures are established and published in a facility directive in accordance with JO 7210.3.
Whenever both controllers have informally agreed to use it on that shift.
Any time traffic volume is high enough to justify faster coordination.
Whenever radar handoff is unavailable, regardless of local directives.

items.question_text: Which of the following is listed in the 5-4-9 note as a proper means of coordination?
items.explanation: The 5-4-9 note lists several proper means of coordination, including radar handoff, automated information transfer, verbal coordination, point out, and prearranged coordination procedures in a facility directive.

choices.text: Point out.
Waiting for the pilot to call the next sector and advise position.
Allowing entry first and coordinating only if traffic becomes a factor.
Treating silence from the other controller as automatic approval.

items.question_text: Under 5-4-9, one controller may never permit an aircraft to enter another controller's airspace without proper coordination.
items.explanation: Per the 5-4-9 note, under no circumstances may one controller permit an aircraft to enter another's airspace without proper coordination.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: What control information may be forwarded in the en route fourth line under 5-4-10?
items.explanation: Per 5-4-10a, only messages specified in the section, a facility directive, or an LOA are authorized in the fourth line for forwarding control information. Additional control information must be sent by other communication methods.

choices.text: Only messages specified in the section, a facility directive, or an LOA.
Any control information the sector team can abbreviate clearly enough.
Only heading changes; all other data must be verbalized.
Any information that fits in the character field after handoff acceptance.

items.question_text: What does acceptance of a handoff constitute with respect to the en route fourth line data block under 5-4-10?
items.explanation: Per 5-4-10l, acceptance of the handoff by the receiving controller constitutes receipt of the information contained in the en route fourth line data block.

choices.text: Receipt of the information contained in the en route fourth line data block.
Automatic approval for either controller to modify the fourth line outside their area of jurisdiction.
Permission to leave free text in the fourth line after identification transfer begins.
A requirement that all remaining coordination be completed by fourth-line entries only.

items.question_text: Under 5-4-10, free text may be used by individual sector teams for recording information they deem appropriate for managing the sector, but it must be removed prior to initiation of identification transfer.
items.explanation: Per 5-4-10a, free text may be used locally for sector management, but it must be removed before identification transfer is initiated.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: To which RNAV aircraft must radar separation be applied under 5-5-1?
items.explanation: Per 5-5-1a, radar separation must be applied to all RNAV aircraft operating at and below FL450 on Q routes or random RNAV routes, excluding oceanic airspace.

choices.text: All RNAV aircraft operating at and below FL450 on Q routes or random RNAV routes, excluding oceanic airspace.
Only RNAV aircraft operating above FL450 on Q routes.
Only RNAV aircraft operating on published ATS routes inside oceanic airspace.
Only point-to-point RNAV routes via an impromptu route.

items.question_text: When may radar separation be applied between an aircraft taking off and another radar-identified aircraft under 5-5-1?
items.explanation: Per 5-5-1b2, radar separation may be applied when the aircraft taking off will be radar-identified within 1 mile of the runway end.

choices.text: When the aircraft taking off will be radar-identified within 1 mile of the runway end.

choices.text: Use the lesser divergence value.
Use the greater divergence value.
Average the two divergence values.
Use whichever value gives the shorter distance.

items.question_text: Under 6-5-2, the diverging-radials procedure may be applied to converging as well as diverging aircraft.
items.explanation: Per 6-5-2, the procedure may be applied to converging as well as diverging aircraft.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 6-5-3, what minimum lateral DME separation applies between different arcs about a NAVAID when the arcs are 35 miles or less from the NAVAID?
items.explanation: Per 6-5-3a1, the minimum is 10 miles.

choices.text: 10 miles.
5 miles.
20 miles.
15 miles.

items.question_text: Under 6-5-3, what minimum applies between an arc about a NAVAID and other airspace to be protected when more than 35 miles from the NAVAID?
items.explanation: Per 6-5-3b2, the minimum is 10 miles.

choices.text: 10 miles.
5 miles.
20 miles.
30 miles.

items.question_text: Under 6-5-3, the minimum between different arcs about a NAVAID is applied regardless of direction of flight.
items.explanation: Per 6-5-3a, the arc minima apply regardless of direction of flight.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 6-5-4a4, what lateral protection applies to a GNSS-equipped RNAV aircraft being provided nonradar separation on a random RNAV route segment?
items.explanation: Per 6-5-4a4(b), protect 4 miles either side of the route centerline.

choices.text: 4 miles either side of the route centerline.
10 miles either side of the route centerline.
14 miles on the overflown side only.
17 miles either side of the route centerline.

items.question_text: What is the maximum distance permitted between point-to-point route segment points for GNSS-equipped RNAV aircraft under 6-5-4a4?
items.explanation: Per 6-5-4a4(a), the maximum distance between points must not exceed 500 miles.

choices.text: 500 miles.
300 miles.
130 miles.
700 miles.

items.question_text: Under 6-5-4a4, assigned altitudes for GNSS-equipped RNAV aircraft on random RNAV route segments must be at or above the highest MIA along the projected route segment being flown, including the protected airspace of that segment.
items.explanation: Per 6-5-4a4(c), the assigned altitude must be at or above the highest MIA along the projected route segment, including the protected airspace.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 6-5-5, when operating along a route that is 8 miles or less in width, how far beyond the point where protected airspace no longer overlaps must an RNAV aircraft be before lateral separation exists?
items.explanation: Per 6-5-5a, the required distance is 4 miles.

choices.text: 4 miles.
9 miles.
15 miles.
18 miles.

items.question_text: Under 6-5-5, what minimum applies along a route segment greater than 8 miles in width when no extended usable distance limitation beyond 130 miles is involved?
items.explanation: Per 6-5-5b, the minimum is 9 miles unless the extended-distance exception requires 15 miles.

choices.text: 9 miles.
4 miles.
15 miles.
20 miles.

items.question_text: Under 6-5-5, when a route segment requires extended usable distance limitation beyond 130 miles of the reference facility, 15 miles must be applied.
items.explanation: Per 6-5-5b, 15 miles applies along that portion of the route segment.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 6-6-1, when may an altitude be assigned to another aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 6-6-1, assign an altitude after the aircraft previously at that altitude has reported leaving the altitude.

choices.text: After the aircraft previously at that altitude has reported leaving the altitude.
As soon as the first aircraft is observed beginning a climb or descent on Mode C.
Only after the first aircraft reports at or passing through another altitude separated by the appropriate minimum in every case.
As soon as the pilot acknowledges the altitude change clearance, even if still level.

items.question_text: If an aircraft is known to be operating at or above the lowest usable flight level under 6-6-1, what phraseology should be used?
items.explanation: Per 6-6-1, if the aircraft is known to be operating at or above the lowest usable flight level, use 'SAY FLIGHT LEVEL.'

choices.text: SAY FLIGHT LEVEL.
SAY ALTITUDE.
SAY ALTITUDE OR FLIGHT LEVEL.
REPORT LEAVING ODD FLIGHT LEVELS.

items.question_text: Under 6-6-1, controllers should consider known aircraft performance characteristics and pilot-furnished or Mode C-detected information indicating that climb or descent will not be consistent with the rates recommended in the AIM.
items.explanation: Per 6-6-1, those factors should be considered when applying the paragraph.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 6-6-2, which operation is one of the exceptions requiring the previously assigned aircraft to report at or passing through another altitude separated by the appropriate minimum before that altitude may be reassigned?
items.explanation: Per 6-6-2, the exceptions include severe turbulence, military aerial refueling, pilot's-discretion climb or descent, and cruise clearances.

choices.text: Severe turbulence is reported.
The previous aircraft has merely reported leaving the altitude under normal conditions.
The aircraft are operating on different airways with standard lateral separation.
The aircraft is climbing at a rate consistent with the AIM recommendations.

items.question_text: Under 6-6-2, what block of airspace does a cruise clearance assign to an aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 6-6-2, a cruise clearance assigns a block of airspace from the minimum IFR altitude up to and including the assigned cruising altitude, with climb and descent within the block at pilot's discretion.

choices.text: From the minimum IFR altitude up to and including the assigned cruising altitude.
Only the single assigned cruising altitude.
From the assigned cruising altitude up to the next higher usable flight level.
From the minimum IFR altitude up to but not including the assigned cruising altitude.

items.question_text: Under 6-6-2, Mode C may be used to effect separation with an aircraft on a cruise clearance.
items.explanation: Per 6-6-2c2, do not use Mode C to effect separation with an aircraft on a cruise clearance.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 7-1-1, which clearance may not be issued in Class A airspace?
items.explanation: Per 7-1-1, do not issue VFR or VFR-on-top clearances in Class A airspace.

choices.text: A VFR-on-top clearance.
An IFR altitude change.
A speed adjustment.
A route amendment.

items.question_text: Under 7-1-1, visual separation may be applied in Class A airspace if both pilots agree.
items.explanation: Per 7-1-1, visual separation is not applied in Class A airspace.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 7-1-2, when may an aircraft be cleared to maintain VFR conditions?
items.explanation: Per 7-1-2a, this may be done when the pilot on an IFR flight plan requests a VFR climb or descent, or in terminal operations when the clearance will result in noise-abatement benefits under the stated conditions.

choices.text: When the pilot on an IFR flight plan requests a VFR climb or descent, or in terminal operations when the clearance yields the stated noise-abatement benefit.
Only when the aircraft is already operating VFR and requests advisories.
Any time the aircraft is in Class A airspace and can remain above the clouds.
Only when the tower has already approved a visual approach.

items.question_text: What must a controller do under 7-1-2 if there is reason to believe that flight in VFR conditions may become impractical?
items.explanation: Per 7-1-2b, issue an alternative clearance that ensures separation from all other aircraft for which the controller has separation responsibility.

choices.text: Issue an alternative clearance that ensures separation from all other aircraft for which the controller has separation responsibility.
Continue the existing clearance until the pilot requests IFR separation restoration.
Cancel the IFR flight plan because VFR-conditions clearances eliminate ATC separation responsibility.
Use visual separation instead of issuing any alternative clearance.

items.question_text: Under 7-1-2, 'IF UNABLE, (alternative procedure), AND ADVISE.' is published phraseology associated with maintaining VFR conditions.
items.explanation: Per 7-1-2, that phraseology is published as part of the alternative-clearance handling when VFR conditions may become impractical.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 7-1-3, which landing information may be omitted when it is contained in the ATIS broadcast and the pilot states the appropriate ATIS code or uses the phrase 'have numbers'?
items.explanation: Per 7-1-3a, wind, runway, and altimeter setting may be omitted in that case.

choices.text: Wind, runway, and altimeter setting.
Traffic information and tower contact instructions.
Only the altimeter setting.
All approach-control landing information.

items.question_text: Under 7-1-3, how is traffic information issued to VFR arriving aircraft contacting approach control for landing information?
items.explanation: Per 7-1-3b, traffic information is issued on a workload-permitting basis.

choices.text: On a workload-permitting basis.
Only when the pilot reports the airport in sight.
Only if the aircraft lacks an ATIS code.
Only after tower requests it from approach control.

items.question_text: Under 7-1-3, a pilot's use of 'have numbers' confirms receipt of the ATIS broadcast.
items.explanation: Per 7-1-3, pilot use of 'have numbers' does not indicate receipt of the ATIS broadcast.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 7-1-4, what type of fix should normally be selected for visual holding of VFR aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 7-1-4a, use selected, prominent geographical fixes which can be easily recognized from the air, preferably those depicted on sectional charts.

choices.text: A selected, prominent geographical fix easily recognized from the air, preferably depicted on sectional charts.
Any unpublished point the controller finds convenient, even if it is hard to recognize visually.
Only the nearest NAVAID, regardless of whether it is visually recognizable.
Only a fix chosen by the pilot after entering the hold.

items.question_text: Under 7-1-4, what must be issued to aircraft cleared to hold at the same visual holding fix?
items.explanation: Per 7-1-4b, issue traffic information to aircraft cleared to hold at the same fix.

choices.text: Traffic information.
Only wake turbulence cautionary advisories.
Only an altitude assignment for each aircraft.
No additional information, because the shared fix itself provides the needed holding awareness.

items.question_text: Under 7-1-4, depicted VFR checkpoints are preferred in selecting geographical fixes unless the pilot exhibits familiarity with the local area.
items.explanation: Per 7-1-4, depicted VFR checkpoints are preferred unless the pilot exhibits familiarity with the local area.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 7-2-1, when is visual separation not authorized based on the lead aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 7-2-1, visual separation is not authorized when the lead aircraft is a super.

choices.text: When the lead aircraft is a super.
When the lead aircraft is a heavy.
When the lead aircraft is a helicopter.

items.question_text: Under 7-2-1 pilot-applied visual separation, what must the pilot state before the controller may approve the operation instead of restating the instruction?
items.explanation: Per 7-2-1a2(c) and b2(d), the pilot must report both that the traffic is in sight and that the pilot will maintain visual separation.

choices.text: That the traffic is in sight and that the pilot will maintain visual separation.
Only that the traffic is in sight.
Only that the pilot can accept wake turbulence responsibility.

items.explanation: Per 7-2-1, visual separation is not authorized when the lead aircraft is a super.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 7-3-1, who may request a VFR-on-top clearance?
items.explanation: Per 7-3-1a, the pilot of an aircraft on an IFR flight plan may request the clearance.

choices.text: The pilot of an aircraft on an IFR flight plan.
Only the controller, without pilot request.
Any VFR aircraft requesting flight following.

items.question_text: Under 7-3-1, what must controllers continue to provide to aircraft operating VFR-on-top even though IFR separation is not applied?
items.explanation: Per 7-3-1a note 2, controllers must continue to provide traffic advisories and safety alerts and apply merging target procedures.

choices.text: Traffic advisories and safety alerts, and merging target procedures.
Only wake turbulence advisories.
IFR vertical separation only.
No additional service once the aircraft is VFR-on-top.

items.question_text: Under 7-3-1, controllers must continue to provide traffic advisories and safety alerts and apply merging target procedures to aircraft operating VFR-on-top.
items.explanation: Per 7-3-1a note 2, those services continue even though IFR separation is not applied.

False

items.question_text: Under 7-4-1, what is a visual approach?
items.explanation: Per 7-4-1, a visual approach is an ATC authorization for an aircraft on an IFR flight plan to proceed visually and clear of clouds to the airport of intended landing.

choices.text: An ATC authorization for an IFR aircraft to proceed visually and clear of clouds to the airport of intended landing.
A standard instrument approach with a published missed approach segment.
A VFR pattern entry clearance for any arriving aircraft.
A pilot-initiated visual separation procedure between two IFR aircraft.

items.question_text: Under 7-4-1, what does a visual approach not have?
items.explanation: Per 7-4-1, a visual approach is not a standard instrument approach procedure and has no missed approach segment.

choices.text: A missed approach segment.
An IFR flight plan requirement.
Cloud-clearance responsibility for the pilot.
Go-around handling by ATC.

items.question_text: Under 7-4-1, an aircraft unable to complete a landing from a visual approach must be handled as any go-around and appropriate IFR separation must be provided until the aircraft lands or the pilot cancels the IFR flight plan.
items.explanation: Per 7-4-1, that is the required handling for a go-around from a visual approach.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 7-4-2, what weather minima are required to initiate a vector for a visual approach at an airport with weather reporting service?
items.explanation: Per 7-4-2, the reported ceiling must be at least 500 feet above the MVA/MIA and the visibility must be 3 miles or greater.

choices.text: Ceiling at least 500 feet above the MVA/MIA and visibility 3 miles or greater.
Ceiling at least 500 feet above airport elevation and visibility 1 mile or greater.
Ceiling at least 1,000 feet above the MVA/MIA and visibility 5 miles or greater.

items.explanation: Per 7-4-2, the pilot must be informed that weather information is not available.

choices.text: That weather information is not available.
That the visual approach is automatically cancelled if the airport is not in sight.
That IFR separation no longer applies once the vector is issued.

items.question_text: Under 7-4-2, when weather information is not available at the airport, the pilot must be informed that weather information is not available.
items.explanation: Per 7-4-2, that advisory is required when vectors for a visual approach are initiated without airport weather reporting service.

False

items.question_text: At an airport without an operating control tower under 7-4-3, how should a visual approach clearance be issued?

choices.text: Issue the visual approach clearance to the airport only and do not specify a runway.
Issue the visual approach clearance to the requested runway so the pilot can line up for landing.
Issue a contact approach clearance instead of a visual approach clearance.

items.question_text: When wake turbulence separation is required under 7-4-3, what must be provided to the aircraft following another aircraft on a visual approach?

choices.text: Information on the lead aircraft's position and its manufacturer/model.
Only the lead aircraft's callsign because wake turbulence responsibility transfers automatically to the pilot.
Only the runway assignment because aircraft type is irrelevant on visual approaches.

items.question_text: Under 7-4-3, visual separation is not authorized when the lead aircraft is a super.
items.explanation: Per 7-4-3, visual separation is not authorized when the lead aircraft is a super aircraft.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 7-4-4, what must all aircraft be told when approaches are being conducted to parallel, intersecting, or converging runways?

items.question_text: When visual approach flight paths intersect under 7-4-4, when may approved separation be discontinued?
items.explanation: Per 7-4-4, approved separation must be maintained until visual separation is applied when flight paths intersect.

choices.text: Only after visual separation is applied.
As soon as both aircraft are cleared for visual approaches.
As soon as both aircraft are switched to the tower frequency.

items.explanation: Per 7-4-4, targets on adjacent final approach courses must not touch unless visual separation is applied.

False

items.question_text: Under 7-5-1, within what airspace may special VFR be authorized?
items.explanation: Per 7-5-1, special VFR is limited to the lateral boundaries of Class B, Class C, Class D, and Class E surface areas below 10,000 feet MSL, excluding Class E extension areas.

choices.text: Within the lateral boundaries of Class B, C, D, or E surface areas below 10,000 feet MSL, excluding Class E extension areas.
Within any controlled airspace, including Class E extension areas and airspace above 10,000 feet MSL.
Only within Class D airspace and only above 10,000 feet MSL.

items.question_text: Under 7-5-1, who must request a special VFR clearance?
items.explanation: Per 7-5-1, special VFR may be issued only when requested by the pilot.

choices.text: The pilot.
The tower controller whenever surface weather drops below basic VFR minima.
The radar controller after coordination with the overlying IFR facility.
The airport operator when local weather deteriorates.

items.question_text: Under 7-5-1, powered-lift aircraft are handled as helicopters for special VFR purposes.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 7-5-2, when may special VFR flights be approved?
items.explanation: Per 7-5-2, SVFR flights may be approved only if arriving and departing IFR flights are not delayed.

choices.text: Only if arriving and departing IFR flights are not delayed.
Any time the pilot requests it, regardless of IFR impact.

items.question_text: If a special VFR clearance cannot be granted because of IFR traffic under 7-5-2, what should the pilot be told?
items.explanation: Per 7-5-2, the pilot must be told the anticipated delay.

choices.text: The anticipated delay.
To depart VFR at the pilot's discretion without a clearance.
That special VFR is unavailable for the rest of the day.

items.question_text: Under 7-5-2, when special VFR cannot be granted because of IFR traffic, the controller should issue an EFC or expected departure time instead of an anticipated delay advisory.

False

items.question_text: Under 7-3-2, when should a controller inform an aircraft maintaining VFR-on-top about the proper cruising levels for its direction of flight?
items.explanation: Per 7-3-2, the controller informs the aircraft when a report indicates the pilot is not complying with 14 CFR section 91.159(a).

choices.text: When a report indicates the pilot is not complying with 14 CFR section 91.159(a).
Whenever any aircraft requests VFR-on-top, regardless of the reported altitude.
Only after the pilot asks ATC which VFR cruising altitude should be used.

items.question_text: For magnetic courses 0 through 179 under 7-3-2, what VFR-on-top cruising levels apply more than 3,000 feet above the surface to and including FL 180?
items.explanation: Per 7-3-2 and 14 CFR section 91.159(a), magnetic courses 0 through 179 use odd cardinal altitudes or flight levels plus 500 feet.

choices.text: Odd altitudes or flight levels plus 500 feet.
Even altitudes or flight levels plus 500 feet.
Any cardinal altitude so long as the pilot remains clear of clouds.
Odd altitudes only, without the additional 500 feet.

items.question_text: Under 7-3-2, for magnetic courses 180 through 359, the proper VFR-on-top cruising levels more than 3,000 feet above the surface to and including FL 180 are even altitudes or flight levels plus 500 feet.

False

items.question_text: Under 7-4-5, what is required before a controller may clear an aircraft for a charted visual flight procedure?
items.explanation: Per 7-4-5, a CVFP may be issued only when there is an operating control tower and the other listed conditions are met.

choices.text: There must be an operating control tower.
The airport must be uncontrolled so the pilot can remain responsible for all separation.
The procedure may be used only when the airport is below basic VFR minima.

items.question_text: For an aircraft not following another aircraft on the approach under 7-4-5, what visual reference qualifies for a CVFP clearance?
items.explanation: Per 7-4-5, the aircraft not following traffic must report a charted visual landmark in sight, or must report the preceding aircraft landing on the same runway in sight and be instructed to follow it.

choices.text: A charted visual landmark in sight, or the preceding aircraft landing on the same runway in sight after being instructed to follow it.
Any airport traffic in sight, even if it is landing on a different runway.
Only the airport in sight; no charted landmark may be used for a CVFP.

items.question_text: Under 7-4-5, the reported ceiling at the airport of intended landing must be at least 500 feet above the MVA/MIA and the visibility must be 3 miles or more, unless higher minimums are published for that CVFP.
items.explanation: Per 7-4-5, those are the baseline weather requirements unless the individual CVFP publishes higher minima.

False

items.question_text: Under 7-4-6, what altitude action is required before issuing an RVFP approach clearance?
items.explanation: Per 7-4-6, an altitude at or above the MVA/MIA must be assigned before issuing the RVFP clearance.

choices.text: Assign an altitude at or above the MVA/MIA before issuing the approach clearance.

items.question_text: Under 7-4-6, what weather must be reported at the airport of intended landing before an RVFP may be cleared?
items.explanation: Per 7-4-6, the official weather must indicate VFR and should meet or exceed the ceiling and visibility specified on the RVFP.

choices.text: Official weather indicating VFR that should meet or exceed the ceiling and visibility specified on the RVFP.
Any weather so long as the pilot is an authorized operator and requests the procedure.

items.question_text: At a location with an operating control tower under 7-4-6, the pilot may satisfy the visual-reference requirement by reporting the preceding aircraft in sight.
items.explanation: Per 7-4-6, at a towered airport the pilot may report either the airport or the preceding aircraft in sight.

False

items.question_text: Under 7-4-7, who must initiate a contact approach request?
items.explanation: Per 7-4-7, the pilot must request a contact approach; controllers do not initiate or suggest it.

items.question_text: What reported ground visibility is required under 7-4-7 before a contact approach may be cleared?
items.explanation: Per 7-4-7, the reported ground visibility must be at least 1 statute mile.

choices.text: At least 1 statute mile.
At least 1/2 statute mile.

False

items.question_text: Under 7-5-3, what types of separation may be applied between an SVFR fixed-wing aircraft and an IFR aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 7-5-3, use nonradar or visual separation between SVFR fixed-wing aircraft and IFR aircraft.

choices.text: Nonradar or visual separation.
IFR radar separation only.

items.question_text: Under 7-5-3, what types of separation may be applied between SVFR helicopters and IFR aircraft?

items.explanation: Per 7-5-3, radar separation is not authorized during SVFR fixed-wing operations, although radar vectors may be used to expedite entrance, exit, or transition.

False

items.question_text: When applying vertical separation under 7-5-4, how should an SVFR aircraft be cleared?
items.explanation: Per 7-5-4, clear the SVFR aircraft at or below an altitude at least 500 feet below conflicting IFR traffic, but not below the minimum safe altitude.

choices.text: At or below an altitude at least 500 feet below conflicting IFR traffic, but not below the minimum safe altitude.
At a fixed altitude exactly 500 feet below conflicting IFR traffic.
At or above an altitude 500 feet below conflicting IFR traffic.

items.question_text: Why are SVFR aircraft not assigned fixed altitudes to maintain under 7-5-4?
items.explanation: Per the note in 7-5-4, SVFR aircraft are not assigned fixed altitudes because of the clearance-from-clouds requirement.

choices.text: Because of the clearance-from-clouds requirement.
Because SVFR aircraft must remain exactly at pattern altitude.

items.question_text: Under 7-5-4, SVFR aircraft are not assigned fixed altitudes to maintain because of the clearance-from-clouds requirement.
items.explanation: Per the note in 7-5-4, that is why fixed altitude assignments are not used for SVFR aircraft in this context.

items.question_text: Under 7-5-5, when may local SVFR operations be authorized upon request?
items.explanation: Per 7-5-5, local SVFR operations may be authorized for a specified period if the aircraft can be recalled when traffic or weather conditions require.

choices.text: When they are for a specified period and the aircraft can be recalled if traffic or weather requires.
Only when the airport is uncontrolled and the pilot agrees to remain outside the traffic pattern.
Only when there is no IFR traffic in the terminal area at all.

items.question_text: Under 7-5-5, when may an FSS be authorized to transmit SVFR clearances allowing more than one aircraft to operate in the surface area?
items.explanation: Per 7-5-5, this may occur only when the pilots agree to maintain visual separation and the authorization is contained in an LOA between the control facility and the FSS.

Enter Charlie airspace and maintain VFR at pilot discretion.
Proceed direct through Charlie airspace and expect services later.
Remain outside the outer area only; entry into Charlie airspace is still authorized.

items.question_text: Under 7-8-4, if workload prevents immediate provision of Class C services, the pilot should be told to remain outside Class C airspace until conditions permit the services to be provided.
items.explanation: That is the instruction required in 7-8-4 when services cannot be provided immediately.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 7-8-8, unless one of the stated exceptions applies, how long should Class C service be provided?
items.explanation: Per 7-8-8, provide services until the aircraft departs the associated outer area unless the aircraft is landing at a secondary airport or has requested termination while in the outer area.

choices.text: Until the aircraft departs the associated outer area.
Only until the aircraft is within 10 miles of any airport in the Class C area.
Only until the aircraft reports the destination airport in sight.
Only until the aircraft passes the Class C primary airport, even if it remains in the outer area.

items.question_text: When an aircraft is landing at other than the primary airport under 7-8-8, when should Class C service be terminated?
items.explanation: Per 7-8-8, terminate service at a sufficient distance from the airport to allow the pilot to change to the appropriate frequency for traffic and airport information.

choices.text: At a sufficient distance to allow the pilot to change to the appropriate traffic and airport-information frequency.
Only after touchdown, because Class C service must continue through the entire landing at every airport in the area.
Immediately upon advising the aircraft of the destination airport location.
Only after the aircraft exits the surface area and reports parked.

items.question_text: Under 7-8-8, a pilot may request termination of Class C service while in the outer area.
items.explanation: The paragraph specifically lists requested termination while in the outer area as one of the exceptions to continuing service until departure from the outer area.

items.question_text: Complete the phraseology from 7-8-8: "CHANGE TO _____ FREQUENCY APPROVED."
items.explanation: Per 7-8-8, the phraseology is CHANGE TO ADVISORY FREQUENCY APPROVED.

choices.text: ADVISORY

items.question_text: Under 7-9-1, where are Class B services and procedures applied?
