items.question_text: Under 7-9-1, what equipment is required for operation within Class B airspace unless an exception applies?
items.explanation: Per 7-9-1b, the aircraft must have an operable two-way radio and the applicable transponder and automatic altitude reporting equipment required by 14 CFR 91.215, except as provided in paragraph (d).

choices.text: An operable two-way radio and the applicable transponder and automatic altitude reporting equipment required by 14 CFR 91.215, unless an exception applies.

False

items.question_text: Under 7-9-2, what must a VFR aircraft obtain before operating in Class B airspace?

items.explanation: Per 7-9-2b, approve or deny the request based on workload, operational limitations, and traffic conditions.

items.question_text: Under 7-9-3, how should large turbine engine-powered airplanes be cleared to or from the primary airport to the extent practical?

items.question_text: Under 7-9-3, what must the controller do if spacing requires extending an aircraft's flight path outside Class B airspace after entry?
items.explanation: Per 7-9-3b, inform the aircraft when leaving and reentering Class B airspace.

Do nothing further as long as the aircraft remains on a vector.

items.explanation: That requirement appears in 7-9-6.

items.question_text: Under 8-1-1, how is ATC service provided in oceanic controlled airspace?

items.question_text: Under 8-3-1, what longitudinal standard is applied when one aircraft requires a different time-based standard than another?

items.question_text: Under 8-4-1, what lateral minimum applies within the Gulf of America Low Offshore airspace between aircraft whose flight paths are defined by published Grid System waypoints?
items.explanation: Per 8-4-1, use 12 NM between those flight paths.

choices.text: 12 NM.
10 NM.
15 NM.
20 NM.

items.question_text: Under 8-4-1, within the Gulf of America Low Offshore airspace, use 12 NM between aircraft whose flight paths are defined by published Grid System waypoints.
items.explanation: That minimum is stated in 8-4-1.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 8-4-2, when may the oceanic-boundary transition method be used where aircraft are entering airspace with a larger lateral minimum than the airspace being exited?
items.explanation: Per 8-4-2a3, the smaller separation must exist at the boundary and the flight paths must diverge by 15 degrees or more until the larger minimum is established.

choices.text: When the smaller separation exists at the boundary and the flight paths diverge by 15 degrees or more until the larger minimum is established.
Whenever the aircraft are crossing a boundary and any divergence exists.
Only when the larger minimum is already established before either aircraft reaches the boundary.
Only when both aircraft are using the same protected-airspace width.

items.question_text: Under 8-4-2, for intersecting flight paths with constant but different width protected airspace, what establishes the applicable lateral separation minimum?
items.explanation: Per 8-4-2c, use the sum of the protected airspace of both flight paths measured perpendicular to the flight path of the other aircraft.

choices.text: The sum of the protected airspace of both flight paths measured perpendicular to the flight path of the other aircraft.
Only the larger of the two protected-airspace widths.
The smaller protected-airspace width so long as the aircraft are not level at the intersection.
The distance from the intersection to the next common fix.

items.question_text: Under 8-4-2, for variable-width protected airspace, the measurement is taken at the first point or the last point of protected-airspace overlap, as applicable.
items.explanation: That is the measurement rule in 8-4-2d.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 8-4-3, what must happen before route protected airspace may be reduced?
items.explanation: Per 8-4-3, the routes must be satisfactorily flight checked and notice must be given to users.

choices.text: The routes must be satisfactorily flight checked and notice must be given to users.
A pilot must request the reduced width and a supervisor must approve it for each flight.
The route must be above FL240 and entirely within radar coverage.
The route must be used only by turbojet aircraft.

items.question_text: Under 8-4-3, below FL240, to what width and distance may protected airspace be reduced before it begins widening again?
items.explanation: Per 8-4-3a, below FL240 it may be reduced to 5 miles on each side of centerline to a distance of 57.14 miles from the NAVAID.

choices.text: To 5 miles on each side of the route centerline to a distance of 57.14 miles from the NAVAID.
To 10 miles on each side of the route centerline to a distance of 114.29 miles from the NAVAID.
To 5 miles on each side of the route centerline to a distance of 114.29 miles from the NAVAID.
To 12 miles on each side of the route centerline to a distance of 57.14 miles from the NAVAID.

items.question_text: Under 8-4-3, at and above FL240, to what width and distance may protected airspace be reduced before it begins widening again?
items.explanation: Per 8-4-3b, at and above FL240 it may be reduced to 10 miles on each side of centerline to a distance of 114.29 miles from the NAVAID.

choices.text: To 10 miles on each side of the route centerline to a distance of 114.29 miles from the NAVAID.
To 5 miles on each side of the route centerline to a distance of 57.14 miles from the NAVAID.
To 10 miles on each side of the route centerline to a distance of 57.14 miles from the NAVAID.
To 12 miles on each side of the route centerline to a distance of 114.29 miles from the NAVAID.

items.question_text: Under 8-4-3, after the reduced-width segment, protected airspace increases on a 5-degree angle from the route centerline as measured at the NAVAID.
items.explanation: That widening rule is stated in both 8-4-3a and 8-4-3b.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 8-4-4, when does same-NAVAID VOR/VORTAC/TACAN track separation exist?
items.explanation: Per 8-4-4a1, the radials must diverge by at least 15 degrees and either aircraft must be clear of the airspace to be protected for the other aircraft, using Table 8-4-1 to determine the required distance.

choices.text: When the aircraft are established on radials of the same NAVAID diverging by at least 15 degrees and either aircraft is clear of the airspace to be protected for the other aircraft.
Whenever the radials diverge by at least 10 degrees, regardless of protected-airspace distance.
Only after both aircraft are 15 miles from the NAVAID, regardless of divergence angle.
Only when both aircraft are inbound to the same NAVAID.

items.question_text: Under 8-4-4, when may dead-reckoning track separation be considered to exist?
items.explanation: Per 8-4-4c1, DR separation exists when tracks diverge by at least 45 degrees and one aircraft is at least 15 miles from the point of intersection.

choices.text: When the tracks diverge by at least 45 degrees and one aircraft is at least 15 miles from the point of intersection.
When the tracks diverge by at least 30 degrees and one aircraft is at least 15 miles from the NAVAID.
When the tracks diverge by at least 15 degrees and both aircraft are 15 miles from the boundary.
Only when the point of intersection is determined by radar and not by reference to a navigation aid.

items.question_text: Under 8-4-4, same-NAVAID NDB track separation must not be used when one or both aircraft are inbound to the aid unless the distance of the aircraft from the facility can be readily determined by reference to the NAVAID.
items.explanation: That inbound limitation is stated in 8-4-4a2(a).

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 8-5-1, when does the altitude/flight level transition rule apply?
items.explanation: Per 8-5-1, it applies when vertical separation is used between aircraft crossing the offshore/oceanic airspace boundary below FL180.

choices.text: When vertical separation is applied between aircraft crossing the offshore/oceanic airspace boundary below FL180.
Whenever any aircraft crosses the offshore/oceanic boundary at any altitude.
Only when lateral separation is being applied below FL180.
Only above FL180 when QNE is used on both sides of the boundary.

items.question_text: Under 8-5-1, what must control action ensure during the transition below FL180?
items.explanation: Per 8-5-1, QNE/QNH differences must not compromise separation.

choices.text: That differences between the standard altimeter setting (QNE) and local altimeter setting (QNH) do not compromise separation.
That both aircraft continue using local altimeter settings until reaching FL180 in oceanic airspace.
That vertical separation is replaced with longitudinal separation before the boundary is crossed.
That all aircraft use the same assigned Mach number during the transition.

items.question_text: Under 8-5-1, when vertical separation is applied between aircraft crossing the offshore/oceanic airspace boundary below FL180, control action must ensure QNE/QNH differences do not compromise separation.
items.explanation: That is the rule stated in 8-5-1.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 8-5-2, what course divergence is required when aircraft are entering oceanic airspace?
items.explanation: Per 8-5-2a, the aircraft must be established on courses diverging by at least 15 degrees until oceanic lateral separation is established.

choices.text: At least 15 degrees until oceanic lateral separation is established.
At least 10 degrees until oceanic longitudinal separation is established.
At least 30 degrees until the aircraft leave radar coverage.
Any divergence is acceptable as long as radar separation exists at the boundary.

items.question_text: Under 8-5-2, what radar condition must exist at the edge of known radar coverage?
items.explanation: Per 8-5-2b, the aircraft must be horizontally radar separated and separation must be increasing at the edge of known radar coverage.

choices.text: The aircraft must be horizontally radar separated and the separation must be increasing.
The aircraft must be vertically radar separated and the separation must be constant.
The aircraft must be within 5 miles of each other so long as the courses diverge.
The aircraft must already have oceanic lateral separation before reaching the edge of coverage.

items.question_text: Under 8-5-2, separation will exist in oceanic airspace when the aircraft are horizontally radar separated with increasing separation at the edge of known radar coverage and are established on courses diverging by at least 15 degrees until oceanic lateral separation is established.
items.explanation: Those are the two conditions listed in 8-5-2.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 8-5-3, when may an outbound aircraft climb through opposite-direction oceanic traffic during the offshore-to-oceanic transition?
items.explanation: Per 8-5-3, vertical separation above the opposite-direction traffic must be established before the outbound crosses the boundary and 15 minutes before the aircraft are estimated to pass.

choices.text: When vertical separation above the opposite-direction traffic is established before the outbound crosses the offshore/oceanic boundary and 15 minutes before the aircraft are estimated to pass.
Whenever the outbound is radar identified before the boundary and can level off if needed.
Only after the aircraft have crossed the boundary and are within 10 minutes of passing.
Only after the opposite-direction traffic has reported the outbound in sight.

items.question_text: Under 8-5-3, how long before the aircraft are estimated to pass must the required vertical separation be established?
items.explanation: Per 8-5-3b, the vertical separation must be established 15 minutes before the aircraft are estimated to pass.

choices.text: 15 minutes before the aircraft are estimated to pass.
10 minutes before the aircraft are estimated to pass.
5 minutes before the aircraft are estimated to pass.
At the moment the outbound crosses the offshore/oceanic boundary.

items.question_text: Under 8-5-3, vertical separation above opposite-direction oceanic traffic must be established before the outbound aircraft crosses the offshore/oceanic boundary.
items.explanation: That requirement appears in 8-5-3a.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 8-5-4, what maximum altitude difference may exist between the following and preceding aircraft when the climb clearance is issued?
items.explanation: Per 8-5-4c, the following aircraft must be separated by not more than 4,000 feet from the preceding aircraft when the climb clearance is issued.

choices.text: Not more than 4,000 feet.
Not more than 2,000 feet.
Not more than 5,000 feet.
Any altitude difference is acceptable if 5 minutes exists.

items.question_text: Under 8-5-4, when must the following aircraft commence climb after passing the required point or radar-observed position?
items.explanation: Per 8-5-4d, the following aircraft must commence climb within 10 minutes after passing the required exact reporting point or radar-observed position.

choices.text: Within 10 minutes after passing the required point or radar-observed position.
Within 15 minutes after the preceding aircraft reports the point.
Any time before reaching oceanic airspace.
Only after vertical separation has already been reestablished.

items.question_text: Under 8-5-4, the following aircraft must remain in direct communication with ATC until vertical separation is established.
items.explanation: That requirement appears in 8-5-4e.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 8-5-5, what communications condition must exist before applying this radar-identification transition procedure?
items.explanation: Per 8-5-5a, direct radio communications must be maintained with one aircraft involved and there must be an ability to communicate with the other.

choices.text: Direct radio communications with one aircraft and the ability to communicate with the other.
Direct radio communications with both aircraft on the same frequency at all times.
Communications relayed only through another ATC facility.
No communications requirement exists if the transiting aircraft is RNAV equipped.

items.question_text: Under 8-5-5, what must be maintained between the radar-identified aircraft and any other observed targets until the transitioning aircraft is radar identified or nonradar separation is established?
items.explanation: Per 8-5-5e, radar separation standards must be maintained between the radar-identified aircraft and any other observed targets until the transitioning aircraft is radar identified or nonradar separation is established.

choices.text: Radar separation standards.
Only advisory traffic information.
Visual separation procedures.
No separation standard is required once the transiting aircraft is in known radar coverage.

items.question_text: Under 8-5-5, this procedure is not authorized if there is insufficient time to establish another approved separation method in the event of a delay or inability to establish radar identification of the transiting aircraft.
items.explanation: That exception is stated in 8-5-5h.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 8-6-1, what are the two approved methods for separating aircraft from a temporary stationary reservation?
items.explanation: Per 8-6-1, aircraft may be separated from a temporary stationary reservation laterally or vertically.

choices.text: Laterally or vertically.
Longitudinally or visually.
By pilot-applied see-and-avoid only.
By radar vectors only.

items.question_text: Under 8-6-1, when vertical separation is used, how is the geographical area defined?
items.explanation: Per 8-6-1b, the geographical area is the stationary reservation plus a buffer around the perimeter equivalent to one-half the lateral separation minimum.

choices.text: The stationary reservation plus a perimeter buffer equal to one-half the lateral separation minimum.
Only the exact reservation boundary with no additional buffer.
The reservation plus a buffer equal to the full lateral separation minimum on every side.
Any area the controller considers reasonable, provided the aircraft remains on course.

items.question_text: Under 8-6-1, lateral separation from a temporary stationary reservation may be achieved by clearing aircraft so that the protected airspace along the route of flight does not overlap the geographical area of the reservation.
items.explanation: That lateral method is stated in 8-6-1a.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 8-6-2, what must the controller tell a pilot who refuses an avoidance clearance for a reservation?
items.explanation: Per 8-6-2, the pilot must be informed of the potential hazard and advised that services will not be provided while the flight is within the reservation.

choices.text: Inform the pilot of the potential hazard and advise that services will not be provided while the flight is within the reservation.
Advise only that the pilot is operating at own risk, with no change to services.
Cancel the flight plan immediately without explanation.
Advise that services continue normally because the refusal transfers all responsibility to the pilot.

items.question_text: Under 8-6-2, what additional coordination should be made if possible after a pilot refuses an avoidance clearance?
items.explanation: Per 8-6-2, if possible, inform the appropriate using agency.

choices.text: Inform the appropriate using agency.
Request pilot approval to continue reservation activities.
Transfer the aircraft to advisory service only with no further notification.
Suspend all nearby reservations automatically.

items.question_text: Under 8-6-2, if a pilot refuses an avoidance clearance, services will not be provided while the flight is within the reservation.
items.explanation: That is explicitly required by 8-6-2.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 8-6-3, what are the approved methods for separating aircraft from a temporary moving airspace reservation?
items.explanation: Per 8-6-3, the approved methods are lateral, longitudinal, or vertical separation.

choices.text: Lateral, longitudinal, or vertical separation.
Vertical or visual separation only.
Lateral separation only, because the reservation is moving.
Longitudinal separation only behind the first aircraft in the reservation.

items.question_text: Under 8-6-3, how is longitudinal separation applied relative to aircraft operating within the moving reservation?
items.explanation: Per 8-6-3b, clear aircraft so that the appropriate longitudinal minimum exists ahead of the first or behind the last aircraft operating within the reservation.

choices.text: Ensure the appropriate longitudinal minimum exists ahead of the first or behind the last aircraft operating within the reservation.
Ensure equal spacing exists between all aircraft inside the reservation.
Apply the longitudinal minimum only behind the first aircraft in the reservation.
Apply the longitudinal minimum only after the reservation stops moving.

items.question_text: Under 8-6-3, when vertical separation is used, the protected geographical area is the moving reservation plus a perimeter buffer equal to one-half the lateral separation minimum.
items.explanation: That buffer rule is stated in 8-6-3c.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 8-7-1, what procedures and minima are used to provide air traffic control services in the North Atlantic ICAO Region?
items.explanation: Per 8-7-1, use the procedures and minima contained in Section 7 except when otherwise noted.

choices.text: The procedures and minima contained in Section 7, except when otherwise noted.
Only domestic radar procedures, unless the pilot requests oceanic handling.
Any combination of offshore or oceanic minima the controller prefers.
Only the procedures in Chapter 4, with no Section 7 exceptions.

items.question_text: Under 8-7-1, North Atlantic ICAO Region services are provided with the procedures and minima contained in this section except when noted otherwise.
items.explanation: That is the rule stated in 8-7-1.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 8-7-2, how is vertical separation provided in the North Atlantic ICAO Region?
items.explanation: Per 8-7-2, provide vertical separation in accordance with Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5, Altitude Assignment and Verification.

choices.text: In accordance with Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5, Altitude Assignment and Verification.
In accordance only with Section 7 longitudinal standards.
By applying local oceanic SOPs instead of Chapter 4 verification standards.
Only through pilot reports, without Chapter 4 verification requirements.

items.question_text: Under 8-7-2, vertical separation in the North Atlantic ICAO Region is provided in accordance with Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5, Altitude Assignment and Verification.
items.explanation: That cross-reference is stated in 8-7-2.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 8-7-3, what longitudinal minimum applies between nonturbojet aircraft operating in the West Atlantic Route System (WATRS)?
items.explanation: Per 8-7-3c1, apply 20 minutes between nonturbojet aircraft operating in the WATRS.

choices.text: 20 minutes.
15 minutes.
30 minutes.
10 minutes.

items.question_text: Under 8-7-3, what must the controller do when an ADS-C periodic or waypoint change event report is overdue by 3 minutes?
items.explanation: Per 8-7-3e3, the controller must take action to obtain an ADS-C report.

choices.text: Take action to obtain an ADS-C report.
Immediately apply a different form of separation with no attempt to obtain a report.
Wait until the report is 6 minutes overdue before taking any action.
Cancel the ADS-C contract automatically.

items.question_text: Under 8-7-3, if no ADS-C report is received within 6 minutes of the time the original report was due, the controller must take action to apply another form of separation.
items.explanation: That requirement is stated in 8-7-3e4.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 8-7-4, when may 23 NM lateral separation be applied?
items.explanation: Per 8-7-4a, 23 NM lateral separation requires approved aircraft, designated airspace, direct voice or CPDLC communications, and required ADS-C contracts maintained and monitored by an automated flight data processor.

choices.text: Between approved aircraft, at a minimum RNP 4, RCP 240, and RSP 180, in designated 23 NM airspace when direct voice or CPDLC communications exist and the required ADS-C contracts are maintained and monitored by an automated flight data processor.
Between any two aircraft in NAT HLA as long as they are on published tracks and voice communication exists.
Only when the aircraft are not approved for RNP 4 or RNP 10.
Whenever CPDLC is unavailable but the aircraft can estimate their own lateral spacing.

items.question_text: Under 8-7-4, what lateral minimum applies between aircraft not approved for RNP 4 or RNP 10 that operate on routes or in areas within WATRS, the San Juan CTA/FIR, or the Atlantic portion of the Miami Oceanic CTA/FIR?
items.explanation: Per 8-7-4d, apply 90 NM or 1 and 1/2 degrees latitude to those aircraft.

choices.text: 90 NM or 1 and 1/2 degrees latitude.
60 NM or 1 degree latitude.
50 NM.
23 NM.

items.question_text: Under 8-7-4, reduced lateral separation must not be used if the track-keeping capability of the aircraft has been reduced for any reason.
items.explanation: That limitation is stated in the notes to 8-7-4.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 8-8-1, what procedures and minima are used to provide air traffic control services in the Caribbean ICAO Region?
items.explanation: Per 8-8-1, use the procedures and minima contained in Section 8 except when otherwise noted.

choices.text: The procedures and minima contained in Section 8, except when otherwise noted.
Only domestic radar procedures unless the pilot requests oceanic handling.
Any mixture of offshore and domestic minima the controller prefers.
Only Chapter 4 procedures, with no Section 8 exceptions.

items.question_text: Under 8-8-1, Caribbean ICAO Region services are provided with the procedures and minima contained in this section except when noted otherwise.
items.explanation: That is the rule stated in 8-8-1.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 8-8-2, how is vertical separation provided in the Caribbean ICAO Region?
items.explanation: Per 8-8-2, provide vertical separation in accordance with Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5, Altitude Assignment and Verification.

choices.text: In accordance with Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5, Altitude Assignment and Verification.
In accordance only with Caribbean longitudinal standards.
By local offshore SOP alone, without Chapter 4 verification standards.
Only through pilot reports, without Chapter 4 verification requirements.

items.question_text: Under 8-8-2, vertical separation in the Caribbean ICAO Region is provided in accordance with Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5, Altitude Assignment and Verification.
items.explanation: That cross-reference is stated in 8-8-2.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 8-8-3, what longitudinal minimum applies between nonturbojet aircraft operating in the West Atlantic Route System (WATRS)?
items.explanation: Per 8-8-3d1, apply 20 minutes between nonturbojet aircraft operating in the WATRS.

choices.text: 20 minutes.
15 minutes.
30 minutes.
10 minutes.

items.question_text: Under 8-8-3, what minimum reported ITP distance is required between the ITP aircraft and any reference aircraft before an ADS-B In Trail Procedure climb or descent may be cleared?
items.explanation: Per 8-8-3e3, the reported ITP distance must be 15 NM or more.

choices.text: 15 NM or more.
10 NM or more.
12 NM or more.
5 NM or more if both aircraft are on the same track.

items.question_text: Under 8-8-3, when an ADS-C periodic or waypoint change event report is overdue by 3 minutes, the controller must take action to obtain an ADS-C report.
items.explanation: That requirement is stated in 8-8-3f3.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 8-8-4, when may 23 NM lateral separation be applied in the Caribbean ICAO Region?
items.explanation: Per 8-8-4a, 23 NM lateral separation requires approved aircraft, designated airspace, direct voice or CPDLC communications, and required ADS-C contracts maintained and monitored by an automated flight data processor.

choices.text: Between approved aircraft, at a minimum RNP 4, RCP 240, and RSP 180, in designated 23 NM airspace when direct voice or CPDLC communications exist and the required ADS-C contracts are maintained and monitored by an automated flight data processor.
Between any two aircraft in the Caribbean ICAO Region as long as they are on published routes and voice communication exists.
Only when the aircraft are not approved for RNP 4 or RNP 10.
Whenever CPDLC is unavailable but the aircraft can estimate their own lateral spacing.

items.question_text: Under 8-8-4, what lateral minimum applies between aircraft not approved for RNP 4 or RNP 10 that operate within WATRS or west of 55 degrees West between the U.S., Canada, or Bermuda and points in the Caribbean ICAO Region?
items.explanation: Per 8-8-4d, apply 90 NM between those aircraft.

choices.text: 90 NM.
60 NM.
50 NM.
23 NM.

items.question_text: Under 8-8-4, reduced lateral separation must not be used if the track-keeping capability of the aircraft has been reduced for any reason.
items.explanation: That limitation is stated in the notes to 8-8-4.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 8-8-5, when may an IFR flight in the Houston, Miami, or San Juan CTA be cleared to climb or descend in VFR conditions?
items.explanation: Per 8-8-5a, only when requested by the pilot and only between sunrise and sunset.

choices.text: Only when requested by the pilot and only between sunrise and sunset.
Any time the controller determines VMC exists, even without a pilot request.
Only at night, because other traffic is easier to identify by lights.
Whenever the aircraft agrees to cancel IFR after the climb or descent begins.

items.question_text: Under 8-8-5, what must the controller do if there is a possibility that VFR conditions may become impractical?
items.explanation: Per 8-8-5b1, issue alternative instructions.

choices.text: Issue alternative instructions.
Continue the VFR climb or descent without change until the pilot reports IMC.
Cancel IFR automatically.
Suspend all traffic advisories because the pilot accepted VFR conditions.

items.question_text: Under 8-8-5, if there is a possibility that VFR conditions may become impractical, the controller must issue alternative instructions.
items.explanation: That instruction is stated in 8-8-5b1.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 8-9-1, what procedures and minima are used to provide air traffic control services in the Pacific ICAO Region?
items.explanation: Per 8-9-1, use the procedures and minima contained in Section 9 except when otherwise noted.

choices.text: The procedures and minima contained in Section 9, except when otherwise noted.
Only domestic radar procedures unless the pilot requests Pacific handling.
Any mixture of offshore and domestic minima the controller prefers.
Only Chapter 4 procedures, with no Section 9 exceptions.

items.question_text: Under 8-9-1, Pacific ICAO Region services are provided with the procedures and minima contained in this section except when noted otherwise.

items.question_text: Under 8-9-2, how is vertical separation provided in the Pacific ICAO Region?
items.explanation: Per 8-9-2, provide vertical separation in accordance with Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5, Altitude Assignment and Verification.

choices.text: In accordance with Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5, Altitude Assignment and Verification.
In accordance only with Pacific longitudinal standards.
By local offshore SOP alone, without Chapter 4 verification standards.
Only through pilot reports, without Chapter 4 verification requirements.

items.question_text: Under 8-9-2, vertical separation in the Pacific ICAO Region is provided in accordance with Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5, Altitude Assignment and Verification.
items.explanation: That cross-reference is stated in 8-9-2.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 8-9-3, what time minimum may be applied between turbojet aircraft whether in level, climbing, or descending flight when they follow the same track or continuously diverging tracks until another form of separation is provided?
items.explanation: Per 8-9-3a2, apply 10 minutes in that case.

choices.text: 10 minutes.
15 minutes.
20 minutes.
30 minutes.

items.question_text: Under 8-9-3, what must the controller do if an aircraft fails to report its position within 3 minutes after the expected time when using distance minima without ADS-C?
items.explanation: Per 8-9-3d3, the controller must take action to establish communication.

choices.text: Take action to establish communication.
Immediately apply a different form of separation without trying to communicate.
Wait until the report is 8 minutes late before taking any action.
Cancel the aircraft's clearance automatically.

items.question_text: Under 8-9-3, if communication is not established within 8 minutes after the time a position report should have been received, the controller must take action to apply another form of separation.
items.explanation: That requirement is stated in 8-9-3d3.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 8-9-4, what lateral minimum applies within areas where Required Navigation Performance separation and procedures are authorized to RNP 4 or RNP 10 approved aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 8-9-4a, apply 50 NM.

choices.text: 50 NM.
23 NM.
60 NM.

items.question_text: Under 8-9-4, when may 23 NM lateral separation be applied in the Pacific ICAO Region?
items.explanation: Per 8-9-4b, 23 NM applies only to approved aircraft, at a minimum RNP 4, RCP 240, and RSP 180, in designated airspace with direct voice or CPDLC communications and required ADS-C contracts maintained and monitored by automation.

choices.text: Only to approved aircraft, at a minimum RNP 4, RCP 240, and RSP 180, in designated 23 NM airspace when direct voice or CPDLC communications exist and the required ADS-C contracts are maintained and monitored by an automated flight data processor.
To any aircraft in the Pacific ICAO Region as long as the controller has voice communication.
Only when the aircraft are not approved for RNP 4 or RNP 10.
Whenever the aircraft are outside designated airspace but both are on published routes.

items.question_text: Under 8-9-4, aircraft not covered by subparagraphs a and b are separated laterally by 100 NM.
items.explanation: That requirement is stated in 8-9-4c.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 8-10-1, what procedures and minima are used to provide air traffic control services in the North American ICAO Region?
items.explanation: Per 8-10-1, use the procedures and minima contained in Section 10.

choices.text: The procedures and minima contained in Section 10.
Only domestic radar procedures unless the pilot requests North American ICAO handling.
Any mixture of offshore and domestic minima the controller prefers.
Only Chapter 4 procedures, with no Section 10-specific guidance.

items.question_text: Under 8-10-1, North American ICAO Region services are provided with the procedures and minima contained in this section.
items.explanation: That is the rule stated in 8-10-1.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 8-10-2, vertical separation is provided in accordance with which sources?
items.explanation: Per 8-10-2, use Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5, Altitude Assignment and Verification, and facility directives depicting the transition between flight levels and metric altitudes.

choices.text: Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5, Altitude Assignment and Verification, and facility directives depicting the transition between flight levels and metric altitudes.
Only Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5, because facility directives do not apply in the region.
Only facility directives on metric altitudes, without Chapter 4 altitude-assignment requirements.
Only local controller preference, provided metric altitudes are coordinated.

items.question_text: Under 8-10-2, vertical separation in the North American ICAO Region is provided in accordance with Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5 and facility directives depicting the transition between flight levels and metric altitudes.
items.explanation: That is the rule stated in 8-10-2.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 8-10-3, what time minimum applies between turbojet aircraft in the North American ICAO Region when using minima based on time?
items.explanation: Per 8-10-3a1, apply 15 minutes between turbojet aircraft.

choices.text: 15 minutes.
10 minutes.
20 minutes.
30 minutes.

items.question_text: Under 8-10-3, what must the controller do when an ADS-C periodic or waypoint change event report is overdue by 3 minutes in the Anchorage Oceanic or Anchorage Continental CTA?
items.explanation: Per 8-10-3c3, the controller must take action to obtain an ADS-C report.

choices.text: Take action to obtain an ADS-C report.
Wait until the report is 6 minutes overdue before taking any action.
Immediately apply a different form of separation without first trying to obtain a report.
Cancel the ADS-C contract automatically.

items.question_text: Under 8-10-3, application of DME/RNAV separation without direct voice communications may not continue for more than 90 minutes.
items.explanation: That limitation is stated in 8-10-3d3.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 8-10-4, what lateral minimum applies within areas where Required Navigation Performance separation and procedures are authorized to RNP 4 or RNP 10 approved aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 8-10-4a, apply 50 NM.

choices.text: 50 NM.
23 NM.
90 NM.

items.question_text: Under 8-10-4, where may the 23 NM lateral minimum be applied when its communications and ADS-C monitoring prerequisites are met?
items.explanation: Per 8-10-4b and its note, the 23 NM minimum applies in the Anchorage Oceanic CTA and Anchorage Continental CTA and is not applicable in the Anchorage Arctic CTA.

choices.text: In the Anchorage Oceanic CTA and Anchorage Continental CTA, but not in the Anchorage Arctic CTA.
In any North American ICAO Region airspace once direct voice communications are established.
Only in the Anchorage Arctic CTA.
Only outside Anchorage airspace because the 23 NM minimum is barred from all Anchorage CTAs.

items.question_text: Under 8-10-4, aircraft not covered by subparagraphs a and b are separated laterally by 90 NM.
items.explanation: That requirement is stated in 8-10-4c.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 9-1-1, what assistance should be provided to aircraft engaged in the flight inspection of NAVAIDs?
items.explanation: Per 9-1-1, provide maximum assistance to aircraft engaged in the flight inspection of NAVAIDs.

choices.text: Provide maximum assistance.
Provide only the same level of assistance given to routine VFR aircraft.
Provide priority only if the aircraft is already on final approach.
Provide special handling only if the pilot requests emergency services.

items.question_text: Under 9-1-1, unless otherwise agreed to, what coordination is maintained with the pilot of a NAVAID flight-inspection aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 9-1-1, maintain direct contact and exchange information regarding known traffic in the area and the pilot's intentions.

choices.text: Maintain direct contact and exchange information regarding known traffic in the area and the pilot's intentions.
Use relay communication only and avoid discussing nearby traffic unless a conflict alert activates.
Maintain direct contact only during takeoff and landing, with no en route coordination required.
Let the pilot initiate all contact because flight-inspection aircraft are expected to operate independently.

items.question_text: Under 9-1-1, many flight inspections use automatic recording equipment and an uninterrupted flight is necessary for successful completion of the mission.
items.explanation: That note is stated in 9-1-1.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 9-1-2, how should a flight-inspection aircraft generally be cleared?
items.explanation: Per 9-1-2a, clear the aircraft according to pilot request as soon as practicable and do not ask the pilot to deviate from planned action except to preclude an emergency situation.

choices.text: According to pilot request as soon as practicable, without asking the pilot to deviate except to preclude an emergency situation.
According to normal traffic flow priorities, even if that requires routine deviations from the pilot's planned action.
According to pilot request only after all other IFR traffic has been sequenced first.
By requiring the pilot to accept vectors whenever workload would otherwise increase.

items.question_text: Under 9-1-2, what does the term 'recorded run' from a Flight Check aircraft indicate?
items.explanation: Per 9-1-2 and its note, 'recorded run' indicates automated flight inspections are in progress and the full procedure may need to be flown, including the missed approach as needed.

choices.text: Automated flight inspections are in progress, requiring the full procedure to be flown and runway or critical-area protection as needed.
The aircraft is conducting a routine practice approach with no additional handling implications.
The aircraft is available to accept vectors off the procedure whenever traffic flow requires it.
The aircraft is a non-FAA Flight Validation flight that receives no special handling.

items.question_text: Under 9-1-2, authorized non-FAA Service Providers using the call sign 'FLIGHT VAL' receive the same additional priority granted to FAA aircraft using the call sign 'Flight Check.'
items.explanation: Per the note to 9-1-2, FLIGHT VAL activities are similar, but no additional priority is granted with that call sign.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 9-1-3, what handling is provided to flight check aircraft inspecting NAVAIDs and RADAR?
items.explanation: Per 9-1-3a, provide special handling as required to expedite the flight inspection.

choices.text: Special handling as required to expedite the flight inspection.
Routine handling with no added priority once the aircraft is airborne.
Only emergency handling if the aircraft requests radar vectors.
Standard IFR handling unless the aircraft is on final approach to land.

items.question_text: Under 9-1-3, what should controllers avoid regarding the route or altitude of a flight check aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 9-1-3b, avoid changes in the route or altitude from that filed by the pilot in the initial flight plan.

choices.text: Avoid changes in the route or altitude from that filed by the pilot in the initial flight plan.
Avoid only altitude changes; route changes are expected during every inspection.
Avoid changes only after the aircraft begins the final approach segment of the inspection.
Avoid route or altitude changes only when the pilot declares an emergency.

items.question_text: Under 9-1-3, the previously assigned discrete beacon code of a special radar accuracy flight check aircraft should not be changed.
items.explanation: That requirement is stated in 9-1-3d.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 9-2-1, when does the special handling for military aircraft carrying dangerous materials apply?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-1a, it applies when the filed flight plan remarks contain 'dangerous cargo' or 'inert devices,' or when the pilot uses those words in radio communication.

choices.text: When the filed flight plan remarks contain 'dangerous cargo' or 'inert devices,' or when the pilot uses those words in radio communication.
Only when the pilot declares an emergency related to the cargo.
Only when the aircraft is landing at a joint-use military field.
Whenever the aircraft is military, regardless of flight-plan remarks or pilot communication.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-1, what must the controller advise if it becomes necessary to issue a clearance amending the route or altitude of an aircraft carrying dangerous materials?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-1b, advise the pilot of the proposed change and the amount of delay to expect if it is necessary to maintain the present route or altitude.

choices.text: The proposed change and the amount of delay to expect if it is necessary to maintain the present route or altitude.
Only the proposed change; expected delay is not discussed with the pilot.
Only the expected delay; the route or altitude amendment is issued without prior discussion.
Nothing, because dangerous-material flights must accept all ATC route and altitude amendments immediately.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-1, when an aircraft carrying dangerous materials is provided an en route descent, the controller should not vector the aircraft from the planned route unless the pilot concurs.
items.explanation: That requirement is stated in 9-2-1d.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 9-2-2, for which aircraft may flight plans specifying celestial navigation be approved?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-2a, approve celestial navigation only when requested for USAF or USN aircraft.

choices.text: Only for USAF or USN aircraft when requested.
For any military aircraft or civil aircraft when requested in the remarks section.
Only for USAF aircraft, not USN aircraft.
For any IFR aircraft equipped with RNAV and a transponder.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-2, what must be ensured prior to control transfer of an aircraft on a celestial navigation training leg?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-2c, ensure the receiving controller is informed of the nature of the celestial navigation training leg.

choices.text: Ensure the receiving controller is informed of the nature of the celestial navigation training leg.
Ensure only that the pilot has resumed conventional navigation before control transfer.
Ensure the receiving controller issues an immediate altitude change after transfer.
Ensure the training leg is terminated before transfer because celestial navigation cannot cross sectors.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-2, within conterminous U.S. airspace, celestial navigation training is limited to transponder-equipped aircraft within areas of ARTCC radar coverage.
items.explanation: That limitation is stated in 9-2-2b.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 9-2-3, when an experimental aircraft requires special handling, how should it generally be cleared?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-3a1, clear the aircraft according to pilot requests as traffic permits and if not contrary to ATC procedures.

choices.text: According to pilot requests as traffic permits and if not contrary to ATC procedures.
According to standard traffic flow priorities, even if the pilot requested special handling.
Only according to local letters of agreement, because pilot requests are not considered.
Only after the aircraft has departed the control zone and is established en route.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-3, once an experimental aircraft's planned action has been approved, when may the controller ask the pilot to deviate from it?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-3a2, do not ask the pilot to deviate from a planned action except to preclude an emergency situation.

choices.text: Only to preclude an emergency situation.
Whenever sequencing convenience would improve.
Whenever workload is high enough to justify a revised route.
Only after a letter of agreement has been executed with the operator.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-3, at locations where the volume or complexity of experimental aircraft operations warrants, a letter of agreement may be consummated between the facility and operator.
items.explanation: That option is stated in 9-2-3b.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 9-2-4, when may requests for special flight procedures from aircraft participating in FAA research and development test activities be approved?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-4, approve them when coordinated in advance and traffic permits.

choices.text: When coordinated in advance and traffic permits.
Only after the aircraft begins the test activity and requests deviations on frequency.
Only when no other IFR aircraft are operating in the area at all.
Whenever the pilot requests them, regardless of prior coordination or traffic conditions.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-4, who must approve special flight procedures for FAA research and development test activities prior to their use?
items.explanation: The note to 9-2-4 states the facility air traffic manager must approve them before use.

choices.text: The facility air traffic manager.
Any controller-in-charge on the shift when the flight launches.
Only the pilot in command conducting the research mission.
The receiving controller at the next facility after departure.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-4, special flight procedures for FAA research and development test activities must be approved by the facility air traffic manager prior to their use.
items.explanation: That requirement is stated in the note to 9-2-4.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 9-2-5, what must the first ATC facility establishing contact with an aircraft using the code word FLYNET provide?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-5a, provide expeditious handling.

choices.text: Expeditious handling.
Standard handling unless the pilot separately requests priority.
Emergency handling only if a hazardous-material declaration is made.
Priority handling only after the destination tower confirms acceptance.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-5, through what reporting path is a FLYNET aircraft notification passed after the first facility establishes contact?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-5b, report it to the OS/CIC for reporting to the NTSO ATSC through the DEN.

choices.text: To the OS/CIC, for reporting to the NTSO ATSC through the DEN.
Directly to the destination airport manager with no DEN notification required.
Only to the next ARTCC sector handling the aircraft.
Only to the military command post associated with the flight.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-5, once DEN notification has been accomplished, that fact is added to the remarks section of the flight plan.
items.explanation: That requirement is stated in 9-2-5c.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 9-2-6, what lateral airspace is protected along an IFR military training route?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-6d, the protected lateral airspace is the designated width of the route.

choices.text: The designated width of the route.
A fixed 5 NM corridor on either side of the route centerline.
Whatever width the pilot requests before entry.
Only the primary entry and exit fixes, not the full route width.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-6, what information must be requested from the pilot prior to entering an IFR military training route?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-6e, request the exit or alternate exit fix estimate, the requested altitude after exit, and if applicable the number of reentries on a Strategic Training Range.

choices.text: The exit or alternate exit fix estimate, the requested altitude after exit, and if applicable the number of reentries.
Only the requested altitude after exit, because the exit estimate is already implied by the route structure.
Only the route entry time and fuel remaining on board.
Only the number of aircraft in the formation and any requested speed changes.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-6, ATC must not amend or restrict operations on a MARSA-designated route in a manner that would compromise MARSA provisions.
items.explanation: That limitation is stated in 9-2-6h.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 9-2-7, what assistance is provided to interceptor aircraft on active air defense scramble missions?

choices.text: Maximum assistance to expedite their movement until the unknown aircraft is identified.
Routine handling only, because air defense scrambles operate independently of ATC assistance.
Priority only after the unknown aircraft has already been identified.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-7, what must the ATC facility do upon request regarding control jurisdiction of interceptors on an active air defense mission?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-7c, upon request the ATC facility must expedite transfer of the control jurisdiction of the interceptors to the requesting ADCF.

choices.text: Expedite transfer of control jurisdiction to the requesting ADCF.
Retain control until the interceptors identify the unknown aircraft, regardless of the ADCF request.
Transfer control only after the next sector accepts a routine handoff through normal procedures.
Terminate ATC services immediately instead of transferring control jurisdiction.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-7, ATC services must be used for active air defense missions insofar as the circumstances and situation permit.
items.explanation: That requirement is stated in 9-2-7b.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 9-2-8, what must be done with reports or information regarding unusual aircraft activities in the vicinity of special interest sites?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-8a, relay them immediately to supervisory or CIC personnel.

choices.text: Immediately relay them to supervisory or CIC personnel.
Hold them until a second controller confirms the activity.
Log them locally and forward them only at the end of the shift.
Broadcast them to all aircraft in the area before telling supervisory personnel.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-8, when do air traffic controllers have responsibility to monitor or observe aircraft in the vicinity of special interest sites?
items.explanation: Per the note to 9-2-8, controllers have no such responsibility unless directed by supervisory or CIC personnel.

choices.text: Only when directed by supervisory or CIC personnel.
At all times whenever aircraft are operating near dams, refineries, or power plants.
Only when the aircraft is VFR and below Class A airspace.
Only after a pilot reports suspicious activity on frequency.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-8, air traffic controllers have no responsibilities to monitor or observe aircraft in the vicinity of special interest sites unless directed by supervisory or CIC personnel.
items.explanation: That limitation is stated in the note to 9-2-8.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 9-2-9, where are the special air traffic rules for designated SATR areas and SFRAs prescribed?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-9, the special air traffic rules are set forth in 14 CFR part 93.

choices.text: 14 CFR part 93.
14 CFR part 91 only.
FAA Order JO 7210.3 only.
Local letters of agreement unique to each SFRA.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-9, how must a person operating an aircraft to, from, or within a SATR area or SFRA comply with the special rules?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-9b, the person must adhere to the applicable special air traffic rules unless otherwise authorized or required by ATC.

choices.text: Adhere to the applicable special air traffic rules unless otherwise authorized or required by ATC.
Follow them only if the area is specifically labeled SFRA and not if it is a SATR area.
Treat them as advisory unless the pilot has filed IFR.
Ignore them whenever ATC has not issued a separate reminder on frequency.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-9, Special Flight Rules Areas are areas of airspace where flight is subject to the special air traffic rules set forth in 14 CFR part 93 unless otherwise authorized by ATC.
items.explanation: That definition is stated in 9-2-9a.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 9-2-10, what are required for operations in the designated Washington, DC SFRA security services area?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-10, two-way radio communications, flight planning, and an operational transponder on an assigned code are required.

choices.text: Two-way radio communications, flight planning, and an operational transponder on an assigned code.
Only an operating transponder and VFR flight following.
Only radio contact and a filed destination airport.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-10, what instruction must be given to an aircraft flying too low for radar coverage in the SFRA?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-10a4, the aircraft must be instructed to report landing or exiting the SFRA.

choices.text: Instruct the aircraft to report landing or leaving the SFRA.
Instruct the aircraft to squawk VFR immediately and frequency change approved.
Tell the aircraft radar service is terminated and no further reports are required.
Direct the aircraft to remain outside the SFRA because low-radar-coverage aircraft may not enter under any circumstances.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-10, a pilot responding with '(aircraft call sign) standby' establishes two-way radio communications for entry into the security services area if all other security requirements have been satisfied.
items.explanation: That rule is stated in 9-2-10b1.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 9-2-11, what must the controller do upon receiving notification of a SECNOT?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-11, the controller must forward all information on the subject aircraft to the OS/CIC.

choices.text: Forward all information on the subject aircraft to the OS/CIC.
Wait for the subject aircraft to check in before telling the OS/CIC anything.
Advise only the next sector and let that sector notify the OS/CIC.
Forward only confirmed position information and keep all other details local until verified.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-11, what must be done if information on the subject aircraft is not known when a SECNOT is received?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-11, broadcast the call sign on all frequencies and advise the OS/CIC of the response.

choices.text: Broadcast the call sign on all frequencies and advise the OS/CIC of the response.
Broadcast the call sign only on the last known frequency and wait for a pilot response before advising the OS/CIC.
Do nothing further until the aircraft appears on radar.
Ask adjacent sectors to handle it, because all-frequencies broadcasts are not authorized.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-11, if information on the subject aircraft is not known, the controller must broadcast the call sign on all frequencies and advise the OS/CIC of the response.
items.explanation: That requirement is stated in 9-2-11.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 9-2-12, when a law enforcement pilot or flight crew requests it, how should communications be handled whenever possible?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-12a1, communicate with law enforcement aircraft on a separate and unique communications frequency whenever possible if requested by the pilot or flight crew.

choices.text: Use a separate and unique communications frequency whenever possible.
Keep the aircraft on the normal sector frequency because separate communications are not authorized for law enforcement missions.
Move the aircraft to Guard because all law enforcement coordination must occur on emergency frequencies.
Use a separate frequency only after the aircraft has landed and coordination is complete.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-12, what must ATC do to support aircraft conducting sensitive government missions?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-12b4, do not change the sensitive beacon codes requested or displayed, ensure the full call sign designator is entered into FAA automation systems to the maximum extent possible, and do not alter or abbreviate the U.S. special call signs used.

choices.text: Do not change the sensitive beacon code, preserve the full special call sign designator in automation to the maximum extent possible, and do not alter or abbreviate the U.S. special call sign.
Assign any convenient discrete code once radar contact is established, as long as the aircraft remains on its original route.
Abbreviate the special call sign after initial contact so other sectors can handle the flight more efficiently.
Suppress the call sign in automation and identify the aircraft only by track number for mission security.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-12, assistance to law enforcement aircraft must not compromise approved separation minima or place the aircraft in unsafe proximity to terrain, obstructions, or other aircraft.
items.explanation: That limitation is stated in 9-2-12a2.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 9-2-13, when does MARSA begin between the tanker and receiver aircraft?
items.explanation: Per note 3 to 9-2-13, MARSA begins when the tanker and receiver have entered the refueling airspace and the tanker advises ATC that it is accepting MARSA.

choices.text: When the tanker and receiver have entered the refueling airspace and the tanker advises ATC that it is accepting MARSA.
As soon as the receiver departs the ARIP, regardless of whether the tanker has accepted MARSA.
Only after the tanker reports the rendezvous complete and both aircraft are within 3 miles.
At the moment the controller issues the initial refueling clearance.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-13, what transponder request may be made to a receiver aircraft in the initial air refueling clearance?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-13c, you may request a receiver to squawk standby when it reaches a point 3 miles from the tanker.

choices.text: Request the receiver to squawk standby when it reaches a point 3 miles from the tanker.
Request the receiver to make a code change any time after leaving the ARIP, regardless of distance from the tanker.
Request the receiver to squawk standby only when it is less than 1 mile from the tanker.
Request the receiver to turn the transponder off entirely throughout the refueling mission.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-13, upon rendezvous completion the tanker must keep receiver aircraft within 3 miles of the tanker until MARSA is terminated.
items.explanation: That requirement is stated in note 3 under 9-2-13c.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 9-2-14, how far in advance are flight plans involving supersonic flight normally required?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-14a, they are required 16 hours in advance of the proposed departure time unless the originating ARTCC waives that requirement.

choices.text: Sixteen hours in advance, unless waived by the originating ARTCC.
Two hours in advance, with no waiver authority.
Twenty-four hours in advance, unless the military mission is classified.
Only before takeoff, because approval occurs after departure.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-14, what vertical separation minimum is used for operations above FL600 in lieu of the normal Chapter 4 value?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-14f1, use 5,000 feet vertical separation.

choices.text: 5,000 feet.
2,000 feet.
1,000 feet.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-14, another factor to consider is that supersonic aircraft during turns may lose a few thousand feet.
items.explanation: That consideration is stated in the note to 9-2-14f.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 9-2-15, which aircraft may be assigned the special use frequency at all altitudes or flight levels except where terminal operations require another frequency?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-15a2, U-2 and B-57 pressure suit flights may be assigned the special use frequency at all altitudes or flight levels except where terminal operations require another frequency.

choices.text: U-2 and B-57 pressure suit flights.
Any five-aircraft military formation at any time, regardless of aircraft type.
Only E-3A AWACS missions, and only below the high-altitude sector base.
Only tanker aircraft conducting aerial refueling near the ARCP.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-15, when may the special use frequency be assigned as backup for the high-altitude sector?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-15b, it may be assigned as backup when direct communications are essential because of a potential emergency control situation.

choices.text: When direct communications are essential because of a potential emergency control situation.
Whenever the sector is busy, so the controller can split routine traffic onto a second frequency.
Whenever any military aircraft asks for it, even if ordinary ARTCC communications are adequate.
Only when the aircraft is in terminal airspace and tower frequencies are congested.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-15, do not assign the special use frequency to the aircraft listed in subparagraph a1 when they will operate in airspace assigned for special military operations.
items.explanation: That restriction is stated in 9-2-15c.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 9-2-16, what must a controller do when a proposed flight path will traverse a reported or forecast hazardous-radiation area?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-16a, advise the pilot and reroute the aircraft when requested by the pilot.

choices.text: Advise the pilot and reroute the aircraft if the pilot requests it.
Reroute the aircraft automatically without pilot input and without discussing the hazardous-radiation area.
Wait until the aircraft is inside the area before issuing any notification or reroute.
Ignore forecast areas and advise only when the radiation hazard has already been measured in flight.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-16, what must a controller do when the pilot's intended landing airfield lies within a reported or forecast hazardous-radiation area?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-16b, inform the pilot and request the pilot to advise his or her intentions.

choices.text: Inform the pilot and request the pilot to advise his or her intentions.
Close the airport to the flight immediately and terminate ATC services.
Issue an automatic alternate airport assignment without consulting the pilot.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-16, if a pilot requests it, the aircraft is rerouted to avoid the hazardous-radiation area.
items.explanation: That instruction is stated in 9-2-16a.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 9-2-17, what handling is provided to government aircraft using the 'SAMP' call sign on aerial sampling or surveying missions?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-17, provide special handling to SAMP aircraft engaged in those contamination missions.

choices.text: Special handling.
Routine IFR handling only, with no special priority.
Only visual-flight-following services unless the aircraft declares an emergency.
Special handling only after the aircraft leaves contaminated airspace.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-17, what may be done with other IFR aircraft so that requests by SAMP aircraft are approved?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-17, other IFR aircraft may be recleared so SAMP requests can be approved.

choices.text: Other IFR aircraft may be recleared so the SAMP aircraft's requests are approved.
Other IFR aircraft must never be recleared because SAMP missions may not affect surrounding IFR routing.
Only VFR aircraft may be rerouted; IFR aircraft must remain on their original clearance.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-17, inflight clearance requests for altitude and route changes from SAMP aircraft are approved to the maximum extent possible.
items.explanation: That instruction is stated in 9-2-17.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 9-2-18, what en route altitude change is acceptable while expediting an AWACS/NORAD Special flight to its destination orbit?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-18a, an altitude change within plus or minus 2,000 feet of the requested flight level is acceptable.

choices.text: An altitude change within plus or minus 2,000 feet of the requested flight level.
Any altitude change necessary, even if it puts the aircraft 6,000 feet away from the requested flight level.
No altitude change at all, because the requested flight level may never be changed for these flights.
Only a descent of exactly 2,000 feet; climbs are not authorized.

items.question_text: Under 9-2-18, what route action is acceptable while expediting an AWACS/NORAD Special flight to the destination orbit?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-18b, radar vectors or minor route changes are acceptable if they do not impede progress toward the destination orbit.

choices.text: Radar vectors or minor route changes that do not impede progress toward the destination orbit.
Any major reroute needed to manage traffic, even if it delays arrival at the orbit.
No route deviation of any kind, because expedition requires the exact filed route.

items.question_text: Under 10-1-3, what level of help is provided to aircraft in distress?

items.question_text: Under 10-1-3, what outside resources may be enlisted to assist an aircraft in distress?

choices.text: Available radar facilities operated by the FAA, the military services, and the FCC, along with their emergency services and facilities.

choices.text: Only when you feel better handling of the emergency will result.

items.question_text: Under 10-1-4, when you receive information about an aircraft in distress, what must be forwarded and to whom?

items.question_text: Under 10-1-5, for which aircraft should efforts be coordinated?

items.question_text: Under 10-1-6, when an emergency occurs on the airport proper, what must be done with other air and ground traffic?

choices.text: Control it to avoid conflicts in the area where the emergency is being handled.
Allow it to continue normally unless the pilot of the emergency aircraft requests otherwise.

items.question_text: Under 10-1-6, when else does this traffic-control requirement apply even if the emergency is outside the airport proper?

choices.text: When routes within the airport proper are needed for local emergency equipment going to or from an outside emergency.

items.question_text: Under 10-1-7, what should generally be avoided during an inflight emergency involving a military fighter-type aircraft?

choices.text: A flameout pattern.
A standard holding pattern only.
A visual approach only if no emergency exists.

items.question_text: Under 10-2-1, when should assistance begin during an inflight emergency?

items.question_text: Under 10-2-1, what is the normal rule about requesting supplemental information from low-altitude military fighter-type aircraft?

choices.text: Normally do not request it, except ask for position if the aircraft is not sighted or on radar and the location was not given.

items.question_text: Under 10-2-2, even though 121.5 MHz and 243.0 MHz are emergency frequencies, what may be the best choice for an emergency aircraft already in contact?

items.question_text: Under 10-2-17, when may the EOVM/EAM be used?

items.question_text: Under 10-2-18, what should ATC do if a volcanic ash cloud is known or forecast to be present?

items.question_text: Under 10-3-1, when is an aircraft considered overdue for ALNOT purposes if neither communications nor radar contact can be established?
