items.question_text: Under 10-7-2, what lateral minimum may be used to reroute IFR or SVFR aircraft around a ground missile emergency when the emergency location can be accurately determined?
items.explanation: Per 10-7-2a, the lateral minimum is 1 mile between the emergency location and the aircraft under radar control or the airspace to be protected for the route being flown.

choices.text: 1 mile lateral.
500 feet lateral.
3 miles lateral in every case.
No fixed lateral minimum is provided.

items.question_text: Under 10-7-2, what vertical separation is used above the surface over the emergency location?
items.explanation: Per 10-7-2b, the vertical minimum is 6,000 feet above the surface over the emergency location.

choices.text: 6,000 feet above the surface over the emergency location.
3,000 feet above the surface over the emergency location.
10,000 feet MSL regardless of terrain.
Only whatever altitude the pilot considers safe.

items.question_text: Under 10-7-2, reroute IFR and SVFR aircraft by greater minima when such minima are suggested by the notifying official.
items.explanation: That allowance appears in the opening sentence of 10-7-2.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 10-7-3, what should all known VFR aircraft in the vicinity of a ground missile emergency be advised to do?
items.explanation: Per 10-7-3, advise them to avoid the emergency location by 1 mile laterally or 6,000 feet vertically, or by greater distance or altitude when suggested by the notifying official.

choices.text: Avoid the emergency location by 1 mile laterally or 6,000 feet vertically, or by greater limits when suggested by the notifying official.
Continue normal operations unless they are IFR on a flight plan.
Avoid the area only if they can remain 500 feet above the smoke or debris.
Follow only airport traffic-pattern restrictions because no missile-emergency minima apply to VFR aircraft.

items.question_text: Under 10-7-3, all known VFR aircraft in the vicinity of a ground missile emergency should be advised to avoid the location by 1 mile laterally or 6,000 feet vertically, or by greater limits when suggested by the notifying official.
items.explanation: That is the full requirement of 10-7-3.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 10-7-4, what additional hazard near a ground missile emergency must be specifically avoided by aircraft when observed?
items.explanation: Per 10-7-4, advise all aircraft to avoid any observed smoke columns in the vicinity of a ground missile emergency.

choices.text: Observed smoke columns in the vicinity of the emergency.
Only visible ground vehicles near the launch area.
Only airport lighting outages caused by the event.
Only the departure corridor, regardless of visible smoke.

items.question_text: Under 10-7-4, all aircraft should be advised to avoid any observed smoke columns in the vicinity of a ground missile emergency.
items.explanation: That is the direct instruction in 10-7-4.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 10-7-5, what additional notification action may be taken when reports indicate a ground missile emergency will exist for an extended period of time?
items.explanation: Per 10-7-5, a Notice to Airmen may be issued when the emergency is expected to exist for an extended period.

choices.text: A Notice to Airmen may be issued.
All flight plans in the area must be canceled automatically.
Only voice advisories may be used and published notice is prohibited.
A nationwide ground stop must be issued in every case.

items.question_text: Under 10-7-5, when reports indicate that a ground missile emergency will exist for an extended period of time, a Notice to Airmen may be issued.
items.explanation: That is the full instruction in 10-7-5.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 11-1-1, what is the mission of the traffic management system?
items.explanation: Per 11-1-1a, the mission is to balance air traffic demand with system capacity to ensure maximum efficient utilization of the NAS.

choices.text: To balance air traffic demand with system capacity to ensure maximum efficient utilization of the NAS.
To reduce demand whenever traffic increases, regardless of available capacity.
To prioritize miles-in-trail restrictions over all other flow-management tools.
To shift all capacity decisions from ATCS personnel to automation systems.

items.question_text: Under 11-1-1, which should be used to the maximum extent feasible in preference to miles-in-trail initiatives?
items.explanation: Per 11-1-1b, TBFM must be used to the maximum extent feasible in preference to MIT initiatives.

choices.text: TBFM.
MIT initiatives.
Ground stop procedures.
Departure holds only.

items.question_text: Under 11-1-1, the ATCS is recognized as integral in the execution of the traffic management mission.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 11-1-1c.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 11-1-2, how often must the STMCIC ensure an operational briefing is conducted during the day and evening shifts?
items.explanation: Per 11-1-2a1, the STMCIC ensures an operational briefing is conducted at least once during the day and evening shifts.

choices.text: At least once during the day and evening shifts.
Only when weather is below minimums or staffing is critical.
Once per week unless a facility directive requires more.
Only when the TMU requests one.

items.question_text: Under 11-1-2, when departure scheduling or Call for Release is in effect, what airborne release window applies unless otherwise coordinated?
items.explanation: Per 11-1-2f3, aircraft should be airborne within a window from 2 minutes prior to 1 minute after the assigned time unless otherwise coordinated.

choices.text: From 2 minutes prior to 1 minute after the assigned time.
From 5 minutes prior to 5 minutes after the assigned time.
Exactly at the assigned time with no tolerance.
From 1 minute prior to 2 minutes after the assigned time.

items.question_text: Under 11-1-2, personnel providing air traffic services must enforce TBM operations and TMIs within their area of responsibility, but those actions do not have priority over maintaining separation of aircraft and procedural integrity of the sector.
items.explanation: That limitation appears in 11-1-2c1.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 11-1-3, during periods of metering, where must TBFM schedule information be displayed?
items.explanation: Per 11-1-3a, personnel providing air traffic services must display TBFM schedule information on the situation display.

choices.text: On the situation display.
Only on the TMU planning display.
Only on paper strips or printed schedules.
Only at the supervisor's position.

items.question_text: Under 11-1-3, what normal metering-time accuracy standard applies during periods of metering?
items.explanation: Per 11-1-3b, personnel providing air traffic services comply with TBFM-generated metering times within plus or minus 1 minute.

choices.text: Within plus or minus 1 minute.
Within plus or minus 3 minutes.
Exactly on time with no tolerance.
Within plus or minus 5 minutes unless TMU specifies otherwise.

items.question_text: Under 11-1-3, when compliance with TBFM-generated metering times is not possible, personnel should coordinate with OS/CIC, traffic management services, and adjacent facilities or sectors as appropriate.
items.explanation: That coordination requirement appears in 11-1-3c.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 12-1-1, when the FAA has been formally delegated control responsibility within Canadian airspace, what general procedures are applied?
items.explanation: Per 12-1-1, apply basic FAA procedures except for the Canadian procedures contained in Chapter 12.

choices.text: Basic FAA procedures, except for the Canadian procedures contained in Chapter 12.
Only Canadian procedures, with no FAA procedures used in delegated airspace.
Only FAA procedures, with all Chapter 12 procedures disregarded.
Only procedures specifically approved by the pilot in command.

items.question_text: Under the note in 12-1-1, Chapter 12 was revised to include only those Canadian procedures that must be used because of a Canadian regulatory or unique operational requirement.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in the note to 12-1-1.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 12-1-2, which flights are permitted in Canadian Class B airspace?
items.explanation: Per 12-1-2b, only IFR and Controlled VFR flights are permitted in Class B airspace.

choices.text: Only IFR and Controlled VFR (CVFR) flights.
Any IFR or ordinary VFR flights without restriction.
Only IFR flights.
Only uncontrolled VFR flights below FL180.

items.question_text: Under 12-1-2, what is required for a VFR aircraft to enter Canadian Class C airspace?
items.explanation: Per 12-1-2c, VFR flights require a clearance from ATC to enter Class C airspace.

choices.text: A clearance from ATC.
Only two-way communications before entry.
No special requirement beyond see-and-avoid.
A CVFR flight plan instead of any ATC clearance.

items.question_text: Under 12-1-2, Class D airspace permits VFR entry without a clearance, but the aircraft must establish two-way communications with the appropriate ATC agency before entering.
items.explanation: That is the Class D rule in 12-1-2d.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 12-1-3, when may an aircraft be cleared to maintain 'at least 1,000 feet-on-top' in lieu of 'VFR-on-top'?
items.explanation: Per 12-1-3, the pilot must request it and the pilot must not operate within Class A or Class B airspace.

choices.text: When the pilot requests it and will not operate within Class A or Class B airspace.
Whenever the pilot requests it, including in Class A or Class B airspace.
Only when ATC initiates it without a pilot request.
Only when the pilot agrees to remain exactly 1,000 feet above the highest cloud layer.

items.question_text: Under the note in 12-1-3, whose responsibility is it to ensure the operation can be conducted at least 1,000 feet above all cloud, haze, smoke, or other formation with 3 miles or more flight visibility?
items.explanation: The note says that responsibility rests with the pilot.

choices.text: The pilot's responsibility.
ATC's responsibility.
The dispatcher's responsibility.
The airport weather office's responsibility.

items.question_text: Under 12-1-3, a pilot request for at least 1,000 feet-on-top may be considered confirmation that conditions are adequate.
items.explanation: That statement appears in the note to 12-1-3.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 12-1-4, what type of separation minimum must be applied between aircraft operating in accordance with an IFR or CVFR clearance?
items.explanation: Per 12-1-4, apply a lateral, longitudinal, or vertical separation minimum between such aircraft.

choices.text: A lateral, longitudinal, or vertical separation minimum.
Only visual separation if both crews report clear weather.
No separation if one aircraft is CVFR rather than IFR.
Only wake turbulence separation and no other standard minimum.

items.question_text: Under 12-1-4, does the weather affect the requirement to apply separation between aircraft operating in accordance with an IFR or CVFR clearance?
items.explanation: Per 12-1-4, the separation requirement applies regardless of the weather conditions.

choices.text: No. The requirement applies regardless of the weather conditions.
Yes. It applies only when weather is below VMC.
Yes. It applies only in IMC when both aircraft are IFR.
Yes. It does not apply to CVFR aircraft in good weather.

items.question_text: Under 12-1-4, apply separation between aircraft operating in accordance with an IFR or CVFR clearance regardless of the weather conditions.
items.explanation: That is the full instruction in 12-1-4.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 12-1-5, for a turbine-powered aircraft in Canadian airspace cleared on departure to a point other than the destination, how long is the last assigned altitude/flight level or MEA, whichever is higher, maintained before proceeding at filed altitude(s)?
items.explanation: Per 12-1-5, the aircraft maintains that altitude until 10 minutes beyond the clearance limit before proceeding at filed altitude(s) or flight level(s).

choices.text: Until 10 minutes beyond the clearance limit.
Until exactly crossing the clearance limit, with no additional time beyond it.
Until reaching the destination airport.
Only until 5 minutes beyond the clearance limit.

items.question_text: Under 12-1-5, when a turbine-powered aircraft will enter U.S. airspace within 10 minutes after passing the clearance limit, when is the climb to the flight-planned border-crossing altitude commenced?
items.explanation: Per 12-1-5, the climb begins at the estimated time of crossing the Canada/U.S. boundary.

choices.text: At the estimated time of crossing the Canada/U.S. boundary.
Immediately after passing the clearance limit.
Ten minutes before reaching the boundary.
Only after entering U.S. airspace and establishing communications there.

items.question_text: Under 12-1-5, unless alternate radio-failure instructions were issued by ATC, a turbine-powered aircraft cleared on departure to a point other than the destination proceeds to the destination airport in accordance with the flight plan.
items.explanation: That is the opening Canadian-difference instruction in 12-1-5.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 12-1-6, what must exist before parachute jumping may be authorized in Canadian airspace?
items.explanation: Per 12-1-6, prior permission from the appropriate Canadian authority is required.

choices.text: Prior permission from the appropriate Canadian authority.
Only verbal coordination with the nearest airport controller.
Only pilot acknowledgment of risk.
Only a NOTAM, without any Canadian authority approval.

items.question_text: Under the note in 12-1-6, Canadian regulations require written authority from the Ministry of Transport.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in the note to 12-1-6.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 12-1-7, when there is an airport controller on duty in a control zone, on what basis is a pilot's request for SVFR approved or refused?
items.explanation: Per 12-1-7a, the decision is based on current or anticipated IFR traffic only.

choices.text: On the basis of current or anticipated IFR traffic only.
On the basis of ceiling and visibility only.
On whether the pilot is IFR qualified and the aircraft is IFR equipped.
On the pilot's willingness to remain clear of all IFR traffic without ATC restriction.

items.question_text: Under 12-1-7, what are the Canadian SVFR weather minima for helicopters?
items.explanation: Per 12-1-7c2, helicopter SVFR weather minimums are flight visibility, or ground visibility when available, of 1/2 mile.

choices.text: 1/2 mile flight visibility, or ground visibility when available.
1 mile flight visibility in all cases.
3 miles flight visibility at night and 1 mile by day.
No stated minimum if the helicopter remains clear of cloud.

items.question_text: Under 12-1-7, pilots do not have to be IFR qualified to fly SVFR at night, nor does the aircraft have to be equipped for IFR flight.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in the opening note to 12-1-7.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 13-1-1, for EDST use, what is the current plan?
items.explanation: Per the note in 13-1-1, the current plan is what EAS predicts an aircraft will fly, and it may include clearances not yet issued.

choices.text: What EAS predicts an aircraft will fly, which may include clearances not yet issued to the aircraft.
Only the last clearance actually acknowledged by the pilot, with no unissued changes included.
The route and altitude in the original flight plan, regardless of later amendments or predictions.
The traffic management initiative currently in effect for the sector.

items.question_text: Under 13-1-1, which data set does EDST use to derive expected aircraft trajectories?
items.explanation: Per 13-1-1, EDST uses current plan data, forecast winds, aircraft performance characteristics, and track data to derive expected aircraft trajectories.

choices.text: Current plan data, forecast winds, aircraft performance characteristics, and track data.
Only filed route and altitude information from the original flight plan.
Only current radar position plus pilot weather reports.
Tower departure messages and runway assignment data only.

items.question_text: Under 13-1-1, EDST's current plan may include clearances that have not yet been issued to the aircraft.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in the note to 13-1-1.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 13-1-2, when a conflict probe alert is displayed, when should the controller evaluate the alert and take appropriate action?
items.explanation: Per 13-1-2b, the alert should be evaluated and acted on as early as practical in accordance with duty priorities.

choices.text: As early as practical, in accordance with duty priorities.
Only after minimum radar separation has already been lost.
Only after another controller confirms the alert by voice coordination.
Only when the aircraft are within final handoff range.

items.question_text: Under 13-1-2, what must be done before transfer of control when the Stop Probe feature has been activated for an aircraft, unless otherwise coordinated?
items.explanation: Per 13-1-2e, conflict probe for that aircraft must be restarted before transfer of control unless otherwise coordinated.

choices.text: Restart conflict probe for that aircraft before transfer of control.
Leave Stop Probe active and let the receiving controller decide whether to restart it after the transfer.
Suppress all alerts for the aircraft until it exits the receiving sector.
Convert the aircraft to an untracked flight data block before the handoff.

items.question_text: Under 13-1-2, conflict probe alerts account for all cases in which greater separation is needed and all cases in which reduced separation is permitted.
items.explanation: The note to 13-1-2 says conflict probe alerts are based on approved radar separation and do not account for all cases where greater or reduced separation rules may apply.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 13-1-3, when EDST is operational at the sector and sector priorities permit, what should the trial plan capability be used to evaluate?
items.explanation: Per 13-1-3, trial planning is used to evaluate solutions to predicted conflicts, user requests, removal of a flight direction constraint, and removal of a static restriction.

choices.text: Predicted-conflict solutions, user requests, removal of a flight direction constraint, and removal of a static restriction.
Only active loss-of-separation events after radar minimums have already been broken.
Only weather deviations requested by the pilot in the current sector.
Only amendments already accepted by adjacent sectors and pilots.

items.question_text: Under 13-1-3, which restriction may be evaluated for removal with the trial plan capability when EDST is operational and priorities permit?
items.explanation: Per 13-1-3c and d, trial planning may evaluate removal of a flight direction constraint and removal of a static restriction.

choices.text: A flight direction constraint or a static restriction.
Only the aircraft's call sign suffix and equipment code.
Only a temporary tower-applied traffic pattern restriction.
No restrictions may be evaluated for removal with trial planning.

items.question_text: Under 13-1-3, when EDST is operational at the sector and sector priorities permit, the trial plan capability is used to evaluate solutions to predicted conflicts.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 13-1-3a.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 13-1-4, when the results of a trial plan based on a user request indicate the absence of alerts, what should be done?
items.explanation: Per 13-1-4, every effort should be made to grant the user request unless the change is likely to adversely affect operations at another sector.

choices.text: Make every effort to grant the user request unless the change is likely to adversely affect operations at another sector.
Deny the user request because alert-free trial plans are not a basis for operational approval.
Grant the user request only if the pilot explicitly waives any concern about adjacent sectors.
Ignore the trial plan and keep the existing clearance unless a supervisor specifically orders otherwise.

items.question_text: Under 13-1-4, what condition can justify not granting a user request even when the trial plan indicates the absence of alerts?
items.explanation: Per 13-1-4, the exception is when the change is likely to adversely affect operations at another sector.

choices.text: The change is likely to adversely affect operations at another sector.
The user requested a route change instead of an altitude change.
The trial plan was performed using EDST rather than manual calculations.
The request would require the pilot to acknowledge a new clearance.

items.question_text: Under 13-1-4, if a user-request trial plan shows no alerts, the request must always be granted even if it is likely to adversely affect operations at another sector.
items.explanation: That statement is false because 13-1-4 allows the request to be withheld when the change is likely to adversely affect another sector's operations.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 13-1-5, what must be used as the sector team's primary source of flight data?
items.explanation: Per 13-1-5a, the ACL is the sector team's primary source of flight data.

choices.text: The ACL.
The DL only.
A locally maintained handwritten traffic list.
The SPA.

items.question_text: Under 13-1-5, what must be reviewed when an ACL or DL entry has a Remarks indication?
items.explanation: Per 13-1-5c, the Remarks field of the flight plan must be reviewed, and changes to the Remarks field must also be reviewed.

choices.text: The Remarks field of the flight plan and any changes to that field.
Only the original filed route.
Only the next sector's frequency assignment.
The remarks field only if the aircraft is nonradar.

items.question_text: Under 13-1-5, highlighting an ACL or DL entry is used to indicate that the flight requires action or special attention.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 13-1-5d.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 13-1-6, when automated coordination with a facility is not available, what should be used to annotate manual coordination status?
items.explanation: Per 13-1-6a, use the Coordination Menu or a flight progress strip in accordance with facility directives.

choices.text: The Coordination Menu or a flight progress strip, in accordance with facility directives.
Only the ACL highlighting function.
Only the Free Text Area on the DL.
No annotation is required when coordination is manual.

items.question_text: Under 13-1-6, when the Coordination Menu has been used and the flight plan is subsequently changed, when is the yellow coding removed from the Coordination Indicator?
items.explanation: Per 13-1-6b, remove the yellow coding after any appropriate action has been taken.

choices.text: After any appropriate action has been taken.
Immediately when the change is first entered, before any follow-up action.
Only after the aircraft has exited the sector.
It is never removed once manual coordination has been displayed.

items.question_text: Under 13-1-6, where automated coordination is not available, manual coordination status may be annotated in accordance with facility directives.
items.explanation: That statement reflects 13-1-6a.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 13-1-7, which methods may be used to annotate holding instructions?
items.explanation: Per 13-1-7, the authorized methods are Hold View, Hold Data Menu, hold message, a flight progress strip, or a facility-approved worksheet.

choices.text: Hold View, Hold Data Menu, a hold message, a flight progress strip, or a facility-approved worksheet.
Only a handwritten note on any sheet of paper at the position.
Only a voice reminder between controllers.
Only the ACL highlighting function.

items.question_text: Under 13-1-7, holding instructions should be annotated in accordance with facility directives.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 13-1-7.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 13-1-8, what must be done with control information not otherwise recorded by automation recordings or voice recordings?
items.explanation: Per 13-1-8a, it must be manually recorded using approved methods.

choices.text: It must be manually recorded using approved methods.
It may be left unrecorded if the controller remembers it.
It must be entered only in plain language and never by abbreviation.
It should be delayed until after the handoff is complete.

items.question_text: Under 13-1-8, when the ACL or DL Free Text Area is used to enter control information, what must be done with that area and the information in it?
items.explanation: Per 13-1-8d, the Free Text Area must remain open and visible, and information entered there must be updated or deleted when no longer relevant.

choices.text: The Free Text Area must remain open and visible, and the information must be updated or deleted when no longer relevant.
The Free Text Area should be closed once the entry is saved, and the information should remain until the aircraft lands.
The area may be hidden at any time, because visibility is not required if the strip is posted.
The information should remain permanently because deleting old Free Text removes the coordination record.

items.question_text: Under 13-1-8, information entered in the Free Text Area passes on handoff automatically and does not need additional coordination.
items.explanation: The note to 13-1-8 states that information entered into the Free Text Area does not pass on handoff and must be coordinated if necessary.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 13-1-9, when may IAFDOF, UTM, or EDCT coding be acknowledged?
items.explanation: Per 13-1-9e, IAFDOF, UTM, or EDCT coding is acknowledged only after the appropriate action has been completed.

choices.text: Only after the appropriate action has been completed.
As soon as the controller recognizes what action is needed, even if it has not been completed yet.
Immediately when the notification first appears on the display.
Only after the aircraft leaves the sector.

items.question_text: Under 13-1-9, what must be true before a sector sends or acknowledges ERT coding when the first ERT indication appears?
items.explanation: Per 13-1-9f, the first sector displaying ERT must issue and send or acknowledge the route before handoff unless otherwise coordinated or directed, and the sector must have track control or otherwise required coordination before sending or acknowledging the coding.

choices.text: The sector must have track control for the flight or the action must otherwise be coordinated as required, and the first sector displaying ERT handles it before handoff unless otherwise directed.
Any sector that sees ERT may acknowledge it immediately, because display visibility alone is enough authority to act.
ERT may be sent or acknowledged only after the next sector accepts the handoff.
ERT may never be handled by the first sector displaying it.

items.question_text: Under 13-1-9, the IAFDOF feature must be used in the automatic mode unless otherwise authorized in a facility directive.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 13-1-9a.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 13-1-13, what is the authorized use of the Graphic Plan Display?
items.explanation: Per 13-1-13a, graphic depictions of flight trajectories may be used only to aid situational awareness and strategic planning.

choices.text: To aid situational awareness and strategic planning.
To replace radar track position for normal separation work.
To provide primary altitude confirmation when Mode C is unavailable.
To conduct point outs and transfer radar identification when workload is high.

items.question_text: Under 13-1-13, what may not be used in lieu of Mode C for altitude confirmation?
items.explanation: Per 13-1-13c, trajectory-based altitude may not be used in lieu of Mode C for altitude confirmation.

choices.text: Trajectory-based altitude.
An approved altimeter readout from the aircraft data block.
A pilot-reported altitude obtained over the frequency.
The assigned altitude in the flight plan.

items.question_text: Under 13-1-13, the GPD may not be used for radar identification, transfer of radar identification, radar separation, correlation, or point outs.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 13-1-13d.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 13-2-1, where is the ATOP ATC System utilized?
items.explanation: Per 13-2-1a, ATOP is utilized in designated en route and oceanic airspace.

choices.text: In designated en route and oceanic airspace.
Only in terminal radar approach control airspace.
Only in nonradar domestic airspace.
Only in military special-use airspace.

items.question_text: Under 13-2-1, which is listed as an ATOP capability?
items.explanation: Per 13-2-1b, ATOP capabilities include items such as conflict prediction and reporting, ADS-B, ADS-C, CPDLC, AIDC, weather deviation tools, and electronic flight data including electronic flight strips.

choices.text: Conflict Prediction and Reporting.
Local airport surface movement radar sequencing only.
Tower light-gun control.
Paper-strip-only oceanic coordination.

items.question_text: Under 13-2-1, ATOP includes both surveillance and flight data processing.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 13-2-1a.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 13-2-2, unless otherwise specified in facility directives, what must the controller do when a red conflict is displayed?
items.explanation: Per 13-2-2a3(c), the controller must take immediate action to resolve any red conflicts unless facility directives specify otherwise.

choices.text: Take immediate action to resolve the conflict.
Wait until the conflict turns amber before acting.
Ignore it if additional decision-support tools look favorable.
Transfer responsibility first and let the receiving sector resolve it.

items.question_text: Under 13-2-2, what must be done before manually accepting an aircraft transfer from an external facility?
items.explanation: Per 13-2-2a5, before manually accepting a transfer, ensure the coordinated flight profile is accurately entered, conflict probe initiated, and action taken if necessary to resolve any potential conflicts.

choices.text: Ensure the coordinated flight profile is accurately entered, conflict probe is initiated, and any necessary conflict action is taken.
Accept the transfer first, then update the profile after the aircraft checks on frequency.
Rely on trial probe only and defer actual conflict probe until after the transfer is completed.
Use only additional decision-support tools and skip probe initiation if the profile appears reasonable.

items.question_text: Under 13-2-2, additional decision-support tools may be used to override supported conflict probe results whenever the controller believes the tools are more accurate.
items.explanation: Per 13-2-2b2, additional decision-support tools must not be used to override probe results when the applicable separation standard is supported by probe and no authorized override condition applies.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 13-2-3, what is the sector team's responsibility for manually entered data and nonconformant messages?
items.explanation: Per 13-2-3a, manually entered data must be accurate and timely, nonconformant messages must be handled in a timely manner, and the flight profile must be updated as necessary.

choices.text: Ensure manually entered data is accurate and timely, handle nonconformant messages promptly, and update the flight profile as necessary.
Delay updates until the end of the sector push unless the aircraft calls to question the data.
Rely on conflict probe to correct stale manually entered data automatically.
Update only the data block, because the flight profile does not need revision when manually entered data changes.

items.question_text: Under 13-2-3, when should the Degraded RNP indicator be selected on the electronic flight strip?
items.explanation: Per 13-2-3c1(c), select Degraded RNP when an aircraft notifies ATC of a reduction in navigation capability that affects the applicable separation minima.

choices.text: When the aircraft has notified ATC of a reduction in navigation capability that affects the applicable separation minima.
Whenever an aircraft requests a weather deviation.
Only when the aircraft is outside ADS-C coverage.
Only after the next sector has already accepted the handoff.

items.question_text: Under 13-2-3, the ASD title bar may be obscured by other windows as long as the aircraft symbols remain visible.
items.explanation: Per 13-2-3e1, ensure that the situation display window title bar is not obscured by other windows or lists.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 13-2-4, when CPDLC is available and a CPDLC-connected aircraft is operating outside VHF coverage, what must be used as the primary means of communication?
items.explanation: Per 13-2-4a1, CPDLC must be used as the primary means of communication in that situation.

choices.text: CPDLC.
HF voice only.
Third-party relay only.
Any available voice frequency must always be primary.

items.question_text: Under 13-2-4, if the automated transfer fails and the controller is unable to complete the data link transfer manually, what should the controller advise the pilot to do?
items.explanation: Per 13-2-4c1, advise the pilot to log on to the next facility and send an End Service message.

choices.text: Log on to the next facility and send an End Service (EOS) message.
Remain on the current facility indefinitely until the data link reconnects automatically.
Stop using CPDLC for the rest of the flight and ignore the next facility logon.
Send only a free-text message to the current facility with no EOS action.

items.question_text: Under 13-2-4, when there is uncertainty that a clearance was delivered via CPDLC, the controller must continue to protect the associated airspace until an appropriate operational response is received.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 13-2-4c5.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 13-2-5, what does the weather deviation tool add to the required separation calculated by ATOP when RNP distance-based longitudinal separation is being applied?
items.explanation: Per 13-2-5, it adds an additional converging, diverging, and intersecting mileage buffer.

choices.text: An additional converging, diverging, and intersecting mileage buffer.
A reduced longitudinal minimum with no added buffer.
A lateral separation minimum based on track miles only.
A discretionary weather note with no effect on spacing calculations.

items.question_text: Under 13-2-5, what does a pink advisory in the deviation monitor window with a zero or positive value in the +/- column indicate?
items.explanation: Per 13-2-5b, it indicates the aircraft pair is either at or close to the required spacing.

choices.text: The aircraft pair is either at or close to the required spacing.
The aircraft pair is safely beyond all required spacing margins.
The weather deviation has already been approved by ATOP.
Conflict probe is unavailable for the aircraft pair.

items.question_text: Under 13-2-5, pink advisories in the deviation monitor window require further evaluation before approving a weather deviation clearance.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 13-2-5a.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 13-2-6, between ATOP sectors, what does automated coordination normally constitute?
items.explanation: Per 13-2-6, automated coordination normally constitutes complete coordination between ATOP sectors, internally and across adjacent ATOP facilities, subject to the listed exceptions.

choices.text: Complete coordination between ATOP sectors, subject to the listed exceptions.
Only preliminary notice that always requires full supplemental verbal coordination.
Coordination only within a single facility, never across adjacent ATOP facilities.
An advisory display with no coordination value.

items.question_text: Under 13-2-6, which is one of the exceptions to automated coordination constituting complete coordination?
items.explanation: Per 13-2-6a and b, exceptions include when the aircraft is in conflict with another in the receiving sector, or when otherwise specified in facility directives or LOA.

choices.text: The aircraft is in conflict with another in the receiving sector.
The receiving sector is also using ATOP.
The aircraft has already checked in on CPDLC.
The transfer is between sectors within the same facility.

items.question_text: Under 13-2-6, automated coordination always constitutes complete coordination with no exceptions.
items.explanation: That statement is false because 13-2-6 lists exceptions involving conflicts in the receiving sector and cases specified by directives or LOA.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 13-2-7, when operating in a multiple controller operation at a workstation, how must ATC tasks be completed?
items.explanation: Per 13-2-7a, ensure all ATC tasks are completed according to their priority of duties.

choices.text: According to their priority of duties.
In any order the team finds most convenient.
Only when assigned by the radar controller.
According to pilot request sequence rather than ATC priority.

items.question_text: Under 13-2-7, how must multiple controller operation be accomplished?
items.explanation: Per 13-2-7b, it must be accomplished according to facility directives.

choices.text: According to facility directives.
According to individual controller preference if all controllers agree.
Only according to pilot workload considerations.
According to the receiving sector's local practice only.

items.question_text: Under 13-2-7, multiple-controller workstation operations may ignore facility directives as long as all tasks eventually get done.
items.explanation: That statement is false because 13-2-7 requires multiple controller operation to be accomplished according to facility directives.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 14-1-1, what kind of flight plans require the clearance to be verbally issued to the flight crew rather than transmitted by PDC?
items.explanation: Per 14-1-1d, revised or amended flight plans require the clearance to be verbally issued to the flight crew.

choices.text: Revised or amended flight plans.
Any clearance containing a SID.
Any clearance transmitted within 60 days of TDLS commissioning.
Any clearance with operationally predefined selectable fields.

items.question_text: Under the note in 14-1-1, what is true about a flight plan that initially generates in the tower with a route assigned by automation?
items.explanation: The note to 14-1-1 says that kind of flight plan is not considered revised or amended and may be transmitted.

choices.text: It is not considered revised or amended and may be transmitted.
It is always considered amended and must be verbally issued.
It may be transmitted only after the ATIS 60-day broadcast period ends.
It must be converted to a CPDLC DCL before it can be sent.

items.question_text: Under 14-1-1, for a minimum of 60 days following the commissioning of a Terminal Data Link System, the facility ATIS must broadcast that PDC is available.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 14-1-1f.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 14-1-2, what does CPDLC permit with respect to amended or revised flight plans?
items.explanation: Per 14-1-2c, CPDLC permits amended or revised flight plans to be transmitted.

choices.text: CPDLC permits amended or revised flight plans to be transmitted.
CPDLC never permits amended or revised flight plans to be transmitted.
Only revised, but not amended, flight plans may be transmitted by CPDLC.
Only amended, but not revised, flight plans may be transmitted by CPDLC.

items.question_text: Under 14-1-2, what must be done if a revised or amended flight plan cannot be delivered using CPDLC?
items.explanation: Per 14-1-2c, it must be verbally issued to the flight crew.

choices.text: It must be verbally issued to the flight crew.
It must be canceled and refiled.
It must be held until ATIS broadcasts CPDLC availability again.
It must be sent by PDC instead.

items.question_text: Under 14-1-2, for a minimum of 60 days following the commissioning of a CPDLC capability, the facility ATIS must broadcast that CPDLC is available.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 14-1-2e.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 14-1-3, if no SID is filed or assigned by EAS and no SID is to be assigned, what must Selectable Field 1 contain?
items.explanation: Per 14-1-3a1(c), the controller must select the NO SID option.

choices.text: The NO SID option.
The ---- option.
MAINTAIN (assigned altitude).
SEE SID.

items.question_text: Under 14-1-3, when no SID is assigned or the assigned SID does not contain either an initial altitude or vertical guidance, what must Selectable Field 5 contain?
items.explanation: Per 14-1-3a5, Field 5 must contain MAINTAIN (assigned altitude).

choices.text: MAINTAIN (assigned altitude).
CLIMB VIA SID.
CLIMB VIA SID EXCEPT MAINTAIN (altitude), regardless of whether a SID is assigned.
NO SID.

items.question_text: Under 14-1-3, the Local Information Field must not contradict information contained elsewhere in a departure clearance.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 14-1-3a9.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 14-2-1, when should CPDLC not be used to issue a clearance unless voice communication is not operationally feasible?
items.explanation: Per 14-2-1d, CPDLC should not be used to issue immediate or expeditious clearances unless voice communication is not operationally feasible.

choices.text: For immediate or expeditious clearances.
For routine altimeter updates only.
For all frequency changes.
For all domestic en route messages.

items.question_text: Under 14-2-1, what must be ensured prior to transfer of communication unless otherwise coordinated?
items.explanation: Per 14-2-1e, ensure there are no open trajectory altering clearances prior to transfer of communication unless otherwise coordinated.

choices.text: There are no open trajectory altering clearances.
There is at least one open CPDLC route change awaiting pilot acknowledgment.
The receiving sector has issued the next weather deviation via CPDLC.
The VCI has been manually deselected.

items.question_text: Under 14-2-1, when responding to a weather-deviation pilot-initiated downlink via CPDLC and the aircraft has a climb-via, descend-via, or crossing restriction, the controller must unable the request and revert to voice communications.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 14-2-1j.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 14-2-2, when an IC mismatch or confirm assigned altitude downlink time-out indicator is displayed, what must the controller who has the aircraft on voice frequency do?
items.explanation: Per 14-2-2a, that controller must use voice communication to verify the assigned altitude of the aircraft and acknowledge the indicator.

choices.text: Use voice communication to verify the assigned altitude and acknowledge the indicator.
Use CPDLC to verify the altitude because the mismatch appeared in the data block.
Wait for the receiving sector to verify the altitude after transfer of communication.
Acknowledge the indicator first and verify the altitude only if the pilot reports a discrepancy.

items.question_text: Under 14-2-2, what is required when overriding an open CPDLC clearance that contains multiple elements?
items.explanation: Per 14-2-2c, the controller uses voice communications and must restate the entire clearance when the CPDLC clearance being overridden contains multiple elements.

choices.text: Use voice communications and restate the entire clearance.
Cancel the CPDLC uplink silently and issue only the changed element by voice.
Use CPDLC free text to replace only the last element of the clearance.
Leave the original uplink open and tell the next sector to resolve the conflict.

items.question_text: Under 14-2-2, a controller may cancel an open uplink before the pilot has been issued and has acknowledged the superseding ATC clearance by voice communication.
items.explanation: That statement is false. Per 14-2-2d, controllers may cancel an open uplink only after ensuring the pilot has been issued and has acknowledged, via voice, the superseding ATC clearance.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 14-2-3, what must a controller do if the CPDLC system fails to provide a necessary automated altimeter setting to an aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 14-2-3a, the controller must issue an altimeter setting in accordance with Chapter 2, Section 7, Altimeter Settings.

choices.text: Issue the altimeter setting in accordance with Chapter 2, Section 7.
Wait for the next monitor transfer so the system can resend the altimeter automatically.
Advise the aircraft to remain on the previous altimeter until CPDLC service is restored.
Use only free text to transmit the altimeter because altimeter settings may not be issued by voice.

items.question_text: Under 14-2-3, what broadcast must controllers make whenever there is a shutdown or failure of CPDLC service?
items.explanation: Per 14-2-3c1, controllers must use voice to alert pilots that CPDLC is no longer in use and request no pilot downlinks until further advised.

choices.text: A voice message that CPDLC is no longer in use and that no pilot downlinks should be sent until further advised.
A CPDLC free-text message that the outage is temporary and pilots should continue downlinking if necessary.
An ATIS-only advisory with no controller voice action required.
A broadcast telling pilots to close all uplinks on their own without further controller involvement.

items.question_text: Under 14-2-3, any open or abnormally closed uplinks at the time of a CPDLC shutdown must be resolved by voice with each aircraft.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 14-2-3c2.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 14-2-4, which messages must not be issued via advisory or free text messages?
items.explanation: Per 14-2-4a1, control instructions and messages that require an acknowledgement from the aircraft must not be issued via advisory or free text messages.

choices.text: Control instructions and messages that require an acknowledgement from the aircraft.
Only weather-related advisory messages.
Only altimeter settings below FL180.
Any message containing a standard abbreviation from JO 7340.2.

items.question_text: Under 14-2-4, when using CPDLC to issue a speed assignment to an aircraft at or above FL390, what pilot response satisfies the concurrence requirement?
items.explanation: Per 14-2-4b1, the WILCO response satisfies the concurrence requirement in 5-7-2b.

choices.text: WILCO.
ROGER.
STANDBY.
No response is required.

items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 14-2-4c1.

False

items.question_text: Under 14-3-1, when CPDLC is available and a CPDLC-connected aircraft is operating outside of VHF coverage, what must be used as the primary means of communication?
items.explanation: Per 14-3-1a, CPDLC must be used as the primary means of communication in that situation.

choices.text: CPDLC.
HF voice only.
Third-party relay only.
Free-text messaging only.

items.question_text: Under 14-3-1, when a pilot communicates via CPDLC, how should the controller respond?
items.explanation: Per 14-3-1d, the response should be via CPDLC.

choices.text: Via CPDLC.
Via voice whenever possible, even if the pilot used CPDLC.
Via free text only.
By waiting for the next sector to respond.

items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 14-3-1c.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 14-3-2, when the receiving facility is capable of CPDLC communications, how is the data link transfer accomplished?
items.explanation: Per 14-3-2a, the data link transfer is automatic and is accomplished within facility adapted parameters.

choices.text: Automatically, within facility adapted parameters.
Only by manual voice coordination between facilities.
Only after the pilot logs off and logs on to the next facility.
By canceling CPDLC service before every transfer.

items.question_text: Under 14-3-2, when a receiving facility is not CPDLC capable, how must the transfer of communications be made?
items.explanation: Per 14-3-2b, the transfer must be made in accordance with local directives and Letters of Agreement.

choices.text: In accordance with local directives and Letters of Agreement.
By automatic CPDLC transfer anyway, because capability only affects acknowledgments.
At the controller's discretion without regard to local directives.
Only after the aircraft exits oceanic airspace.

items.question_text: Under 14-3-2, when the receiving facility is not CPDLC capable, the transfer of communications must be made in accordance with local directives and Letters of Agreement.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 14-3-2b.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 14-3-3, what should a controller attempt first if any portion of the automated transfer fails?
items.explanation: Per 14-3-3a, the controller should attempt to initiate the transfer manually.

choices.text: Initiate the transfer manually.
Immediately terminate CPDLC service to the aircraft.
Assume the next facility will recover the failed transfer automatically.
Wait for the pilot to request a new logon before taking any action.

items.question_text: Under 14-3-3, what must a controller do when there is uncertainty that a clearance was delivered to an aircraft via CPDLC?
items.explanation: Per 14-3-3e, the controller must continue to protect the airspace associated with the clearance until an appropriate operational response is received and take action to ensure the clearance was received.

choices.text: Continue protecting the airspace associated with the clearance until an appropriate operational response is received, and take action to ensure the clearance was received.
Assume the clearance was received if the aircraft remains on the route shown in the data block.
Stop protecting the airspace after a reasonable time and let the next facility resolve any uncertainty.

items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 14-3-3b.

False

items.question_text: Under 2-6-4, what term must be used when describing satellite-derived weather such as Offshore Precipitation Capability (OPC)?
items.explanation: Per 2-6-4c, use the term weather when describing satellite-derived weather, and do not issue intensity based on satellite-derived weather.

choices.text: Weather.
Precipitation.
Turbulence.
Intensity unknown precipitation.

items.question_text: Under 2-6-4, when approving a weather deviation for an aircraft that had previously been issued a crossing altitude, climb via, or descend via clearance, what must the controller issue with the deviation clearance?
items.explanation: Per 2-6-4i2, issue an altitude to maintain and, if necessary, assign a speed; if you intend on clearing the aircraft to resume the procedure, advise the pilot.

choices.text: An altitude to maintain and, if necessary, an assigned speed.
Only a heading change, because the previous crossing restriction remains in effect automatically.
Only the phrase deviation approved, because the aircraft's FMS will preserve all vertical restrictions.
A vector only after the pilot is clear of weather.

items.question_text: Under 2-6-4, when precipitation intensity information is not available, the phraseology includes the words 'intensity unknown.'
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 2-6-4f.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 2-9-3, how long must terminal facilities include a reported unauthorized laser illumination event on the ATIS broadcast following the last report?

choices.text: One hour following the last report.
Thirty minutes following the last report.
Until the next weather observation sequence only.
Until local midnight.

items.question_text: Under 2-9-3, what statement must be included on the ATIS broadcast when a FICON NOTAM indicates runway condition codes X/X/X?
items.explanation: Per 2-9-3k, include the statement 'Runway Condition Codes Missing.'

choices.text: Runway Condition Codes Missing.
Runway Condition Codes Unreliable.
Runway unavailable due reporting outage.
Braking action unknown.

items.question_text: Under 2-9-3, when a runway length has been shortened, the word WARNING must preface the runway number and the word shortened must be included in the ATIS message.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 2-9-3h.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 2-1-29, what is required before a non-RVSM aircraft may be permitted in RVSM airspace as an excepted aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 2-1-29a1, the aircraft must meet excepted-aircraft criteria and be previously approved by the operations supervisor/CIC on a workload- or traffic-permitting basis.

choices.text: It must qualify as an excepted aircraft and be previously approved by the operations supervisor/CIC.
It only needs pilot request and sector controller approval.

items.question_text: Under 2-1-29, what phrase must be used in all verbal ground-to-ground communications involving non-RVSM aircraft while cleared to operate within RVSM airspace?
items.explanation: Per 2-1-29d, use negative RVSM in all such verbal ground-to-ground communications.

choices.text: Negative RVSM.
Unable RVSM.
RVSM caution.
Report able RVSM.

items.question_text: Under 2-1-29, ATC may allow aircraft to remain in RVSM airspace using reduced vertical separation minima after the loss of a transponder or Mode C altitude reporting.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 2-1-29g.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 2-1-28, after an aircraft reports it is responding to a TCAS resolution advisory, what must ATC avoid doing?
items.explanation: Per 2-1-28a, do not issue control instructions contrary to the RA procedure the crew advises it is executing.

choices.text: Issuing control instructions contrary to the RA the crew is executing.
Issuing traffic advisories about nearby aircraft.
Monitoring the aircraft until it reports clear of conflict.
Telling adjacent sectors that an RA is in progress.

items.question_text: Under 2-1-28, when does ATC resume responsibility for approved separation after a reported TCAS RA maneuver?
items.explanation: Per 2-1-28d, responsibility resumes only after one of the specified restoration conditions is met.

choices.text: When the aircraft returns to its assigned altitude, or the crew reports the RA maneuver is complete and approved separation is observed to be reestablished, or an alternate clearance has been executed and approved separation is observed to be reestablished.
As soon as the controller believes the conflict geometry is improving, even if the crew is still maneuvering on the RA.
Immediately after the aircraft first acknowledges the traffic advisory associated with the RA.
Only after all nearby aircraft have been placed on headings assigned by ATC.

items.question_text: Under 2-1-28, controllers should not assume other nearby aircraft are involved in the RA unless those aircraft also report that they are responding to an RA.
items.explanation: That statement appears in 2-1-28c.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 2-1-28, ATC remains responsible for approved separation throughout the entire TCAS RA maneuver once it has begun.
items.explanation: Per 2-1-28d, ATC is not responsible for approved separation between aircraft until one of the listed restoration conditions is met.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 2-1-19, when must controllers apply wake turbulence procedures?
items.explanation: Per 2-1-19a, apply wake turbulence procedures when required behind another aircraft.

choices.text: Whenever wake turbulence separation is required behind another aircraft.
Only when the pilot specifically requests wake turbulence separation.
Only when both aircraft are VFR.
Only after the trailing aircraft reaches the runway threshold.

items.question_text: Under 2-1-19, for which IFR aircraft do required wake turbulence separation minima continue to touchdown?
items.explanation: Per 2-1-19b, wake turbulence separation minima continue to touchdown for all IFR aircraft not making a visual approach or maintaining visual separation.

choices.text: All IFR aircraft not making a visual approach or maintaining visual separation.
Only heavy and super category IFR aircraft.
Only IFR aircraft inside the final approach fix.
Only IFR aircraft when the runway is wet or contaminated.

items.question_text: Under 2-1-19, wake turbulence procedures are applied when wake turbulence separation is required.
items.explanation: That statement appears in 2-1-19a.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 2-1-19, required wake turbulence separation minima stop at the runway threshold for all IFR aircraft.
items.explanation: Per 2-1-19b, wake turbulence separation continues to touchdown for IFR aircraft unless they are making a visual approach or maintaining visual separation.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 2-1-11, to what operations may MARSA be applied?
items.explanation: Per 2-1-11a, MARSA may only be applied to military operations specified in an LOA, letter of procedure, a military directive, or another appropriate agreement.

choices.text: Only military operations specified in appropriate agreements or documents.
Any IFR operation conducted inside special use airspace.
Any operation whenever both pilots agree to accept military separation.
Only military operations conducted above FL180.

items.question_text: Under 2-1-11, what is ATC's sole responsibility when military aircraft are engaged in MARSA operations?
items.explanation: Per 2-1-11c, ATC facilities are responsible only for separation between military aircraft engaged in MARSA and other nonparticipating IFR aircraft.

choices.text: Separating participating MARSA aircraft from other nonparticipating IFR aircraft.
Invoking or denying MARSA for the military unit.
Determining which military aircraft are authorized to enter an ATCAA.
Approving pilot familiarity with MARSA procedures before departure.

items.question_text: Under 2-1-11, ATC facilities do not invoke or deny MARSA.
items.explanation: That statement appears in 2-1-11c1.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 2-1-11, ATC is responsible for determining which military aircraft are authorized to enter SUA or ATCAAs before MARSA may be used.
items.explanation: Per 2-1-11d, ATC is not responsible for determining which military aircraft are authorized to enter SUA or ATCAAs.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 2-1-2, what is the controller's first priority?
items.explanation: Per 2-1-2a, first priority is separating aircraft and issuing safety alerts as required in the order.

choices.text: Separating aircraft and issuing required safety alerts.
Providing requested additional services in the order pilots ask for them.
Broadcasting weather information before all other duties.
Completing administrative coordination before handling traffic.

items.question_text: Under 2-1-2, when may controllers furnish additional services to aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 2-1-2e, furnish additional services only to the extent possible after higher-priority duties and while considering radar limitations, volume of traffic, frequency congestion, and workload.

choices.text: Only after higher-priority duties permit, and only to the extent possible given radar limitations, traffic volume, frequency congestion, and workload.
Whenever a pilot requests them, regardless of traffic, radar limits, or workload.
Only when no safety alerts have been issued during the shift.
Only after the supervisor specifically delegates them on each occasion.

items.question_text: Under 2-1-2, controllers use good judgment in prioritizing all other provisions of the order based on the situation.
items.explanation: That statement appears in 2-1-2b.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 2-1-2, additional services are provided without regard to higher-priority duties, traffic volume, frequency congestion, or workload.
items.explanation: Per 2-1-2e, additional services are furnished only to the extent possible after higher-priority duties and while considering operational limitations.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 2-1-8, what must a controller do when transferring control jurisdiction of an aircraft that has reported minimum fuel?
items.explanation: Per 2-1-8, inform any facility to whom control jurisdiction is transferred of the minimum-fuel problem.

choices.text: Inform the receiving facility of the minimum-fuel problem.
Do nothing unless the pilot also declares an emergency.
Transfer the aircraft without mentioning fuel status so the next facility can assess it independently.
Cancel the flight plan and direct the pilot to land at the nearest airport.

items.question_text: Under 2-1-8, what does a pilot's use of the term minimum fuel indicate?
items.explanation: The note under 2-1-8 says minimum fuel means the pilot recognizes that, upon reaching destination, the aircraft cannot accept any undue delay. It is not an emergency, but an advisory that an emergency situation is possible if undue delay occurs.

choices.text: The aircraft cannot accept any undue delay upon reaching destination, although no emergency has yet been declared.
The aircraft is in an emergency and must automatically receive priority handling.
The pilot has enough fuel for holding and alternate routing but would prefer direct routing.
The pilot is canceling IFR because remaining fuel no longer supports instrument flight.

items.question_text: Under 2-1-8, a minimum-fuel advisory does not by itself imply a need for traffic priority.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in the note under 2-1-8.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 2-1-8, if the remaining usable fuel suggests the need for traffic priority to ensure a safe landing, the pilot should declare an emergency and report fuel remaining in minutes.
items.explanation: That instruction appears in the note under 2-1-8.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 2-1-7, when a pilot reports an inflight equipment malfunction, what must the controller determine first?
items.explanation: Per 2-1-7a, determine the nature and extent of any special handling desired.

choices.text: The nature and extent of any special handling desired.
Whether the pilot intends to cancel IFR immediately.
Whether the aircraft should be reassigned a different call sign.
Whether the pilot can continue without any ATC assistance.

items.question_text: Under 2-1-7, what must controllers do for other controllers or facilities that will subsequently handle the aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 2-1-7c, relay all pertinent details concerning the aircraft and any special handling required or being provided.

choices.text: Relay all pertinent details concerning the aircraft and any special handling required or being provided.
Relay only the aircraft type and destination, because malfunction details are pilot-to-pilot information.
Relay the details only if the pilot declares an emergency.
Avoid discussing the malfunction so the next facility can make an independent decision without prior influence.

items.question_text: Under 2-1-7, controllers should provide the maximum assistance possible consistent with equipment, workload, and any special handling requested.
items.explanation: That statement appears in 2-1-7b.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 2-1-7, equipment-malfunction details do not need to be passed to subsequent controllers or facilities unless the aircraft is no longer able to maintain IFR flight.
items.explanation: That is false. Per 2-1-7c, all pertinent details and any special handling required or being provided must be relayed.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 2-1-4, which aircraft has the right of way over all other air traffic?
items.explanation: Per 2-1-4a, an aircraft in distress has the right of way over all other air traffic.

choices.text: An aircraft in distress.
Any IFR aircraft retaining its IFR flight plan to destination.
Any aircraft with HOSP in flight plan remarks.
Any aircraft that checked in first on the frequency.

items.question_text: Under 2-1-4, when does a civil air ambulance flight receive priority handling as MEDEVAC?
items.explanation: Per 2-1-4b1, provide priority handling when the pilot verbally identifies the flight in radio transmissions by stating MEDEVAC followed by the FAA-authorized call sign or full civil registration letters/numbers.

choices.text: When the pilot verbally identifies the flight by stating MEDEVAC followed by the authorized call sign or full civil registration.
Whenever the flight plan contains the letter L for MEDEVAC, even if the pilot never says MEDEVAC on the radio.
Whenever a medical patient is on board, regardless of what the pilot says.
Only after the pilot declares an emergency and requests medical priority in minutes of fuel remaining.

items.question_text: Under 2-1-4, flight-plan entries such as MEDEVAC, HOSP, or AIR EVAC are informational only unless the pilot makes the required verbal identification or request.
items.explanation: The notes under 2-1-4b1 and 2-1-4b2 state that such entries are informational only and do not by themselves establish operational priority.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 2-1-4, IFR aircraft must have priority over VFR aircraft solely because they have retained an IFR flight plan.
items.explanation: The note under 2-1-4 explains that a pilot's retention of an IFR flight plan does not afford priority over VFR aircraft.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 2-1-1, what is the primary purpose of the ATC system?
items.explanation: Per 2-1-1a, the primary purpose of the ATC system is to prevent a collision involving aircraft operating in the system.

choices.text: To prevent a collision involving aircraft operating in the system.
To provide the most expeditious routing requested by pilots regardless of conflict risk.
To maximize airport capacity even if separation must occasionally be adjusted locally.
To provide advisory service only, leaving collision avoidance primarily to facilities and operators.

items.question_text: Under 2-1-1, when must additional ATC services be provided?
items.explanation: Per 2-1-1c, certain additional services must be provided to the extent permitted when the work situation permits; they are not optional, though they may be precluded by traffic volume, frequency congestion, surveillance quality, workload, higher-priority duties, or physical inability to scan and detect.

choices.text: When the work situation permits and to the extent allowed by traffic, workload, surveillance quality, and higher-priority duties.
Only when a pilot specifically cites the paragraph number authorizing the service.
Only when there is no traffic on frequency and the controller elects to provide them.
Only in domestic U.S. airspace, because additional services are never required outside the United States.

items.question_text: Under 2-1-1, the provision of additional services is not optional on the part of the controller when the work situation permits.
items.explanation: That statement appears in 2-1-1c.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 2-1-1, controllers provide ATC service to model aircraft operating in the NAS and to any UAS operating in the NAS at or below 400 feet AGL in the same manner as other aircraft.
items.explanation: Per 2-1-1e, ATC services are not provided for model aircraft operating in the NAS or to any UAS operating in the NAS at or below 400 feet AGL, subject to the stated note clarifications.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 2-2-11, when do revisions to ETA information need to be forwarded?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-11a, ETA revisions need only be forwarded when the time differs by more than 3 minutes from the estimate given.

choices.text: Only when the time differs by more than 3 minutes from the estimate given.
Whenever the ETA changes by any amount.
Only when the ETA changes by more than 10 minutes.
Only when requested by the receiving facility.

items.question_text: Under 2-2-11, when a route or altitude in a previously issued clearance is amended within 30 minutes of proposed departure time, how must the amending facility coordinate the amendment with the receiving facility?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-11c, coordinate the amendment via verbal and automated means; if automated means are unavailable, verbal coordination is sufficient.

choices.text: By verbal and automated means, unless automated means are unavailable, in which case verbal coordination is sufficient.
By automated means only, because verbal coordination is not acceptable for route or altitude amendments.
By verbal means only, regardless of automation status.
By waiting for the next flight progress strip to print at the receiving facility.

items.question_text: Under 2-2-11, when a controller receives a UTM notification to an FDIO-only facility, the controller must verify the flight plan data to the receiving facility within three minutes of the transfer of control point estimate.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 2-2-11e.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 2-7-2, when clearing an aircraft to descend below the lowest usable flight level, what altimeter setting must the controller issue?
items.explanation: Per 2-7-2e, issue the altimeter setting of the weather reporting station nearest the point the aircraft will descend below that flight level.

choices.text: The altimeter setting of the weather reporting station nearest the point where the aircraft will descend below that flight level.
The destination altimeter only, regardless of where the aircraft is descending.
Any station along the route, even if it is not the nearest point of descent below the flight level.
No altimeter setting unless the pilot requests one after leaving Class A airspace.

items.question_text: Under 2-7-2, when no approach control facility serves the destination airport, when should the destination altimeter setting be issued to arriving aircraft en route?
items.explanation: Per 2-7-2c4, issue the destination airport altimeter to arriving aircraft approximately 50 miles from the destination if no approach control facility serves the airport.

choices.text: Approximately 50 miles from the destination airport.
Immediately after departure from the origin airport.
Only after the aircraft is established on final approach.
Only after the aircraft enters Class D airspace around the destination.

items.question_text: Under 2-7-2, unless specifically requested by the pilot, the altimeter setting need not be issued to local aircraft operators who have requested this omission in writing or to scheduled air carriers.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 2-7-2c2.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 2-7-3, when barometric pressure is greater than 31.00 inches mercury, what instruction is given to en route arrivals regarding the altimeter setting?
items.explanation: Per 2-7-3a, advise pilots to leave the altimeter set to 31.00 until reaching the final approach fix.

choices.text: Leave the altimeter set to 31.00 until reaching the final approach fix.
Set the local altimeter immediately and keep it there until landing.
Leave the altimeter at 29.92 until touchdown.
Set 31.00 only after crossing the final approach fix.

items.question_text: Under 2-7-3, when must departures be advised to set altimeter 31.00?
items.explanation: Per 2-7-3b, departures set 31.00 prior to reaching any mandatory or crossing altitude, or 1,500 feet AGL, whichever is lower.

choices.text: Prior to reaching any mandatory or crossing altitude, or 1,500 feet AGL, whichever is lower.
After passing the first mandatory or crossing altitude.
Only after leaving terminal airspace and checking in with departure control.
At top of climb, after all pressure-related restrictions have ended.

items.question_text: Under 2-7-3, altitude-reporting transponders transmit pressure altitude, and the altimeter setting does not directly affect the transponder-reported altitude.
items.explanation: That statement appears in Note 1 under 2-7-3.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 3-1-8, how long must an ATIS statement about low level wind shear or microburst remain in the ATIS following the last report or indication?
items.explanation: Per 3-1-8a, include the statement on the ATIS for 20 minutes following the last report or indication of wind shear or microburst.

choices.text: For 20 minutes following the last report or indication.
For 10 minutes following the last report or indication.
Until the next hourly weather observation only.
Until the next departing aircraft acknowledges the alert.

items.question_text: Under 3-1-8, what should a controller do after an aircraft reports that it is performing a wind shear escape maneuver?
items.explanation: Per 3-1-8d1, do not issue control instructions contrary to pilot actions, and continue safety alerts and traffic advisories for the escape aircraft as appropriate.

choices.text: Do not issue control instructions contrary to the pilot's actions, and continue safety alerts and traffic advisories as appropriate.
Continue assigning heading and altitude instructions exactly as previously cleared until the pilot confirms the escape is complete.
Stop issuing all alerts because the pilot is now fully responsible for terrain and traffic avoidance.
Assume all aircraft nearby are also escaping and suspend control instructions for the area.

items.question_text: Under 3-1-8, unless advised by additional aircraft that they are also performing an escape procedure, controllers must not presume other aircraft in the proximity of the escape aircraft are also responding to wind shear alerts or events.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 3-1-8d2.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 3-3-6, what is the normal position of arresting systems remotely controlled by ATC during normal operations?
items.explanation: Per 3-3-6a, arresting systems remotely controlled by ATC must remain in the retracted or down position during normal operations.

choices.text: Retracted or down.
Raised or up unless civil traffic objects.
Raised at the departure end whenever military traffic is expected.
Whichever position the previous controller left them in.

items.question_text: Under 3-3-6, what action is required if an aircraft engages a raised barrier or cable?
items.explanation: Per 3-3-6g, initiate crash alarm procedures immediately.

choices.text: Initiate crash alarm procedures immediately.
Wait for the pilot to report damage before activating any alarm procedures.
Activate crash alarm only if the engagement was not requested by military authority.
Treat the engagement as normal and notify operations after the aircraft clears the runway.

items.question_text: Under 3-3-6, information about rubber-supported cables at the approach end of the runway may be omitted if it is published in the Domestic Notices webpage, International Notices webpage, or the DoD FLIP.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 3-3-6d.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 3-4-1, when must a controller take action to provide for the operation of appropriate airport lighting aids?
items.explanation: Per 3-4-1, take action whenever you become aware that an emergency has or will occur.

choices.text: Whenever the controller becomes aware that an emergency has or will occur.
Only after the pilot specifically requests emergency lighting.
Only when the aircraft is on final approach to land.
Only after crash alarm procedures have been activated.

items.question_text: Under 3-4-1, what airport lighting aids should be operated during an emergency?
items.explanation: Per 3-4-1, provide for the operation of all appropriate airport lighting aids as required.

choices.text: All appropriate airport lighting aids as required.
Only runway edge lights unless the pilot asks for more.
Only taxiway lighting, because runway lights are controlled separately.
Only the rotating beacon and approach lights, regardless of the emergency type.

items.question_text: Under 3-4-1, when you become aware that an emergency has or will occur, take action to provide for the operation of all appropriate airport lighting aids as required.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 3-4-1.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 3-4-6, what is one authorized reason to use ALS intensity settings other than those shown in the table?
items.explanation: Per 3-4-6, exceptions include facility directives, pilot request, or controller judgment if not contrary to the pilot's request.

choices.text: When a pilot requests a different setting.
Only when the airport manager is physically present in the tower.
Only when visibility is exactly one mile.
Never; the table values are mandatory in all circumstances.

items.question_text: Under the note in 3-4-6, when may step 4 or step 5 ALS intensity settings be used at night?
items.explanation: Per the note under 3-4-6, at night use step 4 or 5 only when requested by a pilot.

choices.text: Only when requested by a pilot.
Whenever visibility drops below 3 miles, even without pilot request.
Only when approved by the airport manager, regardless of pilot request.
