items.question_text: Under 3-4-6, facility directives may specify ALS intensity settings other than the table values to meet local atmospheric, topographic, and twilight conditions.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 3-4-6a.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 3-7-1, which word must not be used in conjunction with authorization for aircraft taxi or equipment, vehicle, or personnel movement on the movement area?
items.explanation: Per 3-7-1c, do not use the word CLEARED for these movement authorizations.

choices.text: Cleared.
Proceed.
Hold.
Taxi.

items.question_text: Under 3-7-1d, when a controller issues intersection departure remaining length information, how is the distance stated?
items.explanation: Per 3-7-1d, issue the measured distance from the intersection to the runway end rounded down to the nearest 50 feet.

choices.text: As the measured distance from the intersection to the runway end, rounded down to the nearest 50 feet.
As the published declared distance from the Chart Supplement, rounded to the nearest 100 feet.
As the remaining distance rounded up to the nearest 50 feet for safety margin.
As the full published runway length unless the pilot specifically objects.

items.question_text: Under 3-7-1, controllers may issue conditional instructions that depend on an arrival aircraft approaching the runway or a departure aircraft established on takeoff roll.
items.explanation: Per 3-7-1a, those conditional instructions must not be issued.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 3-7-5, when must aircraft and vehicle access to the ILS critical area be controlled to ensure the integrity of ILS course signals?
items.explanation: Per 3-7-5a, protection applies whenever the official weather observation is a ceiling of less than 800 feet or visibility less than 2 miles.

choices.text: Whenever the official weather observation is a ceiling of less than 800 feet or visibility less than 2 miles.
Only when the ceiling is less than 200 feet and RVR is below 2,000 feet.
Whenever any aircraft reports coupled or autoland capability, regardless of weather.

items.question_text: Under 3-7-5b, when should the advisory "ILS CRITICAL AREA NOT PROTECTED" be issued?
items.explanation: Per 3-7-5b, issue it promptly when an arriving aircraft advises it will conduct a coupled, CAT III, autoland, or similar approach and the critical area will not be protected while the weather is ceiling 800 feet or more, or visibility 2 miles or more.

choices.text: When an arriving aircraft advises it will conduct a coupled, CAT III, autoland, or similar approach and the critical area will not be protected while the ceiling is 800 feet or more, or visibility is 2 miles or more.
Only after the aircraft reports the runway in sight and asks whether the area is protected.
Only when the weather is below 200 feet ceiling or RVR 2,000 feet.

items.question_text: Under 3-7-5, if the official weather observation indicates a ceiling of less than 200 feet or RVR 2,000 feet, do not authorize localizer critical area operations when an arriving aircraft is inside the middle marker, or inside one-half mile final if there is no middle marker.
items.explanation: That statement appears in 3-7-5a1(b).

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 3-7-6, when must the precision obstacle free zone be clear of traffic?
items.explanation: Per 3-7-6a, the POFZ must be clear when an aircraft on a vertically guided final approach is within 2 miles of the threshold and the ceiling is below 300 feet or visibility is less than three-quarters statute mile.

choices.text: When an aircraft on a vertically guided final approach is within 2 miles of the threshold and the ceiling is below 300 feet or visibility is less than three-quarters statute mile.
Whenever any aircraft is inside the outer marker, regardless of weather or approach type.
Only when RVR is below 2,000 feet and the aircraft is inside the middle marker.
Only after the aircraft reports the runway in sight.

items.question_text: Under 3-7-6, when must the final approach obstacle clearance surfaces be clear of aircraft or vehicles?
items.explanation: Per 3-7-6b, the OCS must be clear when the aircraft on the vertically guided approach is within 2 miles and the ceiling is below 800 feet or visibility is less than 2 statute miles.

choices.text: When the aircraft is within 2 miles of the threshold and the ceiling is below 800 feet or visibility is less than 2 statute miles.
Only when the aircraft is inside the middle marker and visibility is below three-quarters statute mile.
Whenever the weather is VFR and the aircraft is flying an LPV approach.

items.question_text: Under 3-7-6, if the POFZ or OCS cannot be cleared before the aircraft reaches 2 miles and the weather is below the stated threshold, issue traffic to the landing aircraft and clear the area as soon as practical.
items.explanation: That statement appears in 3-7-6c.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 3-8-1, how do controllers establish the sequence of arriving and departing aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 3-8-1, establish the sequence by requiring aircraft to adjust flight or ground operation as necessary to achieve proper spacing.

choices.text: By requiring them to adjust flight or ground operation as necessary to achieve proper spacing.
By withholding all sequencing instructions until the aircraft report each other in sight.
By issuing only landing clearances and avoiding speed or pattern adjustments.

items.question_text: Under Note 1 in 3-8-1, what does the "Cleared for the Option" procedure permit?
items.explanation: Per Note 1 in 3-8-1, the option permits a touch-and-go, low approach, missed approach, stop-and-go, or full stop landing, subject to ATC approval.

choices.text: A touch-and-go, low approach, missed approach, stop-and-go, or full stop landing.
Only a touch-and-go or full stop landing.

items.question_text: Under 3-8-1, read back of hold short instructions applies when hold instructions are issued to a pilot in lieu of a takeoff clearance.
items.explanation: That statement appears in Note 3 under 3-8-1.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 3-10-10, what is the minimum altitude that may be authorized for an altitude restricted low approach?

choices.text: No less than 500 feet above the airport.
No less than 300 feet above the runway threshold.
No less than 1,000 feet above the airport in every case.

items.question_text: Under 3-10-10, which traffic may not be overflown on an altitude restricted low approach?
items.explanation: Per 3-10-10, restricted altitude low approaches are not authorized over aircraft holding in position or departing aircraft.

choices.text: An aircraft holding in position or a departing aircraft.
A preceding landing or taxiing aircraft.
Any vehicle on a taxiway adjacent to the runway.
Ground personnel who have already been notified by airport authorities.

items.question_text: Under 3-10-10, advise the approaching aircraft of the location of applicable ground traffic, personnel, or equipment.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 3-10-10.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 3-11-1b, when is HOVER-TAXI phraseology used?
items.explanation: Per 3-11-1b, use HOVER-TAXI when the helicopter or powered-lift aircraft is to proceed at a slow speed above the surface, normally below 20 knots and in ground effect.

choices.text: When it proceeds at a slow speed above the surface, normally below 20 knots and in ground effect.
When it proceeds above 20 knots and normally below 100 feet AGL between airport points.
Only when taxiing on wheels on the movement area.
Only when departing the airport environment entirely.

items.question_text: Under 3-11-1c, what is the normal altitude expectation for AIR-TAXI unless otherwise requested or instructed?
items.explanation: Per 3-11-1c, the pilot is expected to remain below 100 feet AGL unless otherwise requested or instructed.

choices.text: Below 100 feet AGL.
At or above 100 feet AGL.
Below 20 feet AGL only.
At the pilot's filed cruising altitude.

items.question_text: Under 3-11-1a, when a wheeled helicopter or powered-lift aircraft taxis on the surface, use the phraseology in 3-7-2.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 3-11-1a.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 4-2-8a, where do you clear an aircraft that will operate IFR for the initial part of flight and VFR for the latter part?
items.explanation: Per 4-2-8a, clear the aircraft to the fix at which the IFR part ends.

choices.text: To the fix at which the IFR part ends.
Direct to the final destination under IFR regardless of where VFR begins.
Only to the first en route fix after departure.

items.question_text: Under 4-2-8, what must the controller do for a Mode-C equipped aircraft when it changes from VFR to IFR?
items.explanation: Per 4-2-8c, assign a beacon code that will allow MSAW alarms.

choices.text: Assign a beacon code that will allow MSAW alarms.
Leave the VFR code in place until the aircraft reaches the minimum IFR altitude.
Assign any discrete code, even if it suppresses MSAW alerts.

items.question_text: Under 4-2-8d3, if the pilot says they are unable to maintain terrain and obstruction clearance during the climb to minimum IFR altitude, instruct the pilot to maintain VFR and state intentions.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 4-2-8d3.

items.question_text: Under 4-3-2b1(a), how must a clearance limit be stated when the clearance limit is an airport?
items.explanation: Per 4-3-2b1(a), the word AIRPORT must follow the airport name.

choices.text: The word AIRPORT must follow the airport name.
State only the airport identifier and omit the word AIRPORT.

items.question_text: Under 4-3-2c2, when must an obstacle departure procedure be included in the ATC clearance?
items.explanation: Per 4-3-2c2, include the ODP when it has been published and pilot compliance is necessary to ensure separation.

choices.text: When an ODP has been published and pilot compliance with it is necessary to ensure separation.
Only when the pilot specifically requests the ODP, regardless of separation needs.
Only at airports without ATC service.
Only when a SID is unavailable because the aircraft is non-RNAV.

items.question_text: Under 4-3-2b2, for Air Force One operations, do not specify the destination airport.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 4-3-2b2.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 4-3-3, when may an abbreviated departure clearance be issued?
items.explanation: Per 4-3-3a, abbreviated clearance may be used when it reduces verbiage and the route has not been changed, all facilities have enough route information, destination relay requirements are met, and the assigned altitude is stated.

choices.text: When it reduces verbiage and the route has not been changed, all facilities have sufficient route information, required destination relay has occurred, and the assigned altitude is stated.
Whenever the pilot is familiar with the route, regardless of changes made before departure.

items.question_text: Under 4-3-3b, what does FRC mean on a flight progress strip?
items.explanation: Per 4-3-3b, FRC means Full Route Clearance Necessary.

choices.text: Full Route Clearance Necessary.
Further Route Coordination.
Filed Route Confirmed.
Flight Release Complete.

items.question_text: Under 4-3-3, SIDs are excluded from "cleared as filed" procedures.
items.explanation: That statement appears in Note 1 under 4-3-3f.

False

items.question_text: Under 4-3-9a, what must you do before authorizing an IFR flight planned aircraft to depart VFR when necessary?
items.explanation: Per 4-3-9a, obtain approval from the facility or sector responsible for issuing the IFR clearance if necessary.

choices.text: Obtain approval from the facility or sector responsible for issuing the IFR clearance if necessary.
Cancel the IFR flight plan before departure.
Issue the IFR clearance yourself regardless of sector responsibility.

items.question_text: Under 4-3-9b, if the responsible facility is unable to issue an IFR clearance and the pilot insists on departing VFR, what information should be passed if possible?

items.question_text: Under 4-3-9, when the IFR clearance cannot be issued, suggest that the delay be taken on the ground.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 4-3-9b.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 4-6-1b1, when may holding instructions be eliminated?
items.explanation: Per 4-6-1b1, holding instructions may be eliminated when you inform the pilot that no delay is expected.

choices.text: When you inform the pilot that no delay is expected.
Whenever the fix has a published hold, regardless of delay.

items.question_text: Under 4-6-1c, when should you omit the EFC item?

Whenever the fix has a charted holding pattern.

items.question_text: Under 4-6-1, when delay is expected, issue the clearance limit and holding items at least 5 minutes before the aircraft is estimated to reach the clearance limit, or immediately if it is less than 5 minutes from the fix.

items.question_text: Under 4-6-4, which item must be specified when issuing holding instructions if it is not already included as the clearance limit?

choices.text: The holding fix or waypoint.
The reason for the hold.

The maximum holding airspeed in every case.

items.question_text: Under 4-6-4e, when must you specify the direction of holding pattern turns?
items.explanation: Per 4-6-4e, specify turn direction only if left turns are to be made, the pilot requests it, or you consider it necessary.

Always, even for standard right turns.

items.question_text: Under 4-6-4f, maximum holding airspeed advisories are issued in specified circumstances such as when an aircraft is approved to exceed the normal maximum holding speed and the holding pattern airspace protects for that greater speed.
items.explanation: That statement reflects 4-6-4f1 through f3.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 4-7-13, when must facilities coordinate with other facilities that use fixes formed by reference to the affected NAVAIDs?

choices.text: When changing from one ILS to another at an airport with multiple systems that are not used simultaneously.

items.question_text: Under 4-7-13, with whom must you coordinate when switching from one ILS to another in this situation?
items.explanation: Per 4-7-13, coordinate with the facilities that use fixes formed by reference to those NAVAIDs.

items.question_text: Under 4-7-13, when changing from one ILS to another at airports with multiple systems not used simultaneously, coordinate with the facilities that use the fixes formed by reference to those NAVAIDs.

False

items.question_text: Under 4-2-9, before processing an airfile aircraft, what jurisdiction check must be satisfied unless otherwise coordinated?

items.question_text: If an airfile clearance under 4-2-9 is issued to the destination airport, what phraseology must be used?

choices.text: CLEARED TO (destination) AIRPORT.

CLEARED AS FILED TO THE AREA.

items.question_text: Under 4-2-9, if an airfile clearance is issued to a NAVAID, intersection, or waypoint and the type is known, the type must follow the name.

items.question_text: Under 4-4-2, which method may be used to effect a transition within or between route structures?
items.explanation: Per 4-4-2, transitions may be made by vectors to or from route radials/courses/azimuths, assigning a SID or STAR, direct-to or between route-defining NAVAIDs, and other listed methods.

choices.text: Assign a SID or STAR.
Clear the aircraft to any unpublished shortcut without reference to a route, fix, or RNAV transition method.
Require the pilot to cancel IFR and refile on the new route structure.
Use route changes only after the aircraft lands and departs again on a new flight plan.

items.question_text: When transitioning an RNAV aircraft between established ATS routes via a random RNAV route to a NAVAID, waypoint, airport, or fix on the new route, what additional service is required?
items.explanation: Per 4-4-2g, provide radar monitoring to aircraft transitioning via random RNAV routes.

choices.text: Provide radar monitoring.
Issue no additional service because RNAV aircraft do not require monitoring.
Transfer the aircraft to advisory frequency until it rejoins the ATS route.
Require the aircraft to remain on the original ATS route until landing.

items.question_text: Under 4-4-2, route structure transitions may be effected by vectoring aircraft to or from radials, courses, or azimuths of the ATS route assigned.
items.explanation: Per 4-4-2a, vectoring to or from route radials, courses, or azimuths is an authorized transition method.

items.question_text: Unless operational necessity dictates, under 4-4-6 when should you not issue a routing clearance that takes an aircraft off its flight plan route?
items.explanation: Per 4-4-6a, do not issue the off-route clearance if the aircraft is part of a known traffic management initiative or the contemplated route is within a protected segment, unless operational necessity dictates.

choices.text: When the aircraft is part of a known traffic management initiative or the route under consideration is within a protected segment.
Only when the aircraft is still on the ground awaiting departure release.
Only when the pilot objects to receiving any direct routing.
Only when weather is the sole reason for the route change.

items.question_text: Under 4-4-6b, when may an en route controller issue a revised routing that takes an aircraft off its flight plan route past the last fix in the facility's airspace?
items.explanation: Per 4-4-6b, do not issue that revised routing unless the pilot requests it or operational necessity dictates.

choices.text: Only when the pilot requests it or operational necessity dictates.
Whenever the amendment shortens the route, regardless of pilot request or necessity.
Only when the aircraft is equipped for RNAV direct routing.
Whenever another facility is expected to accept the amendment after the aircraft leaves your airspace.

items.question_text: If a flight routing within a protected segment is amended under 4-4-6, the required TMU/ATCSCC coordination must be accomplished.
items.explanation: Per 4-4-6a2, protected-segment amendments require the specified coordination.

items.question_text: Under 4-4-6b, an en route controller may routinely take an aircraft off its flight plan route past the last fix in the facility's airspace whenever the direct route looks shorter.
items.explanation: That is false. Per 4-4-6b, this may be done only when requested by the pilot or when operational necessity dictates.

items.question_text: Under 5-10-6, when should the pilot be advised to perform landing check during a normal pattern?
items.explanation: Per 5-10-6, advise the pilot while on downwind leg and in time to complete the check before turning base leg.

choices.text: While on downwind leg and in time to complete it before turning base leg.
After turning base leg, because the checklist must not distract the pilot on downwind.
Only after final approach is established.
Only after touchdown if the approach becomes a full-stop landing.

items.question_text: If an incomplete pattern is used, when must the controller issue the landing-check advisory under 5-10-6?
items.explanation: Per 5-10-6, issue it before handoff to the final controller for a PAR approach, or before starting descent on final approach for a surveillance approach.

choices.text: Before handoff to the final controller for a PAR approach, or before starting descent on final for a surveillance approach.
Only after the aircraft contacts the final controller, regardless of approach type.
Only after the aircraft reports the runway environment in sight.
Only when the pilot requests checklist reminder assistance.

items.question_text: Paragraph 5-10-6 applies to USA/USN aircraft for the landing-check advisory.
items.explanation: The paragraph begins USA/USN and directs controllers to advise the pilot to perform landing check.

items.question_text: Under 5-10-6, if an incomplete pattern is used for a surveillance approach, the landing-check advisory may be delayed until after starting descent on final approach.
items.explanation: That is false. Per 5-10-6, for a surveillance approach issue it before starting descent on final approach.

items.question_text: Under 5-10-10, when should the controller instruct an aircraft not to acknowledge further transmissions?
items.explanation: Per 5-10-10, this instruction is given after contact has been established with the final controller and while on the final approach course.

choices.text: After contact has been established with the final controller and while the aircraft is on the final approach course.
Before the aircraft is transferred to the final controller, while still on base leg.
Only after the aircraft has crossed the threshold.
Only if the pilot specifically requests no further transmissions.

items.question_text: Which phraseology matches 5-10-10?
items.explanation: Per 5-10-10, the phraseology is DO NOT ACKNOWLEDGE FURTHER TRANSMISSIONS.

choices.text: DO NOT ACKNOWLEDGE FURTHER TRANSMISSIONS.
DO NOT TRANSMIT UNTIL MISSED APPROACH.
ACKNOWLEDGE EACH FINAL APPROACH ADVISORY.
CHANGE TO TOWER AND REPORT RUNWAY IN SIGHT.

items.question_text: Under 5-10-10, the instruction not to acknowledge further transmissions applies after contact is established with the final controller and while the aircraft is on final approach course.
items.explanation: That is the exact condition stated in 5-10-10.

items.question_text: Complete the phraseology from 5-10-10: "DO NOT ACKNOWLEDGE FURTHER _____."
items.explanation: Per 5-10-10, the final word is TRANSMISSIONS.

choices.text: TRANSMISSIONS

items.question_text: Under 5-10-14, what action should be taken when the completion of a safe approach is questionable for one of the listed reasons and the runway environment is not in sight?
items.explanation: Per 5-10-14, instruct the aircraft to execute a missed approach if the runway environment is not in sight, or issue alternative climb and course instructions.

choices.text: Instruct the aircraft to execute a missed approach, or issue alternative climb and course instructions as appropriate.
Continue issuing minor corrections until the aircraft reaches decision height regardless of safety concerns.
Allow the pilot to continue without comment unless the aircraft has already begun a missed approach.
Transfer the aircraft to tower and let tower decide whether the approach remains safe.

items.question_text: Which statement about 5-10-14 and PAR decision height is correct?
items.explanation: Per 5-10-14, the conditions in subparagraphs a, b, and c do not apply after the aircraft passes decision height on a PAR approach.

choices.text: The conditions in subparagraphs a, b, and c do not apply after the aircraft passes decision height on a PAR approach.
All listed conditions continue to apply unchanged after decision height on a PAR approach.
Only airport conditions and traffic stop applying after decision height on a PAR approach.
Decision height is irrelevant to abnormality handling on a PAR approach.

items.question_text: Under 5-10-14, if airport conditions or traffic preclude approach completion, the controller may issue missed-approach or alternative instructions.
items.explanation: Per 5-10-14d, airport conditions or traffic that preclude approach completion are grounds for issuing those instructions.

items.question_text: Complete the phraseology from 5-10-14: "IF RUNWAY NOT IN SIGHT, EXECUTE MISSED _____."
items.explanation: Per 5-10-14, the phrase is EXECUTE MISSED APPROACH.

choices.text: APPROACH

items.question_text: Under 5-10-11, when must a controller issue a specific missed approach procedure approved for the radar approach being conducted?
items.explanation: Per 5-10-11, issue it before the aircraft starts final descent for a full stop landing when any portion of the final approach will be conducted in IFR conditions.

choices.text: Before the aircraft starts final descent for a full stop landing when any portion of final will be conducted in IFR conditions.
Only if the pilot later begins an actual missed approach.
After the aircraft reaches the missed approach point.
Only when the weather is VFR for the entire final approach segment.

items.question_text: Under the USAF note in 5-10-11, when may controllers omit issuing missed approach instructions?
items.explanation: Per Note 2 under 5-10-11, at locations where missed approach instructions are published in base flying regulations, controllers need not issue them to locally assigned or attached aircraft.

choices.text: At locations where they are published in base flying regulations, for locally assigned or attached aircraft.
At any military field, for all aircraft regardless of assignment status.
Whenever the aircraft is conducting a practice approach instead of a full stop landing.
Whenever the missed approach procedure appears on an instrument chart, for all pilots.

items.question_text: Under 5-10-11, the specific missed approach procedure is published on FAA Form 8260-4 or the applicable military document.
items.explanation: That statement appears in Note 1 under 5-10-11.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 5-10-12, when must the controller issue the departure instructions for a low approach or touch-and-go?
items.explanation: Per 5-10-12, issue the departure instructions before the aircraft begins final descent.

choices.text: Before the aircraft begins final descent.
After the aircraft completes the low approach or touch-and-go.
Only if the pilot requests departure instructions after touchdown.
Only after the aircraft is switched to tower frequency.

items.question_text: Under 5-10-12, what must climb-out instructions include unless the aircraft will maintain VFR and contact the tower?
items.explanation: Per 5-10-12, climb-out instructions include a specific heading and altitude except when the aircraft will maintain VFR and contact the tower.

choices.text: A specific heading and altitude.
A specific airspeed and route only.
Only a turn direction, with altitude left to pilot discretion.
Only a traffic pattern side and a radio frequency.

items.question_text: Under the note in 5-10-12, the departure instructions may be omitted after the first approach if they remain the same.
items.explanation: That note appears under 5-10-12.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 5-10-13, when an aircraft is on final to an airport served by a tower, what must be obtained and issued to the aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 5-10-13a, obtain a clearance to land, touch-and-go, or make low approach and issue that clearance along with the surface wind.

choices.text: A clearance to land, touch-and-go, or make low approach, and the surface wind.
Only the runway number, because the tower clearance is implied once the aircraft is on final.
Only the tower frequency, because the tower alone issues all landing-related information.
Only the missed approach instructions, because the landing clearance may be omitted until touchdown.

items.question_text: If the tower clearance is not obtained or is canceled under 5-10-13, what must the controller do?
items.explanation: Per 5-10-13b, inform the aircraft and issue alternative instructions.

choices.text: Inform the aircraft and issue alternative instructions.
Say nothing unless the pilot asks whether the clearance was received.
Allow the aircraft to continue because a towered-airport landing clearance is assumed on short final.
Transfer the aircraft to advisory frequency and let the pilot self-announce.

items.question_text: Under 5-10-13, the surface wind is issued with the landing, touch-and-go, or low-approach clearance.
items.explanation: Per 5-10-13a, issue the clearance and the surface wind to the aircraft.

items.question_text: Complete the phraseology from 5-10-13: "TOWER CLEARANCE _____/NOT RECEIVED (alternative instructions)."
items.explanation: Per 5-10-13, the phraseology is TOWER CLEARANCE CANCELED/NOT RECEIVED (alternative instructions).

choices.text: CANCELED

items.question_text: When an Approach Runway Verification alert is received under 5-14-9, what is the first basic controller responsibility?
items.explanation: Per 5-14-9a, evaluate the reason for the alert and take appropriate action as needed.

choices.text: Evaluate the reason for the alert and take appropriate action as needed.
Suppress the alert immediately to reduce display clutter.
Acknowledge the alert without review because acknowledgment only means it was seen.
Transfer the aircraft to another sector before deciding whether the alert is valid.

items.question_text: What does acknowledgment of an ARV alert signify under 5-14-9?
items.explanation: Per 5-14-9c, acknowledgment signifies that appropriate action has or will be taken.

choices.text: That appropriate action has or will be taken.
Only that the display has been silenced, with no operational implication.
That the alert was false and requires no further review.
That responsibility has been transferred automatically to the next control position.

items.question_text: Under 5-14-9, if another controller is involved in the ARV alert, initiate coordination as needed or as specified in a facility directive.
items.explanation: Per 5-14-9b, coordination is required as needed or by facility directive when another controller is involved.

items.question_text: Under 5-10-2, when the pilot states the appropriate ATIS broadcast code, what radar-approach information may be omitted?
items.explanation: Per 5-10-2a, current approach information contained in the ATIS broadcast may be omitted if the pilot states the appropriate ATIS code.

choices.text: Current approach information contained in the ATIS broadcast.
All radar-approach information, including special weather changes and lost communications procedures.
The missed approach point location before final approach.
The type of approach and runway assignment before starting final.

items.question_text: Under 5-10-2, before starting final approach on a surveillance approach, what must the controller tell the aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 5-10-2b, inform the aircraft of the type of approach and runway, airport, heliport, or other point, as appropriate, and for surveillance approaches specify the missed approach point location.

choices.text: The type of approach and runway, airport, heliport, or other point as appropriate, and the missed approach point location for a surveillance approach.
Only the altimeter setting, because the pilot can infer the rest from the vectors.
Only the airport name if it is the primary airport; runway and missed approach point are optional.
Only lost communications procedures, because the final controller will give the rest later.

items.question_text: Under 5-10-2, special weather observations need not be issued after they are included in the ATIS broadcast and the pilot states the appropriate ATIS code.
items.explanation: That statement appears in 5-10-2a3.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 5-10-4, who must issue the final approach lost communications instructions if they are changed, differ from those for the pattern, or were not issued by the pattern controller?
items.explanation: Per 5-10-4b, in those cases the final controller must issue the final approach lost communications instructions.

choices.text: The final controller.
The pattern controller only.
The tower controller after the aircraft reaches the runway threshold.
No one, because the original pattern instructions remain controlling.

items.question_text: Under 5-10-4, what should the controller do if the pilot says the lost communications procedure cannot be accepted because of weather conditions or other reasons?
items.explanation: Per 5-10-4c, request the pilot's intention.

choices.text: Request the pilot's intention.
Require the pilot to accept the procedure before the approach continues.
Cancel radar service immediately without further discussion.
Tell the pilot to continue visually regardless of conditions.

items.question_text: Under 5-10-4, if the lost communications instructions are the same for both pattern and final, the pattern/vector controller must issue both.
items.explanation: That statement appears in 5-10-4a.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 5-11-5, approximately how often should transmissions with aircraft on surveillance final approach occur?
items.explanation: Per 5-11-5a, transmissions with aircraft on surveillance final approach should occur approximately every 15 seconds.

choices.text: Approximately every 15 seconds.
Approximately every minute.
Only when the aircraft requests a correction.
Only at the final approach fix and again at decision altitude.

items.question_text: Under 5-11-5, when must recommended altitudes be furnished?
items.explanation: Per 5-11-5d, recommended altitudes must be furnished if requested, in accordance with 5-11-1.

choices.text: If requested, in accordance with paragraph 5-11-1.
Whenever the aircraft is more than five miles from the runway, whether or not the pilot asks.
Only on PAR approaches, because surveillance approaches do not use altitude recommendations.
Only after the pilot reports the runway in sight.

items.question_text: Under 5-11-5, trend information may be modified by the terms RAPIDLY and SLOWLY as appropriate.
items.explanation: That statement appears in 5-11-5b.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 5-6-2, what happens to published altitude and speed restrictions on a SID or STAR after a climb via or descend via clearance has been issued and the aircraft is vectored off the procedure?
items.explanation: Per the note under 5-6-2d, a vector or deviation off the SID or STAR lateral path cancels all published altitude and speed restrictions on the procedure.

choices.text: All published altitude and speed restrictions on the procedure are canceled.
Only the speed restrictions are canceled; altitude restrictions remain in effect automatically.
Only the altitude restrictions are canceled; speed restrictions remain in effect automatically.
No restrictions are canceled unless the pilot requests radar vectors.

items.question_text: Under 5-6-2, when an aircraft is instructed to resume a SID or STAR that contains published crossing restrictions, what must ATC do?
items.explanation: Per 5-6-2f, issue or reissue all applicable restrictions, or instruct the aircraft to Climb Via or Descend Via the SID or STAR.

choices.text: Issue or reissue all applicable restrictions, or instruct the aircraft to Climb Via or Descend Via the SID or STAR.
Simply say resume the procedure, because the published restrictions automatically reactivate.
Issue only the next crossing restriction and let the rest remain at pilot discretion.
Reclear the aircraft only to the next waypoint, because published restrictions may never be used again after vectors.

items.question_text: Under 5-6-2, aircraft may not be vectored off an Obstacle Departure Procedure until at or above the MVA or MIA.
items.explanation: That requirement appears in 5-6-2g.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 5-6-1, in controlled airspace for which purpose may aircraft be vectored?
items.explanation: Per 5-6-1a, aircraft may be vectored in controlled airspace for separation, safety, noise abatement, operational advantage, confidence maneuver, or when a pilot requests.

choices.text: For separation, safety, noise abatement, operational advantage, confidence maneuver, or when a pilot requests.
Only for separation and never for operational advantage or noise abatement.
Only when the aircraft is already on a published approach procedure.
Only when the pilot requests vectors, regardless of the airspace involved.

items.question_text: When may an aircraft be vectored in Class G airspace under 5-6-1?
items.explanation: Per 5-6-1b, Class G vectoring is provided only upon pilot request and as an additional service.

choices.text: Only upon pilot request and as an additional service.
Whenever the controller believes a vector would improve traffic flow.
Only when the aircraft is IFR, even if the pilot does not request it.
Never, because vectors are prohibited in Class G airspace.

items.question_text: Under 5-6-1, vector aircraft at or above the MVA or minimum IFR altitude except in the specific authorized situations listed in the paragraph.
items.explanation: Per 5-6-1c, vectors are issued at or above the MVA or minimum IFR altitude except as specifically authorized for listed operations and paragraphs.

items.question_text: Under 5-6-1, aircraft may be vectored outside your control jurisdiction without coordination as long as radar contact is maintained.
items.explanation: That is false. Per 5-6-1d, vector only in airspace for which you have control jurisdiction unless otherwise coordinated.

items.question_text: Under 5-6-3 terminal procedures, if the flight path is less than 3 miles from a prominent obstacle and the aircraft is climbing to an altitude at least 1,000 feet above the obstacle, what must ATC do?
items.explanation: Per 5-6-3a2, vector the aircraft to increase lateral separation until the 3-mile minimum is achieved or until the aircraft reports leaving an altitude above the obstacle.

choices.text: Vector the aircraft to increase lateral separation until 3 miles exists or until it reports leaving an altitude above the obstacle.
Maintain the aircraft at any distance from the obstacle as long as it is climbing.
Descend the aircraft until ISR appears in the full data block.
Transfer terrain responsibility to the pilot until the MVA is reached.

items.question_text: When may radar facilities vector aircraft below the MVA or MIA under 5-6-3a3?
items.explanation: Per 5-6-3a3, this is allowed when no prominent obstacles are within 10 NM of the DER and the aircraft is allowed an uninterrupted climb to meet the MVA or MIA within 10 NM of the DER.

choices.text: When no prominent obstacles are within 10 NM of the DER and the aircraft is allowed an uninterrupted climb to meet the MVA or MIA within 10 NM of the DER.
Any time the pilot requests a shortcut after departure.
Only after the aircraft is already above the MVA or MIA.
Whenever DVA and SID procedures are both in effect together.

items.question_text: Under the note in 5-6-3, when IFR aircraft below minimum IFR altitude are taken off departure or missed approach procedures, ATC assumes responsibility for terrain and obstacle avoidance except as stated for DVAs and SIDs.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in the note under 5-6-3.

items.question_text: Under 5-7-4, when should ATC advise an aircraft to 'resume normal speed'?
items.explanation: Per 5-7-4a, use 'resume normal speed' when ATC-assigned speed adjustments are no longer required and no published speed restrictions apply.

choices.text: When ATC-assigned speed adjustments are no longer required and no published speed restrictions apply.
Whenever any ATC-assigned speed ends, even if published speed restrictions remain ahead.
Only after the aircraft lands and clears the runway.
Only when the pilot specifically asks to accelerate.

items.question_text: When should ATC use the phraseology 'resume published speed' under 5-7-4?
items.explanation: Per 5-7-4c, use it when an aircraft has been assigned an unpublished speed and ATC wants the aircraft to comply with subsequent published speed restrictions on the route or procedure.

choices.text: When the aircraft was assigned an unpublished speed and ATC wants later published speed restrictions to apply again.
Whenever ATC wants all speed restrictions, published and unpublished, removed.
Only when the aircraft is transitioning from IFR to VFR.
Only after canceling the charted procedure itself.

items.question_text: Under the note in 5-7-4, 'resume normal speed' does not delete applicable published speed restrictions or 14 CFR 91.117 speed restrictions.
items.explanation: That note appears under 5-7-4a.

items.question_text: DELETE SPEED _____.
items.explanation: Per 5-7-4d, the phraseology is DELETE SPEED RESTRICTIONS.

choices.text: RESTRICTIONS

items.question_text: Under 5-8-2, when an initial heading will take the aircraft off a departure procedure that contains both a published lateral path to a waypoint and crossing restrictions, what must be issued with that heading?
items.explanation: Per 5-8-2c, issue an altitude to maintain with the initial heading in that case.

choices.text: An altitude to maintain.
Only the initial fix or waypoint name.
Only a departure frequency, because the crossing restrictions remain unchanged.
Nothing else, because the heading itself cancels the published restrictions automatically.

items.question_text: Under 5-8-2, what is required to cancel a previously assigned SID and subsequently utilize a DVA, or vice versa?
items.explanation: Per 5-8-2b, an amended departure clearance is required and must be provided in time for the pilot to brief the changes before entering the runway.

choices.text: An amended departure clearance issued in time for the pilot to brief the change before entering the runway.
A heading assignment after takeoff, because that cancels the SID or DVA automatically.
A verbal notice after runway entry, because the pilot does not need time to brief a DVA or SID change.
No amendment at all, because a SID and a DVA may be used together if the runway stays the same.

items.question_text: Under 5-8-2, concurrent use of both a SID and a DVA is not permitted.
items.explanation: That statement appears in 5-8-2a.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 5-9-7, what minimum separation must be provided between aircraft during turn-on to parallel final approach or until aircraft are established on an authorized EoR segment?
items.explanation: Per 5-9-7a1, provide a minimum of 1,000 feet vertical or 3 miles radar separation.

choices.text: A minimum of 1,000 feet vertical or 3 miles radar separation.
A minimum of 500 feet vertical or 2 miles radar separation.
A minimum of 1,000 feet vertical only; radar separation does not apply during turn-on to final.
Standard same-final separation only after both aircraft are established inside the FAF.

items.question_text: Under 5-9-7, what action is required when an aircraft is observed penetrating, or in your judgment will penetrate, the NTZ?
items.explanation: Per 5-9-7, issue the required breakout instructions immediately and, if necessary, alter the course of aircraft on the adjacent final approach course.

choices.text: Issue breakout instructions immediately and alter the course of aircraft on the adjacent final approach course as necessary.
Wait until the aircraft actually crosses through the NTZ centerline before issuing any breakout instruction.
Terminate radar monitoring and let tower resolve the conflict visually.
Continue the approach unless the aircraft on both finals are at the same altitude.

items.question_text: Under 5-9-7, the NTZ must be depicted on the monitor display.
items.explanation: That requirement appears in the simultaneous independent approach procedures in 5-9-7.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 5-9-9, when may the pilot of the trailing offset aircraft continue past the offset approach MAP?
items.explanation: Per the note in 5-9-9d, the pilot may continue past the offset approach MAP only if the straight-in traffic is in sight and expected to remain in sight, the pilot advises ATC that the traffic is in sight, and the runway environment is in sight.

choices.text: Only when the pilot has the straight-in traffic in sight and expects it to remain in sight, advises ATC that the traffic is in sight, and has the runway environment in sight.
Whenever the runway environment is in sight, even if the straight-in traffic is not.
Whenever the tower has taken radio communications, because that transfer authorizes continuation past the MAP.
Only after ATC applies visual separation between the aircraft under paragraph 7-2-1a1.

items.question_text: Under 5-9-9, what must the final monitor controller do if an aircraft is observed to overshoot the turn-on or continue on a track that will penetrate the NTZ?
items.explanation: Per 5-9-9c2, instruct the aircraft to return immediately to the correct final approach course.

choices.text: Instruct the aircraft to return immediately to the correct final approach course.
Wait until the aircraft reports the runway in sight before issuing corrective action.
Continue the approach because the pilot remains primarily responsible for navigation.
Transfer the aircraft to tower and let tower reestablish it on the final approach course.

items.question_text: Under 5-9-9, communications transfer to the tower controller's frequency must be completed prior to losing vertical separation between aircraft.
items.explanation: That note appears under 5-9-9a.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 3-10-12, when is the IFR flight plan canceled for an aircraft conducting an overhead maneuver?
items.explanation: Per 3-10-12d, an aircraft conducting an overhead maneuver is VFR and the IFR flight plan is canceled when the aircraft reaches the initial point on the initial approach portion of the maneuver.

choices.text: When the aircraft reaches the initial point on the initial approach portion of the overhead maneuver.
Only after the aircraft reports break.
Only after the aircraft receives landing clearance.
When the aircraft touches down on the runway.

items.question_text: Under 3-10-12, what may be required if an overhead maneuver cannot be approved?
items.explanation: Per 3-10-12d, the existence of a standard overhead maneuver pattern does not eliminate the possible requirement for an aircraft to conform to conventional rectangular patterns if an overhead maneuver cannot be approved.

choices.text: The aircraft may be required to conform to a conventional rectangular traffic pattern.
The aircraft must cancel the approach and divert immediately.
The aircraft may continue the overhead maneuver without regard to other traffic if it remains VFR.
The aircraft must be cleared for a straight-in landing only.

items.question_text: Under 3-10-12, aircraft operating to an airport without a functioning control tower must initiate cancellation of the IFR flight plan prior to executing the overhead maneuver or after landing.
items.explanation: That note appears under 3-10-12.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 3-11-2, what should be used whenever possible in lieu of extended hover-taxi or air-taxi operations?
items.explanation: Per 3-11-2a, whenever possible, issue takeoff clearance in lieu of extended hover-taxi or air-taxi operations.

choices.text: A takeoff clearance.
A sequence of hover-taxi instructions all the way to the departure route.
Only an air-taxi clearance.
A landing clearance followed by reposition instructions.

items.question_text: Under 3-11-2, when the requested departure point is a non-movement area, an unauthorized area, or off the airport and the operation appears reasonable, what phraseology is used instead of a takeoff clearance?
items.explanation: Per 3-11-2b, use the phraseology that departure from the requested location will be at the pilot's own risk, with additional instructions and caution as necessary.

choices.text: Departure from the requested location will be at your own risk, with any necessary additional instructions or caution.
Cleared for takeoff from the requested location, because a reasonable operation automatically authorizes a normal takeoff clearance.
Taxi to the nearest runway, because off-airport or non-movement departures may never be approved.
Proceed at your own discretion, because ATC does not issue any phraseology for such operations.

items.question_text: Under 3-11-2, unless agreed to by the pilot, do not issue downwind takeoffs if the tailwind exceeds 5 knots.
items.explanation: That statement appears in 3-11-2c.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 3-6-3, which of the following is an authorized use of ASDE system derived information?
items.explanation: Per 3-6-3a, ASDE system derived information may be used to formulate clearances and control instructions, position aircraft and vehicles, determine exact location or spatial relationship, monitor compliance, confirm reported positions, and provide directional taxi information.

choices.text: Providing directional taxi information, as appropriate.
Providing exact taxi headings as a matter of routine.
Delegating all visual separation responsibilities to the ASDE system.
Authorizing a departure roll despite an unidentified runway target if the display seems unreliable.

items.question_text: Under 3-6-3, when may specific navigational guidance in the form of exact headings be provided?
items.explanation: Per 3-6-3b, do not provide specific navigational guidance unless an emergency exists or by mutual agreement with the pilot.

choices.text: Only when an emergency exists or by mutual agreement with the pilot.
Whenever ASDE quality is good enough to depict the taxi route clearly.
Whenever the pilot requests the shortest taxi route across the movement area.
Only when visibility is below takeoff minimums, regardless of pilot agreement.

items.question_text: Under 3-6-3, do not allow an aircraft to begin departure roll or cross the landing threshold whenever there is an unidentified target or track displayed on the runway.
items.explanation: That requirement appears in 3-6-3c.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 5-11-3, what must be issued before final descent on a straight-in surveillance approach?
items.explanation: Per 5-11-3a, issue advance notice of where descent will begin and issue the straight-in MDA prior to issuing final descent for the approaches.

choices.text: Advance notice of where descent will begin and the straight-in minimum descent altitude.
Only the missed approach instructions, because the MDA is published on the chart.
Only the final approach fix crossing altitude, because MDA is given after descent begins.
The aircraft approach category, even for straight-in approaches.

items.question_text: Under 5-11-3, when a surveillance approach will terminate in a circle-to-land maneuver, what must the controller obtain before issuing final descent?
items.explanation: Per 5-11-3b, request the aircraft approach category from the pilot, then provide the applicable circling MDA before issuing final descent.

choices.text: The aircraft approach category, so the applicable circling MDA can be issued before final descent.
Only the runway in use, because circling MDA does not depend on aircraft category.
A pilot report of the airport in sight, because that replaces circling MDA issuance.

items.question_text: Under 5-11-3, the point at which descent to the minimum descent altitude is authorized is the final approach fix unless an altitude limiting stepdown-fix is prescribed.
items.explanation: That note appears under 5-11-3a.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 3-11-6, what should be used whenever possible in lieu of extended hover-taxi or air-taxi operations?
items.explanation: Per 3-11-6a, whenever possible, issue a landing clearance in lieu of extended hover-taxi or air-taxi operations.

choices.text: A landing clearance.
A sequence of hover-taxi instructions all the way to the touchdown point.
Only an air-taxi authorization.
A takeoff clearance followed by a landing clearance.

items.question_text: Under 3-11-6, when a landing is requested to a non-movement area, an unauthorized area, or an area off the airport and the operation appears reasonable, what phraseology is used instead of a landing clearance?
items.explanation: Per 3-11-6b, use the phraseology that landing at the requested location will be at the pilot's own risk, with additional instructions and caution as necessary.

choices.text: Landing at the requested location will be at your own risk, with any necessary additional instructions or caution.
Cleared to land at the requested location, because a reasonable request automatically authorizes a normal landing clearance.
Proceed visually to the requested location at pilot discretion, because ATC uses no landing phraseology there.
Taxi to a movement area first, because non-movement area landings may never be approved.

items.question_text: Under 3-11-6, unless agreed to by the pilot, do not issue downwind landings if the tailwind exceeds 5 knots.
items.explanation: That statement appears in 3-11-6c.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 3-10-13, which condition must exist before military aircraft may be authorized to make simulated flameout, emergency landing pattern, or practice precautionary approaches?
items.explanation: Per 3-10-13a, the operation requires the specified agreement or local operating procedure, required coordination and traffic information, and the applicable high-key or maneuvering altitude information before approval.

choices.text: The required agreement or local operating procedure must be in effect, with the required coordination and altitude information completed before approval.
The aircraft must already be on short final so that the tower can assess the approach visually before approval.
The pilot must waive all traffic information because SFO and ELP operations are treated as simulated emergencies.

items.question_text: Under 3-10-13, what must be obtained prior to approving the approach?
items.explanation: Per 3-10-13a3, the high-key altitude or practice precautionary approach maneuvering altitudes of the aircraft concerned are obtained prior to approval.

choices.text: The high-key altitude or practice precautionary approach maneuvering altitudes of the aircraft concerned.
The final descent clearance altitude only.
The aircraft's rectangular traffic pattern altitude instead of any high-key or maneuvering altitude.
Only the missed approach instructions, because high-key information is not needed until after entry.

items.question_text: Under 3-10-13, the high-key altitude or practice precautionary approach maneuvering altitudes of the aircraft concerned are obtained prior to approving the approach.
items.explanation: That requirement appears in 3-10-13a3.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Complete the phraseology from 3-10-13: "REPORT LOW _____"
items.explanation: Per 3-10-13: "REPORT LOW KEY."

choices.text: KEY

items.question_text: Under 3-3-7, when must the FFM remote sensing unit be operational?
items.explanation: Per 3-3-7d, the FFM remote sensing unit must be operational when the weather is below CAT II ILS minimums.

choices.text: When the weather is below CAT II ILS minimums.
Only when the weather is below CAT III minimums.
Only when any CAT I approach is in use.

items.question_text: Under 3-3-7, what must the controller do if the remote status unit alarm has not cleared before the aircraft reaches the middle marker or, if no middle marker exists, 1/2 mile final?
items.explanation: Per 3-3-7e1, immediately issue an advisory that the FFM remote status sensing unit indicates the localizer is unreliable.

choices.text: Immediately advise that the monitor indicates the localizer is unreliable.
Wait until touchdown because no advisory is required once the aircraft is established on the approach.
Cancel the landing clearance and issue a mandatory missed approach every time, even if the alarm clears before the middle marker.
Issue a glideslope advisory only, because the FFM alarm does not affect localizer use.

items.question_text: Under 3-3-7, once the aircraft has passed the inner marker or the CAT II missed approach point where no inner marker is installed, there is no controller action requirement for the FFM alarm.
items.explanation: That statement appears in 3-3-7e3.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 4-6-2, when no delay is expected, when should a clearance beyond the clearance limit be issued?
items.explanation: Per 4-6-2a, issue it as soon as possible and, whenever possible, at least 5 minutes before the aircraft reaches the fix.

choices.text: As soon as possible and, whenever possible, at least 5 minutes before the aircraft reaches the fix.
After the aircraft reaches the fix, because no expected delay means advance issuance is unnecessary.
Only when the pilot requests it after starting to hold.
At the estimated further clearance time, even if no delay is expected.

items.question_text: Under 4-6-2, which route phrase may be used only when the most recently issued routing to the new clearance limit remains valid and verbiage will be reduced?
items.explanation: Per 4-6-2b2(b), the phrase VIA LAST ROUTING CLEARED may be used only under those conditions.

choices.text: Via last routing cleared.
Hold as published.
Proceed direct when able.
Expect further clearance.

items.question_text: Under 4-6-2, if no holding pattern is charted and holding instructions have not been issued, pilots should ask ATC for holding instructions prior to reaching the fix.
items.explanation: That guidance appears in the note under 4-6-2.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 2-1-10, what should be done when a second aircraft confirms a ground-based NAVAID malfunction, or no second aircraft report is available?
items.explanation: Per 2-1-10a, activate the standby equipment or request the monitor facility to activate it. If it then operates normally, continue use, record the incident, and notify Technical Operations.

choices.text: Activate the standby equipment or request the monitor facility to activate it, then continue use only if it operates normally.
Continue using the primary NAVAID without any change until three aircraft confirm the malfunction.
Cancel all approaches immediately and wait for pilots to troubleshoot the NAVAID on frequency.
Issue a NOTAM first and then take no further action until Technical Operations arrives on site.

items.question_text: When a GPS or WAAS anomaly is reported under 2-1-10, what minimum information must be recorded?
items.explanation: Per 2-1-10b, record the aircraft make/model/call sign, location and altitude, and the date and time of the anomaly.

choices.text: Aircraft make/model/call sign, location and altitude, and the date and time of the anomaly.
Only the aircraft call sign and route of flight, because altitude and time are optional.
The pilot's name, aircraft color, and last maintenance inspection date.
Only the NAVAID identifier and the name of the controller receiving the report.

items.question_text: Under 2-1-10, when a GPS or WAAS anomaly is reported, controllers should inform other aircraft of the anomaly in the affected area.
items.explanation: That requirement appears in 2-1-10b4 and the related phraseology in 2-1-10b5.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 5-11-2, which visual reference may a controller request an aircraft to report in sight?
items.explanation: Per 5-11-2a, the controller may request the runway, approach/runway lights, or the airport in sight.

choices.text: The runway, approach/runway lights, or the airport.
Only the final approach fix, because visual references other than navaids are not requested.
Only the rotating beacon, because runway and airport reports are implied.

items.question_text: What may a helicopter or powered-lift aircraft on a point-in-space approach be requested to report under 5-11-2?
items.explanation: Per 5-11-2b, the request is to report when able to proceed visually to the airport, heliport, or vertiport.

choices.text: When able to proceed visually to the airport, heliport, or vertiport.
When able to descend below decision altitude without any outside visual reference.
When able to accept a contact approach to any nearby landing site.
When able to continue the approach solely by reference to the moving map display.

items.question_text: Under 5-11-2, controllers may request an aircraft to report the runway, the approach or runway lights, or the airport in sight.
items.explanation: That authority appears in 5-11-2a.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: How may uncertified tower display workstations be used under 3-1-9?
items.explanation: Per 3-1-9a, they may be used only as an aid for visually locating aircraft or determining their spatial relationship and may not be used to provide radar services or traffic advisories.

choices.text: Only as an aid to visually locate aircraft or determine spatial relationship; not to provide radar services or traffic advisories.
To provide full radar services whenever the local controller has visual contact with the aircraft.
To provide vectors to any IFR or VFR aircraft if the display target quality appears acceptable.

items.question_text: Under 3-1-9, what may a certified tower radar display be used to provide to VFR aircraft as an advisory aid to navigation?
items.explanation: Per 3-1-9c, certified displays may be used to provide directions or suggested headings to VFR aircraft as an advisory aid to navigation.

choices.text: Directions or suggested headings to VFR aircraft as an advisory aid to navigation.

items.question_text: Under 3-1-9, pilots should not be advised that they are in radar contact when tower radar displays are being used in this way.
items.explanation: The note under 3-1-9 states that pilots should not be advised of being in radar contact when tower displays are used under this paragraph.

False

items.question_text: How often should transmissions occur on precision final under 5-12-4?

items.explanation: Per 5-12-4, trend information may be modified by the words RAPIDLY and SLOWLY.

choices.text: RAPIDLY and SLOWLY.
IMMEDIATELY and CAUTION.
URGENTLY and SLIGHTLY.

items.explanation: That requirement appears in 5-12-4a through 5-12-4c.

False

items.question_text: Under 3-10-11, what governs whether pilot requests to remain in closed traffic are approved or disapproved?

choices.text: Local traffic conditions.

items.question_text: Under 3-10-11, how may segregated traffic patterns for helicopters and powered-lift aircraft be established?

choices.text: By letter of agreement or other local operating procedures.
Only by national rulemaking published in the FARs.

items.question_text: Under 3-10-11, controllers may approve or disapprove pilot requests to remain in closed traffic for successive operations subject to local traffic conditions.

items.question_text: Under 5-9-3, what must the controller do whenever a vector will take an aircraft across the final approach course?

items.question_text: Under 2-1-27, when should a pilot be notified of a possible pilot deviation?

choices.text: Advise you contact the facility at the specified telephone number.

items.question_text: When not being radar monitored, what route must GNSS-equipped RNAV aircraft on random RNAV routes be cleared via or reported established on under 4-4-1?

items.question_text: When applying nonradar separation on a random RNAV point-to-point route under 4-4-1, what is the maximum distance between points?

items.question_text: Under 2-5-2, when a NAVAID is used as a clearance limit, how should it be stated?

items.question_text: Under 4-5-9, when should a pilot be asked to confirm assigned altitude on initial contact and when position reports are received?

items.question_text: When may landing clearance to a succeeding aircraft in a landing sequence be issued without waiting under 3-10-6?

items.question_text: When should traffic information be issued to the succeeding aircraft under 3-10-6?

items.question_text: Under 3-10-7, what must be included as part of the landing clearance when the arriving aircraft reports at a position where it should be seen but has not been visually observed?

items.question_text: Under 3-9-2, when gate hold procedures are in effect, what information may be issued regarding engine startup?

items.question_text: Under 4-2-10, what should a controller do before instructing an IFR aircraft arriving at an untowered airport to change to the common traffic advisory frequency, if necessary?

items.question_text: Under 5-10-3, what must be done before issuing a vector during a no-gyro surveillance or PAR approach?

items.question_text: Under 5-10-8, what must be included when instructing the aircraft to change frequency for final approach guidance?

items.question_text: Under 5-11-4, what should be issued when an aircraft reaches the descent point and no descent restriction exists?
items.explanation: Per 5-11-4a, advise the aircraft to descend to the minimum descent altitude.

choices.text: Descend to the minimum descent altitude.
