choices.text: ALTITUDE

items.question_text: If the elevation portion of PAR equipment fails during a precision approach under 5-12-10, what must the controller do first?
items.explanation: Per 5-12-10a1, discontinue PAR instructions and tell the aircraft to take over visually or, if unable, execute a missed approach.

choices.text: Discontinue PAR instructions and tell the aircraft to take over visually or, if unable, execute a missed approach.
Continue PAR instructions using only azimuth corrections with no advisory to the pilot.
Immediately clear the aircraft to land because elevation failure removes the need for further talkdown guidance.
Transfer the aircraft to tower and stop monitoring the approach frequency.

items.question_text: When a surveillance approach can be given to the same runway after an elevation failure under 5-12-10, what must be said when PAR azimuth is used?
items.explanation: Per 5-12-10a2, inform the pilot that mileage information will be from touchdown, and where applicable, that PAR azimuth will be used for the approach.

choices.text: Inform the pilot that mileage will be from touchdown and, where applicable, that PAR azimuth will be used for the approach.
Inform the pilot that all mileages will be from the outer marker and no descent instructions will be issued.
Inform the pilot that the missed approach point has moved to the runway threshold.
Inform the pilot that glidepath information will resume inside the final approach fix.

items.question_text: Complete the phraseology from 5-12-10: "NO _____ INFORMATION AVAILABLE."
items.explanation: Per 5-12-10, the phraseology is NO GLIDEPATH INFORMATION AVAILABLE.

choices.text: GLIDEPATH

items.question_text: Under 5-12-8, which of the following is one condition that requires discontinuing precision approach guidance?
items.explanation: Per 5-12-8a, guidance is discontinued when requested by the pilot, when continuation of a safe approach is questionable, when the aircraft passes the landing threshold, or when the pilot reports runway or approach lights in sight and requests to or advises the intention to proceed visually.

choices.text: Continuation of a safe approach to the landing threshold is questionable.
The pilot reports runway in sight but does not request to proceed visually.
The aircraft reaches two miles from touchdown with no abnormalities.
The tower issues landing clearance while the aircraft is still on PAR frequency.

items.question_text: After precision approach guidance is discontinued under 5-12-8, what should the controller advise?
items.explanation: Per 5-12-8b, advise the aircraft of its position and to proceed visually, with additional instructions or clearance as required.

choices.text: Its position and to proceed visually, with additional instructions or clearance as required.
Only the runway number, because position is no longer relevant once PAR guidance stops.
Only to change to tower frequency immediately and disregard visual references.
Only the missed approach instructions, regardless of the pilot's intentions.

items.question_text: Under 5-12-8, a pilot's report of runway in sight or visual is automatically a request to proceed visually.
items.explanation: The note under 5-12-8 states that a pilot's report of runway in sight or visual is not a request to proceed visually.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Complete the phraseology from 5-12-8: "(Distance) MILES FROM TOUCHDOWN, PROCEED _____."
items.explanation: Per 5-12-8, the phraseology is (Distance) MILE(S) FROM TOUCHDOWN, PROCEED VISUALLY.

choices.text: VISUALLY

items.question_text: Under 5-11-6, when must surveillance approach guidance be discontinued?
items.explanation: Per 5-11-6a, discontinue guidance when requested by the pilot, when continuation of a safe approach to the MAP is questionable, or when the aircraft is over the MAP.

choices.text: When requested by the pilot, when continuation of a safe approach to the MAP is questionable, or when the aircraft is over the MAP.
Only after the pilot reports the runway in sight and accepts visual separation.
Only when the aircraft is outside the final approach fix and asks for another approach.
Whenever the aircraft is within 2 miles of touchdown, regardless of conditions.

items.question_text: If surveillance approach guidance is discontinued and the aircraft has reported the runway or approach lights in sight, what should ATC advise?
items.explanation: Per 5-11-6c, advise the aircraft of its position and to proceed visually.

choices.text: Advise the aircraft of its position and to proceed visually.
Continue surveillance guidance to touchdown but switch the pilot to tower frequency immediately.
Instruct the aircraft to maintain present heading until landing is assured.
Tell the pilot to circle the airport visually and re-intercept final without further clearance.

items.question_text: If guidance is discontinued and the runway or approach lights are not in sight, ATC should advise position and instruct execution of the missed approach unless the pilot can proceed visually under the stated conditions.
items.explanation: Per 5-11-6d, that is the required action when visual reference has not been reported.

items.question_text: IF RUNWAY OR APPROACH/RUNWAY LIGHTS NOT IN SIGHT, EXECUTE _____ APPROACH.
items.explanation: Per 5-11-6d, the phraseology is EXECUTE MISSED APPROACH.

choices.text: MISSED

items.question_text: Under 5-9-1, where should arriving aircraft normally be vectored to intercept the final approach course?
items.explanation: Per 5-9-1a, aircraft are normally vectored to intercept at least 2 miles outside the approach gate.

choices.text: At least 2 miles outside the approach gate.
At or inside the final approach fix unless the pilot objects.
Exactly at the approach gate in all cases.
Only after the aircraft reports the runway in sight.

items.question_text: When weather is reported at least 500 feet above the MVA or MIA and visibility is at least 3 miles, how close may aircraft be vectored to intercept the final approach course under 5-9-1?
items.explanation: Per 5-9-1a1, aircraft may be vectored closer than 2 miles outside the approach gate, but no closer than the approach gate.

choices.text: Closer than 2 miles outside the approach gate, but no closer than the approach gate.
Anywhere inside the approach gate as long as the airport is VFR.
Directly to the runway threshold if the pilot accepts the approach.
Only outside 5 miles from the approach gate because weather does not change the intercept rule.

items.question_text: If specifically requested by the pilot, aircraft may be vectored to intercept the final approach course inside the approach gate, but no closer than the final approach fix.
items.explanation: Per 5-9-1a2, that is the pilot-request exception.

items.question_text: Under 5-9-2, what is the maximum final approach course interception angle when the interception point is less than 2 miles from the approach gate or triple simultaneous approaches are in use?
items.explanation: Per Table 5-9-1, the maximum angle is 20 degrees in that case.

choices.text: 20 degrees.
25 degrees.
30 degrees.
45 degrees for all aircraft.

items.question_text: When the interception point is 2 miles or more from the approach gate, what is the maximum final approach course interception angle for most aircraft?
items.explanation: Per Table 5-9-1, the maximum is 30 degrees, with 45 degrees allowed for helicopters.

choices.text: 30 degrees.
20 degrees.
35 degrees.
60 degrees.

items.question_text: If deviations from final approach course are observed inside the approach gate after interception, ATC should inform the pilot of observed position and ask intentions.
items.explanation: Per 5-9-2b2, that is the required inside-the-gate response.

items.question_text: (Ident) (distance) MILE(S) FROM THE AIRPORT, (distance) MILE(S) RIGHT/LEFT OF COURSE, SAY _____.
items.explanation: Per 5-9-2b2, the phraseology ends with SAY INTENTIONS.

choices.text: INTENTIONS

items.question_text: When an aircraft reports it is responding to a TAWS alert, what must ATC avoid issuing?
items.explanation: Per 2-1-30a, controllers must not issue control instructions contrary to the TAWS procedure the crew advises it is executing.

choices.text: Control instructions contrary to the TAWS procedure the crew is executing.
Any traffic advisories until the aircraft returns to assigned altitude.
Any altitude assignment at all, even after the maneuver is complete.
Communications with other aircraft in the sector.

items.question_text: When does controller responsibility for approved separation resume after a TAWS maneuver has begun?
items.explanation: Per 2-1-30b, responsibility resumes after return to assigned altitude, after the crew reports the maneuver complete and approved separation is observed reestablished, or after an alternate clearance is executed and approved separation is observed reestablished.

choices.text: When one of the listed restoration conditions is met and approved separation has been observed reestablished where required.
As soon as the controller stops issuing safety alerts.
Only after the aircraft lands.
Immediately after the TAWS aural warning ends in the cockpit.

items.question_text: Once an aircraft has begun a TAWS maneuver, the controller remains responsible for approved separation between that aircraft and terrain or obstructions throughout the maneuver.
items.explanation: Per 2-1-30b, controller responsibility for approved separation does not continue during the maneuver; it resumes only after one of the listed conditions is met.

items.question_text: Under 2-1-5, when should the word "immediately" be used?
items.explanation: Per 2-1-5a, use 'immediately' only when expeditious compliance is required to avoid an imminent situation.

choices.text: Only when expeditious compliance is required to avoid an imminent situation.
Whenever ATC wants prompt action, even if no imminent situation exists.
Only for headings and never for altitude changes.
Whenever a pilot requests priority handling.

items.question_text: Under 2-1-5, when should the word "expedite" be used?
items.explanation: Per 2-1-5b, use 'expedite' only when prompt compliance is required to avoid the development of an imminent situation.

choices.text: Only when prompt compliance is required to avoid the development of an imminent situation.
Any time ATC wants a pilot to read back quickly.
Only when the aircraft is already in an actual collision hazard.
As a synonym for 'immediately' in all situations.

items.question_text: If ATC issues an expedite climb or descent and later changes or restates the altitude without saying expedite again, the expedite instruction is canceled.
items.explanation: Per 2-1-5b, restating or changing the altitude without an expedite instruction cancels the expedite instruction.

items.question_text: Under 2-10-1, which en route sector team position is in direct communication with aircraft and uses radar information as the primary means of separation?
items.explanation: Per 2-10-1b2, that is the Radar Position (R).

choices.text: Radar Position (R).
Radar Coordinator Position (RC).
Radar Flight Data (FD).
Radar Associate (RA).

items.question_text: When the en route Radar Coordinator Position is staffed, what responsibility does the Radar Position retain?
items.explanation: Per the note in 2-10-1c3, the Radar Position retains responsibility for aircraft separation.

choices.text: Aircraft separation.
Only interphone operation.
Weather-message dissemination only.
No responsibility; the RC assumes all sector functions.

items.question_text: The en route sector team as a whole is responsible for the safe and efficient operation of the sector, regardless of whether one, two, or three people are working the positions.
items.explanation: Per 2-10-1a, there are no absolute divisions of responsibility and the team as a whole owns safe and efficient sector operation.

items.question_text: Under 2-10-2, what is the terminal Radar Associate Position commonly referred to as?
items.explanation: Per 2-10-2b3, the Radar Associate is commonly referred to as the Handoff Controller or Radar Data Controller.

choices.text: Handoff Controller or Radar Data Controller.
Local Controller or Ground Controller.
Flight Data or Clearance Delivery.
Nonradar Coordinator only.

items.question_text: When the terminal Radar Associate accepts or initiates an automated handoff to keep traffic flowing, what must also happen?
items.explanation: Per 2-10-2c2f, the Radar Position must be made immediately aware of any action taken.

choices.text: The Radar Position must be made immediately aware of the action taken.
The action must be held until the pilot requests it on frequency.
The Radar Coordinator automatically assumes separation responsibility.
The automated handoff must be canceled and converted to a nonautomated handoff.

items.question_text: In terminal facilities, the radar team as a whole is responsible for the safe and efficient operation of the facility or sector, regardless of whether one, two, or three people are working positions.
items.explanation: Per 2-10-2a, that is the stated team concept and intent.

items.question_text: Under 2-10-3, which tower team position is commonly referred to as "Clearance"?
items.explanation: Per 2-10-3b6, Clearance Delivery (CD) is commonly referred to as Clearance.

choices.text: Clearance Delivery (CD).
Tower Cab Coordinator Position (CC).
Tower Associate Position.
Flight Data (FD).

items.question_text: When the Tower Coordinator Position is staffed, what responsibility do the Tower Positions retain?
items.explanation: Per the note in 2-10-3c3, the Tower Positions retain responsibility for aircraft separation.

choices.text: Aircraft separation.
Only statistical-data compilation.
Only weather observation and reporting.
No responsibility; the coordinator assumes all tower duties.

items.question_text: The Tower Coordinator Position may manage traffic flows when staffed, but the Tower Positions retain responsibility for aircraft separation.
items.explanation: Per 2-10-3, that is the division of responsibilities when the coordinator position is staffed.

items.question_text: Under 2-3-10, where may locally approved identifiers be used?
items.explanation: Per 2-3-10b, locally approved identifiers may be used only within the facility and not on teletypewriter or interphone circuits.

choices.text: Only within the facility, and not on teletypewriter or interphone circuits.
On any interphone circuit if all controllers involved understand the identifier.
Only on teletypewriter circuits, but not inside the facility.
Anywhere, as long as the strip is updated later.

items.question_text: When flight progress strips are not used, what may be used to record information under 2-3-10?
items.explanation: Per 2-3-10c, plain sheets of paper or locally prepared forms may be used when flight progress strips are not used.

choices.text: Plain sheets of paper or locally prepared forms.
Only memory, provided the controller updates the data block later.
Any personal notebook, even if it is not locally approved.
Only plain-language voice coordination with no written record.

items.question_text: Control status of aircraft must always be current.
items.explanation: Per 2-3-10, control status of aircraft must always be current.

items.question_text: Under 2-3-2, what information is recorded in block 27 on an en route flight progress strip?
items.explanation: Per Table 2-3-1, block 27 contains the Mode 3/A beacon code if applicable.

choices.text: Mode 3/A beacon code, if applicable.
Pilot-estimated time over next fix.
Previous fix.
Requested altitude only.

items.question_text: Under 2-3-2, when may latitude/longitude coordinates be substituted for nonadapted NAVAIDs in space 25 of a domestic en route strip?
items.explanation: Per 2-3-2b, this may be done when necessary to accommodate a random RNAV or GNSS route request.

choices.text: When necessary to accommodate a random RNAV or GNSS route request.
Only when the aircraft is operating outside controlled airspace.
Only when the voice recorder is inoperative.
Whenever any controller prefers latitude/longitude over fix names.

items.question_text: Facility air traffic managers may authorize optional use of spaces 13, 14, 14a, 22, 23, 24, and 28 for point out, radar vector, speed adjustment, or transfer-of-control data.
items.explanation: Per 2-3-2c, those spaces may be optionally authorized for that information.

items.question_text: Under 2-3-3, what does ATOP display during normal operations and print during a catastrophic system failure?
items.explanation: Per 2-3-3a, ATOP displays information on electronic flight progress strips and prints flight progress strips with corresponding numbered spaces in a catastrophic failure.

choices.text: Information on flight progress strips in the corresponding numbered spaces.
Only beacon codes and annotations, with all other fields left blank.
Only controller free-text notes, because structured fields are not printed during failures.
Nothing; ATOP cannot produce strip information after a catastrophic failure.

items.question_text: Which field on the oceanic strip contains annotations under 2-3-3?
items.explanation: Per Table 2-3-2, field 22 is for annotations.

choices.text: Field 22.
Field 4.
Field 8.
Field 24.

items.question_text: Standard annotations and abbreviations for Field 22 may be specified by facility directives.
items.explanation: Per 2-3-3b, facility directives may specify standard annotations and abbreviations for Field 22.

items.question_text: Under 2-3-4, who may authorize omissions or optional use of specified terminal strip spaces when no misunderstanding will result?
items.explanation: Per 2-3-4a through c, facility managers may authorize these omissions or optional uses.

choices.text: Facility managers, through a facility directive.
Any controller in charge of the position at the moment.
Only the pilot in command.
Only regional offices through a national waiver.

items.question_text: At automated terminal radar facilities, air traffic managers may waive the requirement to use flight progress strips only if backup systems are utilized, local procedures are documented in a facility directive, and the other listed conditions are met.
items.explanation: Per 2-3-4d, the waiver is conditional and requires backup systems, documented procedures, no misunderstanding, unused strips or forms available, and reversion to strip usage if backups are unavailable.

choices.text: That is correct; all of those listed conditions must be met.
No, strips may be waived whenever the local controller prefers electronic data only.
No, the only requirement is that arrivals are standardized.
No, once strips are waived a facility never needs to revert to strip usage.

items.question_text: If the backup systems referred to in 2-3-4d1 are not available, the facility must revert to flight progress strip usage.
items.explanation: Per 2-3-4d5, the facility must revert to strip usage when those backup systems are not available.

items.question_text: Under 2-4-14, how must the word heavy be used in communications with or about heavy aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 2-4-14c, the word heavy must be used as part of the identification in all communications with or about heavy aircraft, subject to the listed en route omission exceptions.

choices.text: As part of the aircraft identification.
Only in the initial contact and never afterward.
Only when speaking directly to the pilot, never when coordinating with another facility.
Only when the aircraft requests wake turbulence separation.

items.question_text: When communicating with Air Force One or Air Force Two, should ATC add the heavy designator to the call sign?
items.explanation: Per 2-4-14e, do not add the heavy designator; state only Air Force One or Air Force Two.

choices.text: No. State only Air Force One or Air Force Two.
Yes. Always add heavy for those call signs if the aircraft qualifies.
Yes, but only on interphone communications.
No. Replace heavy with super regardless of aircraft type.

items.question_text: En route controllers may omit the words super or heavy in all communications, including communications with terminal facilities and traffic advisories.
items.explanation: Per 2-4-14d, en route omission is limited and does not apply in all communications.

items.question_text: Under 2-6-2, when must controllers solicit PIREPs?
items.explanation: Per 2-6-2a, solicit PIREPs when requested, when deemed necessary, or when listed conditions exist or are forecast for the area of jurisdiction.

choices.text: When requested, when deemed necessary, or when the listed conditions exist or are forecast for the area of jurisdiction.
Only after an urgent PIREP has already been received from another aircraft.
Only during severe thunderstorm activity.
Only when a weather forecaster specifically requests one over the hotline.

items.question_text: How must a low-level wind shear PIREP be classified if the pilot reports airspeed fluctuations of 10 knots or more?
items.explanation: Per 2-6-2b1e, low-level wind shear with airspeed fluctuations of 10 knots or more is classified as an urgent PIREP (UUA).

choices.text: Urgent (UUA).
Routine (UA).
SIGMET only, not a PIREP.
Pilot advisory only, not for dissemination.

items.question_text: Operationally significant terminal PIREPs must be relayed to appropriate intrafacility positions and for long-line dissemination by the listed channels.
items.explanation: Per 2-6-2e3, terminal controllers relay operationally significant PIREPs to intrafacility positions and the proper long-line dissemination point.

items.question_text: REQUEST/SAY FLIGHT _____.
items.explanation: Per 2-6-2d, the solicitation phraseology is REQUEST/SAY FLIGHT CONDITIONS.

choices.text: CONDITIONS

items.question_text: Under 2-6-5, when may specific weather values such as ceiling and visibility be transmitted?
items.explanation: Per 2-6-5b, specific values may be transmitted only if obtained by one of the listed approved means.

choices.text: Only when obtained by one of the listed approved means, such as from the official observer, an approved automation system, or a properly certificated observer.
Whenever the controller can see the conditions clearly from the tower cab.
Only when an airline pilot requests the values twice.
Only when the values are improving rather than worsening.

items.question_text: What must be done if tower-observed weather elements differ from those reported by the weather station?
items.explanation: Per 2-6-5c, the difference must be reported to the official observer for the element concerned.

choices.text: Report the difference to the official observer for the element concerned.
Issue the tower observation as the controlling official weather with no further coordination.
Ignore the difference if pilots have not complained.
Stop disseminating any weather until the next METAR is issued.

items.question_text: General weather information that does not include specific values may be transmitted to pilots or other ATC facilities without consulting the weather reporting station.
items.explanation: Per 2-6-5a, general weather information without specific values may be transmitted without consulting the weather reporting station.

items.question_text: Under 2-8-2, when must current touchdown RVR for the runway in use be issued even if prevailing visibility is more than 1 mile?
items.explanation: Per 2-8-2a2, touchdown RVR must be issued when RVR indicates a reportable value regardless of prevailing visibility.

choices.text: When RVR indicates a reportable value.
Only when prevailing visibility is 1 mile or less.
Only when midpoint RVR is unavailable.
Only when requested by the pilot after landing clearance is issued.

items.question_text: When must both midpoint and rollout RVR be issued?
items.explanation: Per 2-8-2b, issue both when either midpoint or rollout is less than 2,000 feet and touchdown RVR is greater than the midpoint or rollout value.

choices.text: When either midpoint or rollout is below 2,000 feet and touchdown RVR is greater than that value.
Only when touchdown RVR is also below 1,200 feet.
Whenever all three RVR sensors are reporting any value at all.
Only for arrivals, never for departures.

items.question_text: If a reliable source determines that indicated RVR differs by more than 400 feet from actual conditions within the transmissometer area, the RVR data must not be reported.
items.explanation: Per 2-8-2a3, such RVR data is not acceptable and must not be reported.

items.question_text: Under 3-12-2, what minimum distance is required when only Category I aircraft are involved and distance is determined by suitable landmarks?
items.explanation: Per 3-12-2a1, the minimum distance is 1,500 feet.

choices.text: 1,500 feet.
2,000 feet.
3,000 feet.
6,000 feet.

items.question_text: What minimum distance is required when either aircraft in the sea-lane departure sequence is a Category III aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 3-12-2a4, the minimum distance is 6,000 feet.

choices.text: 6,000 feet.
3,000 feet.
1,500 feet.
8,000 feet.

items.question_text: A departing aircraft may be cleared to take off from a sea lane even if a preceding landing aircraft has not yet taxied out of the sea lane, provided the controller judges there is enough room.
items.explanation: Per 3-12-2b, the preceding landing aircraft must have taxied out of the sea lane.

items.question_text: Under 3-12-3, what minimum distance from the landing threshold is required when a Category I aircraft is landing behind a Category I or II aircraft and landmark distance is being used?
items.explanation: Per 3-12-3a1, the minimum is 2,000 feet.

choices.text: 2,000 feet.
2,500 feet.
3,000 feet.
6,000 feet.

items.question_text: What minimum distance from the landing threshold is required when a Category II aircraft is landing behind a Category I or II aircraft and landmark distance is being used?
items.explanation: Per 3-12-3a2, the minimum is 2,500 feet.

choices.text: 2,500 feet.
2,000 feet.
3,500 feet.
6,000 feet.

items.question_text: If a preceding departure is airborne but has not crossed the end of the sea lane, an arriving aircraft may still be allowed to cross the landing threshold when the required landmark distance exists.
items.explanation: Per 3-12-3b, the preceding departure need not have crossed the end of the sea lane if the required airborne distance from the landing threshold exists.

items.question_text: Under 3-3-3, which of the following is specifically listed as airport condition information that should be issued as appropriate?
items.explanation: Per 3-3-3, construction on or adjacent to the movement area is one of the listed items.

choices.text: Construction work on or immediately adjacent to the movement area.
Preferred taxi routing for airline gates.
Daily staffing levels in the tower cab.
The facility's training schedule.

items.question_text: What volcanic-ash information is specifically listed in 3-3-3g?
items.explanation: Per 3-3-3g, controllers issue information about volcanic ash on airport surface areas and whether the ash is wet or dry if known.

choices.text: Volcanic ash on any airport surface area and whether the ash is wet or dry, if known.
Only whether volcanic ash is visible at cruise altitude.
Only whether the weather office has issued a SIGMET for ash.
Only whether ash has been reported by maintenance vehicles, not by pilots.

items.question_text: Airport condition information should be issued in time for it to be useful to the pilot.
items.explanation: Per 3-3-3, airport condition information necessary for safe operation is issued in time for it to be useful.

items.question_text: Under 3-4-10, when must runway edge lights be turned on for departures between sunset and sunrise?
items.explanation: Per 3-4-10a1, for departures the lights are turned on before an aircraft taxies onto the runway.

choices.text: Before an aircraft taxies onto the runway.
Only after the aircraft begins takeoff roll.
After the aircraft is airborne.
Only when the pilot specifically requests them.

items.question_text: When may runway edge lights be left off even if the runway would otherwise be in use?
items.explanation: Per 3-4-10e, runway edge lights are not turned on when a NOTAM closing the runway is in effect.

choices.text: When a NOTAM closing the runway is in effect.
Whenever visibility is more than 2 miles, even at night.
Whenever a pilot says the approach lights are sufficient.
Whenever the runway is used by IFR aircraft only.

items.question_text: Between sunrise and sunset, runway edge lights are turned on as specified when the surface visibility is less than 2 miles.
items.explanation: Per 3-4-10b, that is the daytime visibility threshold.

items.question_text: If a pilot reports part of the RWSL system is illuminated and unable to accept an ATC clearance, and the runway is observed clear, what must ATC do if the lights remain illuminated?
items.explanation: Per 3-4-19a1, the lights must be turned off and the clearance re-issued.

choices.text: Turn the lights off and re-issue the clearance.
Tell the pilot to disregard the lights and proceed on the original clearance.
Leave the lights on and wait for the pilot to accept the clearance anyway.
Permanently close the runway until the next shift change.

items.question_text: What does the RWSL operational status message "Lost Comm with System" mean for tower operations?
items.explanation: Per 3-4-19b, ATC considers the RWSL system out of service until technical operations personnel check and confirm it is operational.

choices.text: Treat the RWSL system as out of service until technical operations confirms it is operational.
Reduce the light intensity and continue normal use.
Use the system only for departures, not arrivals.
Assume the message only affects the ASDE display, not RWSL operation.

items.question_text: Once runway status lights are turned off, they must remain off until returned to service by technical operations personnel.
items.explanation: Per 3-4-19c, once the RWSL system is turned off, it remains off until returned to service by technical operations personnel.

items.question_text: Under 3-4-5, between sunrise and sunset, when must approach lights be operated?
items.explanation: Per 3-4-5b, approach lights are operated when the ceiling is less than 1,000 feet or prevailing visibility is 5 miles or less and approaches are being made to the listed runway or airport situations.

choices.text: When the ceiling is less than 1,000 feet or prevailing visibility is 5 miles or less and approaches are being made as specified.
Only when prevailing visibility is less than 1 mile.
Only when the pilot specifically requests the lights.
Only when aircraft are landing on the exact runway served by the lights at night.

items.question_text: Which of the following is specifically listed as a condition for operating approach lights between sunset and sunrise?
items.explanation: Per 3-4-5a, operate approach lights when they serve the landing runway or serve a runway to which an approach is being made even if landing is on another runway.

choices.text: They serve the landing runway.
The airport manager has requested energy conservation.
The runway is closed by NOTAM.
The surface wind is less than 5 knots.

items.question_text: Approach lights may be operated as requested by the pilot and also as the controller deems necessary if not contrary to the pilot's request.
items.explanation: Per 3-4-5c and d, both pilot request and controller judgment are listed conditions.

items.question_text: Under 3-5-1, when wind is 5 knots or more, which runway should generally be assigned?
items.explanation: Per 3-5-1b, assign the runway most nearly aligned with the wind unless another listed exception applies.

choices.text: The runway most nearly aligned with the wind.
Any runway that reduces taxi time the most.
Always the calm-wind runway.
Any runway preferred by the first departing aircraft.

items.question_text: What takes precedence over delay, capacity, and noise-abatement considerations in runway selection?
items.explanation: Per 3-5-1c, tailwind and crosswind considerations take precedence.

choices.text: Tailwind and crosswind considerations.
Traffic-management delay estimates.
Noise-abatement preferences only.
Pilot convenience.

items.question_text: ATC may honor a pilot request for a different runway as soon as operationally practicable, and will advise the pilot if the requested runway is noise-sensitive.
items.explanation: Per 3-5-1d, that is the handling of pilot runway preference requests.

items.question_text: Under 3-8-3, what minimum runway centerline spacing is required when Category I or Category II aircraft are conducting simultaneous same direction operations?
items.explanation: Per Table 3-8-1, the minimum runway centerline spacing is 300 feet.

choices.text: 300 feet.
200 feet.
500 feet.
700 feet.

items.question_text: If either aircraft is a heavy, what minimum runway centerline spacing is required for simultaneous same direction operations?
items.explanation: Per Table 3-8-1, if either aircraft is a heavy, the minimum runway centerline spacing is 700 feet.

choices.text: 700 feet.
500 feet.
600 feet.
300 feet.

items.question_text: Simultaneous same direction operations require two-way radio communication with the aircraft involved and pertinent traffic information issued.
items.explanation: Per 3-8-3b, both radio communication and pertinent traffic information are required conditions.

items.question_text: Under 3-9-1, when may runway, surface wind, and altimeter setting be omitted from departure information?
items.explanation: Per 3-9-1b, these items may be omitted when the pilot states 'have the numbers' as specified.

choices.text: When the pilot states 'have the numbers' for the applicable items.
Whenever ATIS is available, even if the pilot has not acknowledged it.
Whenever the controller is busy and the aircraft is IFR.
Only when visibility is above VFR minimums.

items.question_text: When must the official ceiling and visibility be issued to an IFR departing aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 3-9-1d2, issue it when weather is below VFR conditions or below the highest takeoff minima, whichever is greater.

choices.text: When weather is below VFR conditions or below the highest takeoff minima, whichever is greater.
Only when the pilot requests an IFR release delay.
Only when the aircraft is departing opposite direction.
Only when ATIS is unavailable.

items.question_text: When ATIS is unavailable and the runway length available for departure has been temporarily shortened, controllers must ensure departing pilots receive the runway number with a shortened announcement.
items.explanation: Per 3-9-1j, that announcement must be provided when ATIS is unavailable and runway length is temporarily shortened.

items.question_text: RUNWAY (NUMBER) _____.
items.explanation: Per 3-9-1j, the phraseology is RUNWAY (NUMBER) SHORTENED.

choices.text: SHORTENED

items.question_text: Under 2-9-2, when must a new ATIS recording be made?
items.explanation: Per 2-9-2a, make a new recording upon receipt of any new official weather, when braking action worsens beyond the current ATIS, or when other pertinent data changes.

choices.text: Upon receipt of any new official weather, when braking action worsens beyond the current ATIS, or when other pertinent data changes.
Only when the ATIS code letter changes because of a runway change.
Only when visibility changes by at least one mile.
Only when a supervisor specifically directs a new recording.

items.question_text: If a pilot is unable to receive the ATIS under 2-9-2, what information must be issued?
items.explanation: Per 2-9-2c2, issue the current weather, runway in use, approach or departure information, pertinent NOTAMs, and airport conditions.

choices.text: The current weather, runway in use, approach or departure information, pertinent NOTAMs, and airport conditions.
Only the ATIS code and the runway in use.
Only the altimeter and current approach in use.
Only the weather, because NOTAMs and airport conditions remain the pilot's responsibility.

items.question_text: Under 2-9-2, after a pilot has initially acknowledged the ATIS, no additional acknowledgement is required when the controller later broadcasts pertinent changes to the ATIS code.
items.explanation: Note 1 under 2-9-2 states that no additional acknowledgement is required after the pilot's initial confirmation of the ATIS.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Complete the example from 2-9-2: "Information CHARLIE current. Advise when you have _____."
items.explanation: Per the example in 2-9-2, the controller says: Information CHARLIE current. Advise when you have CHARLIE.

choices.text: CHARLIE

items.question_text: Under 5-9-4, before the aircraft reaches the approach gate, what position information must be issued?
items.explanation: Per 5-9-4a, issue position relative to a fix on the final approach course, or if none is portrayed or prescribed, relative to the navaid providing final approach guidance or the airport.

choices.text: Position relative to a fix on the final approach course, or if necessary relative to the final-approach navaid or the airport.
Only distance from touchdown, regardless of the type of approach being conducted.
Only the aircraft's current altitude and heading.
No position information is required before the approach gate if the aircraft is on vectors.

items.question_text: Under 5-9-4, when may the approach clearance be issued to a non-radar-approach arrival that is not yet established on a published segment?
items.explanation: Per 5-9-4c2, the aircraft may be issued the approach clearance after being assigned an altitude to maintain until established on a published route or instrument approach segment.

choices.text: After assigning an altitude to maintain until the aircraft is established on a published route or approach segment.
At any time before the approach gate, even without an altitude-to-maintain-until-established instruction.
Only after the aircraft reports the runway in sight.
Only after the aircraft contacts tower on the local control frequency.

items.question_text: Under 5-9-4, the altitude assigned before approach clearance must assure IFR obstruction clearance until the aircraft is established on a published route or instrument approach segment.
items.explanation: That requirement appears in Note 1 under 5-9-4c.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Complete the example from 5-9-4: "Over final approach fix. Contact tower one one eight point _____."
items.explanation: Per the example in 5-9-4d4, the frequency is tower one one eight point one.

choices.text: ONE

items.question_text: Under 5-9-6, what diagonal radar separation applies between successive aircraft on adjacent final approach courses when runway centerlines are at least 2,500 feet but no more than 3,600 feet apart?
items.explanation: Per 5-9-6a2, provide a minimum of 1 mile radar separation diagonally between successive aircraft on adjacent finals in that runway-spacing band.

choices.text: 1 mile diagonal radar separation.
1.5 miles diagonal radar separation.
2 miles diagonal radar separation.
3 miles radar separation between all aircraft on adjacent finals.

items.question_text: Under 5-9-6, what diagonal radar separation applies between successive aircraft on adjacent finals when runway centerlines are more than 3,600 feet but no more than 8,300 feet apart?
items.explanation: Per 5-9-6a3, provide 1.5 miles radar separation diagonally between successive aircraft on adjacent finals in that runway-spacing band.

choices.text: 1.5 miles diagonal radar separation.
1 mile diagonal radar separation.
2 miles diagonal radar separation.
4 miles on the same final only; no diagonal separation is used.

items.question_text: Under 5-9-6, apply the diagonal adjacent-final separation standard only after aircraft are established on the parallel final approach course.
items.explanation: That requirement appears in 5-9-6b1.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Complete the requirement from 5-9-6: "Aircraft are informed that approaches to both runways are in _____."
items.explanation: Per 5-9-6b4, aircraft are informed that approaches to both runways are in use.

choices.text: USE

items.question_text: Under 1-1-10, what must be documented in a letter of agreement?
items.explanation: Per 1-1-10, procedures or minima applied jointly or otherwise requiring the cooperation or concurrence of more than one facility or organization must be documented in an LOA.

choices.text: Procedures or minima applied jointly or otherwise requiring the cooperation or concurrence of more than one facility or organization.
Only local phraseology preferences that do not affect another facility.
Only military procedures, because civilian interfacility procedures do not require LOAs.
Only nonstandard runway configurations at a single airport.

items.question_text: How do LOAs relate to FAA Order JO 7110.65 under 1-1-10?
items.explanation: Per 1-1-10, LOAs only supplement the order and may not specify minima less than those in the order except for the stated military-aircraft authorization case.

choices.text: They only supplement the order, and their minima may not be less than the order's minima except for the stated military-aircraft authorization case.
They replace the order whenever two facilities agree to different local procedures or minima.
They may set any lower minima desired as long as both facilities sign them.
They apply only to administrative coordination and may never address procedures or minima.

items.question_text: Under 1-1-10, LOAs only supplement FAA Order JO 7110.65.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 1-1-10.

items.question_text: Under 1-1-10, any minima specified in an LOA may be less than the minima in the order whenever both facilities concur.
items.explanation: That is false. Minima in an LOA may not be less than the order unless appropriate military authority has authorized reduced separation between military aircraft.

items.question_text: Under 1-1-9, how must requests from Air Traffic Services field personnel be submitted when an interpretation or clarification of this order is needed?
items.explanation: Per 1-1-9a1, the request must be submitted in writing by an Air Traffic Manager to the District General Manager, who forwards it in writing to the Service Area Director of Air Traffic Operations through the Operations Support Group.

choices.text: In writing by an Air Traffic Manager to the District General Manager, then forwarded in writing through the Operations Support Group to the Service Area Director of Air Traffic Operations.
Directly by any controller to AJV-P without routing through local management.
Verbally to the Service Area Director during the next teleconference.
Only through the facility's labor representative to the District office.

items.question_text: Under 1-1-9, where must all requests directed to AJV-P under subparagraphs a through e be sent?
items.explanation: Per 1-1-9f, all requests directed to AJV-P under subparagraphs a through e must be sent to the AJV-P correspondence mailbox.

choices.text: The AJV-P correspondence mailbox at 9-AJV-P-HQ-Correspondence@faa.gov.
The local facility quality assurance mailbox.
The MyFAA interpretations webpage only.
The district office distribution officer.

items.question_text: Under 1-1-9, all non-FAA/DoD requests may be submitted directly to AJV-P.
items.explanation: That statement appears in 1-1-9e.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 1-1-9, requests from DoD personnel may be sent directly by the requestor to AJV-P without going through the chain of command or Military Service Headquarters.
items.explanation: Per 1-1-9d, DoD requests must be submitted in writing to the respective Military Service Headquarters via the appropriate chain of command, and that headquarters then submits valid requests to AJV-P.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 1-1-8, where should personnel submit recommended changes in procedures first?
items.explanation: Per 1-1-8a, personnel should submit recommended changes in procedures to facility management.

choices.text: To facility management.
Directly to any published FAA social media account.
Directly to the District General Manager without informing the facility.
Only through the military chain of command, regardless of origin.

items.question_text: Under 1-1-8, what must a proposed procedural-change submission include when sent electronically to AJV-P?
items.explanation: Per 1-1-8c, the submission should include a description of the recommended change and the proposed language to be used in the order.

choices.text: A description of the recommended change and the proposed language to be used in the order.
Only the paragraph number and a short note saying change requested.
Only a marked-up PDF page from the order with no explanatory text.
A local vote of concurrence from every facility that might be affected.

items.question_text: Under 1-1-8, proposed changes must be submitted electronically to the AJV-P correspondence mailbox.
items.explanation: That requirement appears in 1-1-8c.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 1-1-8, procedural changes may be made to this order before the operational system software has been adapted to accomplish the revised procedures if local facilities agree the revision is needed urgently.
items.explanation: Per 1-1-8d, procedural changes will not be made to the order until the operational system software has been adapted to accomplish the revised procedures.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 1-1-7, when will this order be available on the FAA's website before its effective date?
items.explanation: Per 1-1-7a, the order will be available on the FAA's website 30 days prior to its effective date.

choices.text: 30 days prior to its effective date.
On the exact effective date only.
60 days prior to its effective date.
Only after each facility confirms receipt of paper copies.

items.question_text: Under 1-1-7, whom must an FAA facility notify if it has not received the order or changes at least 30 days before the effective date?
items.explanation: Per 1-1-7b, the FAA facility must notify its service area office distribution officer.

choices.text: Its service area office distribution officer.
The nearest military headquarters distribution contact.
The AJV-P correspondence mailbox.
The local airport manager.

items.question_text: Under 1-1-7, if a military facility has not received the order or changes at least 30 days before the effective date, it must notify the appropriate military headquarters.
items.explanation: That statement appears in 1-1-7c.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 1-1-7, an FAA facility that has not received the order at least 30 days before the effective date must notify AJV-P directly rather than its service area office distribution officer.
items.explanation: Per 1-1-7b, the FAA facility notifies its service area office distribution officer.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 1-2-5, what do bold vertical lines in the margin of changed pages indicate?
items.explanation: Per 1-2-5c, bold vertical lines in the margin indicate the location of substantive revisions to the order.

choices.text: The location of substantive revisions to the order.
Only where phraseology appears for the first time.
Pages that were reprinted without any change.
Paragraphs that apply only to military facilities.

items.question_text: Under 1-2-5, what does the annotation PHRASEOLOGY denote?
items.explanation: Per 1-2-5g, the annotation PHRASEOLOGY denotes the prescribed words and/or phrases to be used in communications.

choices.text: The prescribed words and/or phrases to be used in communications.
Suggested informal wording that may replace standard phraseology at any time.
A paragraph that applies only to terminal facilities.
An example that is never expected to approximate actual controller wording.

items.question_text: Under 1-2-5, paragraphs annotated with EN ROUTE, OCEANIC, or TERMINAL apply only to the designated type facility.
items.explanation: That statement appears in 1-2-5d.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 1-2-5, the exact text contained in an example not preceded by specific prescribed phraseology is mandatory and must always be used verbatim.
items.explanation: Per the note in 1-2-5, the exact text in such an example is not mandatory, though controllers are expected to approximate the wording to the extent practical.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 1-2-2, how are intersecting courses with an angular difference of 45 through 135 degrees inclusive classified?
items.explanation: Per 1-2-2b, these are crossing courses.

choices.text: Crossing courses.
Same courses.
Opposite or reciprocal courses.
Diverging protected courses.

items.question_text: What does the note in 1-2-2 say about protected airspace and lateral separation?
items.explanation: Per the note in 1-2-2, if the protected airspace of two aircraft does not overlap, applicable lateral separation is ensured.

choices.text: If the protected airspace of two aircraft does not overlap, applicable lateral separation is ensured.
Protected airspace is one quarter of the applicable lateral separation on each side of the aircraft's projected path.
Protected airspace applies only to opposite-course operations.
Lateral separation exists only when the projected paths are exactly parallel.

items.question_text: Under 1-2-2, same courses are courses whose protected airspaces are coincident, overlap, or intersect and whose angular difference is less than 45 degrees.
items.explanation: That is the definition in 1-2-2a.

items.question_text: Under 1-2-2, opposite or reciprocal courses are courses whose angular difference is 45 through 135 degrees inclusive.
items.explanation: That is false. Opposite or reciprocal courses are greater than 135 through 180 degrees inclusive.

items.question_text: As used in 1-2-1, what does the word "must" mean?
items.explanation: Per 1-2-1a, must means a procedure is mandatory.

choices.text: A procedure is mandatory.
A procedure is recommended.
A procedure is optional.
A future action is anticipated, but not required.

items.question_text: As used in 1-2-1, what does the word "will" mean?
items.explanation: Per 1-2-1e, will means futurity, not a requirement for the application of a procedure.

choices.text: Futurity, not a requirement for the application of a procedure.
A procedure is mandatory.
A procedure is prohibited.
A controller may choose whether or not to comply.

items.question_text: Under the note in 1-2-1, a pilot's use of the phrase "Going Tactical" indicates Due Regard.
items.explanation: Note 1 under 1-2-1 states that a pilot's use of the phrase Going Tactical does not indicate Due Regard.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 3-9-6, before a succeeding aircraft begins takeoff roll behind a preceding departure on the same runway, what must be ensured?
items.explanation: Per 3-9-6a, the preceding aircraft must have departed and crossed the runway end or turned to avert any conflict, unless distance criteria by suitable landmarks are applied as allowed.

choices.text: The preceding aircraft has departed and crossed the runway end or turned to avert any conflict.
The preceding aircraft is merely airborne anywhere above the runway.
The next aircraft has accepted wake turbulence responsibility.
The local controller has already transferred communications to departure.

items.question_text: Under 3-9-6, how must a controller handle a pilot request to deviate from the required wake intervals in subparagraphs f through k?
items.explanation: Per 3-9-6l, the controller must not approve pilot requests to deviate from those required intervals.

choices.text: Do not approve the request.
Approve it if the pilot says they will accept the wake risk.
Approve it only for Category I aircraft.
Approve it whenever the runway is dry and visibility is good.

items.question_text: Under 3-9-6, a pilot may request additional separation, but the request should be made before taxiing on the runway.
items.explanation: That note appears under 3-9-6e.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 3-9-7, how long must a Category I aircraft wait before beginning takeoff roll from an intersection on the same runway behind a departing Category F, G, or H aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 3-9-7a1, the aircraft must not start takeoff roll until at least 3 minutes after the preceding aircraft has taken off.

choices.text: At least 3 minutes after the preceding aircraft has taken off.
At least 2 minutes after the preceding aircraft has taken off.
Until the preceding aircraft crosses the departure end, with no fixed time requirement.
Only until the runway is clear of the preceding aircraft.

items.question_text: Under 3-9-7, what must a controller do when it is necessary for an aircraft to hold in order to provide the required time interval?
items.explanation: Per 3-9-7a4, inform the aircraft when it is necessary to hold in order to provide the required time interval.

choices.text: Inform the aircraft that it must hold to provide the required time interval.
Allow the aircraft to taxi into position and absorb the interval on the runway.
Issue no advisory unless the pilot asks why there is a delay.
Transfer the aircraft to departure control to wait for the interval.

items.question_text: Under 3-9-7, inform aircraft when it is necessary to hold in order to provide the required time interval.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 3-9-7a4.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 5-7-2, what phraseology is used to obtain pilot concurrence for a speed adjustment at or above FL390?
items.explanation: Per 5-7-2b, use '(speed adjustment), IF UNABLE ADVISE.'

choices.text: (Speed adjustment), IF UNABLE ADVISE.
(Speed adjustment), ROGER REQUIRED.
(Speed adjustment), SAY AIRSPEED NOW.
(Speed adjustment), COMPLY IMMEDIATELY.

items.question_text: Under 5-7-2, when issuing a speed adjustment to aircraft cleared on procedures with published speed restrictions, what must the controller specify?
items.explanation: Per 5-7-2e, specify the point at which the issued restriction begins, ends, or changes the published restrictions.

choices.text: The point at which the issued restriction begins, ends, or changes the published restrictions.
Only the assigned speed; the start and end point are assumed by the pilot.
Only the published chart note authorizing speed control.
Nothing additional, because assigned speed automatically cancels all published restrictions.

items.question_text: Under 5-7-2, the phrases 'maintain maximum forward speed' and 'maintain slowest practical speed' are primarily intended for use when sequencing a group of aircraft.
items.explanation: That statement appears in Note 3 under 5-7-2a.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 5-9-10, when may simultaneous independent approaches to widely-spaced parallel runways without final monitors be conducted?
items.explanation: Per 5-9-10a, they may only be conducted where instrument approach charts specifically authorize simultaneous approaches.

choices.text: Only where instrument approach charts specifically authorize simultaneous approaches.
Whenever runway centerlines are more than 9,000 feet apart, regardless of chart authorization.
Only when final monitors are available but not staffed.
Whenever an NTZ has been established by tower directive.

items.question_text: Under 5-9-10, what minimum separation must be provided between aircraft during turn-on to parallel final approach, or during EoR operations until aircraft are established on a published authorized segment?
items.explanation: Per 5-9-10b1, provide a minimum of 1,000 feet vertical or 3 miles radar separation in that phase.

choices.text: A minimum of 1,000 feet vertical or 3 miles radar separation.
A minimum of 500 feet vertical or 2 miles radar separation.
No separation requirement after the approach clearance is issued.
An NTZ-based buffer only, with no vertical or radar requirement.

items.question_text: Under 5-9-10, aircraft on the final must be on the appropriate final controller frequency for that runway.
items.explanation: That statement appears directly in 5-9-10c5.

choices.text: True
False
